where $l_{clamp}$ is the velocity-saturation clamp length. For sub-100 nm devices, $l_{clamp} \approx 30$–$50$ nm, while $V_{ds} - V_{dsat}$ can still be 0.5–1.5 V. This yields fields exceeding **3 × 10⁵ V/cm** — the regime where hot-carrier physics dominates.

---

## 2.2 The "Lucky Electron" Model (Shockley, 1961; refined by Hu, 1979)

This is the **canonical KTU-asked model** for hot-electron injection. It treats the problem as a sequence of scattering events that an electron must survive to become a "lucky" injector.

### 2.2.1 Three Sequential Lucky Events

For an electron originating at the source to be injected into the gate oxide, it must pass through three probabilistic "filters":

1. **Lucky to gain energy without collision** (red-lucky): The electron must travel a distance $d$ from the pinch-off point toward the drain *without scattering*, gaining energy $qE_y d$ from the field.
2. **Lucky to redirect momentum toward the Si-SiO₂ interface** (momentum-redirection-lucky): Its momentum must have a vertical component to overcome the oxide barrier.
3. **Lucky to traverse the image-force-lowered barrier** (oxide-barrier-lucky): It must have sufficient energy to surmount $\Phi_B$.

### 2.2.2 The Injection Probability

The gate-current density due to channel hot electrons (CHE) is:

$$I_{gate} = I_d \cdot P_{inj}$$

where the injection probability (lucky-electron model) is:

$$P_{inj} = C_1 \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{\Phi_B}{E_m \lambda}\right)$$

with:
- $C_1$ = process-dependent pre-exponential factor (≈ 0.5–5)
- $\Phi_B$ = Si-SiO₂ energy barrier ≈ **3.2 eV**
- $\lambda$ = mean free path of hot electron in Si inversion layer ≈ **9.2 nm** (10 nm typically)
- $E_m$ = peak (maximum) lateral electric field at the drain end

---

## 2.3 Impact Ionization & Substrate Current

When channel electrons gain energy greater than the bandgap $E_g = 1.12$ eV, they can **knock an electron out of the Si valence band**, creating an electron-hole pair. The multiplication factor is:

$$M = \frac{I_d}{I_d - I_{sub}}$$

The **substrate current** (a KTU-favorite measurable quantity) is:

$$I_{sub} = (M-1) \cdot I_d \approx I_d \cdot A_i \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{B_i}{E_m}\right)$$

where:
- $A_i$ ≈ 7 × 10⁵ cm⁻¹
- $B_i$ ≈ 1.23 × 10⁶ V/cm (Si effective ionization field coefficient)
- $E_m$ = maximum channel electric field

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **$I_{sub}$ is the most common experimental proxy for HCI stress.** Because $I_{sub}$ is exponentially sensitive to $E_m$, even tiny variations in $V_{ds}$ cause order-of-magnitude changes in $I_{sub}$.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Quick Reference)

| # | Formula | Meaning / Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_m = \dfrac{V_{ds} - V_{dsat}}{\sqrt{\alpha \cdot x_j \cdot L_{eff}}}$ | Maximum lateral field at drain pinch-off. Used to assess HCI risk. |
| 2 | $P_{inj} = C_1 \exp\left(-\dfrac{\Phi_B}{E_m \lambda}\right)$ | Lucky-electron gate injection probability. |
| 3 | $I_{sub} \approx I_d \cdot A_i \exp\left(-\dfrac{B_i}{E_m}\right)$ | Substrate current — measurable HCI sensor. |
| 4 | $I_{gate} = I_d \cdot P_{inj}$ | Gate oxide hot-electron current. |
| 5 | $I_{sub,max} \approx I_d \cdot (C_2 \cdot V_{ds} \cdot L_{eff}^{-3})$ | Empirically $I_{sub,max}$ peaks at $V_{gs} \approx V_{ds}/2$. |
| 6 | $\Delta V_{th} = A \cdot t^n \cdot \exp\left(\dfrac{\beta}{E_{ox}}\right)$ | Threshold voltage degradation law (power-law aging). |
| 7 | $\tau = \tau_0 \cdot \left(\dfrac{I_{sub,ref}}{I_{sub}}\right)^m$ | Device lifetime model. KTU standard: $m \approx 2.5$–$3.5$ for NMOS. |
| 8 | $E_{ox} = \dfrac{V_{gs} - V_{th} - \Phi_{s}}{t_{ox}}$ | Oxide field that attracts hot electrons toward the gate. |
| 9 | $v_{sat} \approx 1 \times 10^7$ cm/s | Saturation drift velocity in Si. Defines pinch-off. |
| 10 | $I_b = I_{sub} \cdot (1 - \alpha_p)$ | Body (substrate) terminal current at operating point. |

> [!NOTE]
> The **vertical bars** in formulas (such as $E_m$ magnitude) are intentionally written as `\vert` to keep markdown tables safe.

---

## 2.5 Why "How" and "Why" — Physical Insight

- **Why does the field concentrate near the drain?** Because of the abrupt transition from a conducting channel to a depleted drift region. The potential drop is concentrated in $l_{clamp}$, a sub-100 nm region.
- **Why are NMOS devices more vulnerable than PMOS?** Because electrons have higher impact-ionization rates than holes (lower effective mass → higher kinetic energy at the same field). NMOS hot-electron lifetime is **~10× shorter** than PMOS hot-hole lifetime at the same field.
- **Why is $V_{gs} \approx V_{ds}/2$ the worst case for $I_{sub}$?** Because maximum $I_{sub}$ requires *both* high $V_{ds}$ (high $E_m$) *and* moderate $V_{gs}$ (large $I_d$ flowing into the high-field region). At $V_{gs} = V_{th}$, $I_d$ is tiny; at $V_{gs} = V_{ds}$, the channel is open but vertical field pulls electrons to the drain electrode instead of into the substrate.
- **Why does device degradation follow a power law?** Because damage accumulates gradually as localized traps fill up. A power law (vs. exponential) reflects the *saturation* of trap filling at the local injection site.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Reliability sign-off in foundry PDKs**: SPICE models include an HCI aging subcircuit (e.g., BSIM-CMG HCI model).
- **Worst-case corner simulation**: Designers run **10-year lifetime extrapolation** at maximum $V_{ds}$, $T = 125$ °C.
- **Automotive / aerospace ICs**: AEC-Q100 mandates HCI qualification — directly tied to $I_{sub}$ measurements.
- **Low-power IoT nodes**: HCI stress during rare high-frequency transmission bursts can dominate the *end-of-life* failure budget even though DC power is small.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Maximum Lateral Field $E_m$ in a Short-Channel MOSFET

### 3.1.1 Setup

Consider an NMOS transistor in saturation. The 2D Poisson equation in the pinch-off region yields the **lateral field** $E_y$ at the surface. For a Gaussian doping profile with junction depth $x_j$ and effective channel length $L_{eff}$, the peak field is approximated as:

$$E_m = \frac{V_{ds} - V_{dsat}}{\sqrt{\alpha \cdot x_j \cdot L_{eff}}}$$

where $\alpha$ is a fitting parameter (typically 0.2–0.5 depending on substrate doping).

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

We start with the 2D Poisson equation in the pinch-off region, assuming the gradual channel approximation has broken down:

$$\frac{\partial^2 \psi}{\partial x^2} + \frac{\partial^2 \psi}{\partial y^2} = \frac{q N_a}{\varepsilon_{si}}$$

In the velocity-saturation clamp region (length $l_{clamp}$), the current continuity gives:

$$I_d = W C_{ox} (V_{gs} - V_{th}) v_{sat}$$

The potential drop in the clamp region is $V_{ds} - V_{dsat}$. Treating the clamp as a 2D resistive region with effective depth $\sqrt{\alpha x_j L_{eff}}$:

### Step 1 — Effective clamp length
The 2D spreading of field lines into the bulk gives an effective depth proportional to $\sqrt{x_j L_{eff}}$:

$$l_{eff} = \sqrt{\alpha \cdot x_j \cdot L_{eff}}$$

### Step 2 — Apply Ohm's-law analogue
The lateral field averaged over the clamp region is the voltage drop divided by effective depth:

$$E_m = \frac{V_{ds} - V_{dsat}}{l_{eff}}$$

### Step 3 — Substitute $l_{eff}$

$$E_m = \frac{V_{ds} - V_{dsat}}{\sqrt{\alpha \cdot x_j \cdot L_{eff}}}$$

### Step 4 — Apply to a 90 nm node example

Given: $V_{ds} = 1.2$ V, $V_{dsat} = 0.6$ V, $x_j = 30$ nm, $L_{eff} = 90$ nm, $\alpha = 0.3$.

### Step 5 — Numerical evaluation

First compute the denominator:

$$\sqrt{\alpha \cdot x_j \cdot L_{eff}} = \sqrt{0.3 \times 30 \times 90} \text{ nm} = \sqrt{810} \text{ nm}$$

$$\sqrt{810} \approx 28.46 \text{ nm}$$

Then:

$$E_m = \frac{1.2 - 0.6}{28.46 \times 10^{-7} \text{ cm}} = \frac{0.6}{2.846 \times 10^{-6}} \text{ V/cm}$$

$$E_m \approx 2.11 \times 10^5 \text{ V/cm}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This value **exceeds $E_{crit} \sim 1.5 \times 10^5$ V/cm**, confirming that a 90 nm NMOS at $V_{ds} = 1.2$ V operates deep in the hot-electron regime. HCI mitigation (LDD, pocket implants, lightly doped drain extensions) is mandatory.

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Substrate Current Analytical Expression

### 3.2.1 Starting Point — Impact Ionization Rate

The **ionization coefficient** $\alpha_n$ (carriers ionized per unit length) follows the Chynoweth law:

$$\alpha_n = A_n \exp\left(-\frac{B_n}{E_y}\right)$$

where $A_n \approx 7 \times 10^5$ cm⁻¹ and $B_n \approx 1.23 \times 10^6$ V/cm for electrons in Si.

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Derivation of $I_{sub}$

### Step 1 — Multiplication integral
The number of secondary pairs generated per primary electron is the integral of $\alpha_n$ along the field path near the drain:

$$M - 1 = \int_0^{l_{clamp}} A_n \exp\left(-\frac{B_n}{E_y(x)}\right) dx$$

### Step 2 — Approximate constant peak field
Because $E_y(x)$ drops sharply away from the drain, the dominant contribution comes from a thin region near the drain where $E_y \approx E_m$. With effective integration length $l_{eff} = \sqrt{\alpha x_j L_{eff}}$:

$$M - 1 \approx A_n \cdot l_{eff} \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{B_n}{E_m}\right)$$

### Step 3 — Multiply by drain current

$$I_{sub} = (M - 1) \cdot I_d$$

$$\boxed{I_{sub} = I_d \cdot A_i \cdot l_{eff} \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{B_i}{E_m}\right)}$$

### Step 4 — Substitute $l_{eff} = \sqrt{\alpha x_j L_{eff}}$

$$I_{sub} = I_d \cdot A_i \cdot \sqrt{\alpha x_j L_{eff}} \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{B_i}{E_m}\right)$$

### Step 5 — Define lumped constant $A_i^*$

Combining the prefactor into a single empirically fit constant $A_i$:

$$I_{sub} \approx I_d \cdot A_i \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{B_i}{E_m}\right)$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation — Device Lifetime from Substrate Current

The standard industry lifetime model (Hu, Takeda, et al.) uses the substrate current as a damage proxy:

### Step 1 — Empirical degradation rate
The threshold voltage shift grows as:

$$\Delta V_{th}(t) = C \cdot t^n$$

where $n \approx 0.5$ for typical NMOS HCI and $C$ is process-dependent.

### Step 2 — Failure criterion
A device is considered failed when $|\Delta V_{th}| = \Delta V_{th,fail}$ (typically 50 mV).

### Step 3 — Solve for time-to-failure
Setting $\Delta V_{th}(\tau) = \Delta V_{th,fail}$:

$$\tau = \left(\frac{\Delta V_{th,fail}}{C}\right)^{1/n}$$

### Step 4 — Express $C$ in terms of $I_{sub}$
Empirically, $C \propto I_{sub}^m$ where $m \approx 2.5$–$3.5$. Thus:

$$\tau \propto I_{sub}^{-m}$$

### Step 5 — Reference normalization

$$\boxed{\tau = \tau_0 \cdot \left(\frac{I_{sub,ref}}{I_{sub}}\right)^m}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The exponent $m$ is the **most frequently asked numerical value** in ESE questions. Remember: **$m \approx 2.5$ for NMOS (electrons), $m \approx 2.9$ for PMOS (holes)** under typical conditions.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of HCI Sub-phenomena

| Phenomenon | Carrier | Energy Source | Failure Mode | Dominant at |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Channel Hot Electron (CHE) | e⁻ | Lateral $E_y$ | $V_{th}$ shift, $g_m$ drop | High $V_{ds}$, mid $V_{gs}$ |
| Channel Hot Hole (CHH) | h⁺ | Lateral $E_y$ | $V_{th}$ shift (opposite sign) | High $V_{ds}$, low $V_{gs}$ |
| Substrate Hot Electron (SHE) | e⁻ from substrate | Heated by substrate bias | Buried-oxide damage | SOI / stressed body bias |
| Drain-Avalanche Hot Carrier (DAHC) | e⁻ + h⁺ | Avalanche breakdown at drain | Snap-back, $V_{th}$ shift | $V_{ds} > BV_{dss}$ |
| Secondary Impact Ionization | e⁻ | Feedback multiplication | Latch-up trigger | Very high $V_{ds}$, kink regime |

---

## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example — Lifetime Estimation

**Problem:** A 65 nm NMOS operates at $V_{ds} = 1.1$ V, $I_{sub} = 5 \mu$A. The reference device has $I_{sub,ref} = 50 \mu$A and reached 50 mV $V_{th}$ shift in 10 years. Given $m = 3.0$, find the lifetime of the new device.

### Step 1 — Substitute into the lifetime formula

$$\tau = \tau_0 \cdot \left(\frac{I_{sub,ref}}{I_{sub}}\right)^m$$

### Step 2 — Plug numerical values

$$\tau = 10 \text{ years} \cdot \left(\frac{50}{5}\right)^{3.0} = 10 \cdot 10^3 = 10{,}000 \text{ years}$$

### Step 3 — Interpretation
The new device's $I_{sub}$ is **10× lower**, yielding **1000× longer lifetime** due to the cubic exponent. This demonstrates the *super-linear* sensitivity of reliability to operating voltage.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Hot Electron Generation and Damage Pathways

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Source-Drain Bias Applied"] --> nodeB["Channel Electrons Enter Pinch-Off Region"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Lateral Field E_y Drains Energy into Electrons"]
    nodeC --> nodeD{"Electron Energy vs 3.2 eV Barrier?"}
    nodeD -- Below Barrier --> nodeE["Drain Electrode Collection"]
    nodeD -- Above Barrier --> nodeF["Hot Electron Population"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["Impact Ionization Event"]
    nodeF --> nodeH["Oxide Injection Path"]
    nodeF --> nodeI["Substrate Injection Path"]
    nodeG --> nodeJ["Electron-Hole Pair Created"]
    nodeJ --> nodeK["Substrate Current I_sub"]
    nodeJ --> nodeL["Body Terminal Current"]
    nodeH --> nodeM["Gate Oxide Trap Filling"]
    nodeM --> nodeN["V_th Shift and g_m Degradation"]
    nodeI --> nodeM
    nodeN --> nodeO["Device Aging and Failure"]
    nodeK --> nodeO
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — HCI Reliability Verification Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    stepA["Define Mission Profile"] --> stepB["Identify Worst-Case V_ds and T"]
    stepB --> stepC["Measure I_sub on Test Structure"]
    stepC --> stepD{"I_sub within Safe Envelope?"}
    stepD -- No --> stepE["Apply HCI Mitigation: LDD, Pocket Implant, V_ds Reduction"]
    stepE --> stepF["Re-Simulate I_sub"]
    stepF --> stepC
    stepD -- Yes --> stepG["Compute Lifetime Tau using m exponent"]
    stepG --> stepH{"Tau exceeds 10 Years?"}
    stepH -- No --> stepE
    stepH -- Yes --> stepI["Sign-Off Approved"]
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Bias-Dependent Damage Mechanism Selector

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    biasA["V_gs and V_ds Operating Point"] --> biasB{"V_gs Compared to V_ds / 2"}
    biasB -- Below --> biasC["Hot Hole Dominant CHH"]
    biasB -- Near --> biasD["Peak I_sub Maximum HCI"]
    biasB -- Above --> biasE["Che Injection Dominant CHE"]
    biasC --> biasF["V_th Negative Shift"]
    biasD --> biasG["Interface Trap Creation"]
    biasE --> biasH["Oxide Charge Trapping"]
    biasF --> biasI["PMOS-like Aging on NMOS"]
    biasG --> biasJ["Standard NMOS Aging"]
    biasH --> biasJ
```

---

## 4.4 Schematic of Energy Band Diagram (ASCII Representation)

```
Energy (eV)
   ^
   |        E_c (Conduction Band)        ___________
   |                                  /
   |                                /   <-- E_m region
   |                              /         (steep slope)
   |                            /     ____
   |                          /   /    
   |                        /  /      <-- Hot electrons
   |______________________/__/__________________ E_c, source
   |                  /
   |              <-- Si/SiO2 barrier Phi_B = 3.2 eV
   |              /
   |____________/______________________________ E_v (Valence Band)
   |          /
   |        Gate
   |       SiO2         Si        Drain
   |_______________________________________> Position
       Source          Channel    (high V_ds)
```

- The **steep slope** near the drain = high $E_y$
- The **Si/SiO₂ barrier** = 3.2 eV for electrons
- Hot electrons that scatter toward the gate must **clear the barrier** to enter the oxide

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]`
**Define the Hot Electron Effect in a short-channel MOSFET. State the energy threshold for electron injection across the Si-SiO₂ barrier.**

**Model Answer:**

The Hot Electron Effect is the phenomenon in short-channel MOSFETs where electrons traversing the high lateral electric field near the drain acquire kinetic energies far exceeding the thermal energy ($kT \approx 0.025$ eV at 300 K). When a carrier's energy surpasses the Si-SiO₂ conduction-band offset, it can be injected into the gate oxide, leading to interface trap creation, fixed oxide charge accumulation, and gradual device degradation.

**Energy threshold:** $\Phi_B \approx \mathbf{3.2 \text{ eV}}$ for electron injection from Si into SiO₂.

The corresponding field threshold to reach this energy over a mean free path of $\lambda \approx 10$ nm is $E_m \approx \Phi_B / \lambda \approx 3.2 \times 10^6$ V/cm, but in practice energy *gain* across multiple scattering events reaches 3.2 eV at much lower fields, around $1 \times 10^5$ V/cm.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Sample Paper]`
**Why is the substrate current $I_{sub}$ used as a monitor for hot-carrier reliability?**

**Model Answer:**

The substrate current is generated by impact ionization in the high-field region near the drain. Because impact ionization is exponentially sensitive to the peak lateral field $E_m$ (via the relation $I_{sub} \propto \exp(-B_i/E_m)$), $I_{sub}$ serves as a **direct, experimentally measurable proxy** for the maximum channel field. The device lifetime scales as $\tau \propto I_{sub}^{-m}$ with $m \approx 2.5$–$3.5$, so a small change in $I_{sub}$ produces a large change in predicted lifetime. This makes $I_{sub}$ an indispensable parameter for HCI sign-off in foundry PDKs.

---

## Part B — 14-Mark ESE Module Internal Choice

### Question A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Supplemented]`

**(a)** Derive the analytical expression for the maximum lateral electric field $E_m$ in a short-channel MOSFET. Use the result to compute $E_m$ for a device with $V_{ds} = 1.2$ V, $V_{dsat} = 0.6$ V, $x_j = 30$ nm, $L_{eff} = 90$ nm, and $\alpha = 0.3$. Comment on whether hot-carrier degradation is a concern. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the Lucky Electron Model for hot-electron injection into the gate oxide. Derive the gate current expression and identify the three sequential "lucky" events required for injection. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Step 1 — State the formula** for the peak lateral field:

$$E_m = \frac{V_{ds} - V_{dsat}}{\sqrt{\alpha \cdot x_j \cdot L_{eff}}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values**:

$$\sqrt{0.3 \times 30 \times 90} = \sqrt{810} \approx 28.46 \text{ nm}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $E_m$**:

$$E_m = \frac{0.6 \text{ V}}{28.46 \text{ nm}} = \frac{0.6}{2.846 \times 10^{-6} \text{ cm}} \approx 2.11 \times 10^5 \text{ V/cm}$$

**Step 4 — Comment**: Since $E_m \approx 2.11 \times 10^5$ V/cm substantially exceeds the critical field for impact ionization ($E_{crit} \sim 1.5 \times 10^5$ V/cm), this device operates deep in the hot-carrier regime. HCI mitigation is required.

#### Model Solution to (b):

The Lucky Electron Model treats injection as a probabilistic event requiring **three independent "lucky" steps** in series:

1. **Energy Lucky:** An electron must travel from the pinch-off point a distance $d$ *without scattering*, gaining energy $qE_y d$ from the field. Probability $\propto \exp(-d/\lambda)$.
2. **Momentum Redirection Lucky:** The electron's momentum must have a vertical component directed toward the Si-SiO₂ interface. The angular probability scales as $1/\sqrt{E_y}$.
3. **Barrier Lucky:** The electron must have sufficient energy to overcome the image-force-lowered Si-SiO₂ barrier $\Phi_B \approx 3.2$ eV.

Combining these yields the gate current:

$$I_{gate} = I_d \cdot C_1 \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{\Phi_B}{E_m \lambda}\right)$$

with $C_1$ being a process-dependent prefactor (≈ 0.5–5) and $\lambda \approx 10$ nm. The exponential dependence on $1/E_m$ makes $I_{gate}$ highly sensitive to $V_{ds}$.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Adapted]`

**(a)** With a neat schematic, explain the impact ionization process in a short-channel NMOS transistor. Derive the substrate current expression $I_{sub} = I_d \cdot A_i \cdot \exp(-B_i/E_m)$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using the lifetime model $\tau = \tau_0 (I_{sub,ref}/I_{sub})^m$ with $m = 3.0$, calculate the lifetime improvement achieved by reducing $V_{ds}$ from 1.2 V to 1.0 V, given that $E_m \propto (V_{ds} - V_{dsat})$ and $V_{dsat} = 0.6$ V. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

When channel electrons gain kinetic energy greater than the bandgap $E_g = 1.12$ eV in the high-field pinch-off region, they can **knock a valence electron into the conduction band**, generating an electron-hole pair. This is **impact ionization**. The resulting hole flows to the substrate, constituting $I_{sub}$.

**Derivation:**

Step 1 — The ionization rate per unit length follows Chynoweth's law:

$$\alpha_n = A_n \exp(-B_n/E_y)$$

Step 2 — Multiplication factor integral over the high-field region of effective length $l_{eff}$:

$$M - 1 = \alpha_n \cdot l_{eff} = A_n l_{eff} \exp(-B_n/E_m)$$

Step 3 — Substrate current equals primary current times $(M-1)$:

$$I_{sub} = I_d \cdot A_n l_{eff} \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{B_n}{E_m}\right)$$

Step 4 — Define lumped constant $A_i = A_n \cdot l_{eff}$:

$$\boxed{I_{sub} = I_d \cdot A_i \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{B_i}{E_m}\right)}$$

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Step 1 — Compute $E_m$ at $V_{ds} = 1.2$ V**:

$$E_{m,1} \propto (V_{ds,1} - V_{dsat}) = 1.2 - 0.6 = 0.6 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $E_m$ at $V_{ds} = 1.0$ V**:

$$E_{m,2} \propto (V_{ds,2} - V_{dsat}) = 1.0 - 0.6 = 0.4 \text{ V}$$

**Step 3 — Substrate current ratio** (since $I_{sub} \propto I_d \cdot \exp(-B_i/E_m)$, and assuming $I_d$ roughly constant):

$$\frac{I_{sub,1}}{I_{sub,2}} = \exp\left[B_i \left(\frac{1}{E_{m,2}} - \frac{1}{E_{m,1}}\right)\right]$$

Using representative $B_i = 1.23 \times 10^6$ V/cm and normalizing $E_{m,1} = 0.6 E_{m,ref}$ with $E_{m,ref}$ such that $B_i/E_{m,1} = 5$ (a typical HCI-bias operating point):

$$\frac{1}{E_{m,2}} - \frac{1}{E_{m,1}} = \frac{1}{0.4} - \frac{1}{0.6} = 2.5 - 1.667 = 0.833 \text{ (in units of 1/E_{m,ref})}$$

$$\frac{I_{sub,1}}{I_{sub,2}} = \exp(5 \times 0.833) = \exp(4.17) \approx 64.5$$

**Step 4 — Lifetime improvement**:

$$\frac{\tau_2}{\tau_1} = \left(\frac{I_{sub,1}}{I_{sub,2}}\right)^m = (64.5)^{3.0}$$

$$(64.5)^{3.0} = 64.5 \times 64.5 \times 64.5 \approx 2.68 \times 10^5$$

**Step 5 — Conclusion**: Reducing $V_{ds}$ from 1.2 V to 1.0 V extends the device lifetime by a factor of **~2.7 × 10⁵**, demonstrating the dramatic reliability benefit of supply-voltage scaling in low-power VLSI.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Mixing up the units of $E_m$:** Always convert nm to cm before applying the $B_i/E_m$ exponential. $B_i = 1.23 \times 10^6$ V/cm.
> 2. **Forgetting the mean free path $\lambda$:** Students often write $\exp(-\Phi_B/E_m)$ instead of $\exp(-\Phi_B/(E_m \lambda))$. The full expression for $P_{inj}$ requires $\lambda$.
> 3. **Using $V_{ds}$ instead of $(V_{ds} - V_{dsat})$ in the $E_m$ formula:** $E_m$ arises only from the *post-pinch-off* potential drop.
> 4. **Hardcoding $m = 3$** for every device: The exponent $m$ is *process- and technology-dependent*; always state the assumed value.
> 5. **Ignoring temperature dependence:** HCI worsens at *low* $T$ (longer $\lambda$, higher $I_{sub}$) and is partially masked at high $T$ by phonon scattering — a common viva question.
> 6. **Confusing CHE and SHE:** Channel Hot Electrons originate from the channel; Substrate Hot Electrons are injected from the body terminal.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Hot Electron Effect** = high-field transport in short-channel MOSFETs; carriers gain energy > thermal $kT$.
- **Critical energy**: $\Phi_B = 3.2$ eV (Si-SiO₂ electron barrier); $\Phi_B^h = 4.5$ eV (hole barrier).
- **Worst-case bias**: $V_{gs} \approx V_{ds}/2$ gives **maximum $I_{sub}$** and therefore worst HCI stress.
- **Substrate current** is the **experimental reliability proxy**: $I_{sub} \approx I_d A_i \exp(-B_i/E_m)$.
- **Gate injection probability** (Lucky Electron): $P_{inj} = C_1 \exp(-\Phi_B/(E_m \lambda))$ with $\lambda \approx 10$ nm.
- **Lifetime law**: $\tau = \tau_0 (I_{sub,ref}/I_{sub})^m$ with $m \approx 2.5$ (NMOS) and $m \approx 2.9$ (PMOS).
- **NMOS is ~10× more HCI-sensitive than PMOS** due to higher electron impact-ionization rate.
- **Mitigation techniques**: LDD (Lightly Doped Drain), pocket / halo implants, $V_{ds}$ reduction, retrograde well, thicker $t_{ox}$.
- **Power-law aging**: $\Delta V_{th} = A t^n$, with $n \approx 0.5$.
- **$E_m$ formula**: $E_m = (V_{ds} - V_{dsat})/\sqrt{\alpha x_j L_{eff}}$.
- **Industry standard**: 10-year lifetime at $V_{ds,max}$ and $T = 125$ °C is the typical qualification target.
- **AEC-Q100 / JEDEC HCI tests** are mandatory for automotive, military, and medical ICs.
- **In low-power VLSI**: HCI stress during *transient peaks* (e.g., RF burst, charge-pump start-up) can dominate end-of-life failure budget.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Body Effect
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT866 - LOW POWER VLSI
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Body Effect

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In MOSFET physics, the **Body Effect** (also called the **Substrate Bias Effect** or **Back-Gate Effect**) is defined as the modulation of the threshold voltage ($V_{TH}$) of a MOSFET caused by a non-zero potential difference developed between the **Source** and the **Body (Substrate/Bulk)** terminal.

Mathematically, it is expressed through the dependence of $V_{TH}$ on the **Source-to-Body Voltage** ($V_{SB}$). When $V_{SB} > 0$ (for an NMOS transistor), the threshold voltage **increases**, requiring a larger gate-to-source voltage ($V_{GS}$) to form the inversion layer (channel).

The fundamental threshold voltage equation incorporating the body effect is:

$$V_{TH} = V_{TH0} + \gamma \left( \sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}} - \sqrt{2\phi_F} \right)$$

Where:
- $V_{TH0}$ = Zero-bias threshold voltage (when $V_{SB} = 0$)
- $\gamma$ = **Body Effect Coefficient** (a technology parameter, typically $0.3$–$0.7\ \text{V}^{1/2}$)
- $\phi_F$ = **Fermi Potential** of the substrate
- $V_{SB}$ = Source-to-Body reverse bias voltage

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The body effect is a *parasitic effect* in standard bulk CMOS that is undesirable because it **degrades circuit performance** (lower drive current, slower switching) and complicates analog design. It is one of the primary reasons modern low-power designs employ **SOI (Silicon-On-Insulator)** technology or **multi-threshold (MTCMOS)** techniques.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Inflating Balloon" Model

Imagine a **balloon** representing the **depletion region** beneath the gate oxide of a MOSFET.

- The **gate voltage** ($V_{GS}$) is the person trying to *squeeze* the balloon flat to form a conducting channel.
- The **body terminal voltage** ($V_{SB}$) is *additional air being pumped into the balloon* from below.

**Scenario 1 (No Body Effect):** If the body is tied to the source ($V_{SB}=0$), the balloon has a fixed amount of air. You only need a moderate "squeeze" (gate voltage) to flatten it — this corresponds to a *lower threshold voltage* $V_{TH0}$.

**Scenario 2 (With Body Effect):** If the body is biased *negatively relative to the source* (for NMOS, $V_{SB}>0$), extra air is pumped into the balloon from below. Now, the balloon is *larger and harder to squeeze flat*. You must apply a **stronger squeeze** (higher $V_{GS}$) — this is the increased $V_{TH}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this happens physically:** Applying $V_{SB}>0$ widens the depletion region beneath the channel because the source-body junction is reverse-biased. A wider depletion region contains **more immobile ionized acceptor charges** ($Q_B$). To form an inversion layer, the gate must now induce a charge equal to $Q_B$ **plus** the inversion charge — hence, a higher $V_{GS}$ is needed, raising $V_{TH}$.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bold for Emphasis)

- **Electron charge:** $q = 1.6 \times 10^{-19}\ \text{C}$
- **Silicon permittivity:** $\varepsilon_{si} = 11.7 \times \varepsilon_0 = 1.04 \times 10^{-12}\ \text{F/cm}$
- **Oxide permittivity:** $\varepsilon_{ox} = 3.97 \times \varepsilon_0 = 3.45 \times 10^{-13}\ \text{F/cm}$
- **Oxide capacitance per unit area:** $C_{ox} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}}$
- **Body effect coefficient ($\gamma$):** typically in the range **$0.3\ \text{V}^{1/2}$ to $0.7\ \text{V}^{1/2}$** for modern processes
- **Intrinsic carrier concentration of Si:** $n_i \approx 1.45 \times 10^{10}\ \text{cm}^{-3}$ at 300 K

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔬 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Physical Origin of the Body Effect — Step-by-Step Logic

The body effect arises from the **charge balancing** required at the gate-oxide/silicon interface. Let us break down the underlying physics:

1. **Gate Capacitance Formation:** When a positive gate voltage $V_{GS}$ is applied to an NMOS, an electric field is established across the gate oxide. This field attracts electrons toward the oxide-silicon interface.

2. **Depletion Region Formation:** Before inversion, a depletion region of width $W_D$ forms beneath the oxide, containing uncovered (ionized) acceptor atoms ($N_A^-$). This depletion charge is:
$$Q_B = -q N_A W_D$$

3. **Effect of Reverse-Biasing the Body:** Applying $V_{SB} > 0$ reverse-biases the source-body p-n junction. This widens $W_D$ because more ionized acceptors are uncovered.

4. **Increased Charge Required for Inversion:** The gate must now supply additional charge to compensate the larger $Q_B$ before it can induce mobile electrons. Hence, $V_{TH}$ rises.

5. **Threshold Voltage Shift:** The shift $\Delta V_{TH} = V_{TH} - V_{TH0}$ is directly proportional to the *change in depletion charge*, which depends on $\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}}$.

## 2.2 Why the Square-Root Dependence?

The depletion width $W_D$ in a one-sided p-n junction scales as:

$$W_D = \sqrt{\dfrac{2\varepsilon_{si}(\phi_{bi} + V_R)}{q N_A}}$$

Since the threshold voltage shift is proportional to the **depletion charge** (which is $\propto W_D \propto \sqrt{V_{SB}}$), the body effect equation acquires its characteristic square-root form.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — Body Effect

| **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Symbol Definitions** | **Units** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Threshold Voltage with Body Effect | $V_{TH} = V_{TH0} + \gamma \left( \sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}} - \sqrt{2\phi_F} \right)$ | $V_{TH0}$: zero-bias $V_{TH}$; $\gamma$: body coeff.; $\phi_F$: Fermi potential; $V_{SB}$: source-body voltage | Volts (V) |
| Body Effect Coefficient | $\gamma = \dfrac{\sqrt{2 q \varepsilon_{si} N_A}}{C_{ox}} = \dfrac{t_{ox}}{\varepsilon_{ox}} \sqrt{2 q \varepsilon_{si} N_A}$ | $q$: electron charge; $N_A$: substrate doping; $t_{ox}$: oxide thickness; $\varepsilon_{si}$, $\varepsilon_{ox}$: permittivities | $\text{V}^{1/2}$ |
| Fermi Potential | $\phi_F = \dfrac{kT}{q} \ln \left( \dfrac{N_A}{n_i} \right)$ | $kT/q \approx 25.85\ \text{mV}$ at 300 K; $n_i$: intrinsic carrier conc. | Volts (V) |
| Depletion Charge | $Q_B = -\sqrt{2 q \varepsilon_{si} N_A (2\phi_F + V_{SB})}$ | Per unit area | $\text{C/cm}^2$ |
| Oxide Capacitance | $C_{ox} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}}$ | $\varepsilon_{ox} = 3.45 \times 10^{-13}\ \text{F/cm}$ | $\text{F/cm}^2$ |
| Surface Potential at Threshold | $\phi_S = 2\phi_F$ (for NMOS) | Twice the Fermi potential | Volts (V) |
| Depletion Width | $W_D = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \varepsilon_{si} (2\phi_F + V_{SB})}{q N_A}}$ | At onset of strong inversion | cm |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** A *larger* $\gamma$ means the design is **more sensitive to body bias**. This is undesirable in analog circuits (mismatch, body-effect-induced gain reduction) but is *exploited* in adaptive body-biasing (ABB) techniques used in modern dynamic voltage/frequency scaling (DVFS) systems to control leakage.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Static CMOS Logic:** The body effect increases the *effective* threshold voltage of transistors in a logic gate, which **reduces drive current** and **slows switching speed** — a critical concern in high-performance digital design.
- **Analog Design:** In source followers and cascode amplifiers, body effect modulates the threshold of input transistors, causing **DC offset**, **reduced gain**, and **distortion**.
- **Low-Power Design (Adaptive Body Biasing):** Engineers *intentionally* use the body effect to apply **Reverse Body Bias (RBB)** to reduce subthreshold leakage during standby, or **Forward Body Bias (FBB)** to speed up transistors during active mode.
- **Memory Design (DRAM/SRAM):** Body effect influences the *write/read margins* and *stability* of SRAM cells.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Body Effect Coefficient ($\gamma$)

We start from the condition for **strong inversion**: the surface potential $\phi_S$ must equal $2\phi_F$ for an NMOS device.

**Step 1: Express the depletion charge per unit area.**

The total depletion charge at the onset of strong inversion is:

$$Q_B = -q N_A W_D$$

**Step 2: Substitute the depletion width expression.**

For a one-sided abrupt junction (p-type substrate with $N_A \gg n_i$), the depletion width under the gate is:

$$W_D = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \varepsilon_{si} \phi_S}{q N_A}}$$

**Step 3: Apply the surface potential condition.**

Setting $\phi_S = 2\phi_F + V_{SB}$ (since reverse-biasing the source-body junction adds $V_{SB}$ to the surface potential barrier):

$$Q_B = -q N_A \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \varepsilon_{si} (2\phi_F + V_{SB})}{q N_A}} = -\sqrt{2 q \varepsilon_{si} N_A (2\phi_F + V_{SB})}$$

**Step 4: Relate charge to voltage via the gate oxide capacitance.**

The gate voltage must support the voltage drop across the oxide ($-Q_B / C_{ox}$) and the surface potential ($2\phi_F$):

$$V_{GS} = \dfrac{-Q_B}{C_{ox}} + 2\phi_F + \phi_{MS}$$

**Step 5: Define threshold voltage.**

At threshold, $V_{GS} = V_{TH}$. Also, when $V_{SB} = 0$, the threshold is $V_{TH0}$:

$$V_{TH0} = \dfrac{\sqrt{2 q \varepsilon_{si} N_A \cdot 2\phi_F}}{C_{ox}} + 2\phi_F + \phi_{MS}$$

**Step 6: Subtract to find the body-effect contribution.**

The *change* in threshold voltage from $V_{TH0}$ to $V_{TH}$ is:

$$\Delta V_{TH} = V_{TH} - V_{TH0} = \dfrac{\sqrt{2 q \varepsilon_{si} N_A}}{C_{ox}} \left( \sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}} - \sqrt{2\phi_F} \right)$$

**Step 7: Define $\gamma$ and write the final expression.**

$$\gamma \equiv \dfrac{\sqrt{2 q \varepsilon_{si} N_A}}{C_{ox}}$$

$$\boxed{V_{TH} = V_{TH0} + \gamma \left( \sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}} - \sqrt{2\phi_F} \right)}$$

## 3.2 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style Problem)

**Given:**
- $N_A = 10^{16}\ \text{cm}^{-3}$
- $t_{ox} = 10\ \text{nm} = 10 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{cm}$
- $V_{SB} = 1\ \text{V}$
- Temperature = 300 K

**Find:** (a) $\gamma$, (b) $\phi_F$, (c) $\Delta V_{TH}$

**Solution:**

**Part (a): Compute $\gamma$**

$$C_{ox} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}} = \dfrac{3.45 \times 10^{-13}\ \text{F/cm}}{10 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{cm}} = 3.45 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{F/cm}^2$$

$$\gamma = \dfrac{\sqrt{2 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 1.04 \times 10^{-12} \times 10^{16}}}{3.45 \times 10^{-7}}$$

$$\gamma = \dfrac{\sqrt{3.328 \times 10^{-15}}}{3.45 \times 10^{-7}} = \dfrac{5.769 \times 10^{-8}}{3.45 \times 10^{-7}} \approx 0.167\ \text{V}^{1/2}$$

**Part (b): Compute $\phi_F$**

$$\phi_F = \dfrac{kT}{q} \ln \left( \dfrac{N_A}{n_i} \right) = 0.02585 \times \ln \left( \dfrac{10^{16}}{1.45 \times 10^{10}} \right)$$

$$\phi_F = 0.02585 \times \ln(6.897 \times 10^{5}) = 0.02585 \times 13.445 \approx 0.3476\ \text{V}$$

**Part (c): Compute $\Delta V_{TH}$**

$$\Delta V_{TH} = 0.167 \times \left( \sqrt{2(0.3476) + 1} - \sqrt{2(0.3476)} \right)$$

$$\Delta V_{TH} = 0.167 \times \left( \sqrt{1.6952} - \sqrt{0.6952} \right)$$

$$\Delta V_{TH} = 0.167 \times (1.3020 - 0.8338) = 0.167 \times 0.4682 \approx 0.0782\ \text{V}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta V_{TH} \approx 78.2\ \text{mV}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** A common mistake students make is **forgetting to convert units** of $t_{ox}$ from nanometers to centimeters. Always be consistent: $1\ \text{nm} = 10^{-7}\ \text{cm}$. This is a frequent source of unit-related errors in board exams.

## 3.3 Effect of Body Effect on Drain Current

In saturation, the drain current is:

$$I_D = \dfrac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \dfrac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{TH})^2$$

When $V_{TH}$ increases due to body effect (for fixed $V_{GS}$), the **overdrive voltage** ($V_{GS} - V_{TH}$) decreases, causing $I_D$ to drop quadratically. This is why the body effect is a critical concern in **low-power VLSI design**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — MOSFET Cross-Section with Body Effect

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph MOSFET_Cross_Section["NMOS Transistor with Body Bias"]
        gate["GATE METAL/POLY"]
        oxide["SiO2 LAYER tox"]
        nplus_s["n+ SOURCE"]
        nplus_d["n+ DRAIN"]
        channel["INDUCED CHANNEL"]
        depletion["DEPLETION REGION WD"]
        psub["p-TYPE SUBSTRATE BODY"]
        body_terminal["BODY TERMINAL B"]
        vsb["VSB REVERSE BIAS"]
    end

    gate --- oxide
    oxide --- channel
    nplus_s --- oxide
    nplus_d --- oxide
    channel --- depletion
    depletion --- psub
    psub --- body_terminal
    nplus_s -. vsb .-> body_terminal
```

**Caption:** The cross-section illustrates the depletion region widening when a reverse bias $V_{SB}$ is applied between the source and body terminals, which is the physical origin of the body effect.

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Functional Flow of Body-Effect Threshold Voltage Calculation

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["INPUT PARAMETERS: VTH0, gamma, phiF, VSB"] --> B["COMPUTE TERM A: sqrt 2phiF plus VSB"]
    A --> C["COMPUTE TERM B: sqrt 2phiF"]
    B --> D["SUBTRACT: TERM A minus TERM B"]
    C --> D
    D --> E["MULTIPLY BY gamma"]
    E --> F["ADD VTH0"]
    F --> G["OUTPUT: NEW VTH INCREASED"]
    G --> H["VTH SHIFT causes ID reduction and slower switching"]

    style A fill:#FFE4B5
    style G fill:#90EE90
    style H fill:#FFB6C1
```

**Caption:** Functional flow of the body-effect computation, showing how the input parameters combine to produce an elevated threshold voltage and the downstream impact on circuit performance.

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Impact of Body Effect in VLSI Design Domains

```mermaid
graph TD
    BE["BODY EFFECT IN VLSI"] --> DIG["DIGITAL CMOS LOGIC"]
    BE --> ANA["ANALOG CIRCUITS"]
    BE --> LP["LOW POWER DESIGN"]

    DIG --> D1["Reduced drive current"]
    DIG --> D2["Increased propagation delay"]
    DIG --> D3["Reduced noise margin"]

    ANA --> A1["DC offset in differential pairs"]
    ANA --> A2["Reduced gain in source followers"]
    ANA --> A3["Harmonic distortion"]

    LP --> L1["Reverse Body Bias RBB reduces leakage"]
    LP --> L2["Forward Body Bias FBB boosts speed"]
    LP --> L3["Used in DVFS for adaptive control"]

    style BE fill:#FFD700
    style LP fill:#87CEEB
```

**Caption:** Body effect is a double-edged sword — a parasitic issue in conventional design, but a useful *knob* for low-power adaptive biasing techniques.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define the body effect in a MOSFET. Write the threshold voltage equation that incorporates the body effect, and explain the significance of the body effect coefficient $\gamma$.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The body effect is the phenomenon in which the threshold voltage of a MOSFET changes when a voltage is applied between the source and the body (substrate) terminal. The governing equation is:

$$V_{TH} = V_{TH0} + \gamma \left( \sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}} - \sqrt{2\phi_F} \right)$$

The body effect coefficient $\gamma$ quantifies the sensitivity of $V_{TH}$ to changes in $V_{SB}$. A higher $\gamma$ indicates greater threshold modulation, which is undesirable in analog circuits but can be exploited in adaptive body-biasing for low-power design.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**State the expression for the body effect coefficient $\gamma$ and list the technology parameters on which it depends.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

$$\gamma = \dfrac{\sqrt{2 q \varepsilon_{si} N_A}}{C_{ox}} = \dfrac{t_{ox}}{\varepsilon_{ox}} \sqrt{2 q \varepsilon_{si} N_A}$$

$\gamma$ depends on:
1. **Substrate doping concentration** ($N_A$): higher doping → larger $\gamma$
2. **Gate oxide thickness** ($t_{ox}$): thicker oxide → larger $\gamma$
3. **Material constants** ($q$, $\varepsilon_{si}$, $\varepsilon_{ox}$): fixed for a given technology

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### **🅰️ Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the threshold voltage of an NMOS transistor, including the body effect. Clearly state the assumptions made during the derivation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a CMOS process, the following parameters are given: $N_A = 5 \times 10^{16}\ \text{cm}^{-3}$, $t_{ox} = 8\ \text{nm}$, $V_{TH0} = 0.5\ \text{V}$. Compute (i) the body effect coefficient $\gamma$, (ii) the Fermi potential $\phi_F$, and (iii) the threshold voltage when $V_{SB} = 1.5\ \text{V}$. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Detailed Derivation:**

The threshold voltage is derived from the condition of **strong inversion**, where the surface potential $\phi_S = 2\phi_F$.

**Assumptions:**
- One-sided abrupt junction approximation
- Uniform substrate doping
- Neglect of interface traps and oxide charges (for simplicity)
- The source-body junction is reverse-biased by $V_{SB}$

**Derivation Steps:**

1. **Surface potential condition:** At the onset of strong inversion, $\phi_S = 2\phi_F + V_{SB}$.

2. **Depletion charge per unit area:**
$$Q_B = -q N_A W_D = -\sqrt{2 q \varepsilon_{si} N_A (2\phi_F + V_{SB})}$$

3. **Gate voltage balance** (using Gauss's law at the oxide-silicon interface):
$$V_{GS} = -\dfrac{Q_B}{C_{ox}} + \phi_S + \phi_{MS}$$

4. **Substitute and define $V_{TH}$ at $V_{GS} = V_{TH}$:**
$$V_{TH} = \dfrac{\sqrt{2 q \varepsilon_{si} N_A (2\phi_F + V_{SB})}}{C_{ox}} + 2\phi_F + \phi_{MS}$$

5. **Subtract zero-bias case** ($V_{SB} = 0$):
$$V_{TH} - V_{TH0} = \dfrac{\sqrt{2 q \varepsilon_{si} N_A}}{C_{ox}} \left( \sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}} - \sqrt{2\phi_F} \right)$$

6. **Final result:**
$$\boxed{V_{TH} = V_{TH0} + \gamma \left( \sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}} - \sqrt{2\phi_F} \right)}$$

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Body effect coefficient $\gamma$:**

$$C_{ox} = \dfrac{3.45 \times 10^{-13}}{8 \times 10^{-7}} = 4.3125 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{F/cm}^2$$

$$\gamma = \dfrac{\sqrt{2 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 1.04 \times 10^{-12} \times 5 \times 10^{16}}}{4.3125 \times 10^{-7}}$$

$$\gamma = \dfrac{\sqrt{1.664 \times 10^{-14}}}{4.3125 \times 10^{-7}} = \dfrac{1.29 \times 10^{-7}}{4.3125 \times 10^{-7}} \approx 0.299\ \text{V}^{1/2}$$

**(ii) Fermi potential $\phi_F$:**

$$\phi_F = 0.02585 \times \ln \left( \dfrac{5 \times 10^{16}}{1.45 \times 10^{10}} \right) = 0.02585 \times \ln(3.448 \times 10^6)$$

$$\phi_F = 0.02585 \times 15.055 \approx 0.389\ \text{V}$$

**(iii) Threshold voltage at $V_{SB} = 1.5\ \text{V}$:**

$$\Delta V_{TH} = 0.299 \times \left( \sqrt{2(0.389) + 1.5} - \sqrt{2(0.389)} \right)$$

$$\Delta V_{TH} = 0.299 \times \left( \sqrt{2.278} - \sqrt{0.778} \right) = 0.299 \times (1.509 - 0.882)$$

$$\Delta V_{TH} = 0.299 \times 0.627 \approx 0.1875\ \text{V}$$

$$\boxed{V_{TH} = 0.5 + 0.1875 = 0.6875\ \text{V}}$$

---

### **🅱️ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the physical origin of the body effect with a neat sketch. Discuss how the body effect impacts (i) digital CMOS circuits and (ii) analog circuits. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Two NMOS transistors share a common p-substrate. Transistor M1 has its source at ground potential, while M2's source is at $V_{S2} = 0.8\ \text{V}$. The body is tied to ground. Given $\gamma = 0.4\ \text{V}^{1/2}$, $\phi_F = 0.35\ \text{V}$, and $V_{TH0} = 0.45\ \text{V}$, find the threshold voltages of M1 and M2. Comment on the implications for circuit design. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Physical Origin and Impact:**

**Physical Origin:** When a reverse bias $V_{SB}$ is applied between the source and body, the depletion region beneath the gate widens. The depletion region contains immobile ionized acceptor atoms, which the gate must neutralize before forming an inversion channel. This requires additional gate voltage, raising $V_{TH}$.

**(i) Impact on Digital CMOS:**
- Higher $V_{TH}$ → reduced overdrive voltage ($V_{GS} - V_{TH}$)
- Lower drive current → **slower switching speed** and increased propagation delay
- Degraded noise margins in deep-submicron designs

**(ii) Impact on Analog Circuits:**
- **DC offset** in differential pairs due to $V_{TH}$ mismatch
- **Reduced small-signal gain** in source followers (effective $g_m$ is lowered)
- **Harmonic distortion** in highly linear applications

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**For M1:** $V_{S1} = 0\ \text{V}$, $V_{B} = 0\ \text{V}$ → $V_{SB1} = 0\ \text{V}$

$$V_{TH1} = V_{TH0} + 0 = 0.45\ \text{V}$$

**For M2:** $V_{S2} = 0.8\ \text{V}$, $V_{B} = 0\ \text{V}$ → $V_{SB2} = 0.8\ \text{V}$

$$V_{TH2} = 0.45 + 0.4 \times \left( \sqrt{2(0.35) + 0.8} - \sqrt{2(0.35)} \right)$$

$$V_{TH2} = 0.45 + 0.4 \times \left( \sqrt{1.5} - \sqrt{0.7} \right) = 0.45 + 0.4 \times (1.2247 - 0.8367)$$

$$V_{TH2} = 0.45 + 0.4 \times 0.388 = 0.45 + 0.155 = 0.605\ \text{V}$$

**Design Implications:**
- M2 has a **higher threshold** (0.605 V) than M1 (0.45 V) because of the body effect.
- This causes **current mismatch** between the two transistors, leading to systematic errors in current mirrors, differential pairs, and analog bias circuits.
- **Mitigation:** Use **separate body ties** (body contacted to local source) or **triple-well technologies** to eliminate the body effect in critical analog paths.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors:** $t_{ox}$ in nm must be converted to cm (×$10^{-7}$) for consistency with CGS units used in $\varepsilon_{ox}$ and $\varepsilon_{si}$.
> 2. **Sign Convention Mistakes:** For PMOS, $V_{SB} < 0$ and $N_A$ should be replaced by $N_D$. The body effect equation form is the same, but signs and doping type must be handled carefully.
> 3. **Forgetting $\phi_{MS}$ in derivation:** Students often skip the metal-semiconductor work function difference, losing 1–2 marks in derivations.
> 4. **Confusing $2\phi_F$ with $\phi_F$:** The surface potential at strong inversion is $2\phi_F$, *not* $\phi_F$ — this is a common mistake.
> 5. **Not stating assumptions:** In derivations, always explicitly list the assumptions (uniform doping, one-sided junction, etc.) to earn full marks.

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Body Effect** = modulation of $V_{TH}$ by $V_{SB}$ due to widening of the depletion region.
- **Master Equation:** $V_{TH} = V_{TH0} + \gamma \left( \sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}} - \sqrt{2\phi_F} \right)$
- **Body Effect Coefficient:** $\gamma = \dfrac{t_{ox}}{\varepsilon_{ox}} \sqrt{2 q \varepsilon_{si} N_A}$ — measured in $\text{V}^{1/2}$.
- **Fermi Potential:** $\phi_F = (kT/q) \ln(N_A / n_i) \approx 0.35\text{–}0.4\ \text{V}$ for typical doping.
- **Physical Origin:** Reverse-biasing the source-body junction widens the depletion region, increasing the charge $Q_B$ that the gate must compensate.
- **Digital Impact:** Reduced drive current, slower switching, degraded noise margin.
- **Analog Impact:** DC offset, gain reduction in source followers, harmonic distortion.
- **Low-Power Application:** **Adaptive Body Biasing (ABB)** — Reverse Body Bias (RBB) reduces leakage; Forward Body Bias (FBB) increases speed.
- **Mitigation Strategies:** SOI technology, triple-well process, local body ties, multi-$V_{TH}$ design.
- **Square-Root Dependence:** The $\sqrt{V_{SB}}$ dependence is fundamental and arises from the depletion-width geometry in a one-sided p-n junction.
- **PMOS Symmetry:** The same equation applies to PMOS with $V_{SB} < 0$ and appropriate sign conventions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Narrow Width Effect
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT866 - LOW POWER VLSI
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Narrow Width Effect

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Narrow Width Effect (NWE)** is a second-order MOSFET scaling phenomenon in which the **threshold voltage ($V_{TN}$)** of a MOS transistor deviates from its long-wide channel value when the **channel width ($W$)** becomes comparable in magnitude to the **maximum depletion-layer width ($x_{d,max}$)** and the **source/drain junction depth ($x_j$)**.

Mathematically, NWE manifests as an **additive shift** in the long-channel threshold voltage:

$$
V_{TN}(NWE) \;=\; V_{TN}(\text{long-wide}) \;+\; \Delta V_{TN}^{NWE}
$$

For a standard **n-channel MOS** device, $\Delta V_{TN}^{NWE}$ is **positive**, meaning the device becomes *harder to turn on* as $W$ shrinks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Narrow Width Effect is classified under *short-channel and narrow-width secondary effects* and is directly tested in the **Module 1** portion of PEECT866 under the "MOS Transistor Secondary Effects" unit.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Narrow Hallway

Imagine a wide hallway (large $W$) where you can walk comfortably without bumping into walls. Now imagine the same hallway shrinking down to a tight corridor. Any extra people (electric charge) trying to occupy space near the walls will now occupy a much larger fraction of the floor area. The "traffic controller" (gate) has to work much harder to clear the corridor (achieve inversion).

In MOS terms:

| Hallway Element | MOSFET Equivalent |
| :--- | :--- |
| Wide floor | Wide channel ($W \gg x_{d,max}$) |
| Narrow corridor | Narrow channel ($W \approx x_{d,max}$) |
| Wall "fringe" zone | Sidewall depletion regions at STI/LOCOS edges |
| People in corridor | Depletion charge $Q_B$ |
| Traffic controller | Gate oxide / gate voltage |

When the corridor shrinks, the **fringing charge near the sidewalls** can no longer be neglected, demanding more gate voltage to form the inversion layer.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Body factor**: $\gamma = \dfrac{\sqrt{2\varepsilon_{si} q N_A}}{C_{ox}}$ — typical: **0.3 V$^{1/2}$ to 0.7 V$^{1/2}$**
- **Fermi potential**: $\phi_F = \dfrac{kT}{q}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{N_A}{n_i}\right)$ — at **300 K**: ~0.35 V (for $N_A = 10^{16}$ cm$^{-3}$)
- **Surface potential at strong inversion**: $\phi_{s,inv} = 2\phi_F$ — typically **0.7 V**
- **Standard scaling benchmark**: NWE becomes *significant* when $W \le 3 \cdot x_{d,max}$ (rule of thumb)

> [!NOTE]
> In sub-100 nm CMOS technologies, $W$ routinely drops below **250 nm**, making NWE a first-order design concern in SRAM bit-cells, analog input pairs, and I/O transistors.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Physical Origin of the Narrow Width Effect

The threshold voltage derivation for a **wide, long** MOSFET assumes:

1. The depletion region under the gate is one-dimensional (only depth $x$ matters).
2. The depletion charge $Q_B = q N_A x_{d,max}$ is spread uniformly across the full width $W$.

When $W$ shrinks, two new contributions appear:

- **Sidewall (fringe) depletion charges** — extra ionized acceptor ions beneath the **field oxide / shallow-trench isolation (STI)** sidewalls.
- **2-D electrostatic fringing** — the depletion boundary is no longer flat; it curves upward near the channel edges.

Both effects increase the **total bulk charge per unit gate area** that must be mirrored by the gate charge. Consequently, $V_{TN}$ rises.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of the NWE Mechanism

1. **Gate oxide establishes surface potential** $\phi_s$ under the channel.
2. **Bulk depletion** of depth $x_d$ forms under the gate.
3. **Edge fields** at the STI/LOCOS sidewalls penetrate inward, creating a *lateral* depletion component of characteristic extent $\sim x_j$.
4. The **effective depletion charge** becomes $Q_B^{eff} = q N_A \,(x_d \cdot W + 2 x_d \, r_j)$ where $r_j$ is the sidewall curvature factor.
5. Dividing by the gate area $W \cdot L$ gives an **enhanced areal charge density** as $W$ shrinks.
6. The gate must supply **more charge** to reach inversion $\Rightarrow$ **$V_{TN}$ rises**.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Expression | Notes / Units |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Long-wide threshold voltage | $V_{TN0} = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \dfrac{\sqrt{2\varepsilon_{si} q N_A (2\phi_F + V_{SB})}}{C_{ox}}$ | Volts (V) |
| 2 | Maximum depletion width | $x_{d,max} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2\varepsilon_{si}(2\phi_F + V_{SB})}{q N_A}}$ | cm or $\mu$m |
| 3 | Oxide capacitance per unit area | $C_{ox} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}}$ | F/cm$^2$ |
| 4 | Body-effect coefficient | $\gamma = \dfrac{\sqrt{2\varepsilon_{si} q N_A}}{C_{ox}}$ | V$^{1/2}$ |
| 5 | NWE threshold shift (Yau / Jaeger model) | $\Delta V_{TN}^{NWE} \approx \dfrac{q N_A \, x_{d,max}}{C_{ox}} \cdot \dfrac{\pi x_j}{2W}$ | V (positive for NMOS) |
| 6 | Narrow-width $V_{TN}$ | $V_{TN}(NWE) = V_{TN0} + \dfrac{3\pi}{8}\dfrac{q N_A x_j x_{d,max}}{C_{ox} W}$ | V |
| 7 | Effective channel width | $W_{eff} = W_{drawn} - \Delta W$ | $\mu$m |
| 8 | Subthreshold slope factor (advanced) | $S = (\ln 10)\dfrac{kT}{q}\!\left(1+\dfrac{C_{dm}+C_{it}}{C_{ox}}\right)$ | mV/decade |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Conditions for the Formula Sheet:**
> - Valid for $W \ge 3 x_{d,max}$ ⇒ use long-wide $V_{TN0}$ only.
> - For $W < 3 x_{d,max}$, NWE term becomes dominant.
> - For $L < 0.25\,\mu$m, **reverse short-channel effect (RSCE)** may compete with NWE.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **SRAM Bit-Cell Design**: 6T SRAM cells in sub-22 nm nodes have $W \approx 30\text{–}40$ nm. NWE directly raises pull-down $V_{TN}$, degrading write-margin. Designers compensate by **under-driving** word-lines or **boosting** $V_{GS}$.
- **Analog Transistors**: Mismatch due to NWE ($\sigma V_{TN} \propto 1/\sqrt{WL}$) is critical for current mirrors and differential pairs. **Large $W$** (e.g., $> 1\,\mu$m) is deliberately chosen to suppress NWE-induced offset.
- **I/O Transistors**: Thick-oxide I/O FETs exhibit small $x_{d,max}$ but large $x_j$ ⇒ NWE may still be measurable.
- **FinFET / GAA Era**: In multi-gate devices, the *fin width* $W_{fin}$ plays the role of $W$, and *quantum confinement* effects (rather than NWE) dominate below $W_{fin} < 8$ nm.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Threshold Voltage With Narrow Width Effect

We start from Gauss's law applied at the onset of strong inversion for a **long** but **finite-width** MOSFET.

#### Step 1 — Standard Gauss Law at Inversion

At the silicon surface, the vertical electric field $E_s$ relates to the gate charge per unit area $Q_s$:

$$
Q_s \;=\; \varepsilon_{si} E_s \;=\; C_{ox}(V_{GB} - V_{FB} - \phi_s)
$$

where $V_{GB}$ is the gate-to-body voltage and $\phi_s$ is the surface potential.

#### Step 2 — Bulk Depletion Charge (Wide Channel)

For a wide channel, the depletion region extends only in the **depth ($x$) direction**:

$$
Q_B^{wide} \;=\; q N_A x_{d,max} \;=\; \sqrt{2\varepsilon_{si} q N_A\,(2\phi_F + V_{SB})}
$$

#### Step 3 — Add Sidewall (Fringe) Depletion Charge

For a **narrow** channel of drawn width $W$, depletion regions extend inward from **both** sidewalls. Approximating each sidewall contribution as a quarter-cylinder of radius $\approx x_j$ (junction depth) gives a lateral charge:

$$
Q_{sw} \;\approx\; q N_A \cdot 2 x_{d,max} \cdot r_j
$$

where $r_j$ is an effective sidewall radius of curvature, often approximated by $r_j \approx x_j$.

#### Step 4 — Total Depletion Charge

Summing per unit gate area (area $= W \cdot L$):

$$
Q_B^{tot} \;\approx\; q N_A x_{d,max}\!\left(W + \pi x_j\right) \Big/ W
$$

The factor $\pi x_j$ accounts for the **perimeter contribution** of the two sidewalls.

#### Step 5 — Equate to Gate Charge

Setting $Q_s^{inv} \approx 0$ (just at the onset of strong inversion) and solving for $V_{GB} = V_{TN}$:

$$
V_{TN}(NWE) \;=\; V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \dfrac{Q_B^{tot}}{C_{ox}}
$$

#### Step 6 — Substitute and Simplify

Substituting Step 4 into Step 5:

$$
V_{TN}(NWE) \;=\; V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \dfrac{q N_A x_{d,max}}{C_{ox}}\!\left(1 + \dfrac{\pi x_j}{W}\right)
$$

#### Step 7 — Identify the NWE Shift

Expanding the long-wide term:

$$
V_{TN0} \;=\; V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \dfrac{q N_A x_{d,max}}{C_{ox}}
$$

We therefore obtain:

$$
\Delta V_{TN}^{NWE} \;=\; \dfrac{\pi\, q N_A\, x_{d,max}\, x_j}{C_{ox}\, W}
$$

#### Step 8 — Numerical Magnitude Estimate (KTU-style)

Assume:
- $N_A = 5\times 10^{16}$ cm$^{-3}$
- $x_{d,max} = 0.30\,\mu$m
- $x_j = 0.20\,\mu$m
- $t_{ox} = 10$ nm $\Rightarrow C_{ox} = \dfrac{3.9 \times 8.854\times 10^{-14}}{10^{-6}} = 3.45\times 10^{-7}$ F/cm$^2$
- $W = 0.5\,\mu$m

Compute:

$$
\Delta V_{TN}^{NWE} = \dfrac{\pi (1.6\times 10^{-19})(5\times 10^{16})(3\times 10^{-5})(2\times 10^{-5})}{(3.45\times 10^{-7})(5\times 10^{-5})}
$$

$$
\Delta V_{TN}^{NWE} = \dfrac{\pi (1.6\times 10^{-19})(5\times 10^{16})(6\times 10^{-10})}{1.725\times 10^{-11}}
$$

$$
\Delta V_{TN}^{NWE} = \dfrac{\pi (4.8\times 10^{-12})}{1.725\times 10^{-11}} \;\approx\; 0.874\;\text{V}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> A shift of **~0.87 V** is enormous — in modern nodes ($x_j < 50$ nm, $t_{ox} < 2$ nm) this drops to **~50 mV**, which is still *measurable* and *design-relevant*.

### 3.2 Effect on I-V Characteristics

For the linear and saturation regions, $V_{TN}(NWE)$ replaces $V_{TN0}$:

$$
I_D^{lin} \;=\; \mu_n C_{ox}\dfrac{W}{L}\!\left[(V_{GS}-V_{TN}^{NWE})V_{DS} - \dfrac{V_{DS}^2}{2}\right]
$$

$$
I_D^{sat} \;=\; \dfrac{\mu_n C_{ox}}{2}\dfrac{W}{L}(V_{GS}-V_{TN}^{NWE})^2
$$

Practical consequence: **drive current drops** for a given $V_{GS}$ because the effective overdrive $(V_{GS} - V_{TN}^{NWE})$ shrinks.

### 3.3 Summary Matrix — NWE vs Short-Channel Effect (SCE)

| Parameter | Short-Channel Effect (SCE) | Narrow Width Effect (NWE) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Critical dimension | Channel length $L$ | Channel width $W$ |
| Dominant mechanism | Drain-induced barrier lowering | Sidewall depletion charge |
| Sign of $\Delta V_{TN}$ | Negative (roll-off) | Positive (roll-up) |
| Geometry of depletion | 2-D along $L$ | 2-D along $W$ |
| Mitigation | HALO implants, retrograde well | STI rounding, channel stops, large $W$ |
| Counter-effect | Reverse short-channel effect | Reverse narrow-width effect (rare) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of NWE Mechanism

The following Mermaid diagram captures the causal chain leading to the narrow-width threshold-voltage shift.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Apply Gate Voltage V_GS] --> B[Surface Potential phi_s rises]
    B --> C[Vertical Depletion Region Forms depth x_d]
    C --> D[Sidewall Depletion Forms at STI Edges]
    D --> E[Total Bulk Charge Q_B increases per unit gate area]
    E --> F[Gate must supply more charge for inversion]
    F --> G[V_TN shifts upward positive delta V_TN]
    G --> H[Drive current I_D decreases at fixed V_GS]
    H --> I[Device becomes harder to turn ON]
    A --> J[Width W scales down]
    J --> D
    J --> K[Sidewall contribution fraction pi x_j / W grows]
    K --> E
```

### 4.2 Threshold Voltage vs Channel Width — Qualitative Plot

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Wide Channel Region W greater than 3 x_d] --> P2[V_TN plateau equals V_TN0]
    P2 --> P3[Transition region W between 3 x_d and x_d]
    P3 --> P4[V_TN rises monotonically as W decreases]
    P4 --> P5[Very Narrow W below x_j: severe roll-up]
    P5 --> P6[Designers add channel-stop implant or use FinFET]
```

### 4.3 Cross-Sectional Schematic Description (ASCII Map)

```
       Gate (Poly)
   ___________________________
  |          |     |          |
  |  Tox     |     |  Tox     |   <-- Gate oxide
  |__________|_____|__________|
  |                            |
  |   p-substrate (channel)    |
  |  <---- W ---->             |
  |  |||||||||  <- bulk depl. |
  |  |||||||||                 |
  |__/_______\_________________|
   STI      STI                 <-- Sidewall depletion zones (curved)
   side     side
   depl.    depl.
```

- The **straight vertical arrows** under the gate represent the standard 1-D depletion charge.
- The **curved arrows** at the STI corners represent the extra 2-D sidewall depletion that adds to $Q_B$ as $W \to$ small.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. Define the Narrow Width Effect in a MOSFET. How does it affect the threshold voltage?** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> Narrow Width Effect (NWE) is the phenomenon in which the threshold voltage of a MOSFET deviates from its long-channel, wide-channel value when the **channel width $W$** becomes comparable to the maximum depletion width $x_{d,max}$ and junction depth $x_j$. **(1 mark)**
> For an n-channel device, NWE causes an **increase** in the threshold voltage because additional depletion charge accumulates along the sidewall (STI/LOCOS) edges, raising the total bulk charge per unit gate area. **(1 mark)**
> The shift is given by $\Delta V_{TN}^{NWE} \approx \dfrac{\pi q N_A x_{d,max} x_j}{C_{ox} W}$, showing an inverse dependence on $W$. **(1 mark)**

**Q2. List two methods to mitigate the Narrow Width Effect.** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> 1. **Use a channel-stop implant** (e.g., boron for NMOS) beneath the STI sidewalls to suppress lateral depletion. **(1.5 marks)**
> 2. **Increase the effective channel width** by laying out the transistor with multiple fingers or wider geometry so that $W \gg x_{d,max}$. **(1 mark)**
> 3. *(Bonus)* Use advanced multi-gate structures (FinFET, GAA) where the narrow-fin quantum confinement replaces NWE. **(0.5 mark)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for the threshold voltage of a long-channel MOSFET, clearly stating the assumption of one-dimensional depletion. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Extend the derivation to include the **Narrow Width Effect**, and explain why the threshold voltage increases as channel width decreases. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution

**(a) Long-Channel $V_{TN}$ Derivation — 7 marks**

We begin from charge neutrality at the silicon surface under the gate.

1. Apply Gauss's law at the surface, balancing gate charge, bulk depletion charge, and inversion charge: **(1 mark)**

$$
Q_s^{gate} + Q_s^{bulk} + Q_s^{inv} = 0
$$

2. The bulk depletion charge per unit area in a **wide** device is given by integrating Poisson's equation: **(2 marks)**

$$
Q_B \;=\; -q N_A x_d \;=\; -\sqrt{2\varepsilon_{si} q N_A (2\phi_F + V_{SB})}
$$

3. At the onset of strong inversion, surface potential $\phi_s = 2\phi_F$ and inversion charge $Q_s^{inv} \to 0$. **(1 mark)**

4. Solving for gate voltage yields the long-wide threshold voltage: **(3 marks)**

$$
V_{TN0} \;=\; V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \dfrac{\sqrt{2\varepsilon_{si} q N_A (2\phi_F + V_{SB})}}{C_{ox}}
$$

##### Model Solution

**(b) Extension to Narrow Width — 7 marks**

1. Acknowledge that for finite width $W$, **sidewall (fringe) depletion regions** form at the STI edges, contributing extra charge. **(1 mark)**

2. The total depletion charge becomes: **(2 marks)**

$$
Q_B^{tot} \;\approx\; q N_A x_{d,max}\!\left(1 + \dfrac{\pi x_j}{W}\right)
$$

3. Substituting into the threshold expression: **(2 marks)**

$$
V_{TN}(NWE) \;=\; V_{TN0} + \dfrac{\pi q N_A x_{d,max} x_j}{C_{ox} W}
$$

4. The additional term is **positive** and grows as $W$ decreases, explaining why the device becomes harder to turn on. **(2 marks)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**(a)** With the help of a suitable diagram, explain the physical origin of the **Narrow Width Effect** in a short-channel MOSFET. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 0.18 μm NMOS transistor has the following parameters: $N_A = 4\times 10^{16}$ cm$^{-3}$, $t_{ox} = 4$ nm, $x_j = 0.15$ μm, $V_{SB} = 0$ V. Calculate the threshold voltage shift $\Delta V_{TN}^{NWE}$ for $W = 0.5$ μm and $W = 0.25$ μm. Comment on the design implications. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution

**(a) Physical Origin — 7 marks**

1. **Wide-channel assumption** breaks down as $W$ shrinks. The depletion region is no longer purely 1-D. **(1 mark)**
2. **STI/LOCOS sidewalls** introduce a lateral electric field, causing depletion regions to bend upward into the channel. **(2 marks)**
3. **Extra bulk charge** under the sidewalls must be mirrored by the gate charge. **(1 mark)**
4. **Threshold voltage rises** by $\Delta V_{TN}^{NWE} \approx \dfrac{\pi q N_A x_{d,max} x_j}{C_{ox} W}$. **(2 marks)**
5. Include a **cross-section diagram** showing vertical and sidewall depletion with curved arrows. **(1 mark)**

**(b) Numerical Calculation — 7 marks**

Given:
- $N_A = 4\times 10^{16}$ cm$^{-3} = 4\times 10^{22}$ m$^{-3}$
- $t_{ox} = 4$ nm $= 4\times 10^{-9}$ m
- $x_j = 0.15\,\mu$m $= 1.5\times 10^{-7}$ m
- $V_{SB} = 0$ V
- $\varepsilon_{ox} = 3.9 \times 8.854\times 10^{-12} = 3.45\times 10^{-11}$ F/m
- $\varepsilon_{si} = 11.7 \times 8.854\times 10^{-12} = 1.036\times 10^{-10}$ F/m
- $\phi_F = 0.0259 \cdot \ln(4\times 10^{16}/1.5\times 10^{10}) \approx 0.026 \cdot \ln(2.67\times 10^6) \approx 0.026 \cdot 14.80 \approx 0.384$ V
- $2\phi_F = 0.768$ V

Step 1: Calculate $x_{d,max}$: **(2 marks)**

$$
x_{d,max} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2\varepsilon_{si}(2\phi_F + V_{SB})}{q N_A}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2(1.036\times 10^{-10})(0.768)}{(1.6\times 10^{-19})(4\times 10^{22})}}
$$

$$
x_{d,max} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1.591\times 10^{-10}}{6.4\times 10^{3}}} = \sqrt{2.486\times 10^{-14}} \approx 1.577\times 10^{-7}\,\text{m} = 0.158\,\mu\text{m}
$$

Step 2: Calculate $C_{ox}$: **(1 mark)**

$$
C_{ox} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}} = \dfrac{3.45\times 10^{-11}}{4\times 10^{-9}} = 8.625\times 10^{-3}\,\text{F/m}^2
$$

Step 3: Compute $\Delta V_{TN}^{NWE}$ for $W = 0.5\,\mu$m: **(1.5 marks)**

$$
\Delta V_{TN}^{NWE} = \dfrac{\pi q N_A x_{d,max} x_j}{C_{ox} W}
$$

$$
= \dfrac{\pi (1.6\times 10^{-19})(4\times 10^{22})(1.577\times 10^{-7})(1.5\times 10^{-7})}{(8.625\times 10^{-3})(5\times 10^{-7})}
$$

$$
= \dfrac{\pi (1.6\times 10^{-19})(4\times 10^{22})(2.366\times 10^{-14})}{4.313\times 10^{-9}}
$$

$$
= \dfrac{\pi (1.514\times 10^{-10})}{4.313\times 10^{-9}} \approx \dfrac{4.756\times 10^{-10}}{4.313\times 10^{-9}} \approx 0.110\;\text{V}
$$

Step 4: Compute $\Delta V_{TN}^{NWE}$ for $W = 0.25\,\mu$m: **(1.5 marks)**

Since $\Delta V_{TN}^{NWE} \propto 1/W$, halving $W$ doubles the shift:

$$
\Delta V_{TN}^{NWE}(W = 0.25\,\mu\text{m}) \approx 2 \times 0.110 \approx 0.220\;\text{V}
$$

Step 5: **Design Implications — 1 mark**

- The shift is **non-negligible** (~110 mV to 220 mV) at 0.18 μm node.
- For matched analog pairs, transistor mismatch $\sigma V_{TN}$ grows; designers use **large $W$ or common-centroid layout**.
- For digital cells, drive current degrades; cell libraries **characterize NWE** explicitly in the SPICE models.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting units!** KTU examiners deduct 1 mark per instance where SI units are not converted properly (e.g., nm → m, cm$^{-3}$ → m$^{-3}$).
> 2. **Confusing SCE with NWE.** SCE *lowers* $V_{TN}$ (roll-off), NWE *raises* it (roll-up). Mixing them up costs full marks in part (a) of any 14-mark question.
> 3. **Skipping the sidewall term.** Many students write only $V_{TN0}$ and forget the $\pi x_j/W$ correction. Examiners explicitly look for it under **Module 1, Section 1.2**.
> 4. **Not commenting on design impact.** KTU 2024 scheme emphasises **application-level answers**; numerical answers without a concluding remark lose the final 1–2 marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Narrow Width Effect (NWE)** occurs when channel width $W$ becomes comparable to $x_{d,max}$ and $x_j$. **[Critical]**
- **Sign convention**: For NMOS, $\Delta V_{TN}^{NWE} > 0$ (threshold **increases**) as $W$ decreases. **[Critical]**
- **Master formula**: $\Delta V_{TN}^{NWE} = \dfrac{\pi\, q N_A\, x_{d,max}\, x_j}{C_{ox}\, W}$ — memorize this for KTU ESE. **[Critical]**
- **Wide-channel limit** ($W > 3 x_{d,max}$): NWE becomes negligible; use standard $V_{TN0}$. **[Important]**
- **Physical mechanism**: Extra sidewall depletion charge from STI/LOCOS edges. **[Important]**
- **Mitigation strategies**: channel-stop implant, larger $W$, multi-finger layout, FinFET transition. **[Important]**
- **Comparison with SCE**: SCE reduces $V_{TN}$; NWE raises $V_{TN}$. Do not confuse in numerical problems. **[Critical]**
- **Design implications**: SRAM write-margin loss, analog mismatch, I/O drive degradation. **[Application]**
- **Modern relevance**: In FinFET/GAA nodes, NWE is *replaced* by **quantum confinement** effects in the fin. **[Future-trend note]**
- **KTU 2024 weightage**: Expect 3–7 marks from NWE in any Module 1 question paper, either as a stand-alone 3-mark question or as a sub-part of a 14-marker. **[Exam strategy]**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vth Roll-Off
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT866 - LOW POWER VLSI
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Vth Roll-Off

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Vth Roll-Off (Threshold Voltage Roll-Off)** is a **Short-Channel Effect (SCE)** observed in deep-submicron MOSFETs in which the **threshold voltage (Vth)** of the device decreases progressively as the effective **channel length (L_eff)** is reduced, deviating from the constant value predicted by the long-channel square-law model.

In the long-channel regime, the threshold voltage is treated as independent of channel length. However, as the channel length shrinks, the depletion regions of the **source** and **drain** junctions begin to encroach into the channel region. The portion of the bulk depletion charge that is "shared" with these junction depletion regions is no longer controlled by the gate, leading to a net reduction in the gate-controlled bulk charge (Q_B). Since the threshold voltage is directly proportional to Q_B, this charge-sharing phenomenon causes **Vth to roll off** as L decreases.

> [!NOTE]
> **Reverse Notation Warning for KTU Examiners:**
> In KTU 2024 Scheme question papers, Vth Roll-Off is **NOT** the same as the **Reverse Short-Channel Effect (RSCE)** where Vth *increases* for ultra-short channels due to halos/pocket implants. Roll-Off strictly refers to the **decrease** in Vth with decreasing L due to charge sharing.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **wide irrigation canal** carrying water (representing current). As long as the canal is wide, the water level (threshold) needed to start flow is constant, governed only by the gate (dam) at the top.

Now imagine **two side tributaries** (the source and drain depletion regions) joining the main canal. These tributaries "siphon off" or pre-drain some of the water before the dam. Because less water remains in the central section, the **dam (gate) does not need to be lifted as high** to initiate flow. In other words, the **threshold drops**.

- **Canal width** = Channel length (L)
- **Tributaries** = Source/Drain depletion regions
- **Dam height required** = Threshold voltage (Vth)
- **Smaller canal ⇒ Larger tributaries ⇒ Lower dam needed ⇒ Vth rolls off**

The threshold voltage keeps dropping until the source and drain depletion regions **punch through** and merge, at which point the transistor loses gate control entirely — this is the **punch-through** condition.

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Key Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> The following are the **standard physical constants and parameters** governing Vth roll-off, all of which must be **memorized** for KTU numerical problems.

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Magnitude (Sub-100nm Node) |
|---|---|---|
| Permittivity of silicon | $\varepsilon_s$ | **$11.7 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-14}$ F/cm** |
| Permittivity of SiO₂ | $\varepsilon_{ox}$ | **$3.9 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-14}$ F/cm** |
| Elementary charge | $q$ | **$1.6 \times 10^{-19}$ C** |
| Intrinsic carrier concentration | $n_i$ | **$1.45 \times 10^{10}$ cm⁻³** (at 300 K) |
| Thermal voltage | $V_T$ | **25.85 mV** (at 300 K) |
| Built-in potential | $V_{bi}$ | $\approx$ **0.7 V** (for n⁺p junction) |
| Surface potential at strong inversion | $2\phi_F$ | $\approx$ **0.7 V – 0.9 V** |
| Characteristic length | $l_t$ | **5 – 15 nm** (sub-100nm) |

The **roll-off onset** typically begins when $L_{eff} \leq 5 \cdot l_t$, where $l_t$ is the natural length scale of the MOSFET.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Physical Origin: The Charge-Sharing Phenomenon

The threshold voltage of a long-channel MOSFET is given by the classical expression:

$$V_{th} = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \dfrac{\sqrt{2 \, q \, \varepsilon_s \, N_A \, (2\phi_F + V_{SB})}}{C_{ox}}$$

This expression assumes that the **entire bulk depletion charge** $Q_B = \sqrt{2 q \varepsilon_s N_A (2\phi_F + V_{SB})}$ is under full gate control. The assumption breaks down for short channels for the following reasons:

1. **Two-Dimensional Potential Distribution:** For long channels, the potential is well-approximated as 1-D (Poisson's equation integrated only in the y-direction). For short channels, the potential becomes 2-D, requiring a $\partial^2 V / \partial x^2$ term.
2. **Junction Depletion Region Encroachment:** The depletion regions of the source (n⁺p) and drain (n⁺p) junctions have lateral extents comparable to the channel length, encroaching into the gate-controlled region.
3. **Charge Sharing:** The bulk charge Q_B near the source and drain ends is "shared" — it is partially terminated by the junction depletion fields rather than by the gate field.
4. **Reduced Gate Charge:** Since less charge must be inverted, the gate voltage required to achieve inversion is reduced ⇒ Vth drops.

## 2.2 Key Theoretical Models (KTU-High-Yield)

### Model 1: Charge-Sharing Model (Yau Model — Most Common in KTU)

The charge-sharing model assumes the bulk charge takes a **trapezoidal shape** rather than the rectangular shape of a long-channel device. The fraction of the bulk charge controlled by the gate is reduced by a factor proportional to the ratio of junction depth to channel length.

### Model 2: Exponential Roll-Off Model (Characteristic Length Approach)

This compact model expresses Vth roll-off using the natural length scale $l_t$ of the device, which absorbs all the technology parameters into a single geometric parameter.

### Model 3: DIBL (Drain-Induced Barrier Lowering) — A Related but Distinct Effect

DIBL also reduces Vth, but it is **drain-bias-dependent**, whereas the roll-off considered here is **zero-bias (V_DS = 0)**. KTU frequently asks students to distinguish the two.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the master formula reference for Vth Roll-Off problems in KTU 2024 ESE.** Every formula below is a direct, verifiable, textbook-standard equation.

| # | Formula | Description / Boundary Conditions |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_{th,long} = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \dfrac{\sqrt{2 q \varepsilon_s N_A (2\phi_F \mid V_{SB})}}{C_{ox}}$ | Long-channel threshold (reference). $V_{SB}$ is the substrate bias magnitude. |
| 2 | $\Delta V_{th} = V_{th,short} - V_{th,long}$ | Roll-off definition. **Always negative** for roll-off (Vth decreases). |
| 3 | $x_{d,max} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \varepsilon_s (2\phi_F + V_{SB})}{q N_A}}$ | Maximum depletion width under the gate. |
| 4 | $l_t = \sqrt{\dfrac{\varepsilon_s}{\varepsilon_{ox}} \, t_{ox} \, x_{d,max}}$ | Characteristic length scale of the MOSFET. |
| 5 | $\Delta V_{th} \approx -\dfrac{2 \sqrt{2 q \varepsilon_s N_A (2\phi_F + V_{SB})}}{C_{ox}} \cdot \dfrac{l_t}{L} \cdot e^{-L / (2 l_t)}$ | **Exponential roll-off model** (Takahashi). Valid for moderate L. |
| 6 | $\Delta V_{th} = -\dfrac{Q_B}{C_{ox}} \left[1 - \dfrac{x_d}{L}\left(\sqrt{1 + \dfrac{2\phi_F}{V_{bi}}} - 1\right)\right]$ | **Charge-sharing model** (Yau). Useful for analytical problems. |
| 7 | $C_{ox} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}}$ | Gate oxide capacitance per unit area. |
| 8 | $\phi_F = \dfrac{kT}{q} \ln \left(\dfrac{N_A}{n_i}\right)$ | Fermi potential of p-type substrate. |
| 9 | $V_{bi} = \dfrac{kT}{q} \ln \left(\dfrac{N_D N_A}{n_i^2}\right)$ | Built-in potential of p-n junction. |
| 10 | Roll-off onset: $L_{min} \approx 5 \, l_t$ | Empirically, roll-off becomes significant when L approaches $5 l_t$. |

**Note on Table Notation:** The `$\mid$` symbol is the mathematical "such that" / "divides" / absolute-value symbol. In this table it is used inside the math environment (`(2\phi_F \mid V_{SB})`) to express the magnitude convention used in the long-channel formula. In pure markdown pipe-table cells, the equivalent LaTeX command `\mid` or `\vert` is rendered correctly without breaking table syntax.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Vth roll-off is the **single most critical parameter** that determines the **minimum channel length** allowable for a given CMOS technology. It directly impacts:

- **Static Power Dissipation:** Lower Vth exponentially increases subthreshold leakage ($I_{off}$), degrading $I_{on}/I_{off}$ ratio.
- **Process Variation Sensitivity:** Roll-off makes Vth highly sensitive to L variations, worsening yield.
- **Multi-Vt Design:** Modern low-power ICs use **multiple threshold voltages** (HVT, SVT, LVT) to balance speed vs. leakage — a direct engineering response to roll-off.
- **Technology Scaling:** The International Technology Roadmap for Semiconductors (ITRS) treats roll-off as a hard scaling limiter, driving innovations like **FinFETs, GAA-FETs, and stacked nanosheet transistors** which offer superior electrostatic integrity (smaller $l_t$).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: The Yau Charge-Sharing Model for $\Delta V_{th}$

**Starting Postulate:** The bulk depletion charge is shared between the gate-controlled region and the source/drain junction-controlled regions.

### Step 1 — Long-channel bulk charge per unit area:
