Height PhiB equals 3.1 eV
Thickness tox] C[Polysilicon Gate
Conduction Band Edge Ec] D[Electron Wavefunction
Incident Psi in] E[Tunneling Region
Decay Constant kappa] F[Transmitted Wavefunction
Psi out at gate] G[Oxide Field Vector
Eox equals V over tox] H[Classical Turning Points
x1 and x2] A --> D D --> E E --> F B --> E G --> E H --> E F --> C A2[Valence Band Edge Ev] -.-> B2[Hole Tunneling Path
Barrier 4.7 eV] B2 -.-> C2[Gate Hole Collection] A --> A2 C --> C2 style A fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,color:#fff style C fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,color:#fff style B fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#fff style E fill:#ed7d31,stroke:#000,color:#000 style G fill:#70ad47,stroke:#000,color:#fff ``` ## 4.2 Tunneling Mode Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD Start[Carrier approaches Si-SiO2 interface] --> Q1{Oxide thickness tox?} Q1 -->|tox greater than 3 nm| Q2{Oxide field Eox?} Q1 -->|tox less than 3 nm| DT[Direct Tunneling Regime
Use JDT equation] Q2 -->|High field
Eox above 10 to power 9| FN[Fowler-Nordheim Tunneling
Use JFN equation] Q2 -->|Low field| DT2[Direct Tunneling still possible
but very small] FN --> Out1[Triangular Barrier
Carrier tunnels near interface] DT --> Out2[Trapezoidal Barrier
Carrier crosses entire tox] style Start fill:#264478,stroke:#000,color:#fff style DT fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#fff style FN fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#fff style Out1 fill:#70ad47,stroke:#000,color:#fff style Out2 fill:#70ad47,stroke:#000,color:#fff ``` ## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Reducing Gate Tunneling ```mermaid flowchart LR P[Problem: Gate Oxide Tunneling
Static Leakage Power] --> S1{Solution Strategy} S1 --> M1[Material Engineering
High-k Dielectric e.g. HfO2] S1 --> M2[Device Engineering
Double Gate or FinFET] S1 --> M3[Circuit Engineering
Power Gating or Multi Vth] M1 --> R1[Effective Oxide Thickness EOT
increases while capacitance preserved] M2 --> R2[Improved electrostatic control
Lower Eox needed] M3 --> R3[Idle transistors disconnected
from supply rail] R1 --> Goal[Reduced Jgate and Pstatic] R2 --> Goal R3 --> Goal style P fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#fff style S1 fill:#264478,stroke:#000,color:#fff style M1 fill:#ed7d31,stroke:#000,color:#000 style M2 fill:#ed7d31,stroke:#000,color:#000 style M3 fill:#ed7d31,stroke:#000,color:#000 style Goal fill:#70ad47,stroke:#000,color:#fff ``` # Section 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` **What is gate oxide tunneling? Differentiate between direct tunneling and Fowler-Nordheim tunneling.** **Model Answer:** Gate oxide tunneling is the **quantum mechanical transport** of charge carriers through the thin gate dielectric of a MOSFET when the oxide is scaled to a few nanometers. Even when the carrier energy is below the conduction band offset ($\Phi_B$) of SiO₂, the wavefunction has a non-zero amplitude on the gate side, producing a finite leakage current $I_{gate}$. **Differences:** | Feature | Direct Tunneling | Fowler–Nordheim Tunneling | |---------|------------------|----------------------------| | Oxide thickness | $t_{ox} < 3$ nm | $t_{ox} > 3$ nm with high field | | Barrier shape | Trapezoidal | Triangular (field-induced) | | Governing equation | $J_{DT}$ (complex closed form) | $J_{FN} = A_{FN} E_{ox}^{2} \exp(-B_{FN}/E_{ox})$ | | Carrier path | Crosses entire oxide | Tunnels near injection interface | --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]` **State the WKB approximation and write the expression for transmission probability through a potential barrier.** **Model Answer:** The **Wentzel–Kramers–Brillouin (WKB)** approximation is a semi-classical method to estimate the quantum transmission through a slowly varying potential barrier $U(x)$. The transmission probability is: $$T_{WKB} \approx \exp\!\left( -2 \int_{x_1}^{x_2} \kappa(x) \, dx \right)$$ where: $$\kappa(x) = \frac{\sqrt{2 m^{*}_{ox}\,[U(x) - E]}}{\hbar}$$ Here, $x_1$ and $x_2$ are the classical turning points and $m^{*}_{ox}$ is the effective mass of the carrier in the oxide. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### Question A `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` — Module 1 Choice Q1 #### (a) Derive the Fowler–Nordheim tunneling current density expression. List the assumptions made. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Setup:** Consider an n-MOS under inversion with oxide field $E_{ox} = V_{ox}/t_{ox}$. The trapezoidal Si/SiO₂ barrier of height $\Phi_B$ tilts under the field. **Step 2 — Tilted Potential:** $$U(x) = \Phi_B - q E_{ox} x$$ **Step 3 — Classical Turning Point:** $$\Phi_B - q E_{ox} x_2 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x_2 = \frac{\Phi_B}{q E_{ox}}$$ **Step 4 — WKB Integral:** $$I = \int_{0}^{x_2} \frac{\sqrt{2 m^{*}_{ox}(\Phi_B - q E_{ox} x)}}{\hbar} dx$$ Substituting $u = \Phi_B - q E_{ox} x$: $$I = \frac{\sqrt{2 m^{*}_{ox}}}{\hbar q E_{ox}} \int_{0}^{\Phi_B} \sqrt{u}\, du = \frac{2 \sqrt{2 m^{*}_{ox}}}{3 \hbar q E_{ox}} \Phi_B^{3/2}$$ **Step 5 — Transmission:** $$T_{FN} = \exp(-2I) = \exp\!\left( -\frac{4 \sqrt{2 m^{*}_{ox}}\,\Phi_B^{3/2}}{3 \hbar q E_{ox}} \right)$$ **Step 6 — Current Density** (via supply function): $$\boxed{J_{FN} = \frac{q^{3} E_{ox}^{2}}{16 \pi^{2} \hbar \Phi_B} \exp\!\left( -\frac{4 \sqrt{2 m^{*}_{ox}}\,\Phi_B^{3/2}}{3 \hbar q E_{ox}} \right)}$$ **Assumptions:** 1. Effective mass approximation: $m^{*}_{ox} = 0.5 m_0$ constant throughout oxide. 2. Free-electron supply function at the injecting interface. 3. Neglect of image-force barrier lowering (Schottky effect). 4. Parabolic energy-momentum dispersion in Si. 5. Single-band tunneling (no inter-band transitions). --- #### (b) Explain with band diagrams the two modes of gate oxide tunneling. How does the choice of $t_{ox}$ affect the dominant mode? **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Mode 1 — Direct Tunneling:** - Occurs when $t_{ox} < 3$ nm. - Carrier wavefunction penetrates the full oxide thickness. - The barrier is **trapezoidal** in shape. - Dominant at low to moderate oxide fields. - Carrier energy $E < \Phi_B$; transmission coefficient $T = \exp(-2\kappa t_{ox})$. **Mode 2 — Fowler–Nordheim Tunneling:** - Dominates at higher fields or thicker oxides ($t_{ox} > 3$ nm). - Field tilts the barrier into a **triangular** shape. - Carrier tunnels only through a narrow slice near the injecting interface. - $T = \exp(-B_{FN}/E_{ox})$ — strongly field dependent. **Effect of $t_{ox}$:** - For thick oxides, the FN mechanism dominates because direct tunneling is suppressed exponentially. - As $t_{ox}$ shrinks below 3 nm, direct tunneling becomes comparable to or larger than FN. - Below **1.5 nm**, direct tunneling completely dominates and becomes a hard physical limit for continued SiO₂ scaling. | $t_{ox}$ Range | Dominant Mechanism | Engineering Action | |----------------|---------------------|---------------------| | $> 3$ nm | Fowler–Nordheim | Acceptable; legacy nodes | | $1.5$–$3$ nm | Direct tunneling emerging | Use high-k dielectrics | | $< 1.5$ nm | Direct tunneling dominant | Mandatory high-k + metal gate | --- ### Question B `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]` — Module 1 Choice Q2 (Alternative) #### (a) Discuss the impact of gate oxide tunneling on the static power dissipation of modern CMOS circuits. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Impact Summary:** 1. **Static Power Inflation:** Gate tunneling current $I_{gate}$ flows even when the transistor is "off" (no channel conduction). This adds directly to the leakage power: $$P_{tunnel} = I_{gate} \cdot V_{DD}$$ 2. **Technology Node Dependence:** As $t_{ox}$ scales from 5 nm (at 250 nm node) to ~1.2 nm (at 45 nm node), $J_{gate}$ rises by **5–6 orders of magnitude**. 3. **Total Static Power:** In advanced nodes (≤ 65 nm), gate tunneling can account for **30–50%** of the total sub-threshold + gate leakage budget. 4. **Process Variability Impact:** Tunneling is exponentially sensitive to $t_{ox}$. A ±5% variation in $t_{ox}$ produces a **multi-fold** variation in leakage — complicating yield analysis. 5. **Power Crisis:** For chips with billions of transistors, even $\mu$A-level leakage translates into watts of wasted power, contributing to thermal hot-spots and reduced battery life in mobile devices. **Mitigation Strategies:** - **High-k dielectrics (HfO₂)** to increase physical thickness without changing EOT. - **Metal gates** to avoid poly-Si depletion which effectively reduces $t_{ox}$. - **Reverse body biasing** to lower $|V_{ox}|$ in idle modes. - **Multi-$V_{th}$** design to assign LVT only to critical paths. --- #### (b) For a MOSFET with $t_{ox} = 2.0$ nm, $\Phi_B = 3.1$ eV, $m^{*}_{ox} = 0.5 m_0$, and $V_{ox} = 1.2$ V, estimate the FN tunneling current density. Comment on whether this represents a significant leakage. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Given:** - $t_{ox} = 2.0 \times 10^{-9}$ m - $\Phi_B = 3.1$ eV $= 4.96 \times 10^{-19}$ J - $V_{ox} = 1.2$ V - $m^{*}_{ox} = 0.5 \cdot 9.11 \times 10^{-31} = 4.555 \times 10^{-31}$ kg **Step 1 — Oxide Field:** $$E_{ox} = \frac{1.2}{2.0 \times 10^{-9}} = 6.0 \times 10^{8} \, \text{V/m}$$ **Step 2 — Compute $B_{FN}$:** $$B_{FN} = \frac{4 \sqrt{2 \cdot 4.555 \times 10^{-31}} \, (4.96 \times 10^{-19})^{3/2}}{3 \cdot 1.054 \times 10^{-34} \cdot 1.6 \times 10^{-19}}$$ - $\sqrt{2 m^{*}_{ox}} = \sqrt{9.11 \times 10^{-31}} = 9.54 \times 10^{-16}$ - $\Phi_B^{3/2} = (4.96 \times 10^{-19})^{1.5} = 3.49 \times 10^{-28}$ Numerator: $4 \cdot 9.54 \times 10^{-16} \cdot 3.49 \times 10^{-28} = 1.33 \times 10^{-42}$ Denominator: $3 \cdot 1.054 \times 10^{-34} \cdot 1.6 \times 10^{-19} = 5.06 \times 10^{-53}$ $$B_{FN} = 2.63 \times 10^{10} \, \text{V/m}$$ **Step 3 — Compute $A_{FN}$:** $$A_{FN} = \frac{(1.6 \times 10^{-19})^{3}}{16 \pi^{2} \cdot 1.054 \times 10^{-34} \cdot 4.96 \times 10^{-19}} \approx 4.96 \times 10^{-7} \, \text{A/V}^{2}$$ **Step 4 — Compute $J_{FN}$:** $$J_{FN} = 4.96 \times 10^{-7} \cdot (6.0 \times 10^{8})^{2} \cdot \exp\!\left( -\frac{2.63 \times 10^{10}}{6.0 \times 10^{8}} \right)$$ $$= 4.96 \times 10^{-7} \cdot 3.6 \times 10^{17} \cdot \exp(-43.83)$$ $$= 1.79 \times 10^{11} \cdot 9.4 \times 10^{-20}$$ $$\boxed{J_{FN} \approx 1.68 \times 10^{-8} \, \text{A/cm}^{2} \text{ (per device, scaled appropriately)}}$$ > **Comment:** For a device of area $W \cdot L = 100 \, \text{nm} \times 100 \, \text{nm} = 10^{-10} \, \text{cm}^{2}$, this corresponds to $I_{gate} \approx 1.68 \times 10^{-18}$ A. While small per device, across $10^{9}$ transistors on a chip, the cumulative leakage reaches **microamperes**, contributing measurably to total static power — confirming that gate tunneling is a **non-negligible leakage** in modern CMOS. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Missing effective mass value:** Students often write $m_0$ instead of $m^{*}_{ox} = 0.5 m_0$ — this gives a wrong $B_{FN}$ and forfeits 2 marks. > 2. **Confusing units of $E_{ox}$:** Use V/m consistently. A common error is mixing V/cm in the exponent — leads to a wildly wrong answer. > 3. **Direct vs FN confusion:** Examiners expect you to state that direct tunneling is the modern concern; FN applies for thicker oxides or high-field stress. > 4. **No mention of EOT:** In high-k discussions, always introduce the concept of **Equivalent Oxide Thickness** $t_{EOT} = t_{high-k} \cdot (\varepsilon_{SiO_2}/\varepsilon_{high-k})$ to get full marks. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Gate Oxide Tunneling** - **What it is:** Quantum tunneling of carriers through a thin gate dielectric — a static leakage mechanism. - **Two regimes:** **Direct Tunneling** ($t_{ox} < 3$ nm) and **Fowler–Nordheim Tunneling** (high $E_{ox}$ or thicker oxide). - **Master equation:** Transmission probability $T \approx \exp(-2 \int \kappa(x) dx)$ from WKB approximation. - **Decay constant:** $\kappa(x) = \sqrt{2 m^*_{ox}[U(x) - E]}/\hbar$. - **FN current density:** $J_{FN} = A_{FN} E_{ox}^2 \exp(-B_{FN}/E_{ox})$. - **Key constant:** $A_{FN} = q^{3}/(16 \pi^{2} \hbar \Phi_B) \approx 10^{-6} \, \text{A/V}^{2}$. - **Barrier height:** $\Phi_B = 3.1$ eV (electron), $4.7$ eV (hole) for Si/SiO₂. - **Effective mass:** $m^{*}_{ox} = 0.5 m_0$ (assume unless stated otherwise). - **Critical thickness:** $\sim 1.5$ nm — below this, direct tunneling is prohibitive. - **Mitigation:** Use high-k dielectrics (HfO₂), metal gates, FinFET/Multi-gate structures, and circuit-level power gating. - **Power impact:** $P_{tunnel} = I_{gate} \cdot V_{DD}$; small per device, large cumulatively. - **KTU CO mapping:** Directly serves **CO1** — sources of power dissipation. - **Bloom's level:** Frequently tested at **Apply / Analyze** levels in 14-mark questions. - **Examiner hot topics:** Always state assumptions in derivations; compute numerical $J_{FN}$ values; mention EOT when discussing high-k.

topics.title: Subthreshold Leakage Current
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT866 - LOW POWER VLSI
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Subthreshold Leakage Current

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition
**Subthreshold Leakage Current ($I_{sub}$ or $I_{leak}$)** is the parasitic drain-to-source current that flows through a MOSFET when the gate-to-source voltage ($V_{GS}$) is **less than the threshold voltage** ($V_{th}$), i.e., when the transistor is nominally in the "OFF" state ($V_{GS} < V_{th}$). It arises due to the diffusion of charge carriers (electrons in NMOS, holes in PMOS) across the channel under the gate oxide, a phenomenon known as **weak inversion** conduction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Subthreshold leakage is one of the *four* dominant leakage mechanisms in deep-submicron CMOS (along with Gate-Oxide Tunneling, Reverse-Biased Junction Leakage, and Hot-Carrier Injection). In modern nanometer nodes (< 90 nm), $I_{sub}$ typically contributes **30%–50% of total static leakage power** in a chip.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine a **water tap (faucet) installed in a sink**:

- The **handle** of the tap = $V_{GS}$ (the control signal).
- The **"fully closed"** position = $V_{GS} = 0$ (transistor OFF).
- Even when the tap is "fully closed" and you stop applying pressure (i.e., $V_{GS} = 0$ < $V_{th}$), a few stubborn **water droplets still drip out**. You cannot completely stop the flow — this residual dripping is your **subthreshold leakage**.
- The thinner the rubber washer inside the tap, the more droplets leak — analogous to a **thinner gate oxide ($t_{ox}$)** allowing more electron tunneling/leakage.
- Also, the higher the water pressure in the pipeline (analogous to higher $V_{DS}$), the more droplets force their way through — this is precisely the **Drain-Induced Barrier Lowering (DIBL)** effect.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded)
- **Thermal Voltage ($V_T = kT/q$)**: ≈ **25.85 mV at 300 K** (room temperature).
- **Subthreshold Swing ($S$)**: typically **60–100 mV/decade** in bulk CMOS.
- **Minimum theoretical limit of $S$**: **$S_{min} = 60$ mV/decade at 300 K** (cannot be undercut at room temperature without exotic device engineering).
- **Threshold Voltage ($V_{th}$)**: typically **0.3 V to 0.5 V** in modern low-power processes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition to memorize for KTU exams:** "Subthreshold leakage is the weak-inversion diffusion current flowing from drain to source in a MOSFET biased in the cut-off region."

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Mechanism of Subthreshold Conduction
When $V_{GS} < V_{th}$, the surface under the gate oxide is *not* strongly inverted (no continuous conducting channel exists), but it is **weakly inverted**. A small density of minority carriers still exists at the Si–SiO₂ interface, and these carriers diffuse from source to drain, similar to charge transport in a bipolar transistor's base region.

The conduction physics can be broken into 6 logical steps:

1. **Gate voltage is below $V_{th}$** → No inversion layer (channel) is formed. The surface is in the *depletion* or *weak inversion* regime.
2. **A small exponential population of electrons** exists at the Si surface, governed by the Boltzmann distribution: their concentration is $\propto \exp\big((q\psi_s)/{kT}\big)$, where $\psi_s$ is the surface potential.
3. As $V_{GS}$ increases *toward* $V_{th}$ (still below it), the surface potential $\psi_s$ rises linearly, exponentially increasing the carrier density.
4. This exponentially increasing minority-carrier population creates a **diffusion gradient** from source to drain.
5. The diffusion current is collected at the drain terminal → registered as $I_{sub}$.
6. Because the transport is **diffusive (not drift)**, the current shows an **exponential dependence** on $V_{GS}$ — this is the signature of subthreshold conduction.

> [!TIP]
> **Why does it matter for low-power VLSI?** In modern processors, billions of transistors are in standby mode at any given instant. Even nano-amperes per transistor × billions of transistors × supply voltage = **milliwatts of wasted power**, and worse, **elevated chip temperature** which further *increases* leakage (positive feedback).

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description / Key Notes | Units |
|---|---------|------------------------|-------|
| 1 | $I_{sub} = I_0 \cdot \exp\!\left(\dfrac{V_{GS}-V_{th}}{n \cdot V_T}\right)\left[1 - \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{V_{DS}}{V_T}\right)\right]$ | Master equation for subthreshold current (long-channel) | A (Amperes) |
| 2 | $V_T = \dfrac{kT}{q} \approx 25.85 \text{ mV at 300 K}$ | Thermal voltage | V (Volts) |
| 3 | $I_0 = \mu_0 \, C_{ox}\,\dfrac{W}{L}\,V_T^{2}\,\exp(1.8)$ | Pre-exponential constant (technology-dependent) | A |
| 4 | $n = 1 + \dfrac{C_{dm}}{C_{ox}}$ | Subthreshold ideality (body-effect) factor (typically 1.3 – 1.5) | dimensionless |
| 5 | $S = n \cdot V_T \cdot \ln(10) \approx 2.3 \cdot n \cdot V_T$ | Subthreshold swing (inverse slope of log $I_D$ vs $V_{GS}$) | V/decade |
| 6 | $S_{min} = 2.3 \cdot V_T \approx 60 \text{ mV/dec at 300 K}$ | Boltzmann limit (cannot be undercut at room T) | V/decade |
| 7 | $I_{sub,short} = I_0 \cdot \exp\!\left(\dfrac{V_{GS}-V_{th}+\eta \, V_{DS}}{n \, V_T}\right)\left[1 - \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{V_{DS}}{V_T}\right)\right]$ | Short-channel form (includes DIBL) | A |
| 8 | $V_{th}(V_{BS}) = V_{th0} + \gamma\left(\sqrt{\vert 2\phi_F + V_{BS} \vert} - \sqrt{\vert 2\phi_F \vert}\right)$ | Body-bias dependent threshold (body effect) | V |
| 9 | $I_{leak} \propto T^{2} \cdot \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_g}{kT}\right)$ | Temperature dependence of leakage | A |
| 10 | $P_{leak} = I_{sub} \cdot V_{DD}$ | Static leakage power dissipated per OFF transistor | W (Watts) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical exam tip:** When $V_{DS} \geq 3V_T$ (which is almost always true since $V_{DD} \gg 75$ mV), the term $\big[1 - \exp(-V_{DS}/V_T)\big] \to 1$. The equation **collapses to the simpler exponential form** $I_{sub} \approx I_0 \cdot \exp\big((V_{GS}-V_{th})/(nV_T)\big)$. This is the version 90% of KTU problems use.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Standby power budgeting** in mobile SoCs (e.g., smartphone chips must stay below 1 mW leakage in sleep mode).
- **Power-gating design**: when supply is cut, $I_{sub}$ becomes the dominant residual current in headers/footers.
- **Reverse Body Bias (RBB)** is applied during sleep to *raise* $V_{th}$ and exponentially suppress $I_{sub}$ — used in dynamic voltage scaling schemes.
- **Process corner analysis**: "Fast" (FF) corner has *higher* $I_{sub}$ → worst-case leakage; "Slow" (SS) corner has *higher* dynamic delay.
- **Multi-$V_{th}$ design libraries** (HVT, SVT, LVT cells) trade off leakage vs. performance.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Subthreshold Current Equation (Long-Channel)

We derive $I_{sub}$ from first principles using the **charge-sheet approximation** and **Boltzmann statistics** for the minority carrier concentration.

---

#### **Step 1 — Surface potential and inversion charge**

In weak inversion, the inversion-layer charge density per unit area is given by:

$$Q_n(x) = -q \, n_0 \, \exp\!\left(\frac{\psi_s(x)}{V_T}\right)$$

where $n_0$ is the equilibrium electron concentration and $\psi_s(x)$ is the local surface potential at position $x$ along the channel.

*Explanation:* The exponential is the Boltzmann factor — at any point along the channel, the electron density depends exponentially on the local surface potential.

---

#### **Step 2 — Gradual-channel approximation (no drift in subthreshold)**

Since there is no inversion layer (no conducting sheet) in weak inversion, the drain current is **purely diffusive**:

$$I_{sub} = - \mu_n \, W \, Q_n(x) \, \frac{d\psi_s(x)}{dx}$$

*Explanation:* Fick's law of diffusion. The gradient $d\psi_s/dx$ drives the carriers from source to drain.

---

#### **Step 3 — Separate variables and integrate along the channel**

Rearranging:

$$I_{sub} \, dx = \mu_n \, W \, q \, n_0 \, \exp\!\left(\frac{\psi_s}{V_T}\right) d\psi_s$$

Integrating from source ($x=0, \psi_s = \psi_{s0}$) to drain ($x=L, \psi_s = \psi_{sL}$):

$$I_{sub} \int_0^L dx = \mu_n \, W \, q \, n_0 \, V_T \left[ \exp\!\left(\frac{\psi_{sL}}{V_T}\right) - \exp\!\left(\frac{\psi_{s0}}{V_T}\right) \right]$$

This gives:

$$I_{sub} = \frac{\mu_n \, W \, q \, n_0 \, V_T}{L}\left[ \exp\!\left(\frac{\psi_{sL}}{V_T}\right) - \exp\!\left(\frac{\psi_{s0}}{V_T}\right) \right]$$

---

#### **Step 4 — Relate $\psi_s$ to $V_{GS}$, $V_{th}$, and $V_{DS}$**

Using the capacitor divider model (with depletion and oxide capacitances):

$$\psi_s = \frac{V_{GS} - V_{th}}{n} + \frac{V_{DS}}{2}\text{ (approximately, for long channel)}$$

More precisely, the source-end and drain-end surface potentials are:

$$\psi_{s0} = \frac{V_{GS} - V_{th}}{n}, \quad \psi_{sL} = \psi_{s0} - \frac{V_{DS}}{n}$$

*Explanation:* The factor $n = 1 + C_{dm}/C_{ox}$ accounts for the fact that the gate does not fully control $\psi_s$ — the depletion region also takes a share of the gate voltage.

---

#### **Step 5 — Substitute back into the current expression**

$$I_{sub} = \frac{\mu_n \, W \, q \, n_0 \, V_T}{L}\left[ \exp\!\left(\frac{V_{GS} - V_{th} - V_{DS}}{n V_T}\right) - \exp\!\left(\frac{V_{GS} - V_{th}}{n V_T}\right) \right]$$

Factoring out $\exp\!\big((V_{GS}-V_{th})/(nV_T)\big)$:

$$I_{sub} = \frac{\mu_n \, W \, q \, n_0 \, V_T}{L}\,\exp\!\left(\frac{V_{GS} - V_{th}}{n V_T}\right)\left[ \exp\!\left(-\frac{V_{DS}}{n V_T}\right) - 1 \right]$$

---

#### **Step 6 — Final master equation (classical form)**

Multiplying numerator and denominator, defining $I_0 = \mu_n C_{ox}(W/L)V_T^{2} \cdot \exp(1.8)$ (a technology constant that absorbs $n_0, q$, and other material parameters), and noting that for $V_{DS} \gg V_T$ the term in brackets → 1:

$$\boxed{\,I_{sub} = I_0 \, \exp\!\left(\frac{V_{GS} - V_{th}}{n V_T}\right) \left[ 1 - \exp\!\left(-\frac{V_{DS}}{V_T}\right) \right]\,}$$

For $V_{DS} \geq 3V_T$ (the practical case):

$$I_{sub} \approx I_0 \, \exp\!\left(\frac{V_{GS} - V_{th}}{n V_T}\right)$$

---

#### **Step 7 — Derivation of Subthreshold Swing $S$**

By definition, $S$ is the gate-voltage change required to reduce $I_{sub}$ by one decade (factor of 10). Taking $\log_{10}$ of the simplified $I_{sub}$:

$$\log_{10}(I_{sub}) = \log_{10}(I_0) + \frac{V_{GS} - V_{th}}{n V_T \, \ln(10)}$$

Differentiating w.r.t. $V_{GS}$:

$$\frac{d\log_{10}(I_{sub})}{dV_{GS}} = \frac{1}{n V_T \ln(10)}$$

Therefore, $S$ (the inverse slope, measured in V/decade) is:

$$S = n V_T \ln(10) = 2.3 \, n \, V_T$$

*Interpretation:* $S$ is essentially the *gain* of the transistor's switch-off characteristic. A smaller $S$ means a sharper transition between ON and OFF.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why $S_{min} = 60$ mV/dec at 300 K?** When $n = 1$ (perfect gate control, no depletion capacitance, ideal case), $S_{min} = 2.3 \cdot V_T = 2.3 \cdot 25.85 \text{ mV} \approx 59.5 \text{ mV/dec} \approx 60 \text{ mV/dec}$. This is a **thermodynamic limit** — to go below 60 mV/dec you would need to change $kT$ (i.e., cool the chip) or use quantum-mechanical tunneling transistors (e.g., TFETs).

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

**Problem:** A 65 nm NMOS transistor has $W/L = 1$, $V_{th} = 0.35$ V, $n = 1.4$, $I_0 = 1 \mu$A, and operates at $T = 300$ K with $V_{GS} = 0$ V and $V_{DS} = 1.0$ V. Find $I_{sub}$ and the subthreshold swing $S$.

**Solution:**

Given: $V_T = 25.85$ mV, $V_{DS}/V_T = 1.0 / 0.02585 \approx 38.7 \gg 3$, so the bracket $\approx 1$.

$$I_{sub} = (1 \,\mu\text{A}) \cdot \exp\!\left(\frac{0 - 0.35}{1.4 \cdot 0.02585}\right)$$

Exponent:

$$\frac{-0.35}{0.03619} = -9.671$$

$$I_{sub} = 1 \,\mu\text{A} \cdot e^{-9.671} = 1 \,\mu\text{A} \cdot (6.23 \times 10^{-5}) = 62.3 \text{ nA}$$

Subthreshold swing:

$$S = 2.3 \cdot 1.4 \cdot 25.85 \text{ mV} = 83.3 \text{ mV/decade}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Notice:** Even with $V_{GS} = 0$ (logic "0" = OFF), the transistor leaks **62.3 nA** — this is the off-state leakage. In a chip with $10^9$ such transistors, total OFF-leakage ≈ 62 A — which is why **leakage control is critical**.

---

### 3.3 Effect of DIBL (Drain-Induced Barrier Lowering)

In short-channel devices, the drain field penetrates to the source junction, effectively *lowering* the source-side barrier. This causes $V_{th}$ to depend on $V_{DS}$:

$$V_{th}(V_{DS}) = V_{th0} - \eta \, V_{DS}$$

where $\eta$ is the DIBL coefficient (units: V/V). Substituting:

$$I_{sub,short} = I_0 \, \exp\!\left(\frac{V_{GS} - V_{th0} + \eta V_{DS}}{n V_T}\right)$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's pitfall:** Many students forget that **DIBL exponentially multiplies leakage**. A small DIBL coefficient $\eta = 0.1$ with $V_{DS} = 1$ V effectively shifts $V_{th}$ down by 100 mV, which can **increase $I_{sub}$ by 10×** (since 100 mV ≈ 1 decade at $S = 100$ mV/dec). Always include DIBL in nanometer-node analyses.

---

### 3.4 Body-Bias Effect on $I_{sub}$

Applying reverse body bias ($V_{BS} < 0$ for NMOS) raises $V_{th}$:

$$V_{th}(V_{BS}) = V_{th0} + \gamma\Big(\sqrt{\vert 2\phi_F + V_{BS} \vert} - \sqrt{\vert 2\phi_F \vert}\Big)$$

A small increase $\Delta V_{th}$ suppresses $I_{sub}$ exponentially:

$$I_{sub}' = I_{sub} \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{\Delta V_{th}}{n V_T}\right)$$

**For example:** if $V_{BS} = -0.5$ V raises $V_{th}$ by 100 mV, then $I_{sub}$ is reduced by a factor of $\approx 10$ (one decade).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Subthreshold Leakage Path

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional flow** of how subthreshold leakage forms and the engineering knobs available to suppress it. This is a system-level block architecture suitable for visualizing the interaction between device physics, control terminals, and design techniques.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Power Supply V_DD] --> B[MOSFET Device]
    C[Gate Input V_GS] --> B
    D[Body Terminal V_BS] --> B
    E[Drain V_DS] --> B
    B --> F[Subthreshold Current I_sub]
    F --> G[Leakage Power P_leak = I_sub x V_DD]
    B --> H[Subthreshold Swing S = 2.3 n V_T]
    H --> I{Engineering Knobs}
    I --> J[Threshold Engineering: High V_th Cells]
    I --> K[Body Bias: Reverse Bias Raises V_th]
    I --> L[Stack Effect: Series OFF Transistors]
    I --> M[Power Gating: Sleep Transistor Cutoff]
    I --> N[Multi V_th Library: HVT SVT LVT Selection]
    J --> O[Reduced I_sub]
    K --> O
    L --> O
    M --> O
    N --> O
    O --> P[Low Power Standby State]
```

### 4.2 Transistor Off-State Cross-Sectional Schematic (Textual Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Source N+ Region] --> B[Channel Weak Inversion Layer]
    B --> C[Drain N+ Region]
    D[Gate Oxide SiO2 Layer] --> B
    E[Poly Silicon Gate V_GS less than V_th] --> D
    F[P-Substrate Body Terminal V_BS] --> B
    B -.Diffusion Path.-> A
    B -.Diffusion Path.-> C
    style A fill:#f9f,stroke:#333
    style C fill:#f9f,stroke:#333
    style D fill:#ff9,stroke:#333
    style E fill:#9cf,stroke:#333
```

### 4.3 Leakage Reduction Hierarchy (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Total Static Leakage Power] --> L1[Subthreshold Leakage]
    Start --> L2[Gate Oxide Tunneling Leakage]
    Start --> L3[Junction Leakage]
    L1 --> R1[Reverse Body Bias RBB]
    L1 --> R2[Stack Forcing in Logic]
    L1 --> R3[Power Gating with Sleep Transistor]
    L1 --> R4[Multi Vth Cell Assignment]
    R1 --> Result[Leakage Reduction by 5x to 50x]
    R2 --> Result
    R3 --> Result
    R4 --> Result
    style Start fill:#bbf,stroke:#333
    style Result fill:#9f9,stroke:#333
```

### 4.4 Annotated Mermaid: $I_{sub}$ vs $V_{GS}$ Characteristic Curve

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Strong Inversion V_GS greater than V_th] --> B[Quadratic or Velocity Saturated I_D]
    C[Weak Inversion V_GS less than V_th Subthreshold] --> D[Exponential I_sub Dependency]
    E[Cutoff V_GS less than 0 approx] --> F[Negligible I_sub - Reverse Biased]
    B --> G[Log I_D Linear in V_GS]
    D --> G
    G --> H[Slope of Line in Subthreshold Region equals 1 over S]
    style C fill:#ffd,stroke:#333
    style D fill:#fdd,stroke:#333
    style H fill:#dfd,stroke:#333
```

> [!TIP]
> **How to read the diagrams in the KTU exam:** Always annotate the **three operating regions** of a MOSFET (cutoff, subthreshold, strong inversion) and indicate the **slope discontinuity** at $V_{GS} = V_{th}$ in the log scale — this is the heart of any KTU subthreshold question.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**  
Define subthreshold leakage current in a MOSFET. Why is it important in modern nanometer CMOS design?

**Model Answer (≈ 3-mark length):**

**Definition:** Subthreshold leakage current ($I_{sub}$) is the drain-to-source current that flows through a MOSFET even when the gate-to-source voltage is below the threshold voltage ($V_{GS} < V_{th}$), i.e., the transistor is nominally OFF.

**Why it matters:**  
(i) In nanometer CMOS (< 90 nm), supply voltage and threshold voltage are both scaled down, so $V_{th}$ becomes comparable to thermal voltage $V_T$, making $I_{sub}$ significant.  
(ii) The number of OFF transistors at any instant in a modern VLSI chip is in the order of **billions**, so even a few nA per device sums to mA of static current.  
(iii) $I_{sub}$ contributes to **static (leakage) power dissipation** even when the circuit is idle, which is critical for **battery-powered portable devices**.  
(iv) It grows **exponentially with temperature**, creating a thermal-runaway risk in densely packed chips.

---

**Q2.** **[KTU University Exam – Jun 2023]**  
What is subthreshold swing? What is its minimum theoretical value at room temperature and why?

**Model Answer:**

The subthreshold swing $S$ is defined as the change in gate voltage required to change the subthreshold drain current by one decade (factor of 10):

$$S = \frac{d V_{GS}}{d \log_{10}(I_{sub})} = 2.3 \, n \, V_T = 2.3 \, n \, \frac{kT}{q}$$

**Minimum theoretical value:** At 300 K, with $n = 1$ (ideal gate control), $S_{min} = 2.3 \cdot 25.85 \text{ mV} \approx 60 \text{ mV/decade}$.

**Reason:** The 60 mV/dec limit arises from the **Boltzmann distribution** of carriers at thermal equilibrium. The factor $kT/q$ in the carrier statistics sets a thermodynamic floor — to go below 60 mV/dec, one must either **reduce temperature** (cryogenic operation) or use **non-Boltzmann transport** devices such as Tunnel FETs (TFETs) or impact-ionization MOSFETs.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the expression for subthreshold leakage current in a long-channel MOSFET, clearly stating all assumptions used. Show that the subthreshold swing $S = 2.3 \, n \, V_T$, where $n$ is the subthreshold ideality factor.

**(b) [7 Marks]** An NMOS transistor in 45 nm technology has the following parameters: $V_{th} = 0.4$ V, $n = 1.5$, $I_0 = 5$ μA, $V_T = 25.85$ mV, $W/L = 2$.  
(i) Calculate the subthreshold leakage current when $V_{GS} = 0$ V and $V_{DS} = 1.0$ V.  
(ii) If reverse body bias of $V_{BS} = -0.6$ V increases $V_{th}$ by 90 mV, find the new leakage.  
(iii) Calculate the subthreshold swing in mV/decade and comment on its deviation from the 60 mV/dec limit.

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Derivation:**  
The detailed 7-step derivation is **exactly the one presented in Section 3.1 of these notes** — students should write it as:  
- Start from weak-inversion inversion charge $Q_n(x) = -q n_0 \exp(\psi_s/V_T)$.  
- Use Fick's diffusion law: $I_{sub} = -\mu_n W Q_n (d\psi_s/dx)$.  
- Separate variables, integrate from 0 to $L$.  
- Substitute $\psi_{s0} = (V_{GS}-V_{th})/n$ and $\psi_{sL} = \psi_{s0} - V_{DS}/n$.  
- Arrive at $I_{sub} = I_0 \exp\big((V_{GS}-V_{th})/(nV_T)\big)\big[1 - \exp(-V_{DS}/V_T)\big]$.  
- For $V_{DS} \gg V_T$, the bracket → 1, leaving the simple exponential.  
- Differentiate $\log_{10}(I_{sub})$ w.r.t. $V_{GS}$ to get $S = 2.3 \, n \, V_T$.

**Part (b) — Numerical solution:**

**(i)** With $V_{DS}/V_T = 1.0/0.02585 \approx 38.7 \gg 3$, the bracket term $\approx 1$.

$$I_{sub} = 5 \,\mu\text{A} \cdot \exp\!\left(\frac{0 - 0.4}{1.5 \cdot 0.02585}\right) = 5 \,\mu\text{A} \cdot e^{-10.32}$$

$$e^{-10.32} \approx 3.31 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$I_{sub} = 5 \,\mu\text{A} \cdot 3.31 \times 10^{-5} = 165.5 \text{ nA}$$

**(ii)** New threshold: $V_{th}' = 0.4 + 0.090 = 0.49$ V.

$$I_{sub}' = 5 \,\mu\text{A} \cdot \exp\!\left(\frac{-0.49}{0.0388}\right) = 5 \,\mu\text{A} \cdot e^{-12.63}$$

$$e^{-12.63} \approx 3.24 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$I_{sub}' = 5 \,\mu\text{A} \cdot 3.24 \times 10^{-6} = 16.2 \text{ nA}$$

**Reduction factor:** $165.5 / 16.2 \approx 10.2 \times$ — *One decade suppression of leakage just by 90 mV $V_{th}$ bump from RBB*.

**(iii)** Subthreshold swing:

$$S = 2.3 \cdot 1.5 \cdot 25.85 \text{ mV} = 89.2 \text{ mV/decade}$$

**Comment:** $S = 89.2$ mV/dec is **48% above the Boltzmann limit of 60 mV/dec**, indicating a non-ideal (real) device with $n = 1.5$ instead of 1. The extra gate voltage "wasted" on the depletion capacitance ($C_{dm}/C_{ox} = 0.5$) is the cause. This is typical of bulk CMOS — fully-depleted SOI or FinFET technologies get closer to the limit.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — **[KTU University Exam – Jun 2022, Modified]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of the $I_D$–$V_{GS}$ characteristic curve (semi-log plot), explain the three operating regions of a MOSFET. Clearly mark the subthreshold region and derive the relationship between subthreshold swing and ideality factor.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the impact of DIBL (Drain-Induced Barrier Lowering) on subthreshold leakage in short-channel MOSFETs. Explain how the modified $I_{sub}$ equation incorporates DIBL and the body effect. List any **four** circuit-level techniques to reduce subthreshold leakage.

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Three operating regions:**

Refer to a semi-log plot of $I_D$ vs $V_{GS}$ for a fixed $V_{DS}$:

- **Region 1 — Cutoff (strong reverse bias, $V_{GS} \ll 0$):** $I_D \approx 0$ (junction leakage only, negligible).
- **Region 2 — Subthreshold / Weak Inversion ($0 < V_{GS} < V_{th}$):** $I_D$ rises **exponentially** with $V_{GS}$, appearing as a **straight line on a log scale** with slope $1/S$. This is the leakage region.
- **Region 3 — Strong Inversion ($V_{GS} > V_{th}$):** $I_D$ follows the **quadratic or velocity-saturated** equation, much larger currents.

The **transition point** at $V_{GS} = V_{th}$ marks the **knee** of the curve. The **subthreshold swing** is the inverse slope in Region 2:

$$S = \frac{\Delta V_{GS}}{\Delta \log_{10} I_D} = 2.3 \, n \, V_T$$

**Part (b) — DIBL and reduction techniques:**

**DIBL effect:** In short-channel devices (L < ~100 nm), the drain electric field penetrates to the source, effectively lowering the source-side potential barrier. This causes the threshold voltage to **decrease as $V_{DS}$ increases**:

$$V_{th}(V_{DS}) = V_{th0} - \eta \, V_{DS}$$

**Modified subthreshold current:**

$$I_{sub,short} = I_0 \, \exp\!\left(\frac{V_{GS} - V_{th0} + \eta V_{DS}}{n V_T}\right)$$

This is **exponentially sensitive** to $V_{DS}$ — even a small DIBL coefficient ($\eta \approx 0.05$ to $0.2$) can multiply leakage by **5–10×** in nanometer devices.

**Four circuit-level techniques to reduce subthreshold leakage:**

1. **Reverse Body Bias (RBB):** Apply $V_{BS} < 0$ to NMOS in standby. This raises $V_{th}$ and exponentially suppresses $I_{sub}$.
2. **Stack Effect (Forcing in Sleep):** Use two or more series OFF transistors instead of one. Each OFF device has a smaller $V_{DS}$, which (via DIBL) raises its effective $V_{th}$ and reduces leakage.
3. **Multi-$V_{th}$ Cell Library:** In non-critical timing paths, use **High-$V_{th}$ (HVT)** cells which have lower leakage. Reserve Low-$V_{th}$ (LVT) cells for critical paths only.
4. **Power Gating (Sleep Transistor):** Insert a header or footer transistor that is turned OFF in sleep mode, completely cutting off $V_{DD}$ to the logic block. This is the most effective technique, giving leakage reduction of **100–1000×**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Critical for Marks):**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse the subthreshold equation with the strong-inversion (square-law) equation** — they apply in *different* regions. Many students incorrectly write $I_D = \frac{1}{2}\mu C_{ox}(W/L)(V_{GS}-V_{th})^2$ in subthreshold questions. The square law is for $V_{GS} > V_{th}$ only.
> 2. **Always verify the approximation** $V_{DS} \gg 3V_T$ before dropping the bracket term — losing this step costs 1 mark.
> 3. **Don't forget the units in $S$** — it is mV/decade, not V. A 60 mV swing means a *factor of 10*, not 60 V!
> 4. **DIBL is NOT a separate leakage mechanism** — it is a *modifier* of subthreshold leakage. Examiners deduct marks if you list it as a standalone leakage source.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Print this section and stick it on your wall before the exam.**

- [ ] **Definition:** $I_{sub}$ is the weak-inversion *diffusion* current when $V_{GS} < V_{th}$ — the transistor is "OFF" but still leaks.
- [ ] **Master equation:** $I_{sub} = I_0 \exp\!\big((V_{GS}-V_{th})/(nV_T)\big)\big[1 - \exp(-V_{DS}/V_T)\big]$; for $V_{DS} \geq 3V_T$, bracket → 1.
- [ ] **Thermal voltage:** $V_T = kT/q \approx 25.85$ mV at 300 K — appears in *every* subthreshold problem.
- [ ] **Subthreshold Swing:** $S = 2.3 \, n \, V_T$; **minimum 60 mV/decade** (Boltzmann limit) at 300 K.
- [ ] **Ideality factor:** $n = 1 + C_{dm}/C_{ox}$, typically 1.3 – 1.5 in bulk CMOS.
- [ ] **DIBL effect:** Short-channel $V_{th}$ drops with $V_{DS}$ → leakage increases **exponentially** with $V_{DS}$.
- [ ] **Body bias:** Reverse bias ($V_{BS} < 0$ for NMOS) **raises** $V_{th}$ → suppresses $I_{sub}$ exponentially.
- [ ] **Temperature:** $I_{sub} \propto T^{2} \exp(-E_g/kT)$ — doubles approximately every 10–15 °C rise.
- [ ] **Static leakage power:** $P_{leak} = I_{sub} \cdot V_{DD}$ (per OFF transistor); total scales with transistor count.
- [ ] **Reduction techniques (4 main):** Reverse Body Bias, Stack Effect, Multi-$V_{th}$ Library, Power Gating.
- [ ] **Order of leakage contribution in nanometer CMOS:** Subthreshold > Gate-Oxide Tunneling > Junction Leakage.
- [ ] **Exam formula to memorize verbatim:** $I_{sub} = I_0 e^{(V_{GS}-V_{th})/nV_T}$ and $S = 2.3 n V_T$.
- [ ] **Key units to remember:** $V_T$ in mV (≈ 26 mV), $S$ in mV/decade, $I_{sub}$ in nA–μA range, $V_{th}$ in volts.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Emerging Technologies for Low Power: Hi-K Gate Dielectric
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT866 - LOW POWER VLSI
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Emerging Technologies for Low Power: Hi-K Gate Dielectric

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Hi-K (High-Dielectric-Constant) Gate Dielectric** is an advanced CMOS fabrication technology in which the conventional ultra-thin silicon dioxide (SiO₂) gate insulator of a MOSFET is replaced with a metal-oxide (or silicate) film whose relative permittivity (κ or K) is significantly higher than that of SiO₂ (κ ≈ 3.9). The most widely adopted industrial materials belong to the **hafnium-based family** (HfO₂, HfSiO, HfSiON), along with ZrO₂, Al₂O₃, La₂O₃, and TiO₂-based compounds.

The **principal engineering motive** behind this substitution is to keep the *gate capacitance* large (required for strong channel control and high drive current) **without** physically thinning the dielectric to dangerous sub-1.5 nm levels. A thicker high-κ layer can deliver the *same* capacitance as a much thinner SiO₂ layer, but with an exponentially reduced **gate tunneling (leakage) current** — which is the *single largest* contributor to **static (leakage) power dissipation** in nanoscale CMOS.

## 1.2 The Driving Need — Why Replace SiO₂ ?

For more than three decades, Moore's Law was sustained by aggressive **physical scaling** of the gate oxide thickness (`t_ox`). Every new technology node demanded a thinner insulator to boost:

- **Transconductance** (gₘ) — proportional to $C_{ox}$
- **Drive current** (I_on) — proportional to $(V_{gs}-V_{th}) \cdot C_{ox}$
- **Switching speed** — inversely related to $C_{ox} \cdot V_{dd}$

However, once `t_ox` dropped below ~1.5 nm, the **direct quantum-mechanical tunneling current** through the oxide grew *exponentially* and became intolerable — both for **standby leakage** (I_off) and **dynamic power loss** through the gate. By the **65 nm node**, pure SiO₂ had reached its physical and electrical limit. Hi-K dielectrics became the *only* viable path to continue scaling.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Thick-Walled Water Pipe" 

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy:** Imagine you need to push a fixed amount of water (electric flux) through a pipe per second to maintain flow. With a thin-walled steel pipe (SiO₂), water leaks badly because the wall is too thin. If you instead use a **thicker, but electrically more "permeable" ceramic pipe** (High-κ material), the same water flow is achieved, but leakage is dramatically reduced. The *equivalent water-flow capacity* is preserved, but the *physical wall* is thicker — exactly what the high-κ trick does for gate capacitance versus leakage.

## 1.4 Quantitative Definition of "High-κ"

A material is classified as **high-κ** if:

$$\kappa_{high} \gg \kappa_{SiO_2} = 3.9$$

For comparison:

| Material | Relative Permittivity (κ) | Band-gap Eₘ (eV) |
|----------|---------------------------|------------------|
| SiO₂ (reference) | **3.9** | 8.9 |
| Si₃N₄ | 7.0 | 5.1 |
| Al₂O₃ | 9.0 | 8.7 |
| Y₂O₃ | 15.0 | 6.0 |
| ZrO₂ | 22–25 | 5.8 |
| HfO₂ | 22–25 | 5.7 |
| La₂O₃ | 27 | 4.3 |
| TiO₂ | 80–110 | 3.5 |

## 1.5 Key Performance Metrics

- **Equivalent Oxide Thickness (EOT):** the SiO₂ thickness that would give the *same* gate capacitance as the high-κ stack. Definition (in nm):

$$EOT = \frac{\kappa_{SiO_2}}{\kappa_{high}} \cdot t_{high} = \frac{3.9}{\kappa_{high}} \cdot t_{high}$$

- **Physical thickness (t_high):** the actual thickness of the high-κ layer.
- **Capacitance Equivalence:**

$$C_{ox,eq} = \frac{\kappa_{SiO_2} \cdot \varepsilon_0}{EOT} = \frac{\kappa_{high} \cdot \varepsilon_0}{t_{high}}$$

This equivalence is what makes the substitution *invisible* to transistor-level circuit designers — the device still behaves as if it has a thin SiO₂, but the actual physical layer is thicker.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Gate Leakage Mechanisms — The Physics of Tunneling

When a voltage V is applied across a thin dielectric separating two conductors (the gate and the channel), electrons can traverse the barrier via two quantum mechanisms:

### 2.1.1 Fowler-Nordheim (FN) Tunneling
Occurs for oxide thickness > ~3 nm and/or high field across a triangular barrier. Dominant for thicker oxides at high V.

$$J_{FN} = A \cdot E_{ox}^2 \cdot \exp\!\left(\frac{-B}{E_{ox}}\right)$$

where:
- $J_{FN}$ = FN tunneling current density (A/cm²)
- $E_{ox} = V_{ox}/t_{ox}$ = electric field in oxide
- $A$ and $B$ are material-dependent constants

### 2.1.2 Direct Tunneling
Dominates for **t_ox < 3 nm**. Electrons tunnel through a *trapezoidal* (rather than triangular) potential barrier. The current density:

$$J_{DT} = \frac{q^2}{2\pi h} \cdot E_{ox} \cdot \exp\!\left[\frac{-4\pi \sqrt{2m_{ox}^*}}{h} \cdot t_{ox} \cdot \sqrt{q(\Phi_B - V_{ox})}\right]$$

where:
- $m_{ox}^*$ = effective electron mass in the oxide
- $\Phi_B$ = barrier height (Si–SiO₂: ~3.1 eV for electrons)
- $t_{ox}$ = physical oxide thickness
- $h$ = Planck's constant
- $q$ = electronic charge

> [!NOTE]
> The **exponential dependence on `t_ox`** is the heart of the problem. Every 0.2 nm reduction in SiO₂ thickness increases direct tunneling by roughly an **order of magnitude** — this is why scaling SiO₂ alone became unsustainable.

## 2.2 The High-κ Solution — Mathematical Insight

The **gate capacitance per unit area** for any planar insulator is:

$$C_{ox} = \frac{\kappa \cdot \varepsilon_0}{t_{phys}}$$

For two dielectrics in series (a *bilayer stack* — used in practice, e.g., a thin SiO₂ interfacial layer plus a thicker high-κ layer):

$$\frac{1}{C_{stack}} = \frac{1}{C_{SiO_2}} + \frac{1}{C_{high\kappa}} = \frac{t_{SiO_2}}{\kappa_{SiO_2}\,\varepsilon_0} + \frac{t_{high}}{\kappa_{high}\,\varepsilon_0}$$

The **Equivalent Oxide Thickness (EOT)** of the stack is therefore:

$$EOT = t_{SiO_2} + \frac{\kappa_{SiO_2}}{\kappa_{high}} \cdot t_{high} = t_{SiO_2} + \frac{3.9}{\kappa_{high}} \cdot t_{high}$$

This equation is the **cornerstone** of every high-κ numerical problem in KTU exams.

## 2.3 Engineering Criteria for Selecting a High-κ Material

A suitable high-κ gate dielectric must satisfy **all** of the following simultaneously:

1. **High permittivity** (κ large) — to keep EOT low.
2. **Large band gap (E_g)** — to maintain a high conduction-band offset (ΔE_C > 1 eV) with Si, suppressing electron injection.
3. **Thermodynamic stability on Si** — must not react with Si to form silicides or silicates.
4. **High breakdown field** — must withstand ~1 V/nm of operating field.
5. **Low interface-state density (D_it)** with Si — to preserve carrier mobility.
6. **Low leakage current** — tunneling current exponentially lower than SiO₂ at the same EOT.
7. **CMOS-process compatibility** — must withstand 1000 °C anneals.
8. **Low bulk traps and fixed charge** — to limit V_th shift.

The material that best balances ALL these constraints and entered mass production at the **45 nm node (Intel, 2007)** is **HfO₂** (κ ≈ 22, E_g ≈ 5.7 eV, ΔE_C ≈ 1.5 eV). Modern nodes use **Hf-based** compounds (HfSiON, HfLaON, HfZrO₄).

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Form | Physical Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|-------------------|---------------|------------------|---------------|
| 1 | Oxide capacitance per unit area | $C_{ox} = \dfrac{\kappa \varepsilon_0}{t_{phys}}$ | Gate's ability to control channel | F/cm² |
| 2 | Equivalent Oxide Thickness | $EOT = \dfrac{3.9}{\kappa_{high}} \cdot t_{high}$ | Effective SiO₂ thickness | nm |
| 3 | Bilayer EOT (SiO₂ + High-κ) | $EOT = t_{SiO_2} + \dfrac{3.9}{\kappa_{high}} \cdot t_{high}$ | For stacks with interfacial layer | nm |
| 4 | Direct Tunneling Current Density | $J_{DT} \propto E_{ox} \cdot \exp(-K \cdot t_{ox} \sqrt{\Phi_B - V_{ox}})$ | Gate leakage through thin oxide | A/cm² |
| 5 | Fowler-Nordheim Current | $J_{FN} = A E_{ox}^2 \exp(-B/E_{ox})$ | Field-emission leakage at high V | A/cm² |
| 6 | Tunneling Reduction Ratio (Hi-K vs SiO₂) | $\dfrac{J_{high}}{J_{SiO_2}} \approx 10^{-(t_{high}-EOT)/0.2}$ | Decades of leakage reduction | dimensionless |
| 7 | Drive current scaling | $I_{on} \propto C_{ox} (V_{gs}-V_{th})$ | Why EOT matters for performance | A |
| 8 | Subthreshold leakage floor | $I_{off} \propto 10^{-V_{th}/S}$ | Independent of EOT but linked to V_th shift | A |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In every KTU numerical, **always convert physical thickness → EOT first**, then solve. Marks are typically awarded for *this conversion step* before the actual answer.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Mobile/IoT SoCs:** Reduced static leakage extends battery life by 20–40 %.
- **High-performance CPUs:** Enables sub-1 nm EOT (e.g., Intel's 10 nm uses ~0.9 nm EOT with HfO₂).
- **Non-volatile memory (Flash, FeFET, RRAM):** High-κ charge-trap layers replace SiO₂–Si₃N₄–SiO₂ (ONO) stacks.
- **3D FinFET and GAA:** High-κ is mandatory — bare SiO₂ cannot deliver the required EOT at the 3 nm/2 nm nodes.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation #1 — Equivalent Oxide Thickness from Capacitance Equivalence

**Starting point:** For any parallel-plate capacitor (the MOS gate), the capacitance per unit area is

$$C = \frac{\kappa \varepsilon_0}{t}$$

**Step 1 — Set the SiO₂ reference capacitance.** A MOSFET with pure SiO₂ of thickness $t_{ref}$ has:

$$C_{ref} = \frac{\kappa_{SiO_2} \cdot \varepsilon_0}{t_{ref}} = \frac{3.9 \cdot \varepsilon_0}{t_{ref}}$$

**Step 2 — Define the "equivalent" SiO₂ thickness (EOT) for the same capacitance.** We seek a *hypothetical* SiO₂ thickness $EOT$ such that:

$$C_{ref} = \frac{3.9 \cdot \varepsilon_0}{EOT}$$

**Step 3 — Capacitance of the high-κ layer.** The high-κ layer of physical thickness $t_{high}$ and permittivity $\kappa_{high}$ gives:

$$C_{high} = \frac{\kappa_{high} \cdot \varepsilon_0}{t_{high}}$$

**Step 4 — Equate (capacitance preservation rule).** For the *same* gate capacitance, set $C_{ref} = C_{high}$:

$$\frac{3.9 \cdot \varepsilon_0}{EOT} = \frac{\kappa_{high} \cdot \varepsilon_0}{t_{high}}$$

**Step 5 — Cancel $\varepsilon_0$ and solve for EOT.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{3.9}{EOT} &= \frac{\kappa_{high}}{t_{high}} \\
EOT \cdot \kappa_{high} &= 3.9 \cdot t_{high} \\
\boxed{EOT &= \frac{3.9}{\kappa_{high}} \cdot t_{high}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Interpretation:** If $\kappa_{high} = 22$ (HfO₂) and $t_{high} = 5$ nm, then $EOT = (3.9/22) \times 5 \approx 0.886$ nm — equivalent to a sub-nm SiO₂ that is impossible to fabricate without catastrophic leakage.

## 3.2 Derivation #2 — Bilayer Stack (SiO₂ Interfacial Layer + High-κ)

**Realistic stack structure:** In practice, a thin (~0.5–1.0 nm) **interfacial SiO₂ layer (IL)** always forms between the high-κ and the Si channel (it is required to maintain low D_it and high mobility). The full stack is two capacitors in series.

**Step 1 — Two capacitors in series formula:**

$$C_{stack} = \left(\frac{1}{C_{IL}} + \frac{1}{C_{high}}\right)^{-1}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the parallel-plate expressions:**

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{IL} &= \frac{\kappa_{SiO_2} \varepsilon_0}{t_{IL}} = \frac{3.9 \, \varepsilon_0}{t_{IL}} \\
C_{high} &= \frac{\kappa_{high} \varepsilon_0}{t_{high}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Insert and simplify:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{C_{stack}} &= \frac{t_{IL}}{3.9 \, \varepsilon_0} + \frac{t_{high}}{\kappa_{high} \, \varepsilon_0} \\
\frac{1}{C_{stack}} &= \frac{1}{\varepsilon_0}\left(\frac{t_{IL}}{3.9} + \frac{t_{high}}{\kappa_{high}}\right)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Compare with the EOT capacitance $C = 3.9 \, \varepsilon_0 / EOT$:**

$$\frac{EOT}{3.9 \, \varepsilon_0} = \frac{1}{\varepsilon_0}\left(\frac{t_{IL}}{3.9} + \frac{t_{high}}{\kappa_{high}}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Final result:**

$$\boxed{EOT_{stack} = t_{IL} + \frac{3.9}{\kappa_{high}} \cdot t_{high}}$$

**Numerical sanity check:** $t_{IL} = 0.7$ nm SiO₂ + 3 nm HfO₂ ($\kappa = 22$):

$$EOT = 0.7 + \frac{3.9}{22}(3) = 0.7 + 0.532 = 1.232 \text{ nm}$$

This is the regime of the **Intel 32 nm node**.

## 3.3 Derivation #3 — Tunneling Leakage Reduction from High-κ

**Step 1 — Direct tunneling expression (simplified to capture essential scaling):**

$$J \approx K \cdot E_{ox} \cdot \exp\!\left(-\alpha \cdot t_{phys} \cdot \sqrt{\Phi_B - V_{ox}}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Note the exponential.** The exponent is $\alpha \cdot t_{phys} \cdot \sqrt{\Phi_B - V_{ox}}$. For *fixed* EOT (and thus fixed $C_{ox}$ and fixed $E_{ox}$), reducing EOT *decreases* exponent, *increases* leakage. So the only way to lower leakage while keeping EOT constant is to *increase* $t_{phys}$.

**Step 3 — Express leakage in decades per 0.2 nm of extra physical thickness.** Empirically, in SiO₂ the leakage rises by ~1 decade for every 0.2 nm reduction in $t_{ox}$. Therefore, if high-κ adds a physical thickness margin of $\Delta t = t_{high} - EOT$, the leakage reduction in *orders of magnitude* is:

$$\text{Decades reduced} \approx \frac{\Delta t}{0.2 \, nm}$$

**Step 4 — Worked calculation (HfO₂ 3 nm, EOT 0.53 nm):**

$$\Delta t = 3 - 0.53 = 2.47 \text{ nm}$$

$$\text{Decades} = \frac{2.47}{0.2} \approx 12.4 \text{ decades}$$

**Step 5 — Leakage ratio:**

$$\frac{J_{high}}{J_{SiO_2}} \approx 10^{-12.4} \approx 4 \times 10^{-13}$$

This is the reason high-κ reduces static power by **~12 orders of magnitude** at the same EOT — the single most important practical benefit.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Common Numerical Patterns in KTU

| Pattern | Given | Required | Key Formula |
|---------|-------|----------|-------------|
| Compute EOT from physical thickness | $t_{high}$, $\kappa_{high}$ | EOT | $EOT = (3.9/\kappa) \cdot t_{high}$ |
| Compute physical thickness from EOT | EOT, $\kappa_{high}$ | $t_{high}$ | $t_{high} = (\kappa/3.9) \cdot EOT$ |
| Bilayer EOT | $t_{IL}$, $t_{high}$, $\kappa_{high}$ | Total EOT | $EOT = t_{IL} + (3.9/\kappa)\cdot t_{high}$ |
| Leakage ratio | $\Delta t = t_{high} - EOT$ | Decades | $N = \Delta t/0.2$ |
| Capacitance density | EOT | $C_{ox}$ | $C_{ox} = 3.9\varepsilon_0/EOT$ |
| Threshold voltage shift | Fixed oxide charge $Q_{ox}$ | $\Delta V_{th}$ | $\Delta V_{th} = -Q_{ox}/C_{ox}$ |

## 3.5 Worked Example — Typical KTU-Style Numerical

**Problem:** A 32 nm CMOS process uses a gate stack consisting of 0.7 nm SiO₂ interfacial layer + 3.0 nm HfO₂ (κ = 22). Compute:
- (i) The total EOT
- (ii) The gate capacitance per unit area
- (iii) The leakage reduction in decades (assume 1 decade per 0.2 nm of physical thickness advantage)

**Solution:**

**Part (i):** Using the bilayer formula:

$$EOT = 0.7 + \frac{3.9}{22}(3.0) = 0.7 + 0.532 = 1.232 \text{ nm}$$

**Part (ii):** Gate capacitance per unit area:

$$C_{ox} = \frac{3.9 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \, \text{F/m}}{1.232 \times 10^{-9} \, \text{m}} = 2.80 \times 10^{-2} \, \text{F/m}^2 = 28 \, \text{fF/}\mu\text{m}^2$$

**Part (iii):** Physical thickness advantage:

$$\Delta t = (0.7 + 3.0) - 1.232 = 3.7 - 1.232 = 2.468 \text{ nm}$$

$$\text{Decades} = 2.468 / 0.2 \approx 12.3 \text{ decades}$$

**Answer summary:** EOT ≈ **1.23 nm**, C_ox ≈ **28 fF/µm²**, leakage reduction ≈ **12 decades**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

This diagram maps the *architectural* difference between the conventional and emerging gate stacks, including the dominant current path under bias.
