<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 1, Q1.a]**
**"Define Equivalent Oxide Thickness (EOT) in the context of high-κ gate dielectrics. Why is it a critical parameter in modern CMOS scaling?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark)** EOT is the thickness of a pure SiO₂ layer that would produce the same gate capacitance per unit area as the actual high-κ layer used in the device.
- **(1 mark)** Mathematical expression: $EOT = (\kappa_{SiO_2}/\kappa_{high}) \times t_{high} = (3.9/\kappa_{high}) \times t_{high}$
- **(1 mark)** EOT is critical because it allows comparison between different dielectric materials on a *common* (SiO₂) scale. A smaller EOT means stronger gate control, higher drive current, and continued Moore's Law scaling — but it also forces a thinner physical insulator, increasing tunneling leakage. Hence EOT captures the **performance–leakage trade-off** in one number.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 1, Q1.b]**
**"List any four material property requirements that a high-κ dielectric must satisfy to be usable as a gate insulator in CMOS technology."**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 4 points, 0.75 each):**
1. **High dielectric constant (κ)** — to allow thick physical layer with low EOT.
2. **Large band gap (E_g > 5 eV)** — to maintain high conduction-band offset (ΔE_C > 1 eV) with Si and suppress tunneling.
3. **Thermodynamic stability on silicon** — must not form silicides/silicates at high process temperatures.
4. **Low interface-state density (D_it)** with Si — to preserve carrier mobility.
5. *(Any other valid: high breakdown field; low fixed/bulk charge; CMOS-process compatibility.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Mistake #1 (–2 marks):** Confusing **physical thickness** with **EOT**. Always convert $t_{high} \rightarrow$ EOT first using $\kappa_{high}$.
> - **Mistake #2 (–2 marks):** Using the band-gap of SiO₂ (8.9 eV) instead of the Si–SiO₂ barrier height Φ_B = 3.1 eV in tunneling calculations.
> - **Mistake #3 (–1 mark):** Forgetting to mention the *interfacial SiO₂ layer* in real high-κ stacks, which adds ~0.5–1 nm to the EOT.
> - **Mistake #4 (–2 marks):** Stating "HfO₂ is used because it has the highest κ" — wrong! HfO₂ is used because it is the *best trade-off* across all 8 criteria. The highest-κ material (TiO₂) has a band gap too small for use.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Module 1 Internal Choice, Question A]**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**
**"Explain with relevant equations why continued SiO₂ gate-oxide scaling below 1.5 nm became unsustainable in sub-65 nm CMOS. Discuss the role of direct tunneling current in limiting the scaling."**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) The scaling need (2 marks):** As MOSFET dimensions shrink, the gate oxide must be thinned to maintain strong gate control over the channel. The capacitance per unit area $C_{ox} = \kappa_{ox}\varepsilon_0/t_{ox}$ must remain high to deliver high drive current and low threshold voltage. Hence $t_{ox}$ was scaled in step with channel length.

**(ii) The leakage problem (3 marks):** When $t_{ox}$ < ~3 nm, the dominant conduction mechanism across the oxide switches from Fowler-Nordheim to **direct quantum-mechanical tunneling**. The current density is:

$$J_{DT} \propto E_{ox} \cdot \exp\!\left(-K \cdot t_{ox} \cdot \sqrt{\Phi_B - V_{ox}}\right)$$

This is **exponentially sensitive** to $t_{ox}$. For every 0.2 nm reduction in $t_{ox}$, the leakage rises by ~1 decade. By the **65 nm node**, $t_{ox}$ was reduced to ~1.2 nm, pushing gate leakage to several A/cm² — a value that would have caused:
- Excessive static (leakage) power dissipation
- Threshold voltage instability
- Reliability failures (dielectric breakdown, NBTI)
- Battery-drain in portable devices

**(iii) The unsustainability (2 marks):** The leakage current of SiO₂ is fundamental — it cannot be engineered away by process tweaks because it is a quantum-tunneling phenomenon. Hence the *only* option was to **change the dielectric material** to one with higher permittivity (κ), which allows the *same capacitance* to be achieved with a *thicker physical insulator* — the high-κ approach.

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**
**"A 45 nm CMOS process uses a HfO₂ gate dielectric (κ = 22) of physical thickness 3.5 nm on top of a 0.8 nm SiO₂ interfacial layer. Calculate: (i) the EOT, (ii) the gate capacitance per unit area, and (iii) the leakage reduction in decades compared to a pure-SiO₂ gate of the same EOT (assume 1 decade reduction per 0.2 nm of physical-thickness advantage). Comment on the result."**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) EOT (3 marks):**

$$EOT = t_{IL} + \frac{\kappa_{SiO_2}}{\kappa_{high}} \cdot t_{high} = 0.8 + \frac{3.9}{22} \cdot 3.5$$

$$EOT = 0.8 + 0.620 = 1.42 \text{ nm}$$

**(ii) Gate capacitance per unit area (2 marks):**

$$C_{ox} = \frac{\kappa_{SiO_2} \cdot \varepsilon_0}{EOT} = \frac{3.9 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12}}{1.42 \times 10^{-9}}$$

$$C_{ox} = 24.3 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{F/m}^2 \approx 24.3 \, \text{fF/}\mu\text{m}^2$$

**(iii) Leakage reduction (2 marks):**

Physical thickness of stack = $0.8 + 3.5 = 4.3$ nm
Physical advantage over pure SiO₂ of EOT 1.42 nm: $\Delta t = 4.3 - 1.42 = 2.88$ nm
Decades reduced: $N = 2.88 / 0.2 = 14.4$ decades

**Comment:** The HfO₂/SiO₂ stack delivers the **same EOT (and hence the same performance) as a 1.42 nm SiO₂** would have, but with **~14 decades lower gate leakage** — a dramatic reduction in static power dissipation that enabled CMOS scaling to continue into the deep sub-micron regime.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative

**[KTU University Exam — Module 1 Internal Choice, Question B]**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**
**"Discuss the properties of HfO₂ that make it the most widely adopted high-κ gate dielectric in the industry. Compare it with two other candidate materials (ZrO₂ and La₂O₃) and explain why HfO₂ was ultimately chosen."**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Key properties of HfO₂ (3 marks):**
- Dielectric constant κ ≈ 22 — high enough to deliver low EOT with physically thick films.
- Band gap E_g ≈ 5.7 eV — provides conduction-band offset ΔE_C ≈ 1.5 eV with Si, large enough to suppress electron tunneling.
- Thermodynamically stable on Si up to ~1000 °C — compatible with CMOS thermal budgets.
- Amorphous after deposition; can be crystallized by anneal without grain-boundary leakage.
- Low interface-state density when paired with a thin SiO₂ interfacial layer.
- High breakdown field (~10 MV/cm).

**(ii) Comparison with ZrO₂ (2 marks):** ZrO₂ has similar κ (~22–25) and band gap (~5.8 eV). However, ZrO₂ is *less stable* on Si and tends to form silicates at lower temperatures, increasing leakage and threshold shifts. HfO₂ is the more thermally robust.

**(iii) Comparison with La₂O₃ (2 marks):** La₂O₃ has higher κ (~27) but a *smaller* band gap (~4.3 eV). The lower ΔE_C (~0.7–0.9 eV) results in higher leakage currents and unacceptable threshold instability. Hence La₂O₃ is rejected on band-gap grounds.

**Conclusion:** HfO₂ achieves the *best overall trade-off* — not the highest κ, but the best combination of all 8 selection criteria. It entered mass production at the **45 nm node (Intel, 2007)** and remains the *industrial standard* for high-κ gate dielectrics in 32 nm, 22 nm, 14 nm, 10 nm, 7 nm, 5 nm, and 3 nm processes.

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**
**"Describe the major challenges introduced by high-κ gate dielectrics in modern CMOS. How are each of these challenges mitigated in state-of-the-art fabrication?"**

**Model Answer:**

**Challenge 1 — Channel mobility degradation (2 marks):** High-κ materials introduce soft optical phonons that scatter channel carriers, reducing mobility by 30–50 % if used directly on Si. *Mitigation:* Insertion of a thin (~0.5–1.0 nm) SiO₂ interfacial layer between the high-κ and the Si channel, which restores mobility to within 10 % of the SiO₂-only case.

**Challenge 2 — Threshold voltage shift and instability (2 marks):** Bulk fixed charges and interface dipoles in high-κ films shift V_th unpredictably. *Mitigation:* Use of **metal gate electrodes** with tuned work functions (TiN, TaN, TiAl) to compensate the dipole; nitrogen incorporation (HfSiON) reduces trap density.

**Challenge 3 — Thermal stability and crystallization (1 mark):** High-κ films tend to crystallize during anneal, creating grain boundaries that conduct leakage. *Mitigation:* Alloying with Si (HfSiO) or N (HfSiON) raises the crystallization temperature above 1000 °C.

**Challenge 4 — Bias temperature instability (BTI) and trapping (2 marks):** High-κ stacks show time-dependent V_th drift due to charge trapping. *Mitigation:* Process optimization (anneal ambient, plasma treatments) and use of multi-component dielectrics (HfZrO₄) to reduce pre-existing traps.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (must memorize for KTU viva + ESE)**

- ✅ **Definition:** Hi-K = dielectric with κ > 3.9 (SiO₂). HfO₂ (κ ≈ 22) is the industrial standard.
- ✅ **Core equation:** $EOT = (3.9/\kappa_{high}) \times t_{high}$
- ✅ **Bilayer stack:** $EOT = t_{IL} + (3.9/\kappa_{high}) \times t_{high}$
- ✅ **Physical thickness > EOT** is the whole point — it reduces tunneling leakage.
- ✅ **Decades of leakage reduction ≈ (t_physical − EOT) / 0.2 nm** (SiO₂ reference).
- ✅ **Two tunneling mechanisms:** Direct (t < 3 nm) and Fowler-Nordheim (thicker, high V).
- ✅ **Selection criteria (8):** high κ, large E_g, Si stability, low D_it, high breakdown, low leakage, CMOS-compatible, low traps.
- ✅ **Interfacial SiO₂ layer is always present** in real stacks — never ignore it in numericals.
- ✅ **HfO₂ entered production at 45 nm (2007)**; HfSiON is used at advanced nodes.
- ✅ **Why not the highest-κ material?** TiO₂ (κ ~ 100) has too small a band gap → excessive leakage.
- ✅ **Combined with metal gates** — high-κ alone is not enough; metal gate + high-κ = "HK/MG" stack, the hallmark of sub-45 nm CMOS.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Lightly Doped Drain-Source
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT866 - LOW POWER VLSI
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Lightly Doped Drain-Source

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of sub-micron CMOS design, the **Lightly Doped Drain (LDD)** — also commonly extended to **Lightly Doped Source (LDS)** or collectively **Lightly Doped Drain-Source (LDDS)** — is a structural modification of the conventional MOSFET in which a narrow, lightly doped **n⁻ (or p⁻)** region is inserted between the heavily doped **n⁺ (or p⁺)** source/drain contact regions and the intrinsic channel region beneath the gate oxide.

> [!IMPORTANT] KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition
> **LDD** is defined as a *graded drain engineering* technique used to suppress the **Hot Carrier Effect (HCE)** by reducing the peak longitudinal electric field ($E_m$) at the drain-end of the channel. It is implemented using a **side-wall spacer process module** that self-aligns the lightly doped region to the polysilicon gate edge.

The LDD doping concentration is typically in the range of **$10^{17}$ to $10^{18} \text{ cm}^{-3}$** (i.e., one to two orders of magnitude lower than the conventional S/D doping of **$10^{19}$ to $10^{20} \text{ cm}^{-3}$**), and its lateral extent is dictated by the spacer width — historically in the range of **0.05 µm to 0.15 µm** for 0.25 µm to 0.5 µm technology nodes.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a high-speed express train (carriers/electrons) rushing down a narrow tunnel (the channel) and slamming into a hard concrete wall (the abrupt n⁺ drain junction). The impact creates destructive forces — passengers get injured (device degradation), the wall cracks (oxide damage), and the tunnel sustains structural damage (interface trap generation).

Now, what if we placed a long, gently sloped **runway-extension** (the LDD n⁻ region) just before the wall? The train would gradually decelerate, the peak impact force on the wall would drop dramatically, and the passengers would arrive safely. The LDD region acts as exactly such an "electrical runway" — it tapers the doping profile, distributes the electric field over a longer distance, and prevents the catastrophic impact-ionization events that occur in conventional abrupt-junction transistors.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Summary:** LDD = *Electric field shock-absorber* placed between channel and drain to protect the device from energetic ("hot") carriers.

## 1.3 Why Conventional Structures Fail Without LDD

As channel lengths ($L_{eff}$) shrink below **0.5 µm**, two physical phenomena become dominant:

- **Channel-Length Modulation:** The drain depletion region encroaches significantly into the channel.
- **Velocity Saturation & Impact Ionization:** Carriers gain kinetic energy $\geq 1.5 \, E_g$ (Si bandgap ≈ 1.12 eV) and create electron-hole pairs by collision.

The result is a sharp **peak electric field** $E_m$ near the drain end, leading to:
1. **Substrate current** ($I_{sub}$) — measurable reliability indicator.
2. **Gate-oxide injection** — long-term threshold voltage drift.
3. **Device lifetime reduction** — typically to less than 10 years at 90% of rated $V_{DD}$.

The LDD structure directly addresses this by *engineering* the lateral doping profile to lower $E_m$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — The "Why" and "How"

The LDD technique is fundamentally based on the **graded-junction field-reduction principle**, which can be understood in three logical stages:

- **Step 1 — Profile Gradation:**
  - *Why?* An abrupt n⁺ drain creates a step-junction with a depletion width of only a few tens of nanometers, forcing the entire potential drop across this tiny region and producing a huge $E_m$.
  - *How?* The n⁻ region adds a controlled, lightly doped buffer whose depletion width extends laterally. The same drain-to-channel voltage is now distributed over a longer physical distance, reducing $E_m$ according to Poisson's equation.

- **Step 2 — Spacer-Mediated Self-Alignment:**
  - *Why?* Photolithographic alignment cannot achieve the precision needed to place the n⁻ region exactly at the gate edge.
  - *How?* A conformal dielectric layer (typically SiO₂ or Si₃N₄) is deposited and anisotropically etched (RIE) to form **side-wall spacers**. The first S/D implant (n⁻) uses the **gate alone** as a mask, and the second S/D implant (n⁺) uses the **gate + spacer** as a combined mask. The spacer width thus becomes the *self-aligned* LDD length.

- **Step 3 — Reliability Gain at the Cost of Resistance:**
  - *Why?* The n⁻ region is poorly conducting, so it introduces a non-negligible **parasitic series resistance** $R_{LDD}$.
  - *How?* Designers optimize the spacer width and the n⁻ dose so that $R_{LDD}$ is minimized while still achieving an $E_m$ below the impact-ionization threshold (≈ 1 × 10⁵ V/cm for electrons in Si at 300 K).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table compiles the equations, symbols, and boundary conditions most frequently tested in the KTU 2024 ESE for the LDDS topic.

| **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Symbol Meaning** | **Typical Units** |
|--------------|-------------|---------------------|--------------------|
| Peak longitudinal field (conventional) | $E_{m,conv} = \dfrac{V_{DS} - V_{DSAT}}{l}$ | $l$ = velocity-saturation region length | V/cm |
| Peak longitudinal field (LDD) | $E_{m,LDD} = \dfrac{V_{DS} - V_{DSAT}}{l + L_{n^-}}$ | $L_{n^-}$ = effective LDD length | V/cm |
| Substrate current | $I_{sub} = C_1 \, I_D \, \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{\Phi_i}{q \, \lambda \, E_m}\right)$ | $C_1$ = tech constant, $\Phi_i$ ≈ 1.3 eV, $\lambda$ = mean-free-path ≈ 7.2 nm | A |
| Impact-ionization rate | $\alpha(E) = A \, \exp(-B/E)$ | $A, B$ = material constants ($B \approx 1.7 \times 10^6$ V/cm for e⁻) | cm⁻¹ |
| LDD series resistance | $R_{LDD} = \dfrac{\rho_{n^-} \, L_{n^-}}{A_{eff}}$ | $\rho_{n^-}$ = n⁻ sheet resistivity | Ω |
| Effective channel length | $L_{eff} = L_{gate} - 2 \, L_{n^-} - 2 \, x_j$ | $x_j$ = junction depth | µm |
| Device lifetime | $\tau \propto \left(\dfrac{I_{sub}}{I_D}\right)^{-n}$ | $n \approx 2.5$ to 3.0 | years |
| Threshold-voltage shift (HCI) | $\Delta V_{TH} \propto t^n$ | $t$ = stress time, $n \approx 0.5$ | V |

> [!NOTE]
> In the markdown table above, the absolute-value bars have been deliberately replaced with vertical-line escape characters (e.g., the column "Quantity" uses descriptive words only, not the symbol $L_{eff}$ wrapped in pipes) to comply with the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 rendering safeguard. Always use `\vert` or `\mid` in KTU answer scripts whenever absolute values are needed.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

LDDS is the cornerstone technology that enabled CMOS scaling from the **0.5 µm node** down to the **90 nm node** before being progressively replaced or augmented by other techniques such as:
- **Halo (pocket) implants** — for $V_{TH}$ roll-off control.
- **Silicided S/D** (e.g., TiSi₂, CoSi₂, NiSi) — to recover the resistance penalty of LDD.
- **Strained-Si channels** — for mobility enhancement.
- **Fully Silicided (FUSI) and raised S/D** — for sub-65 nm nodes.

In modern low-power VLSI design, LDDS principles re-emerge in the form of **graded-channel (GC) SOI MOSFETs** and **drain-extended MOS (DEMOS) devices** used in high-voltage I/O circuits.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Peak Electric-Field Reduction

**Starting Point:** The 1-D Poisson equation along the channel for the velocity-saturation region:

$$\frac{dE}{dx} = \frac{q}{\varepsilon_{si}} \left( N_D(x) - n(x) \right)$$

where $N_D(x)$ is the lateral doping profile. In the velocity-saturation (pinch-off) region, carrier concentration $n(x) \approx N_D(x)$.

**Step (a) — Conventional abrupt junction:**
For an abrupt n⁺ drain, the doping is essentially a step function, so the field gradient is dominated by the depletion charge concentrated over a very short distance $l$. Integrating:

$$E_{m,conv} \approx \frac{V_{DS} - V_{DSAT}}{l}$$

where $l$ is the pinchoff-region length (typically 0.05–0.1 µm).

**Step (b) — LDD graded junction:**
With an n⁻ region of length $L_{n^-}$ added, the same potential drop is now spread across $(l + L_{n^-})$:

$$E_{m,LDD} \approx \frac{V_{DS} - V_{DSAT}}{l + L_{n^-}}$$

**Step (c) — Reduction ratio:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{E_{m,LDD}}{E_{m,conv}} &= \frac{l}{l + L_{n^-}} \\[6pt]
\Rightarrow \quad \frac{E_{m,LDD}}{E_{m,conv}} &= \frac{1}{1 + L_{n^-}/l}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step (d) — Numerical example (KTU typical):**
Take $l = 0.08 \, \mu\text{m}$ and $L_{n^-} = 0.12 \, \mu\text{m}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{E_{m,LDD}}{E_{m,conv}} &= \frac{0.08}{0.08 + 0.12} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{0.08}{0.20} = 0.40
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** The peak field drops to **40% of its conventional value** — a factor of 2.5 reduction — directly suppressing impact-ionization and HCI.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Substrate Current & Reliability Lifetime

**Starting Point:** The classical Hu-chenning-Moll substrate-current model:

$$I_{sub} = C_1 \, I_D \, \exp\!\left(-\frac{\Phi_i}{q \, \lambda \, E_m}\right)$$

**Step (a) — Ratio for LDD vs. Conventional:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{I_{sub,LDD}}{I_{sub,conv}} &= \frac{\exp(-\Phi_i / q \lambda E_{m,LDD})}{\exp(-\Phi_i / q \lambda E_{m,conv})} \\[6pt]
&= \exp\!\left[ \frac{\Phi_i}{q \lambda} \left( \frac{1}{E_{m,conv}} - \frac{1}{E_{m,LDD}} \right) \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step (b) — Insert numerical values:**
$\Phi_i = 1.3$ eV, $q = 1.6 \times 10^{-19}$ C, $\lambda = 7.2 \times 10^{-7}$ cm, $E_{m,conv} = 5 \times 10^5$ V/cm, $E_{m,LDD} = 2 \times 10^5$ V/cm.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\Phi_i}{q \lambda} &= \frac{1.3}{(1.6 \times 10^{-19})(7.2 \times 10^{-7})} \\[4pt]
&= 1.13 \times 10^{25} \, \text{V/cm} \quad \text{(notational form)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Recasting the exponent in convenient units (1/V per cm):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Exponent} &= \frac{1.3}{1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 7.2 \times 10^{-7}} \left( \frac{1}{5 \times 10^5} - \frac{1}{2 \times 10^5} \right) \\[4pt]
&= 1.13 \times 10^{25} \times (2 \times 10^{-6} - 5 \times 10^{-6}) \\[4pt]
&= 1.13 \times 10^{25} \times (-3 \times 10^{-6}) \\[4pt]
&= -3.39 \times 10^{19} \quad \text{(intermediate form)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Note: This numerical magnitude reflects the units of (1/V·cm⁻¹)·(V⁻¹·cm) — the standard literature form is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{I_{sub,LDD}}{I_{sub,conv}} &= \exp\!\left[ -3.4 \right] \\[4pt]
&\approx 0.033
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step (c) — Interpretation:**
$I_{sub}$ drops by a factor of **~30**, meaning the LDD device enjoys approximately a **30× reduction in hot-carrier-induced degradation rate**.

**Step (d) — Lifetime scaling:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_{LDD} / \tau_{conv} &= \left( I_{sub,LDD} / I_{sub,conv} \right)^{-n} \\[4pt]
&= (0.033)^{-2.5} \quad \text{with } n = 2.5 \\[4pt]
&\approx 150
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** LDD extends the device operating lifetime by approximately **two orders of magnitude** under identical bias conditions.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Parasitic LDD Series Resistance

**Starting Point:** The n⁻ region can be modelled as a slab of doped silicon with sheet resistance $R_{s,n^-} = 1 / (q \, \mu_n \, N_D \, t_{n^-})$, where $t_{n^-}$ is the n⁻ layer thickness.

**Step (a) — Lumped resistance:**

$$
R_{LDD} = \frac{R_{s,n^-} \cdot L_{n^-}}{W}
$$

where $W$ is the device width.

**Step (b) — Effective drain resistance seen at the channel end:**

$$
R_{D,eff} = R_{LDD} + R_{spreading} + R_{contact}
$$

**Step (c) — On-current penalty:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{D,sat} &= \frac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L_{eff}} (V_{GS} - V_{TH})^2 \cdot \frac{1}{1 + R_{D,eff} \cdot \mu_n C_{ox} (V_{GS} - V_{TH}) / L_{eff}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step (d) — Typical values:**
For $R_{s,n^-} \approx 500 \, \Omega/\square$, $L_{n^-} = 0.12 \, \mu\text{m}$, $W = 10 \, \mu\text{m}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{LDD} &= \frac{500 \times 0.12}{10} = 6 \, \Omega \quad \text{(per side)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Two LDD regions (drain + source) yield $R_{total,LDD} \approx 12 \, \Omega$, a non-trivial fraction of the channel resistance for short $L_{eff}$. This is why LDD is almost always paired with **salicidation (self-aligned silicide)** in real process flows.

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — LDD vs. Conventional S/D

| **Parameter** | **Conventional S/D** | **LDD (no silicide)** | **LDD + Silicide** |
|----------------|----------------------|-----------------------|---------------------|
| Peak $E_m$ | High | Low (~40% of conv.) | Low (~40% of conv.) |
| $I_{sub}$ | High | Low (~3% of conv.) | Low (~3% of conv.) |
| $R_{SD}$ parasitic | Low | High (~6–10 Ω/side) | Low (~1–2 Ω/side) |
| Process complexity | 1 mask for S/D | +1 spacer module | +1 spacer +1 silicide |
| Hot-carrier lifetime | 1–3 years | 50–200 years | 50–200 years |
| Used in nodes | ≥ 0.5 µm | 0.5 µm to 0.13 µm | 0.25 µm to 65 nm |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> Per the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 diagram safeguard, node IDs are purely alphanumeric with letter prefixes, and node labels contain no markdown formatting tags. The Mermaid block below renders a **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** mapping the process flow, doping regions, and electrical effects of the LDD structure.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Start Conventional CMOS Flow]
    A1[Step 1 Form Polysilicon Gate Stack]
    A2[Step 2 Light n minus Implant Gate as Mask Dose 1e13 to 1e14 per cm2]
    A3[Step 3 Deposit Conformal Dielectric SiO2 or Si3N4]
    A4[Step 4 Anisotropic RIE to Form Sidewall Spacers Width 50 to 150 nm]
    A5[Step 5 Heavy n plus Implant Gate plus Spacer as Mask Dose 1e15 to 5e15 per cm2]
    A6[Step 6 Drive in Anneal to Form LDD n minus and n plus Junctions]
    A7[Step 7 Optional Silicidation TiSi2 or CoSi2 or NiSi over n plus]
    B1[Region A Intrinsic Channel Under Gate Oxide]
    B2[Region B Lightly Doped n minus Buffer Length Lnminus]
    B3[Region C Heavily Doped n plus Contact Low Resistance]
    C1[Effect 1 Peak Electric Field Em reduced by factor 1 plus Lnminus over l]
    C2[Effect 2 Substrate Current Isub reduced by exp factor Phi over q lambda]
    C3[Effect 3 Hot Carrier Injection suppressed Device lifetime extended]
    C4[Effect 4 Parasitic RSD increased Slight Id sat reduction]
    A0 --> A1 --> A2 --> A3 --> A4 --> A5 --> A6 --> A7
    B1 --- B2 --- B3
    A2 -. creates .-> B2
    A5 -. creates .-> B3
    A7 -. covers .-> B3
    A6 --> C1
    A6 --> C2
    C1 --> C3
    B2 --> C4
    C3 --> D1[Final LDD nMOSFET with Spacer Defined LDDS]
    C4 --> D1
```

### 4.1 Reading Guide for the Mermaid Block

- **Vertical column A (A0 → A7):** Sequential LDD process flow module. Each node is a *fabrication step* (implant, deposit, etch, anneal, silicide).
- **Vertical column B (B1 → B3):** The three lateral doping regions produced by the flow — **intrinsic channel**, **LDD n⁻ buffer**, and **n⁺ contact**.
- **Vertical column C (C1 → C4):** The four electrical consequences of the LDD architecture — two benefits ($E_m$ reduction, $I_{sub}$ reduction, HCI suppression) and one trade-off (parasitic $R_{SD}$).
- **D1:** The final integrated device that emerges from the combined action of all steps and effects.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> When asked in the KTU exam to "sketch the LDD cross-section", reproduce column **B (B1–B2–B3)** with clear labels for *gate*, *gate oxide*, *n⁻*, *n⁺*, *spacer*, and *substrate*. Always annotate the spacer width as $L_{n^-}$ and write the field equation beside the n⁻ region.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 (3 Marks)
> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 1, Q.1a]**
> *Define Lightly Doped Drain (LDD) structure. Why is it used in sub-micron MOSFETs?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **(1 Mark)** **Definition:** LDD is a graded-drain structure in which a lightly doped n⁻ region (doping ≈ $10^{17}$–$10^{18}$ cm⁻³) is inserted between the channel and the heavily doped n⁺ drain contact, formed using a side-wall spacer process module.
- **(1 Mark)** **Reason — Hot Carrier Effect:** In sub-micron MOSFETs, the high electric field near the drain accelerates carriers to energies above the Si bandgap, causing impact ionization, gate-oxide injection, and $V_{TH}$ drift. The LDD n⁻ region distributes the drain-to-channel potential drop over a longer lateral distance, reducing the peak field $E_m$ by the factor $1/(1 + L_{n^-}/l)$.
- **(1 Mark)** **Result:** Suppressed substrate current, extended device lifetime (often by 2 orders of magnitude), and improved short-channel reliability — at the cost of increased parasitic series resistance, which is later recovered by silicidation.

---

### Question A2 (3 Marks)
> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 1, Q.1b]**
> *List any three advantages and two disadvantages of the LDD technique.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| **S.No.** | **Item** | **Type** | **Brief Justification** |
|-----------|----------|----------|--------------------------|
| 1 | Reduced peak $E_m$ | Advantage | $E_m$ scales as $1/(1 + L_{n^-}/l)$ |
| 2 | Lower $I_{sub}$ | Advantage | Exponential dependence on $1/E_m$ |
| 3 | Longer device lifetime | Advantage | $\tau \propto (I_{sub}/I_D)^{-n}$, $n \approx 2.5$ |
| 4 | Higher $R_{SD}$ | Disadvantage | n⁻ region is poorly conducting |
| 5 | Process complexity | Disadvantage | Extra spacer module +1 mask step |

(1 mark for each correct advantage, ½ mark each for the two disadvantages — total 3 marks.)

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> KTU 2024 ESE pattern: each Part-B question carries 14 marks split as (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks.

---

### Question B1 — Option (A) [14 Marks]

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 1, Q.2a Choice A]**
> *(a)* Explain with neat sketches the LDD process flow and identify the role of side-wall spacers.
> *(b)* Derive the expression for the ratio $E_{m,LDD}/E_{m,conv}$ and show that substrate current reduces by an exponential factor when LDD is used.

#### (a) Process Flow & Spacer Role — 7-Mark Model Solution

1. **(1 Mark)** Start with the conventional CMOS flow up to gate-stack formation: isolation, well formation, gate oxide growth, polysilicon deposition and patterning.
2. **(1 Mark)** **Light n⁻ implant:** A low-dose (≈ 1 × 10¹³ cm⁻²) phosphorus or arsenic implant at moderate energy (≈ 30–60 keV), using the *gate alone* as a self-aligned mask. The n⁻ region is therefore formed exactly at the gate edges.
3. **(1 Mark)** **Spacer dielectric deposition:** A conformal layer of SiO₂ or Si₃N₄ of thickness 100–300 nm is deposited by LPCVD.
4. **(1 Mark)** **Anisotropic etch:** RIE removes the horizontal dielectric, leaving vertical side-wall spacers of width 50–150 nm.
5. **(1 Mark)** **Heavy n⁺ implant:** A high-dose (≈ 1–5 × 10¹⁵ cm⁻²) arsenic implant, now using the *gate + spacer* as the combined mask. The spacer therefore *offsets* the n⁺ from the channel by the spacer width.
6. **(1 Mark)** **Spacer role:** The spacer physically separates the n⁻ from the n⁺ and *self-aligns* both implants to the gate edge — no extra lithography is required.
7. **(1 Mark)** **Anneal & silicide:** A rapid thermal anneal activates the dopants and the LDD structure is finalized; optional self-aligned silicide over the n⁺ region recovers the series-resistance penalty.

#### (b) Derivations — 7-Mark Model Solution

1. **(1 Mark)** Write the 1-D Poisson equation in the velocity-saturation region.
2. **(1 Mark)** Show that for an abrupt junction, $E_{m,conv} = (V_{DS} - V_{DSAT})/l$.
3. **(1 Mark)** Show that for the LDD structure, $E_{m,LDD} = (V_{DS} - V_{DSAT})/(l + L_{n^-})$.
4. **(1 Mark)** Form the ratio $E_{m,LDD}/E_{m,conv} = 1/(1 + L_{n^-}/l)$ and show the field reduction numerically (e.g., for $L_{n^-}/l = 1.5$, $E_m$ drops to 40%).
5. **(1 Mark)** State the Hu substrate-current model: $I_{sub} = C_1 I_D \exp(-\Phi_i / q \lambda E_m)$.
6. **(1 Mark)** Form the ratio $I_{sub,LDD}/I_{sub,conv}$ as a function of $E_m$ ratio.
7. **(1 Mark)** Conclude: the substrate current drops exponentially with $1/E_m$, so a 2.5× field reduction yields ~30× lower $I_{sub}$ and ~150× longer lifetime (using $n = 2.5$).

---

### Question B1 — Option (B) [14 Marks — Alternative Choice]

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 1, Q.2a Choice B]**
> *(a)* Compare conventional S/D MOSFET and LDD MOSFET structures in terms of doping profile, peak field, and hot-carrier reliability.
> *(b)* A 0.35 µm nMOS device has $l = 0.08$ µm and an LDD length of 0.12 µm. Compute the percentage reduction in $E_m$ and estimate the lifetime improvement factor assuming $n = 2.5$ and $I_{sub} \propto \exp(-B/E_m)$ with $B = 1.7 \times 10^6$ V/cm.

#### (a) Comparison — 7-Mark Model Solution

1. **(1 Mark)** Doping profile: conventional is a step n⁺–channel; LDD is a graded n⁺/n⁻/channel.
2. **(1 Mark)** Junction abruptness: conventional has a sharp depletion edge; LDD has a wider, gradual depletion.
3. **(1 Mark)** Peak field: conventional has $E_{m,conv} = (V_{DS} - V_{DSAT})/l$; LDD has $E_{m,LDD} = (V_{DS} - V_{DSAT})/(l + L_{n^-})$.
4. **(1 Mark)** Hot-carrier substrate current: LDD gives $\exp(-B/E_m)$ reduction.
5. **(1 Mark)** Device lifetime: LDD offers 2–3 orders of magnitude improvement.
6. **(1 Mark)** Parasitic resistance: LDD incurs higher $R_{SD}$, compensated by silicide.
7. **(1 Mark)** Process steps: LDD adds 2 extra steps (spacer deposition + RIE) compared to conventional.

#### (b) Numerical Computation — 7-Mark Model Solution

**Given:** $l = 0.08$ µm, $L_{n^-} = 0.12$ µm, $n = 2.5$, $B = 1.7 \times 10^6$ V/cm.

1. **(1 Mark)** Compute $E_m$ ratio:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{E_{m,LDD}}{E_{m,conv}} &= \frac{l}{l + L_{n^-}} = \frac{0.08}{0.08 + 0.12} = \frac{0.08}{0.20} = 0.40
\end{aligned}
$$

2. **(1 Mark)** Percentage reduction in $E_m$ = $(1 - 0.40) \times 100\% = \mathbf{60\%}$.

3. **(1 Mark)** Assume $E_{m,conv} = 5 \times 10^5$ V/cm → $E_{m,LDD} = 0.40 \times 5 \times 10^5 = 2 \times 10^5$ V/cm.

4. **(1 Mark)** Substrate-current ratio:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{I_{sub,LDD}}{I_{sub,conv}} &= \exp\!\left[ -B \left( \frac{1}{E_{m,LDD}} - \frac{1}{E_{m,conv}} \right) \right] \\[4pt]
&= \exp\!\left[ -1.7 \times 10^6 \left( \frac{1}{2 \times 10^5} - \frac{1}{5 \times 10^5} \right) \right] \\[4pt]
&= \exp\!\left[ -1.7 \times 10^6 \times (3 \times 10^{-6}) \right] \\[4pt]
&= \exp(-5.1) \approx 0.0061
\end{aligned}
$$

5. **(1 Mark)** Lifetime-improvement factor:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\tau_{LDD}}{\tau_{conv}} &= \left( \frac{I_{sub,LDD}}{I_{sub,conv}} \right)^{-n} = (0.0061)^{-2.5} \\[4pt]
&\approx 1.16 \times 10^5
\end{aligned}
$$

6. **(1 Mark)** Comment: The LDD device lifetime is approximately **$1.16 \times 10^5$ times** that of the conventional device — a dramatic reliability gain.

7. **(1 Mark)** Practical caveat: this theoretical gain is rarely achieved in real silicon because of secondary effects such as spacer-overlap capacitance and series-resistance-induced self-heating.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in LDDS Questions (KTU 2024 ESE pattern)**
> 1. **Skipping the spacer explanation** — examiners allocate 2–3 marks specifically for the *role of side-wall spacers*. Merely stating "spacers are used" without mentioning *self-alignment* loses full credit.
> 2. **Forgetting the units of $B$ and $E_m$** — both must be in V/cm; mixing V/m and V/cm causes the substrate-current exponent to be off by 10000×.
> 3. **Confusing $l$ (pinch-off length) with $L_{eff}$ (effective channel length)** — they are *not* the same. $l$ is the velocity-saturation region only; $L_{eff}$ is the total conducting channel.
> 4. **Omitting the silicide-recovery discussion** — modern LDD is meaningless without salicidation; examiners frequently award bonus marks for this link.
> 5. **Not stating the operating-temperature assumption** (300 K) for the mean-free-path $\lambda$ — the standard KTU value is $\lambda = 7.2$ nm at 300 K.
> 6. **Ignoring the trade-off equation** — students often present only the benefits; the $R_{LDD}$ penalty is *expected* to be discussed in any 7-mark question.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **LDD = Graded drain engineering** using **side-wall spacers** for self-aligned, lightly doped n⁻ region.
- **Primary purpose:** reduce **peak electric field** $E_m$ to suppress **hot-carrier injection (HCI)** and extend device lifetime.
- **Field reduction formula:** $E_{m,LDD}/E_{m,conv} = 1/(1 + L_{n^-}/l)$.
- **Substrate current model:** $I_{sub} \propto I_D \exp(-\Phi_i / q \lambda E_m)$ — exponential sensitivity makes LDD very effective.
- **Lifetime scaling:** $\tau \propto (I_{sub}/I_D)^{-n}$, with $n \approx 2.5$ to 3.0.
- **Main drawback:** increased **parasitic series resistance** $R_{LDD} = R_{s,n^-} L_{n^-} / W$.
- **Industrial remedy:** pair LDD with **self-aligned silicide (salicide)** to recover $R_{SD}$ while keeping the field-reduction benefit.
- **Process sequence to memorize:** *Gate → n⁻ implant → Spacer deposit → Spacer RIE → n⁺ implant → Anneal → Salicidation*.
- **Typical numbers:** n⁻ dose ≈ 10¹³–10¹⁴ cm⁻², n⁺ dose ≈ 10¹⁵–10¹⁶ cm⁻², spacer width ≈ 50–150 nm.
- **KTU exam keywords to use verbatim:** *side-wall spacer, self-aligned, graded junction, peak longitudinal field, substrate current, hot-carrier injection, velocity saturation, salicide, mean-free-path, reliability lifetime.*
- **Modern descendants:** Halo implants, drain-extended MOS (DEMOS), and graded-channel SOI devices all carry forward the LDDS graded-profile philosophy.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Silicon on Insulator
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT866 - LOW POWER VLSI
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Silicon on Insulator

# Silicon on Insulator (SOI) — Section 1: Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Silicon on Insulator (SOI)** is an advanced CMOS fabrication technology in which a **thin active silicon film** (typically **50 nm – 100 nm** thick) is electrically isolated from the bulk silicon substrate by means of a **buried insulating layer**, most commonly a **Buried OXide (BOX)** of **SiO₂** (thickness ≈ **50 nm – 400 nm**). All active MOSFET devices are fabricated *inside* this top silicon film rather than in the bulk silicon, yielding dramatically reduced junction capacitances and superior low-power performance.

The standard SOI stack (from top to bottom) is:

1. **Active Si film** — where the channel and S/D regions are built.
2. **Buried Oxide (BOX)** — a thermally grown or deposited **SiO₂** dielectric layer.
3. **Handle wafer (Si substrate)** — provides mechanical robustness only; it is *electrically* isolated from the active devices.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Floating Concrete Slab House**
> Imagine a regular house built directly on soft soil. Rain, earthquakes, and moisture from below seep into the foundation, weakening the structure (this is your *bulk CMOS*, where leakage paths and latch-up go straight down into the substrate).
> Now picture the same house built on a thick **concrete slab** that sits on the soil. The slab **electrically and thermally isolates** the house from the ground noise. Bugs, dampness, and short-circuit currents cannot climb through concrete. SOI does exactly this for transistors: the **BOX layer is the concrete slab** that *insulates* the transistors from the noisy, leaky silicon ground.

## 1.3 Why SOI Matters for KTU Low-Power VLSI

| Conventional Bulk CMOS | SOI CMOS |
|---|---|
| Source/Drain pn-junctions reach deep into the Si substrate | S/D junctions are bounded by the BOX layer |
| Large **junction capacitance** ⇒ high dynamic power | Junction capacitance drops by **≈ 5× – 10×** ⇒ low dynamic power |
| Latch-up possible (parasitic SCR) | **Latch-up free** (no parasitic thyristor path) |
| Moderate radiation tolerance | **Radiation-hard** (charge cannot collect in the active region) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For the KTU 2024 Scheme paper, students **must explicitly mention** the role of the BOX layer whenever answering "Why SOI is low-power?" — this is a high-frequency marking keyword.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics to Remember

- **Buried Oxide thermal conductivity** $k_{SiO_2} \approx 1.4 \, \text{W/m·K}$ (≈ 100× worse than silicon's 150 W/m·K) → this is the *origin* of the **self-heating effect** in SOI.
- **Active silicon film resistivity** is engineered between **5–20 Ω·cm** (p-type, lightly doped).
- **Threshold body coefficient** for FD-SOI is typically $\gamma \approx 0.3\text{–}0.6 \, \text{V}^{1/2}$ (much smaller than bulk).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Silicon on Insulator — Section 2: Deep Theory, Types & KTU Formula Cheat-Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of SOI MOSFETs

SOI MOSFETs are divided into two fundamental classes based on the **relationship between the depletion-region width and the silicon film thickness $t_{si}$**.

### 2.1.1 Partially Depleted SOI (PD-SOI)

> [!NOTE]
> In **PD-SOI**, the gate-induced depletion region **does not reach the BOX layer**. A neutral (un-depleted) region of silicon remains in the body, hence the name *partially depleted*.

**Condition:** $t_{si} > W_{d,max}$, where $W_{d,max}$ is the maximum depletion width.

**Consequence:** The body becomes a **floating node** because the source/body and drain/body junctions are reverse-biased and the body has no direct contact. This causes:
- **Floating Body Effect (FBE)**
- **Kink Effect** (anomalous I–V hump at high $V_{DS}$)
- **Bipolar Parasitic Action**
- **Threshold Voltage Instability** (history effect)

### 2.1.2 Fully Depleted SOI (FD-SOI)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In **FD-SOI**, the silicon film is **fully depleted** of free carriers in the off-state. The depletion region extends from the gate oxide all the way down to the buried oxide interface.

**Condition:** $t_{si} < W_{d,max}$, typically engineered at $t_{si} \approx \textbf{5–20 nm}$ for the 28 nm/22 nm nodes.

**Consequence:**
- **No kink effect** (no neutral body to collect impact-ionization charges).
- **Sharper subthreshold slope** ($S \approx 65 \text{ mV/dec}$, close to ideal $60 \text{ mV/dec}$ at 300 K).
- **Near-ideal threshold voltage** (steep retrograde doping or undoped film).
- **Excellent $I_{on}/I_{off}$ ratio** — the headline benefit for **low-power VLSI**.

### 2.1.3 Comparison Table (High-Yield for KTU)

| Parameter | PD-SOI | FD-SOI |
|---|---|---|
| $t_{si}$ | 50–200 nm | 5–20 nm |
| Body | Floating (neutral region exists) | Fully depleted |
| Kink effect | **Present** (a problem) | **Absent** |
| Subthreshold slope | 75–85 mV/dec | ≈ 60–70 mV/dec |
| SCE / DIBL | Moderate | Excellent |
| Back-gate control | Weak | Strong (used for adaptive body biasing) |
| Process maturity | Older nodes (e.g., IBM 0.18 µm) | Modern 28/22/14 nm (STMicro, Samsung) |

## 2.2 The Floating Body Effect (FBE)

When the body is *electrically floating* (PD-SOI), majority carriers generated by **impact ionization** at the drain-end of the channel have no path to escape. They accumulate at the source/body junction, **forward-biasing it** and **lowering the threshold voltage $V_{th}$**, which in turn **raises $I_D$**. This positive feedback loop creates the famous **kink** in the $I_D$–$V_{DS}$ curve.

**Why it matters in low-power design:** $V_{th}$ drifts unpredictably with switching history, leading to timing errors and standby-current instability.

## 2.3 Self-Heating Effect (SHE)

> [!WARNING]
> The BOX layer has $k_{SiO_2} \approx 1.4 \, \text{W/m·K}$, about **100× worse** than Si. Heat generated in the channel cannot easily conduct downward, so **device temperature rises** during sustained operation, *reducing mobility* and *decreasing drain current*. This is the **self-heating effect**, unique to SOI.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat-Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize these equations; they appear in **Part A (3 marks)** and **Part B (14 marks)** every year. Use `\vert` for absolute values to avoid markdown breakage.

| Symbol | Formula | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| $V_{th,FD}$ | $V_{th,FD} = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \dfrac{q N_A t_{si}}{C_{ox,f}}$ | Front-gate threshold of FD-SOI (Q\_B fixed by film thickness) |
| $V_{th,PD}$ | $V_{th,PD} = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \dfrac{\sqrt{2 \varepsilon_{si} q N_A (2\phi_F)}}{C_{ox,f}}$ | Same as bulk CMOS (depletion width not limited) |
| $\Delta V_{th,back}$ | $\Delta V_{th,f} = -\dfrac{C_{si} \, C_{ox,f}}{C_{ox,b} (C_{ox,f} + C_{si})}(V_{G,b} - V_{th,b} - 2\phi_F)$ | Front- $V_{th}$ shift from back-gate biasing (FD-SOI body-biasing equation) |
| $C_{si}$ | $C_{si} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_{si}}{t_{si}}$ | Silicon-film capacitance per unit area |
| $C_{ox,f}, C_{ox,b}$ | $C_{ox,f} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox,f}}, \; C_{ox,b} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_{ox}}{t_{box}}$ | Front / buried oxide capacitances |
| $S$ (subthreshold slope) | $S = (\ln 10) \left( 1 + \dfrac{C_{dep}}{C_{ox}} \right) \dfrac{k_B T}{q}$ | FD-SOI: $S \approx 65 \text{ mV/dec}$ (bulk ≈ 90 mV/dec) |
| $I_{D,sat}$ (FD-SOI) | $I_{D,sat} = \dfrac{\mu C_{ox,f} W}{2L}(V_{GS} - V_{th})^2 (1 + \lambda V_{DS})$ | Same form as bulk, but $\lambda$ is much smaller |

**Boundary conditions to memorize:**

- **FD-SOI** valid only when $t_{si} < W_{d,max} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2\varepsilon_{si} 2\phi_F}{q N_A}}$.
- **PD-SOI** valid when $t_{si} > W_{d,max}$.
- **No kink** if the body is fully depleted ⇒ use FD-SOI for kink-free design.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Mobile SoCs (Apple A-series, Qualcomm Snapdragon):** FD-SOI or FinFET-on-SOI substrates reduce dynamic power by 30–40 %.
- **Aerospace & Defence:** SOI is **rad-hard**; charges from cosmic rays cannot penetrate the active film because the BOX acts as a charge-collecting shield.
- **RF CMOS:** Lower parasitic capacitances push $f_T$ and $f_{max}$ above 400 GHz.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Silicon on Insulator — Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Walk-Throughs

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Below are the **three most exam-relevant derivations** for the KTU 2024 Scheme. Every algebraic step is shown in full, with a text-row explanation beside the math block.

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Threshold Voltage of an FD-SOI MOSFET

We start from the **bulk-CMOS threshold-voltage framework** and then impose the FD-SOI condition $W_d = t_{si}$ to clamp the depletion charge.

**Step 1 — Write the standard threshold expression for a bulk MOSFET.**

$$V_{th,\text{bulk}} = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \frac{\sqrt{2 \varepsilon_{si} q N_A (2\phi_F)}}{C_{ox,f}}$$

This says: the threshold is the flat-band voltage $V_{FB}$, plus twice the Fermi potential $2\phi_F$ (the surface potential needed for strong inversion), plus the voltage drop across the gate oxide required to support the bulk depletion charge $Q_B = \sqrt{2 \varepsilon_{si} q N_A (2\phi_F)}$.

**Step 2 — Express the depletion width in terms of $Q_B$.**

$$W_{d,\text{max}} = \frac{\sqrt{2 \varepsilon_{si} (2\phi_F)}}{q N_A} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad Q_B = q N_A W_{d,\text{max}}$$

For a bulk device, $W_{d,\text{max}}$ is reached at the onset of strong inversion, and $Q_B$ takes the value above.

**Step 3 — Impose the FD-SOI condition: $W_{d,\text{max}} \to t_{si}$.**

In FD-SOI, the silicon film is **thinner than** the natural bulk depletion width. The depletion region simply cannot grow past the BOX, so the *maximum* depletion charge becomes:

$$Q_{B,\text{FD}} = q N_A t_{si}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute this $Q_B$ back into the threshold expression.**

$$V_{th,FD} = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \frac{Q_{B,\text{FD}}}{C_{ox,f}}$$

$$V_{th,FD} = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \frac{q N_A t_{si}}{C_{ox,f}}$$

**Step 5 — Engineering insight.**

The depletion-charge term is now **independent of $V_{SB}$** (no body effect!). This is why FD-SOI has an *almost constant* threshold voltage and is **immune to body-bias fluctuations**, ideal for low-power analog/RF design.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Back-Gate Coupling in FD-SOI (Body-Bias Effect)

In FD-SOI, the **back gate** (handle wafer under the BOX) is *fully functional* because the film is thin. Applying a voltage $V_{G,b}$ to the back gate modulates the front-gate $V_{th}$. This is the **key mechanism exploited for adaptive power management** in modern low-power SoCs.

**Step 1 — Write Gauss's law at the silicon-film centre.**

The net induced charge in the silicon film is split between the front-oxide and back-oxide interfaces. Treating the film as a parallel-plate capacitor $C_{si} = \varepsilon_{si} / t_{si}$:

$$C_{ox,f}(V_{G,f} - V_{th,f}^0) - C_{ox,b}(V_{G,b} - V_{th,b}^0) = (C_{ox,f} + C_{si}) \, \phi_s$$

where $\phi_s$ is the front-surface potential at threshold, and $V_{th,f}^0, V_{th,b}^0$ are the zero-coupling thresholds.

**Step 2 — Solve for the threshold surface potential condition $\phi_s = 2\phi_F$.**

Substitute and isolate $V_{G,f}$:

$$V_{G,f} = V_{th,f}^0 + \frac{C_{ox,f} + C_{si}}{C_{ox,f}} 2\phi_F + \frac{C_{ox,b}}{C_{ox,f}}(V_{G,b} - V_{th,b}^0 - 2\phi_F)$$

**Step 3 — Identify the front-gate threshold shift $\Delta V_{th,f}$.**

$$\Delta V_{th,f} = \frac{C_{ox,b}}{C_{ox,f}} (V_{G,b} - V_{th,b}^0 - 2\phi_F)$$

Replacing $C_{ox,b} = \varepsilon_{ox}/t_{box}$, $C_{ox,f} = \varepsilon_{ox}/t_{ox,f}$, we get a more symmetric form:

$$\Delta V_{th,f} = -\frac{C_{si} \, C_{ox,f}}{C_{ox,b} (C_{ox,f} + C_{si})} (V_{G,b} - V_{th,b} - 2\phi_F)$$

(The minus sign convention: a *positive* back-gate voltage *lowers* the front-gate threshold, equivalent to forward-biasing the body in bulk CMOS.)

**Step 4 — Physical interpretation.**

The factor $\dfrac{C_{si} \, C_{ox,f}}{C_{ox,b} (C_{ox,f} + C_{si})}$ is called the **back-gate coupling coefficient** $\alpha$. Typical values: $\alpha \approx 0.10$–$0.25$. This means a **1 V swing on the back gate shifts the front-gate $V_{th}$ by ≈ 100–250 mV** — enough to change $I_{on}$ by **2×–4×** in a low-power FD-SOI design.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Subthreshold Slope of an FD-SOI MOSFET

**Step 1 — Start from the subthreshold current expression.**

$$I_D = \mu C_{ox,f} \frac{W}{L} (n-1) \phi_t^2 \, e^{\frac{V_{GS} - V_{th}}{n\phi_t}} \left(1 - e^{-\frac{V_{DS}}{\phi_t}}\right)$$

where $\phi_t = k_B T / q \approx 25.85 \text{ mV}$ at 300 K, and $n$ is the body-effect factor.

**Step 2 — Take the derivative of $\log_{10}(I_D)$ with respect to $V_{GS}$.**

$$S = \ln(10) \cdot \frac{n \phi_t}{1} = (2.3 \, n) \phi_t$$

For bulk CMOS:

$$n = 1 + \frac{C_{dep}}{C_{ox,f}} = 1 + \frac{\varepsilon_{si}/W_{d,max}}{\varepsilon_{ox}/t_{ox,f}}$$

**Step 3 — For FD-SOI, replace $W_{d,max}$ with $t_{si}$.**

$$n_{FD} = 1 + \frac{\varepsilon_{si}/t_{si}}{\varepsilon_{ox}/t_{ox,f}}$$

**Step 4 — Plug in typical numbers** (e.g., $t_{si}=10$ nm, $t_{ox,f}=1.5$ nm):

$$n_{FD} = 1 + \frac{3.9 \times 8.85\times 10^{-12} / 10\times 10^{-9}}{3.9 \times 8.85\times 10^{-12} / 1.5\times 10^{-9}} = 1 + 0.15 = 1.15$$

$$S_{FD} = 2.3 \times 1.15 \times 25.85 \text{ mV/dec} \approx 68 \text{ mV/dec}$$

This is much closer to the **Boltzmann limit of 60 mV/dec** than bulk CMOS (which gives 85–90 mV/dec at 90 nm), giving FD-SOI its **superior leakage suppression**.

## 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Quantity | Bulk CMOS | PD-SOI | FD-SOI |
|---|---|---|---|
| $V_{th}$ formula | $V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \sqrt{2\varepsilon_{si}qN_A(2\phi_F)}/C_{ox}$ | Same as bulk | $V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + qN_A t_{si}/C_{ox}$ |
| Body effect ($\gamma$) | $0.4$–$0.7 \,\text{V}^{1/2}$ | Same | $0.05$–$0.20 \,\text{V}^{1/2}$ |
| Kink effect | None | **Yes** | No |
| Subthreshold slope | 85 mV/dec | 75 mV/dec | **≈ 65 mV/dec** |
| Back-gate coupling | N/A | Weak | **Strong (α ≈ 0.1–0.25)** |
| Self-heating | Low | **High** | **High** |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Silicon on Insulator — Section 4: Structural Schematics & Block Diagrams

> [!NOTE]
> Because physical cross-sections of MOSFETs are difficult to render with Mermaid, the diagrams below use a **Block-Level Functional Architecture** to map the device structure, signal flow, and thermal/electrical domains. This is the same style used in KTU board answer-sheets for full marks.

## 4.1 Layered Structure of a SOI Wafer (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TOPLAYER ["ACTIVE SILICON FILM (t_si)"]
        A1["GATE OXIDE t_ox_f"] --> A2["CHANNEL REGION"]
        A2 --> A3["SOURCE / DRAIN"]
    end

    subgraph MIDLAYER ["BURIED OXIDE BOX (SiO2)"]
        B1["t_box = 50 to 400 nm"]
        B1 -. "thermal insulator" .-> MIDLAYER
    end

    subgraph BOTLAYER ["HANDLE WAFER Si SUBSTRATE"]
        C1["Mechanical support only"]
        C2["Back-gate contact V_Gb"]
    end

    A3 --> B1
    B1 --> C1
    C2 -. "electrically isolated by BOX" .-> B1
```

**Reading the diagram:** The top block contains all active device physics. The middle BOX block is the *dielectric isolator*. The bottom handle block is *electrically decoupled* from the active region — but in FD-SOI it is reused as a second gate.

## 4.2 PD-SOI vs FD-SOI Decision Flow (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S0["START: Choose SOI technology"] --> S1{"Is t_si less than W_d_max?"}
    S1 -- YES --> S2["FD-SOI"]
    S1 -- NO --> S3["PD-SOI"]
    S2 --> S4["Kink free, sharp subthreshold, body-bias capable"]
    S3 --> S5["Floating body, kink effect, history effect present"]
    S2 --> S6["Use for low power mobile SoC"]
    S3 --> S7["Use for legacy high voltage analog"]
```

## 4.3 Floating-Body / Kink-Effect Cause-and-Effect Chain

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1["High V_DS at drain end"] --> P2["Impact ionization generates e-h pairs"]
    P2 --> P3["Holes accumulate in floating body"]
    P3 --> P4["Body potential V_B rises"]
    P4 --> P5["Source-body junction becomes forward biased"]
    P5 --> P6["V_th decreases"]
    P6 --> P7["I_D increases sharply kink in I-V curve"]
    P7 --> P8["Power dissipation and timing error in PD-SOI"]
```

## 4.4 Self-Heating Heat-Flow Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Q1["Channel heat generation Q_ch"] --> Q2["Top metal interconnect path k ~ 400 W/mK"]
    Q1 --> Q3["Downward into BOX layer k ~ 1.4 W/mK"]
    Q3 --> Q4["Heat trapped: local T rises by 30 to 80 K"]
    Q4 --> Q5["Mobility mu drops"]
    Q5 --> Q6["I_D reduces negative differential resistance possible"]
    Q2 --> Q7["Heat spreads laterally much faster"]
```

## 4.5 Body-Bias Adaptive Power Control (FD-SOI Functional Block)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CTRL["Adaptive Body-Bias Controller"] -- "V_Gb dynamic" --> BACKGATE["Back Gate of FD-SOI NMOS and PMOS"]
    BACKGATE -- "shifts V_th_f" --> CHANNEL["Front-Gate Channel"]
    CHANNEL --> ID["I_on and I_off modulated"]
    ID -- "Active mode" --> P1["Forward bias: V_Gb high: V_th down: high speed"]
    ID -- "Standby mode" --> P2["Reverse bias: V_Gb low: V_th up: low leakage"]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Silicon on Insulator — Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Question Bank & Rapid Recap

> [!NOTE]
> All questions below are modeled on **KTU University (2019–2024 Scheme)** past-paper patterns for **PEECT866 — Low Power VLSI**. Each carries a simulated past-year tag. Mark distribution follows the KTU ESE pattern: **Part A = 3 marks, Part B = 14 marks (with internal choice)**.

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]
**Define Silicon on Insulator (SOI) technology. List any two advantages of SOI over bulk CMOS for low-power VLSI design.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** SOI is a CMOS fabrication technology in which a thin silicon film is fabricated on top of a **buried insulating layer (BOX, typically SiO₂)** which in turn sits on a silicon handle wafer. The active transistors are built *inside* the top Si film, fully isolated from the bulk by the BOX.

**Advantages over bulk CMOS (1 mark each, any two):**

1. **Reduced parasitic junction capacitance** — because the source/drain pn-junctions terminate at the BOX, junction capacitance drops by 5–10×, giving a proportional reduction in dynamic power $P = \alpha C V_{DD}^2 f$.
2. **Latch-up immunity** — the BOX breaks the parasitic p–n–p–n thyristor path between NMOS and PMOS, eliminating latch-up entirely.
3. **Lower short-channel effects** — fully depleted FD-SOI offers near-ideal subthreshold slope (≈ 65 mV/dec) and excellent DIBL control.
4. **Radiation hardness** — charge generated by ionising radiation cannot collect in the active region because the BOX layer is a perfect charge collector.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]
**What is the "kink effect" in PD-SOI MOSFETs? State its physical origin.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** The kink effect is an *anomalous sudden rise* in the drain current $I_D$ observed in the $I_D$–$V_{DS}$ saturation region of a **Partially Depleted SOI MOSFET**, appearing as a "kink" or upward bend in the curve.

**Physical origin (1.5 marks):** At high $V_{DS}$, **impact ionisation** near the drain generates electron-hole pairs. In bulk CMOS, the holes flow to the body contact; in PD-SOI, the body is **floating**, so the holes accumulate, raising the body potential $V_B$. This forward-biases the source-body junction, **lowers the threshold voltage $V_{th}$**, and **increases $I_D$** — producing the kink.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For the 14-mark KTU question, KTU always offers **Module-Internal Choice** between two sub-questions. Below are **Question A** and **Question B** as the two choice options. Each is broken into (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 | Module 1 | Choice 1]**

**(a)** With a neat cross-section, explain the structure of a **Silicon on Insulator (SOI) MOSFET**. Compare **PD-SOI** and **FD-SOI** in terms of film thickness, depletion width, kink effect, and subthreshold slope. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **threshold voltage expression for an FD-SOI MOSFET** and show that it is **independent of the source-body bias $V_{SB}$**. Comment on the engineering significance of this result for low-power VLSI. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q-A (a) [7 marks]:**

**Structure (3 marks):** A SOI MOSFET consists of three layers from top to bottom: (i) a thin p-type silicon film (50–200 nm for PD, 5–20 nm for FD) where the channel and S/D are formed, (ii) a buried oxide (BOX) layer of SiO₂ (50–400 nm), and (iii) a silicon handle wafer that provides mechanical support and (in FD-SOI) acts as a back-gate.

The gate is built on top of the silicon film separated by a thin gate oxide. The S/D regions are doped n⁺ in the active film only; the junctions stop at the BOX, eliminating the deep-well capacitances found in bulk CMOS.

**Comparison table (4 marks):**

| Property | PD-SOI | FD-SOI |
|---|---|---|
| Silicon film thickness $t_{si}$ | 50–200 nm | 5–20 nm |
| Depletion condition | $W_d < t_{si}$ (neutral body remains) | $W_d = t_{si}$ (fully depleted) |
| Kink effect | Present (floating body) | Absent |
| Subthreshold slope | 75–85 mV/dec | ≈ 60–70 mV/dec |
| Body effect | Strong | Very weak (≈ 0) |
| Back-gate coupling | Poor | Strong (used for body-biasing) |

---

**Model Solution for Q-A (b) [7 marks]:**

Starting from the bulk threshold expression:

$$V_{th} = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \frac{Q_{B}}{C_{ox,f}}$$

For a bulk MOSFET, the depletion charge at strong inversion is:

$$Q_{B,\text{bulk}} = \sqrt{2 \varepsilon_{si} q N_A (2\phi_F)}$$

In **FD-SOI**, the silicon film thickness is *less* than the natural depletion width, so the depletion charge is **clamped** at:

$$Q_{B,\text{FD}} = q N_A t_{si}$$

This is **independent of $V_{SB}$**, because the depletion region physically cannot grow past the BOX. Substituting:

$$\boxed{V_{th,FD} = V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + \frac{q N_A t_{si}}{C_{ox,f}}}$$

Differentiating with respect to $V_{SB}$:

$$\frac{\partial V_{th,FD}}{\partial V_{SB}} = 0$$

Hence the **body-effect coefficient $\gamma = 0$** in ideal FD-SOI.

**Engineering significance (2 marks):** This $\gamma \approx 0$ property means the threshold is **insensitive to body bias**, making FD-SOI ideal for **low-leakage standby** because one can use a single global $V_{th}$ across the chip. Furthermore, the *back gate* can be used to **independently tune** $V_{th}$ for dynamic power-performance trade-offs.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 | Module 1 | Choice 2]**

**(a)** What is the **floating body effect (FBE)** in PD-SOI? Explain with a block diagram how impact ionisation at the drain leads to a **kink in the $I_D$–$V_{DS}$ curve**. State **two techniques** used to suppress the kink effect. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **self-heating effect** in SOI MOSFETs. Why is it more severe than in bulk CMOS? How does it affect the device $I$–$V$ characteristics and what are the standard layout-level remedies? **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q-B (a) [7 marks]:**

**Floating Body Effect (2 marks):** In a PD-SOI MOSFET, the silicon body is **electrically isolated** (no body contact) because the source-body and drain-body pn junctions are reverse-biased and the BOX cuts off the path to the substrate. The body therefore acts as a *floating node* whose potential is determined solely by the net generation-recombination currents.

**Kink effect chain (3 marks):** At high $V_{DS}$, carriers accelerate near the drain and **impact ionisation** creates electron-hole pairs. The generated **holes drift toward the body** (which is at the lowest potential in an nMOS), and because the body is floating, they have no escape path. They accumulate, **raising $V_B$**, which **forward-biases the source-body junction** and **injects electrons** into the body. This reduces $V_{th}$ and **increases $I_D$** — the kink. (See the Mermaid flowchart in Section 4.3 for the visual.)

**Suppression techniques (1 mark each, any two):**

1. **Use FD-SOI** — fully depleted body has no neutral region to collect holes.
2. **Body-contacted (tied-body) PD-SOI** — provide a direct ohmic contact to the body via a p⁺ region tied to ground, draining the holes.
3. **Source-body tie** — connect the body to the source with a local p⁺ strap in the layout.
4. **Recessed S/D or Ge-implanted S/D** to reduce impact-ionisation rate.

---

**Model Solution for Q-B (b) [7 marks]:**

**Origin (2 marks):** The buried oxide (SiO₂) has a thermal conductivity of $k_{SiO_2} \approx 1.4 \,\text{W/m·K}$, which is roughly **100× lower** than silicon's $k_{Si} \approx 150 \,\text{W/m·K}$. Heat generated in the SOI channel has no easy conductive path to the substrate because the BOX layer is a *thermal* as well as electrical insulator.

**Effect on $I$–$V$ (3 marks):** As the device operates, channel temperature $T_{ch}$ rises by 30–80 K above ambient. This:
- **Reduces carrier mobility** ($\mu \propto T^{-1.5}$ for phononic scattering).
- **Reduces saturation drain current** $I_{D,sat}$ (negative differential resistance region may appear at high $V_{GS}$).
- **Reduces $V_{th}$** slightly (weak temperature dependence), partially compensating the mobility drop.

**Layout-level remedies (1 mark each, any two):**

1. **Source-body and drain-body LDD engineering** to spread heat-generating regions.
2. **Use of buried oxide "windows" (partial SOI)** — etch pits through the BOX to provide direct Si-to-Si thermal contact between active film and handle.
3. **Multi-finger layout** to spread heat laterally and lower the per-finger power density.
4. **Thermally conductive interconnects** (e.g., Cu) and dense metal stack to carry heat upward.
5. **Body-contacted SOI layout** where a body tie also acts as a heat sink.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks on SOI questions (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> 1. **Forgetting to mention the BOX layer explicitly** when defining SOI — KTU examiners treat this as a 1-mark miss.
> 2. **Confusing PD-SOI and FD-SOI conditions** — write $t_{si} < W_{d,max}$ for FD and $t_{si} > W_{d,max}$ for PD; do not write the reverse.
> 3. **Writing "FD-SOI has zero capacitance"** — it does not. The reduction is *junction* capacitance, not *gate* capacitance.
> 4. **Omitting the thermal-conductivity numbers** ($k_{SiO_2} \approx 1.4$ vs $k_{Si} \approx 150$ W/m·K) in self-heating questions. KTU board examiners award a free mark for this numerical comparison.
> 5. **Forgetting the back-gate coupling equation** in FD-SOI body-biasing derivations. Always state the back-gate coefficient $\alpha = C_{si} C_{ox,f} / [C_{ox,b} (C_{ox,f} + C_{si})]$ and its typical range 0.1–0.25.
> 6. **In Part A (3 marks), writing more than 1.5 pages** — KTU awards marks based on *keywords*. Be concise and bulleted.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **SOI = Active Si film + Buried Oxide (BOX) + Handle wafer.** Memorise this 3-layer stack.
- [x] **PD-SOI** → $t_{si} > W_{d,max}$, floating body, kink effect present, $S \approx 75\text{–}85$ mV/dec.
- [x] **FD-SOI** → $t_{si} < W_{d,max}$, no kink, near-ideal $S \approx 60\text{–}70$ mV/dec, back-gate tunable.
- [x] **FD-SOI threshold** = $V_{FB} + 2\phi_F + q N_A t_{si}/C_{ox,f}$ — *independent of $V_{SB}$*.
- [x] **Back-gate coupling coefficient** $\alpha = C_{si} C_{ox,f} / [C_{ox,b} (C_{ox,f} + C_{si})] \approx 0.1\text{–}0.25$.
- [x] **Kink effect** = impact-ionisation → hole accumulation in floating body → $V_B \uparrow$ → $V_{th} \downarrow$ → $I_D \uparrow$.
- [x] **Self-heating** = $k_{SiO_2} \approx 1.4$ W/m·K (vs $k_{Si} \approx 150$ W/m·K) → trapped heat → mobility drop → $I_D$ drop.
- [x] **Latch-up immunity** = BOX breaks the parasitic p–n–p–n thyristor path.
- [x] **Radiation hardness** = BOX acts as a charge-collecting shield.
- [x] **Junction capacitance drops 5×–10×** in SOI vs bulk — this is the *headline* low-power advantage.
- [x] **Subthreshold slope** in FD-SOI approaches the Boltzmann limit of 60 mV/dec at 300 K.
- [x] Always mention **both electrical and thermal** isolation of the BOX in essay-type answers.
- [x] For numerical questions: $k_B T / q \approx 25.85$ mV at 300 K; $\varepsilon_{si} = 11.7 \varepsilon_0$; $\varepsilon_{ox} = 3.9 \varepsilon_0$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Short Circuit Power: Short Circuit Current of Inverter
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEECT866 - LOW POWER VLSI > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Short Circuit Power: Short Circuit Current of Inverter ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Short Circuit Power (P_sc)** is the power dissipated in a CMOS inverter due to the momentary conduction of a **direct current path from V_DD to GND** through both the PMOS (pull-up) and NMOS (pull-down) transistors simultaneously during the **input signal transition** (slew). The current flowing through this direct V_DD → GND path is termed the **Short Circuit Current (I_sc)** or, colloquially, the **"Crowbar Current."** The instantaneous short circuit current is non-zero only during the finite interval when the input voltage V_in satisfies the simultaneous conduction condition: $$V_{tn} < V_{in} < V_{DD} - \vert V_{tp} \vert$$ Outside this window, only one transistor conducts (or both are off) and the crowbar current is effectively zero. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition) > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — The Double Door Problem:** > Imagine two automatic sliding doors at the entrance and exit of a corridor. The first door is the PMOS (entrance, slides to open from V_DD side), and the second is the NMOS (exit, slides to open to GND side). For a brief moment while a person walks through and the input signal is "changing," **both doors are simultaneously open**, allowing cold air from outside to blow straight through the corridor from V_DD to GND. This wasteful air current is analogous to the **short circuit current**. The longer it takes for someone to walk through (slow input ramp), the more cold air leaks in (more P_sc). In CMOS, this leakage "wastes" energy as heat inside the chip. ### 1.3 Physical Context & Relevance - Short circuit power is part of the **dynamic power** of a CMOS gate (along with **switching/load capacitive power**). - It occurs in **every transition** of the input signal (both rising and falling). - It is distinct from **static leakage power** (sub-threshold, gate leakage) which exists even when the circuit is idle. - A properly designed inverter minimizes I_sc by ensuring the PMOS/NMOS threshold ratio creates sharp switching at V_DD/2. ### 1.4 Standard Metrics Used | Metric | Symbol | Typical Range | |---|---|---| | Supply Voltage | **V_DD** | 0.7 V – 5.0 V | | NMOS Threshold Voltage | **V_tn** | 0.3 V – 0.7 V | | PMOS Threshold Voltage | **V_tp** | -0.3 V to -0.7 V | | Input Rise Time | **τ_r** | 10 ps – 1 ns | | Input Fall Time | **τ_f** | 10 ps – 1 ns | | Switching Frequency | **f_sw** | 1 MHz – 3 GHz | | Transconductance Parameter | **k = μ C_ox (W/L)** | 50–200 μA/V² | > [!TIP] > **Examiner's Mnemonic:** "**SCOPE**" — **S**imultaneous **C**onduction causes **O**pposite **P**ath **E**nergy waste. Remember this whenever the question asks "what is short circuit power?" ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Physical Origin of Short Circuit Current During a low-to-high transition at the input of a CMOS inverter, the following sequence occurs: 1. **t = 0⁻:** V_in = 0 V → PMOS ON, NMOS OFF → V_out = V_DD 2. **t = 0⁺ (V_in starts rising):** PMOS begins to turn OFF as V_in approaches |V_tp| below V_DD 3. **At V_in = V_tn:** NMOS turns ON, but PMOS is still ON (since V_in < V_DD - |V_tp|) 4. **Window of Crowbar:** When V_tn < V_in < V_DD - |V_tp|, **BOTH transistors are in saturation** 5. **At V_in = V_DD/2 (symmetric):** V_GS,n = V_SG,p = V_DD/2 → **Peak Crowbar Current** 6. **At V_in = V_DD - |V_tp|:** PMOS turns OFF 7. **t = τ_r:** V_in = V_DD → PMOS OFF, NMOS ON → V_out = 0 V ### 2.2 The Two Critical Transistor Equations For the **NMOS** (in saturation during the window): $$I_{sc,n} = \frac{k_n}{2}(V_{in} - V_{tn})^2$$ For the **PMOS** (in saturation during the window): $$I_{sc,p} = \frac{k_p}{2}(V_{DD} - V_{in} - \vert V_{tp} \vert)^2$$ Since they are in **series**, the same current flows through both, and it is limited by the **weaker driver** (the one with smaller overdrive). ### 2.3 Peak Short Circuit Current For a **symmetric inverter** (k_n = k_p = k, V_tn = |V_tp| = V_t), the current is symmetric about V_in = V_DD/2, and the **peak** occurs at V_in = V_DD/2: $$I_{peak} = \frac{k}{2}\left(\frac{V_{DD}}{2} - V_t\right)^2 = \frac{k}{8}(V_{DD} - 2V_t)^2$$ ### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table is the **most heavily tested formula set** in KTU ESE Module 2. Commit it to memory. | # | Parameter | Formula | Description | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Simultaneous Conduction Window | $$V_{tn} < V_{in} < V_{DD} - \vert V_{tp} \vert$$ | Voltage range where both transistors conduct | | 2 | Width of Conduction Window | $$\Delta V = V_{DD} - V_{tn} - \vert V_{tp} \vert$$ | Larger window → more I_sc | | 3 | Peak Short Circuit Current (symmetric) | $$I_{peak} = \dfrac{k}{8}(V_{DD} - 2V_t)^2$$ | Maximum crowbar current | | 4 | NMOS Saturation Current | $$I_{sc,n} = \dfrac{k_n}{2}(V_{in} - V_{tn})^2$$ | When V_in > V_tn and V_out > V_in - V_tn | | 5 | PMOS Saturation Current | $$I_{sc,p} = \dfrac{k_p}{2}(V_{DD} - V_{in} - \vert V_{tp} \vert)^2$$ | When V_in < V_DD - \|V_tp\| and V_out < V_in + \|V_tp\| | | 6 | Average Short Circuit Current | $$I_{mean} \approx \dfrac{I_{peak}}{2} = \dfrac{k}{16}(V_{DD} - 2V_t)^2$$ | Time-averaged during transition | | 7 | **Energy per Transition** | $$E_{sc} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{mean} \cdot \tau = \dfrac{k \cdot \tau}{16} \cdot V_{DD}(V_{DD} - 2V_t)^2$$ | Energy wasted per switching event | | 8 | **Short Circuit Power (Standard Text Form)** | $$P_{sc} = \dfrac{k \cdot \tau}{12}(V_{DD} - 2V_t)^3 \cdot f_{sw}$$ | The KTU-classic expression (Rabaey/Weste form) | | 9 | **Short Circuit Power (Approximate Form)** | $$P_{sc} \approx \dfrac{k \cdot \tau \cdot f_{sw}}{16} \cdot V_{DD}(V_{DD} - 2V_t)^2$$ | Simple triangular approximation | | 10 | Ratio of P_sc to P_switching | $$\dfrac{P_{sc}}{P_{sw}} = \dfrac{\tau \cdot (V_{DD} - 2V_t)^3}{12 \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^3}$$ | Indicates relative importance | > [!NOTE] > **Caveat on Formula 8 vs 9:** Formula 8 results from a more rigorous integration assuming a **linear input ramp** and ramp duration τ. Formula 9 is the simpler triangular approximation. KTU typically accepts **either** if derived properly. We will use Formula 8 as the canonical form. ### 2.5 Engineering Design Rules to Minimize P_sc 1. **Keep input rise/fall times fast** — small τ → less time spent in the crowbar window. 2. **Increase V_tn and |V_tp|** — narrows the simultaneous conduction window. 3. **Lower V_DD** — cubic dependence (Formula 8) makes P_sc drop rapidly. 4. **Buffer long wires** — prevents slow ramps from reaching internal gates. 5. **Use steeper input transitions** — through tapered buffer chains. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Detailed Derivation of Short Circuit Power (P_sc) We consider a **symmetric CMOS inverter** with: - k_n = k_p = k - V_tn = |V_tp| = V_t - Input rise time = τ (linear ramp from 0 to V_DD) - Switching frequency = f_sw #### **Step 1: Model the Input as a Linear Ramp** During the rising transition, we assume V_in is a linear function of time: $$V_{in}(t) = \dfrac{V_{DD}}{\tau} \cdot t, \quad 0 \le t \le \tau$$ #### **Step 2: Identify the Saturation Region** Both NMOS and PMOS are in **saturation** when: $$V_{tn} < V_{in} < V_{DD} - \vert V_{tp} \vert$$ Converting to time-domain boundaries: $$t_1 = \dfrac{V_{tn} \cdot \tau}{V_{DD}} = \dfrac{V_t \cdot \tau}{V_{DD}}$$ $$t_2 = \dfrac{(V_{DD} - V_t)\tau}{V_{DD}}$$ #### **Step 3: Express the Short Circuit Current** The current is limited by both transistors in series. For the symmetric case, the current is: $$I_{sc}(t) = \dfrac{k}{2}\bigl(V_{in}(t) - V_t\bigr)^2 = \dfrac{k}{2}\left(\dfrac{V_{DD} \cdot t}{\tau} - V_t\right)^2$$ (valid in the interval t_1 ≤ t ≤ t_2) #### **Step 4: Compute the Charge Transferred During the Transition** $$Q_{sc} = \int_{t_1}^{t_2} I_{sc}(t)\, dt = \int_{t_1}^{t_2} \dfrac{k}{2}\left(\dfrac{V_{DD} t}{\tau} - V_t\right)^2 dt$$ Let us substitute u = (V_DD · t/τ) - V_t, so du = (V_DD/τ) dt → dt = (τ/V_DD) du. When t = t_1, u = 0. When t = t_2, u = V_DD - 2V_t. Therefore: $$Q_{sc} = \dfrac{k}{2} \cdot \dfrac{\tau}{V_{DD}} \int_{0}^{V_{DD} - 2V_t} u^2 \, du$$ $$Q_{sc} = \dfrac{k \cdot \tau}{2 V_{DD}} \cdot \left[\dfrac{u^3}{3}\right]_{0}^{V_{DD} - 2V_t}$$ $$Q_{sc} = \dfrac{k \cdot \tau}{6 V_{DD}} \cdot (V_{DD} - 2V_t)^3$$ #### **Step 5: Compute the Energy Dissipated per Transition** $$E_{sc} = V_{DD} \cdot Q_{sc} = V_{DD} \cdot \dfrac{k \cdot \tau}{6 V_{DD}} \cdot (V_{DD} - 2V_t)^3$$ $$E_{sc} = \dfrac{k \cdot \tau}{6} (V_{DD} - 2V_t)^3$$ > [!NOTE] > Wait — this is the energy for **one** transition (0 → 1). For symmetric switching, the same energy is dissipated during the **falling** transition (1 → 0). So per clock cycle (one full switching event), the total energy is: > $$E_{cycle} = 2 \cdot E_{sc} = \dfrac{k \cdot \tau}{3}(V_{DD} - 2V_t)^3$$ > However, for a single edge (commonly assumed in KTU problems), the formula simplifies. #### **Step 6: Convert to Power** The number of transitions per second = f_sw. Therefore: $$P_{sc} = E_{sc} \cdot f_{sw} = \dfrac{k \cdot \tau}{6}(V_{DD} - 2V_t)^3 \cdot f_{sw}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > This is the **single-edge** short circuit power. Some textbooks (e.g., Rabaey) include a factor of 2 for both edges and write: > $$P_{sc} = \dfrac{k \cdot \tau}{12}(V_{DD} - 2V_t)^3 \cdot f_{sw}$$ > (This is the per-cycle-averaged form, where f_sw already represents full clock frequency.) **Both forms appear in KTU exams; the question wording dictates which is expected.** #### **Step 7: Numerical Verification with Simple Approximation** Using the triangular current approximation: $$E_{sc}^{approx} = V_{DD} \cdot \dfrac{I_{peak}}{2} \cdot \tau = V_{DD} \cdot \dfrac{k}{16}(V_{DD} - 2V_t)^2 \cdot \tau$$ $$P_{sc}^{approx} = \dfrac{k \cdot V_{DD} \cdot \tau \cdot (V_{DD} - 2V_t)^2}{16} \cdot f_{sw}$$ The exact and approximate forms differ by a factor related to the ramp shape. KTU accepts either, provided the student states the assumption. ### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example > [!NOTE] > **Given:** A symmetric CMOS inverter has k_n = k_p = 100 μA/V², V_DD = 3.3 V, V_t = 0.5 V, input rise time τ = 1 ns, switching frequency f_sw = 100 MHz. **Step 1: Compute the conduction window width.** $$V_{DD} - 2V_t = 3.3 - 2(0.5) = 2.3 \, \text{V}$$ **Step 2: Compute peak short circuit current.** $$I_{peak} = \dfrac{100 \times 10^{-6}}{8}(2.3)^2 = 12.5 \times 10^{-6} \times 5.29$$ $$I_{peak} = 66.13 \, \mu A$$ **Step 3: Compute energy per transition (using exact formula).** $$E_{sc} = \dfrac{k \cdot \tau}{6}(V_{DD} - 2V_t)^3 = \dfrac{100 \times 10^{-6} \times 10^{-9}}{6}(2.3)^3$$ $$E_{sc} = \dfrac{10^{-13}}{6} \times 12.167 = 2.028 \times 10^{-13} \, \text{J} = 0.2028 \, \text{pJ}$$ **Step 4: Compute short circuit power (per-edge).** $$P_{sc} = E_{sc} \cdot f_{sw} = 0.2028 \times 10^{-12} \times 100 \times 10^6$$ $$P_{sc} = 20.28 \, \mu W$$ > [!TIP] > Notice that for typical digital circuits, P_sc is usually **5–20%** of the dynamic switching power P_sw = α C_L V_DD² f. If τ becomes comparable to the output rise time, P_sc can dominate the dynamic budget. ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — When Does P_sc Matter? | Condition | Impact on P_sc | Dominant Power | |---|---|---| | τ ≪ T_output (fast input, fast output) | Negligible | **P_switching** (C·V²·f) | | τ ≈ T_output (matched rise times) | Significant (~30% of P_sw) | **P_switching** + P_sc | | τ ≫ T_output (slow input, fast output) | Approaches 100% of dynamic power | **P_sc dominates** | | V_t close to V_DD/2 | **Maximum** (wide conduction window) | P_sc is critical | | V_t ≪ V_DD/2 | **Minimum** (narrow window) | P_switching dominates | | Low V_DD (e.g., 0.7 V near-threshold) | **Vanishingly small** | Leakage dominates | | High V_DD (e.g., 5 V) | **Cubic growth** | P_sc + P_switching both large | ## 4.
