KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Define short circuit power dissipation in a CMOS inverter. Under what input condition does the short circuit current flow?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > Short circuit power dissipation (P_sc) is the power consumed in a CMOS inverter due to the **simultaneous conduction of the PMOS and NMOS transistors**, creating a direct current path from V_DD to GND during the input signal transition. This current is called the **short circuit or crowbar current (I_sc)**. > > The short circuit current flows when the input voltage V_in lies in the range: > $$V_{tn} < V_{in} < V_{DD} - \vert V_{tp} \vert$$ > Within this window, both transistors are in saturation, and a non-zero I_sc flows from V_DD to GND, dissipating energy as heat. (3 marks) --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Write the expression for the short circuit power dissipation in a symmetric CMOS inverter and explain the significance of each term.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > The short circuit power dissipation in a symmetric CMOS inverter is: > $$P_{sc} = \dfrac{k \cdot \tau}{12}(V_{DD} - 2V_t)^3 \cdot f_{sw}$$ > where: > - **k** = μC_ox(W/L) — transconductance parameter of the transistors > - **τ** = input rise/fall time — longer τ increases the time spent in the crowbar region > - **V_t** = threshold voltage — higher V_t reduces the conduction window > - **f_sw** = switching frequency — higher frequency means more transitions per second > - The cubic dependence on (V_DD − 2V_t) shows that **P_sc grows rapidly with V_DD**. (3 marks) --- ### 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – May 2023]** **(a)** Derive the expression for short circuit power dissipation in a symmetric CMOS inverter. State clearly the assumptions made. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A symmetric CMOS inverter has k_n = k_p = 75 μA/V², V_DD = 2.5 V, V_tn = |V_tp| = 0.4 V, input rise time τ = 0.5 ns, and operates at 200 MHz. Calculate the short circuit power dissipation and compare it with the switching power if C_L = 50 fF and α = 0.5. **(7 marks)** --- ### **Model Solution for Question A:** **Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):** > **Assumptions:** > 1. Symmetric inverter: k_n = k_p = k and V_tn = |V_tp| = V_t > 2. Input is a linear ramp: V_in(t) = (V_DD/τ) · t for 0 ≤ t ≤ τ > 3. Both transistors are in saturation during the crowbar window > 4. Short circuit current has triangular shape approximation > > **Derivation:** > > The simultaneous conduction window in time-domain: > $$t_1 = \dfrac{V_t \cdot \tau}{V_{DD}}, \quad t_2 = \dfrac{(V_{DD} - V_t)\tau}{V_{DD}}$$ > > Current during this window (NMOS in saturation, current limited by it): > $$I_{sc}(t) = \dfrac{k}{2}\left(\dfrac{V_{DD} \cdot t}{\tau} - V_t\right)^2$$ > > Charge transferred: > $$Q_{sc} = \int_{t_1}^{t_2} I_{sc}(t)\, dt = \dfrac{k \cdot \tau}{6 V_{DD}}(V_{DD} - 2V_t)^3$$ > > Energy per transition: > $$E_{sc} = V_{DD} \cdot Q_{sc} = \dfrac{k \cdot \tau}{6}(V_{DD} - 2V_t)^3$$ > > For both edges (full cycle), P_sc becomes: > $$P_{sc} = \dfrac{k \cdot \tau}{12}(V_{DD} - 2V_t)^3 \cdot f_{sw} \quad \text{(1 mark each step)}$$ **Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):** > **Given:** k = 75 μA/V², V_DD = 2.5 V, V_t = 0.4 V, τ = 0.5 ns, f_sw = 200 MHz, C_L = 50 fF, α = 0.5 > > **Step 1: Compute V_DD − 2V_t** > $$V_{DD} - 2V_t = 2.5 - 0.8 = 1.7 \, \text{V}$$ > > **Step 2: Compute P_sc (per cycle form)** > $$P_{sc} = \dfrac{75 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.5 \times 10^{-9}}{12} (1.7)^3 \times 200 \times 10^6$$ > $$P_{sc} = \dfrac{37.5 \times 10^{-15}}{12} \times 4.913 \times 2 \times 10^8$$ > $$P_{sc} = 3.125 \times 10^{-15} \times 4.913 \times 2 \times 10^8$$ > $$P_{sc} = 3.07 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{W} = 3.07 \, \mu W$$ > > **Step 3: Compute P_switching** > $$P_{sw} = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f_{sw} = 0.5 \times 50 \times 10^{-15} \times (2.5)^2 \times 200 \times 10^6$$ > $$P_{sw} = 0.5 \times 50 \times 10^{-15} \times 6.25 \times 2 \times 10^8$$ > $$P_{sw} = 1.5625 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{W} = 15.625 \, \mu W$$ > > **Step 4: Comparison** > $$\dfrac{P_{sc}}{P_{sw}} = \dfrac{3.07}{15.625} \approx 0.196 \approx 19.6\%$$ > > **Conclusion:** The short circuit power is approximately **19.6% of the switching power** in this design. To reduce P_sc, we should reduce τ (use sharper input transitions) or increase V_t. (2 marks for comparison and conclusion) --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** **(a)** With a neat circuit diagram and waveform, explain the origin of short circuit current in a CMOS inverter. Mark clearly the conduction window and the peak current point. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain the dependence of short circuit power on input rise time, threshold voltage, and supply voltage. How does the short circuit power compare with the switching power in modern nanometer technologies? **(7 marks)** --- ### **Model Solution for Question B:** **Part (a) — Origin & Waveform (7 marks):** > The short circuit current in a CMOS inverter originates during the **input transition phase**, when the gate voltage V_in is neither fully 0 nor fully V_DD. (1 mark) > > **Circuit Diagram (to be drawn):** > - Standard CMOS inverter with PMOS (source at V_DD) and NMOS (source at GND) > - Both gates tied to V_in > - V_out connected to load capacitance C_L > - Mark I_sc arrow from V_DD through PMOS, through NMOS to GND > > **Waveform Description (3 marks):** > - At t = 0: V_in = 0, V_out = V_DD, I_sc = 0 > - For 0 < t < t_1: V_in rises from 0 to V_tn → NMOS starts to turn ON, PMOS fully ON > - For t_1 < t < t_2: V_tn < V_in < V_DD − V_tp → **BOTH transistors ON, I_sc flows** > - At t = τ/2 (V_in = V_DD/2): **Peak I_sc = (k/8)(V_DD − 2V_t)²** > - For t_2 < t < τ: V_in > V_DD − V_tp → PMOS turns OFF > - At t = τ: V_in = V_DD, V_out = 0, I_sc = 0 > > **Conclusion (1 mark):** The short circuit current exists only during the finite transition time and its magnitude depends on V_in, V_DD, V_t, and device sizing. **Part (b) — Parameter Dependence (7 marks):** > **1. Dependence on Input Rise Time τ (2 marks):** > - P_sc is **directly proportional** to τ. > - **Longer τ** → more time spent in the crowbar window → higher P_sc. > - **Shorter τ** → narrower crowbar exposure → lower P_sc. > - Practical limit: when τ < output rise time, P_sc becomes negligible. > > **2. Dependence on Threshold Voltage V_t (2 marks):** > - P_sc is **inversely proportional** to V_t (cubic dependence on V_DD − 2V_t). > - **Higher V_t** → narrower conduction window (V_DD − 2V_t shrinks) → less P_sc. > - **Lower V_t** → wider window → more P_sc. > - This creates a **trade-off**: low V_t is needed for speed, but increases P_sc. > > **3. Dependence on Supply Voltage V_DD (2 marks):** > - P_sc has a **cubic dependence** on (V_DD − 2V_t) and linear dependence on V_DD, giving an overall strong growth. > - **Lowering V_DD** is the most effective way to reduce P_sc. > - This is why **voltage scaling** is the primary low-power design strategy. > > **4. Comparison with Switching Power in Nanometer Technologies (1 mark):** > - In modern nanometer CMOS (e.g., 7 nm, 5 nm), V_DD is reduced to ~0.7–1.0 V. > - Consequently, **P_sc becomes a small fraction** of total dynamic power. > - **Leakage power** (sub-threshold, gate tunneling) now dominates the static budget. > - P_sc remains relevant in mixed-voltage I/O pads and analog/digital interfaces. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark-Deduction Mistakes:** > 1. **Forgetting the symmetric assumption** (k_n = k_p and V_tn = |V_tp|). KTU deducts 1 mark if this is not stated. > 2. **Using V_DD instead of (V_DD − 2V_t)** in the cubic term. This is a frequent error. > 3. **Confusing single-edge vs. full-cycle P_sc.** The factor of 2 difference (giving 1/6 vs 1/12) often appears in numerical answers. > 4. **Writing I_peak as (k/2)(V_DD/2 − V_t)² but squaring wrongly** — it should be (k/8)(V_DD − 2V_t)², not (k/2)(V_DD/2 − V_t)² without simplification. > 5. **Not specifying units** in numerical problems. Always write V, A, W explicitly. > 6. **Missing the input rise time** in problem statements — short circuit power is meaningless without τ. > 7. **Writing the conduction window as 0 < V_in < V_DD** — this is WRONG. The correct range is V_tn < V_in < V_DD − |V_tp|. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — Short Circuit Power** - **Definition:** P_sc = power wasted during the brief interval when both PMOS and NMOS conduct simultaneously. - **Trigger Condition:** V_tn < V_in < V_DD − |V_tp| (the **conduction window**). - **Peak Current (symmetric):** I_peak = (k/8)(V_DD − 2V_t)² at V_in = V_DD/2. - **Canonical Power Formula (per full cycle):** P_sc = (k·τ/12)·(V_DD − 2V_t)³·f_sw - **Single-Edge Formula:** P_sc = (k·τ/6)·(V_DD − 2V_t)³·f_sw - **Key Dependencies:** - ∝ τ (input rise time) - ∝ (V_DD − 2V_t)³ — **cubic** growth with overdrive - ∝ f_sw (switching frequency) - ∝ k = μC_ox(W/L) (transistor sizing) - **Comparison with P_switching:** P_sc = α C_L V_DD² f; typically P_sc is 10–20% of P_sw. - **When P_sc Dominates:** Slow input ramps (τ large), high V_DD, low V_t, large transistors. - **When P_sc is Negligible:** Fast ramps, low V_DD, high V_t (e.g., near-threshold designs). - **Design Strategies to Reduce P_sc:** 1. Use tapered buffer chains for sharp input edges. 2. Lower V_DD (most effective). 3. Use multi-V_t cells (high-V_t for non-critical paths). 4. Avoid long wire delays without buffering. 5. Use clock-gating to reduce switching activity. - **Mnemonic to remember formula:** "**CROW-BAR**" — **C**ubic in overdrive, **R**ise time linear, **O**verdrive = V_DD − 2V_t, **W**indow of conduction, **B**oth transistors ON, **A**verage I_peak/2, **R**amp duration τ. - **Historical Note:** P_sc was a major concern in older 5 V and 3.3 V designs. In modern sub-1 V nanometer designs, leakage power has overtaken both P_sw and P_sc as the primary power concern. - **Quick Numerical Heuristic:** For a typical 0.18 μm inverter with V_DD = 1.8 V, V_t = 0.4 V, τ = 100 ps, f = 100 MHz, W/L = 2, μC_ox ≈ 100 μA/V², the P_sc is on the order of a few μW per gate. - **Difference from Static Leakage:** P_sc is **dynamic** (transition-dependent). Leakage is **static** (always present, even when the inverter is idle). - **The Three Pillars of CMOS Power:** P_total = P_switching + P_short_circuit + P_leakage. **All three must be addressed for true low-power design.**

topics.title: Short Circuit Current Dependency on Input Rise and Fall Time
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT866 - LOW POWER VLSI
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Short Circuit Current Dependency on Input Rise and Fall Time

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In CMOS digital circuits, the **Short Circuit Current** ($I_{sc}$) is the direct current that flows from the supply rail ($V_{DD}$) to the ground rail (GND) through the simultaneous conduction paths of the **PMOS** (pull-up) and **NMOS** (pull-down) transistors during a logic transition. This phenomenon occurs exclusively when the input signal is neither a clean logic '0' nor a clean logic '1', but is sweeping through the intermediate voltage region where **both transistors are simultaneously ON** (i.e., in the saturation region).

The **dependency on input rise time ($t_r$) and fall time ($t_f$)** defines how strongly the magnitude and duration of this crowbar current are coupled to the slope (slew rate) of the input waveform. A slow-rising/falling input keeps the inverter biased in its high-gain transition region for a longer duration, thereby increasing the total short circuit energy dissipated per switching event.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** When analyzing the six sources of power dissipation in CMOS — $P_{switching}$, $P_{short\_circuit}$, $P_{leakage}$, $P_{glitching}$, $P_{direct-path}$, and $P_{static}$ — the short circuit component is the *only* one that is **parametrically controlled by the input signal slope** ($t_r$, $t_f$), making it a critical design consideration for low-power VLSI.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

Imagine a **two-door hallway** in a building — one door opens to a sunny street (the $V_{DD}$ side) and the other opens to a dark alley (the GND side). In normal operation, only one door is open at a time. But during a quick **"changing of the guard"**, both doors are momentarily open together, and people walk straight through from the sunny street to the dark alley. This wasted traffic is your **short circuit current**.

Now, the slower the guards change shifts (i.e., the longer the input rise/fall time), the **longer both doors remain open**, and **more people leak through**. Conversely, if the shift change is instantaneous (ideal step input with $t_r = t_f = 0$), the doors never overlap — no leakage traffic occurs.

| Input Slope Condition | Door Analogy | Short Circuit Current |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Very fast ($t_r \to 0$) | Instant shift change | **Zero** (no overlap) |
| Moderate ($t_r \approx t_{p}$) | Brief overlap | **Moderate** |
| Very slow ($t_r \gg t_{p}$) | Prolonged overlap | **Significant** |

## 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics Used

The key measurable parameters governing this dependency are:

- **Input rise time ($t_r$)** — Time for input to transition from 10% to 90% of $V_{DD}$ (units: **seconds**)
- **Input fall time ($t_f$)** — Time for input to transition from 90% to 10% of $V_{DD}$ (units: **seconds**)
- **Output fall time ($t_{HL}$)** — Time for output to fall from 90% to 10% of $V_{DD}$ when the load is charged/discharged by the transistors
- **Ratio parameter $\beta$** — Ratio of PMOS transconductance to NMOS transconductance, $\beta = \frac{k_p}{k_n}$
- **Threshold voltages** $V_{tn}$ and $V_{tp}$ — both **bolded** as critical design parameters

> [!NOTE]
> **Fundamental KTU Principle:** Short circuit power is *zero* only under the assumption of a **zero-rise-time (ideal step) input**. In any realistic digital system, interconnects, gate capacitances, and driver limitations ensure that $t_r$ and $t_f$ are always finite, guaranteeing some non-zero $I_{sc}$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Physics — Step-by-Step Logic

The CMOS inverter during a low-to-high input transition goes through **three distinct operating regions**:

### Region I: Initial Phase ($V_{in} < V_{tn}$)
- The **NMOS** transistor is in **cutoff** ($I_{DS,n} = 0$).
- The **PMOS** transistor is in **linear (triode)** region.
- The output node is being charged to $V_{DD}$ through the PMOS.
- **No short circuit current** flows because NMOS is OFF.

### Region II: Critical Overlap Region ($V_{tn} \leq V_{in} \leq V_{DD} - \vert V_{tp}\vert$)
- The **NMOS** enters **saturation** region.
- The **PMOS** also remains in **saturation** region.
- A direct conduction path exists from $V_{DD}$ through PMOS, through NMOS, to GND.
- **Short circuit current $I_{sc}$ reaches its peak** in this region.
- Peak current magnitude:

$$I_{peak} = \frac{\mu_n C_{ox}}{2} \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_n (V_{DD} - V_{tn})^2$$

### Region III: Final Phase ($V_{in} > V_{DD} - \vert V_{tp}\vert$)
- The **PMOS** enters **cutoff** ($I_{DS,p} = 0$).
- The **NMOS** is in **linear (triode)** region.
- The output node is being discharged to GND through the NMOS.
- **No short circuit current** flows because PMOS is OFF.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **duration of Region II** is what couples $I_{sc}$ to $t_r$ and $t_f$. If the input ramps slowly, the inverter lingers in Region II for a longer absolute time, increasing the integrated $I_{sc}$ charge and hence the **short circuit energy** $E_{sc} = \int V_{DD} \cdot I_{sc} \, dt$.

## 2.2 Boundary Conditions for Short Circuit Current

The KTU curriculum recognizes **three canonical regimes** for $I_{sc}$ behavior based on the ratio of input slope to output slope:

| Regime | Condition | Short Circuit Current | Engineering Implication |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **A — No Short Circuit** | $t_r \ll t_{HL}$ and $t_f \ll t_{LH}$ | $I_{sc} \approx 0$ | Ideal case; sharp inputs |
| **B — Linear Dependence** | $t_r \approx t_{HL}$, $t_f \approx t_{LH}$ | $I_{sc} \propto t_r$, $t_f$ | Realistic case; matched slopes |
| **C — Saturated (Max)** | $t_r \gg t_{HL}$ and $t_f \gg t_{LH}$ | $I_{sc}$ reaches **maximum plateau** | Worst case; very slow inputs |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Boundary Condition |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $I_{sc,avg} = \dfrac{1}{t_r} \int_0^{t_r} I_{sc}(t) \, dt$ | Time-averaged short circuit current | General |
| 2 | $E_{sc} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{sc,peak} \cdot t_{overlap}$ | Total short circuit energy per cycle | Linear approximation |
| 3 | $I_{sc,peak} = \dfrac{\beta}{2} (V_{DD} - 2V_t)^2$ | Peak short circuit current (matched) | $\beta = k_p/k_n$, symmetric |
| 4 | $P_{sc} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{sc,avg} \cdot f_{clk}$ | Short circuit power | Per cycle integration |
| 5 | $t_{overlap} = \dfrac{(V_{DD} - 2V_t)}{V_{DD}} \cdot t_r$ | Time spent in Region II | For low-to-high transition |
| 6 | $I_{sc,avg} = \dfrac{\beta}{12} \cdot (V_{DD} - 2V_t)^3 \cdot \dfrac{t_r}{V_{DD}}$ | Avg $I_{sc}$ assuming linear input ramp | Slow input regime |
| 7 | $P_{sc,total} = \dfrac{\beta \cdot f_{clk}}{12} \cdot (V_{DD} - 2V_t)^3 \cdot (t_r + t_f)$ | Full short circuit power | Symmetric inverter |

> [!WARNING]
> **Absolute Value Notation:** In all KTU examinations and answer scripts, use $\vert V_{tp}\vert$ notation — **never** use the bare vertical pipe `|Vtp|` inside markdown tables, as it breaks the table parser. Always escape using `\vert` or `\mid`.

## 2.4 Why This Matters in Real VLSI Engineering

In modern deep-submicron designs (45 nm, 28 nm, 7 nm, 5 nm), the input signal slope to a gate is **never** a perfect step function. It is shaped by:

- **Driver gate strength** (previous stage's $W/L$)
- **Wire RC interconnect** loading
- **Fan-out capacitance** of the load
- **Supply voltage noise** and ground bounce

If a slow input slope is presented to a large buffer chain, the cumulative short circuit power can rival or **exceed** the dynamic switching power — violating low-power budgets in battery-powered IoT, mobile SoCs, and biomedical implants. This is why KTU emphasizes this topic: it directly links **input signal integrity** to **system-level power dissipation**.

## 2.5 The Critical Relationship: Input Slope vs. Short Circuit Energy

The mathematical heart of this topic is the recognition that:

$$E_{sc} \propto (V_{DD} - 2V_t)^3 \cdot \left(\frac{t_r + t_f}{2}\right)$$

This **cubic dependence on voltage** and **linear dependence on input slope** is a direct exam-favorite relationship in KTU Board Examinations.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Peak Short Circuit Current

**Starting Point:** The CMOS inverter with a slowly ramping input $V_{in}(t) = \frac{V_{DD}}{t_r} \cdot t$.

**Step 1: Identify the region of interest.**

The short circuit path is active only when **both transistors are in saturation**, which requires:

$$V_{tn} \leq V_{in} \leq V_{DD} - \vert V_{tp}\vert$$

Assuming a symmetric inverter where $V_{tn} = \vert V_{tp}\vert = V_t$, this window becomes:

$$V_t \leq V_{in} \leq V_{DD} - V_t$$

**Step 2: Write the saturation current equation for the NMOS.**

The NMOS drain current (source at GND) in saturation is:

$$I_{DS,n}^{sat} = \frac{\mu_n C_{ox}}{2} \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_n (V_{GS} - V_{tn})^2$$

Substituting $V_{GS} = V_{in}$ and $V_{tn} = V_t$:

$$I_{DS,n}^{sat} = \frac{\mu_n C_{ox}}{2} \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_n (V_{in} - V_t)^2$$

**Step 3: Equate PMOS and NMOS currents (KCL at output node).**

Since the output is held approximately at $V_{DD}/2$ in the middle of transition, the current through the PMOS equals that through the NMOS (both in saturation). By KCL:

$$I_{sc} = I_{DS,n}^{sat} = I_{DS,p}^{sat}$$

**Step 4: Compute the peak current at $V_{in} = V_{DD}/2$.**

At the midpoint of transition, the maximum gate overdrive is achieved:

$$V_{GS} - V_t = \frac{V_{DD}}{2} - V_t = \frac{V_{DD} - 2V_t}{2}$$

Squaring this overdrive:

$$(V_{GS} - V_t)^2 = \frac{(V_{DD} - 2V_t)^2}{4}$$

**Step 5: Substitute back to obtain peak current.**

$$I_{sc,peak} = \frac{\mu_n C_{ox}}{2} \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_n \cdot \frac{(V_{DD} - 2V_t)^2}{4}$$

This simplifies to:

$$I_{sc,peak} = \frac{\mu_n C_{ox}}{8} \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_n (V_{DD} - 2V_t)^2$$

**Step 6: Introduce the $\beta$ ratio (PMOS/NMOS strength ratio).**

For a **symmetric inverter** where the transconductance parameter is matched ($\mu_n C_{ox} (W/L)_n = \mu_p C_{ox} (W/L)_p = K$), the peak current expression can be written compactly as:

$$I_{sc,peak} = \frac{\beta}{2} (V_{DD} - 2V_t)^2$$

where $\beta = \frac{k_p}{k_n} = \frac{\mu_p C_{ox} (W/L)_p}{\mu_n C_{ox} (W/L)_n}$.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Time-Averaged Short Circuit Current for Linear Input Ramp

**Starting Point:** Assume $V_{in}(t) = \frac{V_{DD} \cdot t}{t_r}$ for a low-to-high transition.

**Step 1: Express the instantaneous $I_{sc}$ as a function of time.**

In Region II, the short circuit current is approximately (using saturation approximation):

$$I_{sc}(t) = K_n (V_{in}(t) - V_t)^2 = K_n \left(\frac{V_{DD} \cdot t}{t_r} - V_t\right)^2$$

**Step 2: Determine the integration limits.**

The overlap region begins at $V_{in} = V_t$, which corresponds to:

$$t_1 = \frac{V_t \cdot t_r}{V_{DD}}$$

The overlap region ends at $V_{in} = V_{DD} - V_t$, which corresponds to:

$$t_2 = \frac{(V_{DD} - V_t) \cdot t_r}{V_{DD}}$$

**Step 3: Compute the total charge delivered (integral of current).**

$$Q_{sc} = \int_{t_1}^{t_2} I_{sc}(t) \, dt = K_n \int_{t_1}^{t_2} \left(\frac{V_{DD} \cdot t}{t_r} - V_t\right)^2 dt$$

**Step 4: Perform the substitution $u = \frac{V_{DD} \cdot t}{t_r} - V_t$.**

Then $du = \frac{V_{DD}}{t_r} dt$, hence $dt = \frac{t_r}{V_{DD}} du$.

The new limits are $u_1 = 0$ and $u_2 = V_{DD} - 2V_t$.

$$Q_{sc} = K_n \cdot \frac{t_r}{V_{DD}} \int_{0}^{V_{DD} - 2V_t} u^2 \, du$$

**Step 5: Evaluate the polynomial integral.**

$$\int_{0}^{V_{DD} - 2V_t} u^2 \, du = \frac{(V_{DD} - 2V_t)^3}{3}$$

**Step 6: Substitute back and compute the average current.**

The average current over the full transition period $t_r$ is:

$$I_{sc,avg} = \frac{Q_{sc}}{t_r} = \frac{K_n \cdot (V_{DD} - 2V_t)^3}{3 \cdot V_{DD}}$$

**Step 7: Introduce the symmetric inverter $\beta$ factor.**

For a symmetric design, $K_n = K$ and using $\beta$ as the ratio:

$$\boxed{I_{sc,avg} = \frac{\beta \cdot (V_{DD} - 2V_t)^3}{12 \cdot V_{DD}} \cdot t_r \cdot f_{clk}}$$

This is the **canonical KTU 2024 formula** for average short circuit current in a symmetric CMOS inverter driven by a linear ramp input.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Total Short Circuit Power Per Clock Cycle

**Step 1: Combine both transitions (low-to-high and high-to-low).**

For symmetric rise and fall times, $t_r = t_f$:

$$P_{sc} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{sc,avg} \cdot f_{clk} \cdot 2$$

The factor of 2 accounts for both edges of the clock cycle.

**Step 2: Substitute the average current expression.**

$$P_{sc} = V_{DD} \cdot \frac{\beta \cdot (V_{DD} - 2V_t)^3}{12 \cdot V_{DD}} \cdot (t_r + t_f) \cdot f_{clk}$$

**Step 3: Simplify the expression.**

The $V_{DD}$ in numerator and denominator cancel:

$$\boxed{P_{sc} = \frac{\beta \cdot f_{clk}}{12} \cdot (V_{DD} - 2V_t)^3 \cdot (t_r + t_f)}$$

**Step 4: Key observations from the formula.**

- $P_{sc}$ is **linear** in clock frequency $f_{clk}$.
- $P_{sc}$ is **linear** in input slope $(t_r + t_f)$.
- $P_{sc}$ has a **cubic** dependence on the effective voltage swing $(V_{DD} - 2V_t)$.
- $P_{sc}$ is **proportional to $\beta$**, meaning sizing imbalances directly affect short circuit power.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination Insight:** KTU frequently asks students to compare the **order of magnitude** of $P_{sc}$ with $P_{switching} = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f_{clk}$. A common exam question is: "For a given clock frequency, what is the condition under which $P_{sc}$ dominates $P_{switching}$?" The answer hinges on the input slope being comparable to or greater than the output load time constant.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 CMOS Inverter Schematic with Short Circuit Path

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[VDD Supply Rail] --> B[PMOS Source]
    B --> C[PMOS Drain]
    C --> D[Output Node Vout]
    D --> E[NMOS Drain]
    E --> F[NMOS Source]
    F --> G[GND Rail]
    H[Input Signal Vin with tr and tf] --> I[PMOS Gate]
    H --> J[NMOS Gate]
    C -. Short Circuit Current Isc .-> D
    D -. Short Circuit Current Isc .-> E
    K[Capacitive Load CL] --- D

    classDef supplyStyle fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#c92a2a,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef pmosStyle fill:#4dabf7,stroke:#1864ab,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef nmosStyle fill:#51cf66,stroke:#2b8a3e,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef ioStyle fill:#ffd43b,stroke:#f08c00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef loadStyle fill:#e599f7,stroke:#862e9c,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff

    class A,G supplyStyle
    class B,C,I pmosStyle
    class E,F,J nmosStyle
    class D,H ioStyle
    class K loadStyle
```

**Description:** This block diagram shows the canonical CMOS inverter topology with the input signal $V_{in}$ (characterized by rise time $t_r$ and fall time $t_f$) driving the gates of the PMOS and NMOS transistors. The red dashed line indicates the direct current path from $V_{DD}$ to GND that exists **only during the input transition window** when both transistors are simultaneously conducting.

## 4.2 Timing Diagram: Input Slope vs. Short Circuit Current

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph TIMING[Timing Relationship During Transition]
        T1[Input Vin Rising with finite tr]
        T2[Region I: Vin less than Vtn, NMOS OFF]
        T3[Region II: Both ON, Isc PEAKS]
        T4[Region III: Vin greater than VDD minus Vtp, PMOS OFF]
        T5[Isc Pulse with width proportional to tr]
        T6[Output Vout Falling with delay tHL]
    end

    T1 --> T2
    T2 --> T3
    T3 --> T4
    T3 --> T5
    T5 --> T6

    classDef regionStyle fill:#74c0fc,stroke:#1971c2,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef peakStyle fill:#ff8787,stroke:#e03131,stroke-width:3px,color:#fff
    classDef inputStyle fill:#ffd8a8,stroke:#e8590c,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class T2,T4 regionStyle
    class T3,T5 peakStyle
    class T1,T6 inputStyle
```

**Description:** This timing flow visualizes the three operating regions traversed by the inverter as the input ramps from 0 to $V_{DD}$. The **central Region II** is the critical window where short circuit current flows. The **width of the $I_{sc}$ pulse** is directly proportional to $t_r$, demonstrating the linear dependency of short circuit charge on input rise time.

## 4.3 Architectural Block Diagram: Short Circuit Power Estimation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Define Input Signal] --> B{Input has finite tr and tf?}
    B -->|No, ideal step| C[Isc equals 0, No Short Circuit Power]
    B -->|Yes, realistic ramp| D[Calculate Voltage Window VDD minus 2Vt]
    D --> E[Compute Peak Isc using beta and Vt]
    E --> F[Compute Overlap Time toverlap proportional to tr]
    F --> G[Integrate Isc over toverlap to find Charge Qsc]
    G --> H[Compute Energy Esc equals VDD times Qsc]
    H --> I[Multiply by fclk to get Power Psc]
    I --> J[Add Psc to Pswitching for Total Pdynamic]
    J --> K[End: Report Psc Component]

    classDef startEndStyle fill:#b2f2bb,stroke:#2f9e44,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef decisionStyle fill:#ffec99,stroke:#f08c00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef computeStyle fill:#a5d8ff,stroke:#1971c2,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef outputStyle fill:#ffc9c9,stroke:#e03131,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class A,K startEndStyle
    class B decisionStyle
    class D,E,F,G,H,I,J computeStyle
    class C outputStyle
```

**Description:** This block-level functional flow illustrates the complete estimation procedure for short circuit power in a CMOS gate, starting from the input signal characterization and ending with the total power budget. Each block represents a computational step in the standard KTU analytical methodology.

## 4.4 Comparative Regime Diagram

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph R1[Regime A: tr less than tHL]
        R1A[Sharp Input]
        R1B[Isc approx 0]
        R1C[Total Power approx Pswitching]
    end

    subgraph R2[Regime B: tr approx equal to tHL]
        R2A[Matched Slopes]
        R2B[Isc moderate]
        R2C[Total Power approx Pswitching plus Psc]
    end

    subgraph R3[Regime C: tr much greater than tHL]
        R3A[Sluggish Input]
        R3B[Isc max plateau reached]
        R3C[Psc can dominate Pswitching]
    end

    R1 --> R2
    R2 --> R3

    classDef regimeAStyle fill:#d3f9d8,stroke:#2f9e44,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef regimeBStyle fill:#fff3bf,stroke:#f08c00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef regimeCStyle fill:#ffe3e3,stroke:#c92a2a,stroke-width:3px,color:#000

    class R1A,R1B,R1C regimeAStyle
    class R2A,R2B,R2C regimeBStyle
    class R3A,R3B,R3C regimeCStyle
```

**Description:** The three operating regimes are visualized with color-coded severity: **green** (safe, negligible $I_{sc}$), **yellow** (caution, moderate $I_{sc}$), and **red** (danger, $I_{sc}$ dominates total power). This diagram is directly applicable to **KTU design viva questions** where students must identify under what conditions a low-power design remains truly low-power.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Q: Define short circuit current in a CMOS inverter. Why is it dependent on the input rise and fall time?**

**Model Answer:**

Short circuit current ($I_{sc}$) is the direct current that flows from $V_{DD}$ to GND through the CMOS inverter when both the PMOS and NMOS transistors are simultaneously ON during an input logic transition. This occurs when $V_{in}$ lies in the voltage range $V_{tn} \leq V_{in} \leq V_{DD} - \vert V_{tp}\vert$.

The dependency on input rise time ($t_r$) and fall time ($t_f$) arises because the **duration of the simultaneous conduction window** is directly proportional to the input slope. A slow input ramp keeps the inverter in its high-gain transition region longer, increasing the integrated $I_{sc}$ charge and hence the short circuit energy $E_{sc}$. Conversely, a sharp step input ($t_r \to 0$) results in near-zero overlap and negligible $I_{sc}$.

**Key formula cited:**

$$E_{sc} \propto (V_{DD} - 2V_t)^3 \cdot (t_r + t_f)$$

**(3 Marks — CO2, Bloom Level: Remember/Understand)**

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Q: State the condition under which short circuit power becomes comparable to switching power in a CMOS inverter.**

**Model Answer:**

Short circuit power ($P_{sc}$) becomes comparable to switching power ($P_{switching} = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f$) when the **input rise/fall time is comparable to or greater than the output load time constant**, i.e., when:

$$t_r \geq \beta \cdot \frac{C_L}{\mu_n C_{ox} (W/L)_n (V_{DD} - V_t)}$$

Equivalently, when the input slope is so slow that the output node tracks the input during transition, both transistors remain in saturation for a significant fraction of the clock period. Under this condition, the short circuit energy per cycle can dominate the dynamic energy budget, making low-power design strategies focused solely on $C_L$ reduction insufficient.

**(3 Marks — CO2, Bloom Level: Understand/Apply)**

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — Option 1 (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q: (a)** With the help of a circuit diagram and timing waveforms, explain the mechanism of short circuit current flow in a CMOS inverter during a low-to-high input transition. Clearly identify the three operating regions and indicate where the direct conduction path from $V_{DD}$ to GND exists. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A symmetric CMOS inverter is operating at $V_{DD} = 3.3\text{ V}$ with $V_{tn} = \vert V_{tp}\vert = 0.5\text{ V}$. The input rise time is $t_r = 2\text{ ns}$ and the clock frequency is $f_{clk} = 100\text{ MHz}$. Assuming $\beta = 1$ (matched PMOS/NMOS), compute: (i) the peak short circuit current, (ii) the time-averaged short circuit current, and (iii) the total short circuit power dissipated per clock cycle. Take $t_f = t_r$ for symmetric switching. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

**Region Analysis (7 marks breakdown):**

1. **Region I ($V_{in} < V_{tn} = 0.5\text{ V}$):** NMOS is in cutoff, PMOS is in linear region charging the load capacitance $C_L$ to $V_{DD}$. No $I_{sc}$ flows. **(2 marks)**

2. **Region II ($0.5 \text{ V} \leq V_{in} \leq 2.8 \text{ V}$):** Both PMOS and NMOS are in **saturation region**. A direct current path from $V_{DD}$ to GND exists through both transistors. The short circuit current reaches its **peak value** when $V_{in} = V_{DD}/2 = 1.65\text{ V}$. **(3 marks)**

3. **Region III ($V_{in} > 2.8\text{ V}$):** PMOS enters cutoff, NMOS enters linear region discharging $C_L$ to GND. No $I_{sc}$ flows. **(2 marks)**

**Timing Waveform Description:** The $I_{sc}$ pulse has a **trapezoidal shape** with width approximately equal to $t_{overlap} = \frac{V_{DD} - 2V_t}{V_{DD}} \cdot t_r$.

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

**Given:** $V_{DD} = 3.3\text{ V}$, $V_t = 0.5\text{ V}$, $t_r = t_f = 2\text{ ns}$, $f_{clk} = 100\text{ MHz}$, $\beta = 1$.

**(i) Peak Short Circuit Current:**

$$I_{sc,peak} = \frac{\beta}{2} (V_{DD} - 2V_t)^2$$

Substituting values:

$$V_{DD} - 2V_t = 3.3 - 2(0.5) = 3.3 - 1.0 = 2.3\text{ V}$$

$$I_{sc,peak} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot (2.3)^2 = \frac{5.29}{2} = 2.645\text{ A (normalized to device K)}$$

*(In practice, the actual current is $I_{sc,peak} = K \cdot 2.645$ where $K = \frac{1}{2}\mu_n C_{ox}(W/L)_n$.)* **(2 marks)**

**(ii) Time-Averaged Short Circuit Current:**

$$I_{sc,avg} = \frac{\beta \cdot (V_{DD} - 2V_t)^3 \cdot t_r \cdot f_{clk}}{12 \cdot V_{DD}}$$

$$I_{sc,avg} = \frac{1 \cdot (2.3)^3 \cdot (2 \times 10^{-9}) \cdot (100 \times 10^6)}{12 \cdot 3.3}$$

$$(2.3)^3 = 12.167$$

Numerator:

$$12.167 \cdot 2 \times 10^{-9} \cdot 10^8 = 12.167 \cdot 0.2 = 2.4334$$

Denominator:

$$12 \cdot 3.3 = 39.6$$

$$I_{sc,avg} = \frac{2.4334}{39.6} = 0.0615\text{ A (normalized to K)} = 61.5\text{ mA (normalized)}$$ **(3 marks)**

**(iii) Total Short Circuit Power:**

$$P_{sc} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{sc,avg}$$

$$P_{sc} = 3.3 \cdot 0.0615 = 0.203\text{ W (normalized to K)}$$

If the device transconductance $K = 1\text{ mA/V}^2$, then actual $P_{sc} = 0.203\text{ mW}$ per gate. **(2 marks)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students frequently make the following mistakes in such numerical problems:
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2** for both edges (low-to-high AND high-to-low) in the final power formula — this causes a 50% undervaluation.
> 2. **Using $V_{DD}$ instead of $(V_{DD} - 2V_t)$** in the peak current formula — this is the most common error and loses **2-3 marks** instantly.
> 3. **Mixing up units** of $t_r$ (ns vs. s) and $f_{clk}$ (MHz vs. Hz) — always convert to SI base units first.
> 4. **Omitting the $\beta$ factor** when the inverter is **not** symmetric.

---

### Question B — Option 2 (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q: (a)** Derive the expression for the average short circuit current in a symmetric CMOS inverter driven by a linear input ramp. Clearly state all assumptions used. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Consider a CMOS inverter chain driving a large capacitive load $C_L = 5\text{ pF}$ at $V_{DD} = 2.5\text{ V}$ with $V_t = 0.4\text{ V}$ and $f_{clk} = 250\text{ MHz}$. The final stage has an NMOS transconductance parameter $k_n = 100\text{ }\mu\text{A/V}^2$ and a width-to-length ratio $(W/L)_n = 50$. Calculate: (i) the output fall time $t_{HL}$ of the final stage, and (ii) determine whether the short circuit power is negligible, significant, or dominant for an input rise time of $t_r = 1.5\text{ ns}$. Justify with calculations. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

**Assumptions:**

1. The CMOS inverter is **symmetric** ($\beta = k_p/k_n = 1$).
2. The input is a **linear ramp** $V_{in}(t) = (V_{DD}/t_r) \cdot t$.
3. The output node capacitance $C_L$ is large enough that the output voltage remains approximately constant at $V_{DD}/2$ during the overlap window.
4. Short channel effects, leakage, and body effect are neglected.

**Derivation Steps:**

**Step 1:** Saturation current of NMOS during the overlap region:

$$I_{DS,n} = \frac{1}{2} k_n \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_n (V_{in}(t) - V_t)^2$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the linear ramp $V_{in}(t) = \frac{V_{DD}}{t_r} t$:

$$I_{sc}(t) = \frac{1}{2} k_n \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_n \left(\frac{V_{DD}}{t_r} t - V_t\right)^2$$

**Step 3:** The overlap window is $V_t \leq V_{in} \leq V_{DD} - V_t$, which corresponds to time limits $t_1 = \frac{V_t t_r}{V_{DD}}$ and $t_2 = \frac{(V_{DD} - V_t) t_r}{V_{DD}}$.

**Step 4:** Charge delivered during overlap (using substitution $u = V_{in} - V_t$):

$$Q_{sc} = \int_{t_1}^{t_2} I_{sc}(t) dt = \frac{1}{2} k_n \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_n \cdot \frac{t_r}{V_{DD}} \int_0^{V_{DD} - 2V_t} u^2 du$$

**Step 5:** Evaluating the integral:

$$Q_{sc} = \frac{1}{2} k_n \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_n \cdot \frac{t_r}{V_{DD}} \cdot \frac{(V_{DD} - 2V_t)^3}{3}$$

**Step 6:** Average current over one transition $t_r$:

$$I_{sc,avg} = \frac{Q_{sc}}{t_r} = \frac{k_n (W/L)_n (V_{DD} - 2V_t)^3}{6 V_{DD}}$$

**Step 7:** Including $\beta$ for general case and both transitions:

$$\boxed{I_{sc,avg}^{total} = \frac{\beta \cdot k_n (W/L)_n (V_{DD} - 2V_t)^3 \cdot (t_r + t_f) \cdot f_{clk}}{12 V_{DD}}}$$

**(7 Marks — CO3, Bloom Level: Apply/Analyze)**

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

**Given:** $C_L = 5\text{ pF}$, $V_{DD} = 2.5\text{ V}$, $V_t = 0.4\text{ V}$, $f_{clk} = 250\text{ MHz}$, $k_n = 100\text{ }\mu\text{A/V}^2$, $(W/L)_n = 50$, $t_r = 1.5\text{ ns}$.

**(i) Output Fall Time $t_{HL}$:**

For a CMOS inverter discharging a capacitive load, the output fall time is approximately:

$$t_{HL} \approx \frac{C_L}{k_n (W/L)_n (V_{DD} - V_t)}$$

Substituting:

$$k_n (W/L)_n = 100 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 50 = 5 \times 10^{-3}\text{ A/V}^2$$

$$V_{DD} - V_t = 2.5 - 0.4 = 2.1\text{ V}$$

$$t_{HL} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-12}}{5 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 2.1} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-12}}{10.5 \times 10^{-3}} = 4.76 \times 10^{-10}\text{ s} = 0.476\text{ ns}$$ **(3 marks)**

**(ii) Short Circuit Power Analysis:**

Compare $t_r$ with $t_{HL}$:

$$\frac{t_r}{t_{HL}} = \frac{1.5\text{ ns}}{0.476\text{ ns}} = 3.15$$

Since $t_r / t_{HL} \approx 3.15 > 1$, the input rise time is **slower** than the output fall time. This places the inverter in **Regime B (significant short circuit current)** as the input is still in the transition region when the output has already switched.

**Quantitative Calculation of $P_{sc}$:**

$$I_{sc,avg} = \frac{\beta \cdot k_n (W/L)_n (V_{DD} - 2V_t)^3 \cdot (t_r + t_f) \cdot f_{clk}}{12 V_{DD}}$$

$$V_{DD} - 2V_t = 2.5 - 0.8 = 1.7\text{ V}$$

$$(1.7)^3 = 4.913$$

Assuming $t_f = t_r = 1.5\text{ ns}$:

$$I_{sc,avg} = \frac{1 \cdot 5 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 4.913 \cdot (3 \times 10^{-9}) \cdot (2.5 \times 10^8)}{12 \cdot 2.5}$$

Numerator:

$$5 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 4.913 \cdot 3 \times 10^{-9} \cdot 2.5 \times 10^8 = 5 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 4.913 \cdot 0.75 = 0.01842$$

Denominator:

$$30$$

$$I_{sc,avg} = \frac{0.01842}{30} = 6.14 \times 10^{-4}\text{ A} = 0.614\text{ mA}$$

$$P_{sc} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{sc,avg} = 2.5 \cdot 0.614 = 1.535\text{ mW}$$

**Switching Power for comparison:**

$$P_{switching} = C_L V_{DD}^2 f_{clk} = 5 \times 10^{-12} \cdot (2.5)^2 \cdot 2.5 \times 10^8 = 7.81\text{ }\mu\text{W}$$

**Comparison:** $P_{sc} = 1.535\text{ mW}$ vs. $P_{switching} = 7.81\text{ }\mu\text{W}$.

**Conclusion:** $P_{sc}$ is approximately **196 times larger** than $P_{switching}$. The short circuit power is **DOMINANT** in this scenario, and the design violates the low-power design intent. The slow input rise time (1.5 ns) combined with the fast output stage (0.476 ns) creates a prolonged overlap window. **(4 marks)**

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Valuation Trap:** Many students calculate $P_{sc}$ but forget to **compare** it with $P_{switching}$. The question explicitly asks for a **justification** — a numerical value of $P_{sc}$ alone is insufficient. KTU examiners deduct **2 marks** for missing the comparison step. Additionally, students often incorrectly apply the formula assuming $\beta = 1$ when the problem does not state symmetric design — always re-verify the assumption.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exams:**

- **Definition:** $I_{sc}$ flows only when **both PMOS and NMOS are simultaneously ON** during input transition. **(High Frequency Question)**

- **Critical Voltage Window:** Short circuit path exists for $V_{tn} \leq V_{in} \leq V_{DD} - \vert V_{tp}\vert$. **(Always quote this range explicitly)**

- **Peak Current Formula:** $I_{sc,peak} = \frac{\beta}{2} (V_{DD} - 2V_t)^2$ — symmetric inverter, evaluated at $V_{in} = V_{DD}/2$.

- **Average Current Formula:** $I_{sc,avg} = \frac{\beta (V_{DD} - 2V_t)^3 t_r f_{clk}}{12 V_{DD}}$ — assumes **linear ramp input**.

- **Power Formula:** $P_{sc} = \frac{\beta f_{clk}}{12} (V_{DD} - 2V_t)^3 (t_r + t_f)$ — **memorize this verbatim**.

- **Three Regimes:** 
  - $t_r \ll t_{HL}$: **No $I_{sc}$** (ideal)
  - $t_r \approx t_{HL}$: **Linear dependence** on $t_r$ (realistic)
  - $t_r \gg t_{HL}$: **Saturated maximum** (worst case)

- **Cubic Voltage Dependence:** $P_{sc} \propto (V_{DD} - 2V_t)^3$ — this is why **voltage scaling** is the most effective way to suppress short circuit power.

- **Design Rule of Thumb:** To minimize $I_{sc}$, ensure the input slope is **steeper** than the output slope. This is achieved by **tapering** the buffer chain (each successive stage is sized 2-4x larger than the previous one).

- **Common Pitfall:** Short circuit power is often **ignored** in textbook examples assuming step inputs, but in **all real silicon**, it must be accounted for, especially at the **90 nm node and below** where $V_{DD} \approx 1.0\text{ V}$ and $V_t \approx 0.3\text{ V}$ make $(V_{DD} - 2V_t)$ very small, effectively suppressing $I_{sc}$ in modern designs.

- **Units Vigilance:** $t_r$ in **seconds** (not ns), $f_{clk}$ in **Hz** (not MHz), $C_L$ in **Farads** (not pF) — convert before substituting.

- **Symmetric vs. Asymmetric:** The $\beta$ factor appears **only** in the general formula. Set $\beta = 1$ only when the problem explicitly states a **symmetric** or **matched** inverter.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Variation of Short Circuit Current with Load Capacitance. Switching Power Dissipation: Switching Power of CMOS Inverter
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT866 - LOW POWER VLSI
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Variation of Short Circuit Current with Load Capacitance. Switching Power Dissipation: Switching Power of CMOS Inverter

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Short Circuit Current ($I_{sc}$) in CMOS Inverter

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (2024 Scheme):**
> The **Short Circuit Current** (also called **Crowbar Current** or **Shoot-Through Current**) in a CMOS inverter is the direct current that flows from the supply rail ($V_{DD}$) to the ground rail ($GND$) through the simultaneously ON PMOS and NMOS transistors during the finite duration of input signal transition (slew), when neither transistor is fully cut-off or fully ON in saturation.

**Physical Origin:** During the input transition from logic `0` to logic `1` (or vice versa), there exists a brief time window where the input voltage $V_{in}$ lies in the region between $V_{tn}$ (NMOS threshold) and $V_{DD} - \vert V_{tp} \vert$ (PMOS threshold). In this window:
- **NMOS** is in saturation (partially ON)
- **PMOS** is also in saturation (partially ON)
- A direct low-resistance path exists from $V_{DD} \to PMOS \to NMOS \to GND$

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Real-World Analogy:**
> Imagine a **two-gate water pipeline** between an overhead tank ($V_{DD}$) and a drain ($GND$). When the upstream gate (input) is being slowly rotated, **both inner gates stay partially open simultaneously**, allowing water to leak directly from tank to drain without doing any useful work. The faster the upstream rotation, the less leakage. This wasted flow is the **short circuit current**.

### 1.2 Switching Power Dissipation ($P_{switch}$)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (2024 Scheme):**
> **Switching Power Dissipation** is the dynamic power consumed in a CMOS inverter due to the charging and discharging of the load capacitance ($C_L$) at the output node during logic transitions. It is the dominant component of total power dissipation in modern digital CMOS circuits.

**Key Insight:** Even though CMOS logic is theoretically "static power-free" (no current flows in steady state), power is consumed every time the output node capacitance is charged to $V_{DD}$ or discharged to $GND$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Real-World Analogy:**
> Think of the output capacitance $C_L$ as a **small water bucket** at the inverter output. Every time the inverter switches:
> 1. **Charging phase** → The PMOS "lifts" the bucket full of charge from $GND$ to $V_{DD}$. Work is done against gravity (energy dissipated in PMOS resistance).
> 2. **Discharging phase** → The NMOS empties the bucket back to $GND$. The stored charge is wasted as heat in the NMOS resistance.
> This "fill-and-empty" work, repeated millions of times per second, is the switching power.

### 1.3 Variation of $I_{sc}$ with Load Capacitance ($C_L$)

The relationship is **inverse and critical for low-power design**:

| Load Capacitance Condition | Short Circuit Current Behavior |
|---------------------------|--------------------------------|
| $C_L \to 0$ (No load) | **Maximum** $I_{sc}$ — Output switches instantly, input has full time to leak |
| Small $C_L$ | **High** $I_{sc}$ — Output transition much faster than input |
| Large $C_L$ | **Low** $I_{sc}$ — Input completes transition before output can respond |
| $C_L \to \infty$ | $I_{sc} \to 0$ — Input transition is infinitely fast compared to output |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Does $I_{sc}$ Depend on $C_L$? — The Time-Ratio Argument

The short circuit current depends fundamentally on the **ratio of input slew rate to output slew rate**.

**Step 1: Define the input rise/fall time ($\tau_{in}$)**
- Determined by the driving stage's strength and output impedance.
- Independent of $C_L$ at the load of the current inverter.

**Step 2: Define the output rise/fall time ($\tau_{out}$)**
- Given by: $\tau_{out} = \frac{C_L \cdot V_{DD}}{I_{driver}}$
- Directly proportional to $C_L$.

**Step 3: Cross-coupled relationship**
- If $C_L$ is **small** → $\tau_{out}$ is small → input has "more time" relative to output to leak current → **higher $I_{sc}$**.
- If $C_L$ is **large** → $\tau_{out}$ is large → input finishes its transition quickly → **lower $I_{sc}$**.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **frequently asked** in KTU 2024 Scheme End Semester Examinations. Memorize the table thoroughly.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Description |
|---|-----------|---------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | Energy stored in capacitor | $E_{stored} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$ | Joules (J) | Energy retained in $C_L$ at full charge |
| 2 | Energy supplied by $V_{DD}$ source | $E_{source} = C_L V_{DD}^2$ | Joules (J) | Total energy drawn from supply |
| 3 | Energy dissipated per transition (in switch) | $E_{diss} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$ | Joules (J) | Heat lost in ON transistor |
| 4 | Switching Power Dissipation | $P_{switch} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f_{clk}$ | Watts (W) | Dominant dynamic power |
| 5 | Total Energy per Clock Cycle | $E_{cycle} = C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2$ | Joules (J) | Charge-discharge full cycle energy |
| 6 | Short Circuit Power (approx) | $P_{sc} \approx \frac{\beta}{12} \left(V_{DD} - 2V_T\right)^3 \cdot \tau_{in} \cdot f_{clk}$ | Watts (W) | Function of input slew time |
| 7 | Ratio condition for $I_{sc}$ impact | $\frac{\tau_{in}}{\tau_{out}} = \frac{\tau_{in} \cdot I_{driver}}{C_L \cdot V_{DD}}$ | Dimensionless | Determines short circuit magnitude |
| 8 | Switching Activity Factor | $\alpha = \frac{\text{Number of transitions}}{\text{Total clock cycles}}$ | Unitless | 0 to 1, $\alpha = 0.5$ for random data |

> **Notation Key:** $\alpha$ = switching activity factor, $C_L$ = load capacitance, $V_{DD}$ = supply voltage, $f_{clk}$ = clock frequency, $\tau_{in}$ = input transition time, $\tau_{out}$ = output transition time, $\beta$ = transconductance parameter, $V_T$ = threshold voltage.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Mobile/IoT SoC Design**: Engineers deliberately add **dummy load capacitance** to gates driving large fan-out networks to suppress $I_{sc}$ and reduce total dynamic power.
- **High-Speed Microprocessors**: The switching power equation $P = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f$ drives the industry-wide shift to **lower $V_{DD}$** (e.g., from 5V → 1.0V) since power has **quadratic dependence** on $V_{DD}$.
- **Clock Tree Synthesis (CTS)**: In ASICs, clock networks are the largest $C_L$ contributors; the formula justifies aggressive **clock gating** to reduce $\alpha$ for clock nets.
- **Battery-Powered Devices**: Sleep-mode transistors cut off $C_L$ charging paths to set $\alpha \approx 0$, validating the formula for ultra-low-power applications.

### 2.4 The Three Regimes of $C_L$ Impact on $I_{sc}$

**Regime 1: $C_L = 0$ (Pure Short Circuit)**
- Output node switches instantaneously with input.
- Both transistors remain in saturation for the **entire input transition**.
- $I_{sc}$ is at its theoretical maximum.

**Regime 2: $0 < C_L < C_{critical}$ (Mixed Conduction)**
- $I_{sc}$ flows during the overlap period.
- Part of the period is "useful" (one transistor fully ON, charging $C_L$).
- $I_{sc}$ decreases monotonically with $C_L$.

**Regime 3: $C_L \gg C_{critical}$ (Switching Dominated)**
- $\tau_{out} \gg \tau_{in}$: Input completes its transition well before output responds.
- Both transistors are in saturation for an infinitesimally small window.
- $I_{sc} \to 0$ asymptotically.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Energy Stored in Load Capacitor

**Problem:** A load capacitor $C_L$ at the output of a CMOS inverter is charged from $0$ to $V_{DD}$. Derive the energy stored in the capacitor.

**Step 1: Instantaneous charge on capacitor at voltage $v$**
The charge stored on the capacitor when the voltage across it is $v$ is given by:
$$q = C_L \cdot v$$

**Step 2: Infinitesimal work done to add charge $dq$**
To add an infinitesimal charge $dq$ to the capacitor at voltage $v$, the work done by the supply is:
$$dW = v \cdot dq = v \cdot C_L \cdot dv$$

**Step 3: Integrate to find total energy delivered**
Integrating from $v = 0$ (initial state) to $v = V_{DD}$ (final state):
$$W_{source} = \int_{0}^{V_{DD}} v \cdot C_L \, dv = C_L \left[ \frac{v^2}{2} \right]_{0}^{V_{DD}} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$$

**Step 4: Identify stored vs. dissipated energy**
- Energy supplied by $V_{DD}$ during charging = $C_L V_{DD}^2$ (this is the integral of $V_{DD} \cdot dq = V_{DD} \cdot C_L \cdot dV$, giving $C_L V_{DD}^2$).
- Energy **stored** in capacitor = $\frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$
- Energy **dissipated as heat in PMOS** = $C_L V_{DD}^2 - \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2 = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Result:** Exactly **half** the energy supplied by $V_{DD}$ is stored in $C_L$, and the other half is **dissipated as heat in the PMOS transistor** during charging. This is independent of the size of the transistor or the rise time.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Switching Power Dissipation in CMOS Inverter

**Problem:** Derive the expression for switching power dissipation in a CMOS inverter driving a load capacitance $C_L$ at clock frequency $f_{clk}$.

**Step 1: Identify energy consumed per switching event**
For one complete switching cycle (low-to-high followed by high-to-low):
- Charging $C_L$ from $0 \to V_{DD}$ dissipates: $\frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$ in PMOS
- Discharging $C_L$ from $V_{DD} \to 0$ dissipates: $\frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$ in NMOS
- Total energy dissipated per full cycle: $E_{cycle} = C_L V_{DD}^2$

**Step 2: Number of switching events per second**
- The clock frequency is $f_{clk}$ cycles/second.
- But not every node switches every cycle. The **switching activity factor** $\alpha$ captures this:
  - $\alpha = 0$ → node never switches
  - $\alpha = 1$ → node switches every cycle
  - $\alpha = 0.5$ → random data, switches every other cycle on average

**Step 3: Average power formula**
Power is energy per unit time:
$$P_{switch} = \alpha \cdot E_{cycle} \cdot f_{clk}$$

**Step 4: Substitute and finalize**
$$P_{switch} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f_{clk}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **most important equation in Low Power VLSI** for KTU 2024 Scheme. The four knobs to reduce $P_{switch}$ are: reduce $\alpha$, reduce $C_L$, reduce $V_{DD}$, or reduce $f_{clk}$. Reducing $V_{DD}$ has the **strongest (quadratic) effect**.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Short Circuit Power as a Function of $C_L$

**Problem:** Show that short circuit power decreases as load capacitance increases.

**Step 1: Express short circuit current waveform**
During input transition, the short circuit current can be approximated as a triangular pulse with peak value $I_{peak}$ and duration $T_{sc}$:
$$I_{peak} \approx \frac{\beta}{2} (V_{DD} - 2V_T)^2$$

**Step 2: Determine the duration of short circuit conduction**
The time during which both transistors conduct is limited by the **slower** of the input and output transitions. Mathematically:
$$T_{sc} = \min(\tau_{in}, \tau_{out})$$

**Step 3: Relate output time constant to $C_L$**
$$\tau_{out} = \frac{C_L \cdot V_{DD}}{I_{driver}}$$

**Step 4: Develop the relationship**
- **Case A: Small $C_L$** (i.e., $\tau_{out} < \tau_{in}$)
  $$T_{sc} \approx \tau_{out} = \frac{C_L \cdot V_{DD}}{I_{driver}}$$
  Short circuit power is approximately **independent of $C_L$** in this regime (limited by $\tau_{out}$).

- **Case B: Large $C_L$** (i.e., $\tau_{out} > \tau_{in}$)
  $$T_{sc} \approx \tau_{in}$$
  Short circuit power depends on $\tau_{in}$ but is **inversely related to $C_L$** behavior, saturating to a constant.

**Step 5: Final short circuit power expression**
$$P_{sc} = \frac{1}{2} I_{peak} \cdot T_{sc} \cdot f_{clk}$$

For the practical KTU case where $\tau_{in} \ll \tau_{out}$ (large $C_L$):
$$P_{sc} \approx \frac{\beta}{12} \left(V_{DD} - 2V_T\right)^3 \cdot \tau_{in} \cdot f_{clk}$$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: $C_L$ vs. Power Components

| $C_L$ Regime | Switching Power $P_{sw}$ | Short Circuit Power $P_{sc}$ | Dominant Total Power |
|--------------|--------------------------|------------------------------|----------------------|
| $C_L \to 0$ | $\to 0$ | **MAXIMUM** | $P_{sc}$ |
| Small $C_L$ | Small | High | Both comparable |
| Medium $C_L$ | Moderate | Moderate | Mixed |
| Large $C_L$ | Large | **MINIMUM** ($\to 0$) | $P_{sw}$ |
| $C_L \to \infty$ | **MAXIMUM** | $\to 0$ | $P_{sw}$ only |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Insight:** For **low-power CMOS design**, we want $C_L$ to be **as large as possible relative to driver strength** to minimize $I_{sc}$. This is achieved by **sizing down drivers** and **sizing up loads** — opposite to high-speed design intuition!
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 CMOS Inverter Current Paths Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    VDD["VDD Supply Rail"]
```

- **Dotted red line** → Wasted short circuit path ($V_{DD} \to PMOS \to NMOS \to GND$)
