a 50 mV $\sigma_{V_t}$ at $V_t = 0.3$ V causes orders-of-magnitude variation in $I_{off}$ across the die.

### 2.2 The Subthreshold Swing (Boltzmann Tyranny)

The **subthreshold swing $S$** quantifies how sharply the transistor turns off:

$$S \;=\; \left(\frac{d(\log_{10} I_{ds})}{d V_{GS}}\right)^{-1} \;=\; n \cdot V_T \cdot \ln(10) \;\approx\; 60 \cdot n \;\text{mV/decade}$$

at room temperature ($T = 300\;\text{K}$, $V_T = 25.85$ mV).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boltzmann Tyranny:** $S \geq 60$ mV/decade is a **fundamental physics limit** set by the Boltzmann distribution. Even an "ideal" transistor cannot turn off faster than this. To reduce $V_t$ without exploding $I_{off}$, the only knob is **reducing $n$** (the body-effect coefficient), where:
> $$n \;=\; 1 + \frac{C_{dm}}{C_{ox}}$$
> $C_{dm}$ is the depletion-region capacitance. Reducing $C_{dm}$ (e.g., via SOI, multi-gate/FET devices) lowers $n$ toward 1, approaching the **60 mV/decade floor**.

### 2.3 The Fundamental $V_t$ Trade-Off Triangle

Three quantities are linked through $V_t$:

| Quantity | Functional Dependence on $V_t$ | Behavior |
|----------|-------------------------------|----------|
| **Propagation Delay ($t_{pd}$)** | $t_{pd} \propto \dfrac{V_{DD}}{(V_{DD} - V_t)^\alpha}$ | **Decreases** as $V_t$ lowers |
| **Static / Leakage Power ($P_{stat}$)** | $P_{stat} \propto I_{off} \propto \exp(-V_t/nV_T)$ | **Increases** as $V_t$ lowers |
| **Variability ($\sigma_{V_t}/V_t$)** | $\sigma_{V_t}/V_t \propto 1/V_t$ | **Worsens** as $V_t$ lowers |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Engineering Utility |
|---|---------|-------------|---------------------|
| 1 | $I_{sub} = I_0 \cdot \exp\!\left(\dfrac{V_{GS} - V_t}{n V_T}\right)\left[1 - \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{V_{DS}}{V_T}\right)\right]$ | Subthreshold current | Off-state leakage estimator |
| 2 | $I_{off} = I_0 \cdot \exp(-V_t/nV_T)$ | OFF-current (leakage) | Static power $P_{stat} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{off}$ |
| 3 | $V_t(V_{DS}) = V_t(V_{DS0}) - \eta_{DIBL} \cdot (V_{DS} - V_{DS0})$ | DIBL model | SCE characterization |
| 4 | $S = n \cdot V_T \cdot \ln(10) \geq 60\;\text{mV/dec}$ | Subthreshold swing | Limit of $V_t$ scaling |
| 5 | $n = 1 + C_{dm}/C_{ox}$ | Body-effect factor | Body-bias design |
| 6 | $J_{FN} = A E_{ox}^2 \exp(-B/E_{ox})$ | Fowler-Nordheim tunneling | Gate leakage estimation |
| 7 | $\mu_{eff} = \mu_0 / [1 + \theta(V_{GS} - V_t)]$ | Mobility degradation | $I_{on}$ modeling |
| 8 | $I_{on} = \frac{1}{2}\mu_n C_{ox}\frac{W}{L}(V_{GS} - V_t)^2$ | Saturation current (long channel) | Drive strength |
| 9 | $t_{pd} \approx \dfrac{C_L V_{DD}}{I_{on}}$ | Gate delay | Performance metric |
| 10 | $\dfrac{I_{on}}{I_{off}} \approx 10^{(V_{DD} - V_t)/S}$ | On/Off ratio | $V_t$ design trade-off |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** All these formulas are used in **SPICE-level leakage power estimation, technology node selection (e.g., 28 nm vs 14 nm), and the design of multi-$V_t$ libraries** in modern ASIC/SoC flows (e.g., Cadence, Synopsys).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Valuation Tip:** Derivations are worth 5–7 marks. Show **every algebraic transition** with a one-line explanation. Examiners allocate marks for **logical coherence, not just the final answer**.

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Subthreshold Current $I_{sub}$ (Diffusion Model)

**Starting physical assumption:** For $V_{GS} < V_t$, the channel is **weakly inverted** and current flows by **diffusion** of minority carriers (electrons for NMOS) from source to drain — analogous to a $pn$-junction.

**Step 1:** The electron concentration at the surface (at source end, $x = 0$) is given by the Boltzmann distribution:

$$n(0) \;=\; n_{p0} \cdot \exp\!\left(\frac{V_{GS} - V_t}{V_T}\right)$$

**Step 2:** The electron concentration at the drain end ($x = L$), accounting for the applied $V_{DS}$:

$$n(L) \;=\; n_{p0} \cdot \exp\!\left(\frac{V_{GS} - V_t - V_{DS}}{V_T}\right)$$

**Step 3:** Apply the **1-D diffusion equation** (Fick's law) across the channel:

$$I_{ds} \;=\; q A D_n \frac{dn}{dx} \;=\; q A D_n \frac{n(L) - n(0)}{L}$$

Substituting Steps 1 and 2:

$$I_{ds} \;=\; q A D_n \,\cdot\, \frac{n_{p0}}{L} \left[\exp\!\left(\frac{V_{GS} - V_t}{V_T}\right) - \exp\!\left(\frac{V_{GS} - V_t - V_{DS}}{V_T}\right)\right]$$

**Step 4:** Factor out the common exponential term:

$$I_{ds} \;=\; \frac{q A D_n n_{p0}}{L} \,\cdot\, \exp\!\left(\frac{V_{GS} - V_t}{V_T}\right) \cdot \left[1 - \exp\!\left(-\frac{V_{DS}}{V_T}\right)\right]$$

**Step 5:** Express $n_{p0} = n_i^2 / N_A$ and combine geometric/physical constants into a single $I_0$ term. Also introduce the **body-effect coefficient** $n$ (to account for capacitance division between oxide and depletion regions):

$$I_{sub} \;=\; \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} (n-1) V_T^2 \,\cdot\, \exp\!\left(\frac{V_{GS} - V_t}{n V_T}\right) \cdot \left[1 - \exp\!\left(-\frac{V_{DS}}{V_T}\right)\right]$$

**Interpretation of each symbol:**

- $\mu_n$ → electron mobility
- $C_{ox}$ → gate oxide capacitance per unit area
- $n$ → ideality factor, $n = 1 + C_{dm}/C_{ox}$
- $V_T = kT/q$ → thermal voltage (~25.85 mV at 300 K)
- $W/L$ → transistor aspect ratio

**Step 6 — OFF state:** Set $V_{GS} = 0$ and $V_{DS} = V_{DD}$. Since $V_{DD} \gg V_T$, the bracket tends to 1:

$$I_{off} \;=\; I_0 \,\cdot\, \exp\!\left(\frac{-V_t}{n V_T}\right)$$

This is the **single most important equation** in low-power VLSI leakage analysis.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — The Subthreshold Swing $S$ and Boltzmann Tyranny

**Step 1:** Define $S$ as the inverse slope of $\log_{10}(I_{ds})$ vs $V_{GS}$ in the subthreshold region:

$$S \;\equiv\; \left[\frac{d(\log_{10} I_{ds})}{d V_{GS}}\right]^{-1}$$

**Step 2:** Take the log of the subthreshold current (with $V_{DS} \gg V_T$):

$$\log_{10}(I_{ds}) \;=\; \log_{10}(I_0) \;+\; \frac{V_{GS} - V_t}{n V_T \ln(10)}$$

since $\ln(10)$ converts natural log to base-10.

**Step 3:** Differentiate with respect to $V_{GS}$:

$$\frac{d(\log_{10} I_{ds})}{d V_{GS}} \;=\; \frac{1}{n V_T \ln(10)}$$

**Step 4:** Invert to get $S$:

$$S \;=\; n V_T \ln(10)$$

**Step 5:** Plug in $V_T = kT/q = 25.85$ mV at $T = 300$ K and $\ln(10) \approx 2.3026$:

$$S \;\geq\; 1 \cdot 25.85 \cdot 2.3026 \;\text{mV/decade} \;\approx\; 60\;\text{mV/decade}$$

**Interpretation:** The factor $n$ multiplies the floor. For bulk CMOS, $n \approx 1.3$–$1.5$, giving $S \approx 80$–$90$ mV/decade. The **60 mV/decade** is the **Boltzmann tyranny** floor — cannot be beaten by conventional MOSFETs.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** The Boltzmann tyranny is the **single most important concept** for justifying why $V_t$ cannot scale below ~0.3 V in conventional devices. This is the **gateway concept** to advanced devices (FinFET, GAA, NCFET, TFET) that aim to break the limit.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — DIBL Effect on $V_t$

**Step 1:** Consider the channel potential barrier. In a long-channel device, the gate alone controls the source-side barrier. In a short channel, $V_{DS}$ also **depresses** the barrier.

**Step 2:** Use a 2-D Poisson solution. The barrier lowering at the source end is:

$$\Delta \Phi_B \;\approx\; \eta_{DIBL} \cdot V_{DS}$$

where:

$$\eta_{DIBL} \;\approx\; \frac{\epsilon_{si}}{\epsilon_{ox}} \cdot \frac{t_{ox}}{L} \cdot \frac{1}{1 + 2 X_j / L}$$

**Step 3:** This barrier lowering subtracts from the threshold:

$$V_t(V_{DS}) \;\approx\; V_t(V_{DS0}) \;-\; \eta_{DIBL} \cdot (V_{DS} - V_{DS0})$$

**Step 4:** Plug into the $I_{off}$ equation to get the leakage boost from DIBL:

$$I_{off}^{DIBL} \;=\; I_0 \exp\!\left(\frac{-V_t + \eta_{DIBL}(V_{DS} - V_{DS0})}{n V_T}\right) \;=\; I_{off,noDIBL} \cdot \exp\!\left(\frac{\eta_{DIBL}\cdot V_{DD}}{n V_T}\right)$$

**Interpretation:** DIBL **multiplicatively amplifies** leakage. At $V_t = 0.3$ V, $\eta_{DIBL} = 100$ mV/V, $V_{DD} = 1$ V, this factor is $\exp(100/26) \approx 46\times$ — enough to swamp any "savings" from $V_t$ reduction.

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — The $I_{on}/I_{off}$ Trade-Off (Designer's Key Equation)

**Step 1:** $I_{on}$ in velocity-saturated regime:

$$I_{on} \;\approx\; W C_{ox} (V_{DD} - V_t) \cdot v_{sat}$$

**Step 2:** $I_{off}$:

$$I_{off} \;=\; I_0 \exp(-V_t / nV_T)$$

**Step 3:** Form the ratio:

$$\frac{I_{on}}{I_{off}} \;=\; \frac{W C_{ox} (V_{DD} - V_t) v_{sat}}{I_0 \exp(-V_t/nV_T)}$$

**Step 4:** Recognize that the subthreshold current drops by a decade per $S$ mV of $V_{GS}$ increase, so over the range $V_{GS} = 0$ to $V_{DD}$:

$$\frac{I_{on}}{I_{off}} \;\approx\; 10^{\,(V_{DD} - V_t)/S}$$

**Step 5:** At a target ratio, e.g., $I_{on}/I_{off} = 10^5$, solve for $V_t$:

$$V_t \;\geq\; V_{DD} \;-\; 5 \cdot S$$

For $V_{DD} = 1$ V, $S = 90$ mV/decade:

$$V_t \;\geq\; 1.0 \;-\; 0.45 \;=\; 0.55\;\text{V}$$

**Conclusion:** A **lower $V_t$** forces a **worse $I_{on}/I_{off}$ ratio** — there is no free lunch.

### 3.5 Derivation 5 — Gate Oxide Tunneling (Fowler-Nordheim)

**Step 1:** Treat the gate oxide as a triangular potential barrier under high field. The tunneling probability (WKB approximation):

$$T \;\approx\; \exp\!\left(-\frac{4\sqrt{2m^*_{ox}\,\phi_B}}{3\hbar\, q} \cdot \frac{\phi_B}{E_{ox}}\right) \;=\; \exp\!\left(-\frac{B}{E_{ox}}\right)$$

where $\phi_B$ is the barrier height, $m^*_{ox}$ is the effective mass in oxide, and $B$ is a constant ($\sim 200$–$300$ MV/cm for SiO$_2$).

**Step 2:** The tunneling current density:

$$J_{FN} \;=\; A \cdot E_{ox}^2 \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{B}{E_{ox}}\right)$$

**Step 3:** Express in terms of $t_{ox}$ and $V_{ox} = V_{GS} - V_{FB} - \psi_s$:

$$J_{FN} \;=\; A \left(\frac{V_{ox}}{t_{ox}}\right)^2 \exp\!\left(-B \cdot \frac{t_{ox}}{V_{ox}}\right)$$

**Step 4:** As $t_{ox} \to 1.5$ nm, even small $V_{GS} = 1$ V gives $E_{ox} \sim 7$ MV/cm — entering the **direct tunneling** regime. The current is **exponentially** sensitive to $t_{ox}$.

**Interpretation:** Gate tunneling is **not directly** a $V_t$ effect, but $V_t$ scaling demands thinner oxide to maintain $C_{ox}$ and control $S$, creating a **coupled constraint**.

### 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Constraint | Primary Quantity Affected | Effect on $V_t$ | Mitigation Strategy |
|------------|---------------------------|------------------|---------------------|
| Subthreshold conduction | $I_{off}$ | Limits minimum $V_t$ | High-$\kappa$ gate stack, FinFET |
| DIBL | $V_t(V_{DS})$ effective | Lowers $V_t$ at high $V_{DS}$ | Short-channel design, halo doping |
| Gate oxide tunneling | $I_{gate}$ | Indirectly (via $t_{ox}$ scaling) | High-$\kappa$ dielectrics |
| $V_t$ roll-off (SCE) | $V_t(L)$ | $V_t$ drops with $L$ | Retrograde well, pocket implant |
| Mobility degradation | $I_{on}$ | Caps current gain | Strain engineering, low-field mobility boosters |
| Process variability | $\sigma_{V_t}$ | Exponential leakage spread | Multi-$V_t$ design, adaptive body bias |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Hierarchical Map of $V_t$ Reduction Constraints

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA["VT REDUCTION"] --> nodeB["Desired Outcomes"]
    nodeA --> nodeC["Physical Constraints"]
    nodeB --> nodeB1["Higher I_on"]
    nodeB --> nodeB2["Lower t_pd"]
    nodeB --> nodeB3["Enables VDD Scaling"]
    nodeC --> nodeC1["Subthreshold Leakage"]
    nodeC --> nodeC2["DIBL Effect"]
    nodeC --> nodeC3["Gate Tunneling"]
    nodeC --> nodeC4["VT Roll-off SCE"]
    nodeC --> nodeC5["Mobility Degradation"]
    nodeC --> nodeC6["Process Variability"]
    nodeC --> nodeC7["Punchthrough"]
    nodeC --> nodeC8["Boltzmann Tyranny 60 mV"]
    nodeC1 --> nodeD["Multi VT Design"]
    nodeC2 --> nodeE["Halo Implants"]
    nodeC3 --> nodeF["High K Dielectric"]
    nodeC4 --> nodeG["Retrograde Well"]
    nodeC8 --> nodeH["FinFET GAA NCFET"]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — The $V_t$ Trade-Off Triangle (Block-Level Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["HIGH VT"] --> nodeB["Low Leakage I_off"]
    nodeA --> nodeC["High Delay t_pd"]
    nodeA --> nodeD["Low Variability"]
    nodeE["LOW VT"] --> nodeF["High Leakage I_off"]
    nodeE --> nodeG["Low Delay t_pd"]
    nodeE --> nodeH["High Variability"]
    nodeI["VARIABILITY sigma VT"] --> nodeJ["Die-to-Die Spread"]
    nodeI --> nodeK["Within-Die Mismatch"]
    nodeJ --> nodeL["Static Power Crisis"]
    nodeK --> nodeL
    nodeB --> nodeM["Leakage Power Pstat Low"]
    nodeF --> nodeN["Leakage Power Pstat High"]
    nodeM --> nodeO["Performance Pwr Trade-off"]
    nodeN --> nodeO
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Block-Level Architecture of Multi-$V_t$ Compensation

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA["Design Target"] --> nodeB["Compute Required VT"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["VT Selection"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Critical Path Low VT"]
    nodeC --> nodeE["Non Critical High VT"]
    nodeC --> nodeF["Sleep Transistor Ultra High VT"]
    nodeD --> nodeG["Performance Boost"]
    nodeE --> nodeH["Leakage Reduction"]
    nodeF --> nodeI["Standby Power near Zero"]
    nodeG --> nodeJ["Overall Power Performance Optimized"]
    nodeH --> nodeJ
    nodeI --> nodeJ
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — DIBL & $V_t$ Roll-Off as Function of $L$

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA["Channel Length L"] --> nodeB["Long Channel"]
    nodeA --> nodeC["Short Channel"]
    nodeA --> nodeD["Very Short Channel"]
    nodeB --> nodeE["VT Constant"]
    nodeC --> nodeF["VT Rolls off"]
    nodeD --> nodeG["DIBL Severe"]
    nodeF --> nodeH["I_off Increases"]
    nodeG --> nodeH
    nodeH --> nodeI["VT Reduction Hard Limit"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading aid for Block Diagram 4.4:** As $L$ shrinks, the **barrier-lowering effect** of the drain becomes comparable to that of the gate, causing $V_t$ to drop and $I_{off}$ to rise nonlinearly. This is why $V_t$ **cannot be scaled indefinitely** — the curve becomes steeper with every node.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mark Distribution (KTU 2024 ESE Pattern):**
> - **Part A:** 2 marks × 5 questions (Module-wise). Short definitions and derivations.
> - **Part B:** 14 marks × 2 from 3 choices (Module-internal choice). Long answer with sub-parts.
> - Bloom's Levels tested: **L2 (Understand), L3 (Apply), L4 (Analyze), L5 (Evaluate)**.

---

### 📘 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`**
**Define subthreshold swing $S$ of a MOSFET. What is the theoretical minimum value of $S$ at room temperature, and why is it called the "Boltzmann tyranny"?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Definition (1 mark):** The subthreshold swing $S$ is defined as the inverse slope of the $\log_{10}(I_{ds})$–$V_{GS}$ characteristic in the subthreshold region:

$$S \;=\; \left[\frac{d(\log_{10} I_{ds})}{dV_{GS}}\right]^{-1} \;=\; n V_T \ln(10)$$

- **Minimum value (1 mark):** At $T = 300$ K, with $n = 1$, $S = 25.85 \cdot \ln(10) \approx 60$ mV/decade. This is the **theoretical floor**; bulk CMOS typically has $S \approx 80$–$90$ mV/decade.

- **Boltzmann tyranny (1 mark):** It is a **fundamental physics limit** imposed by the **Boltzmann distribution** of carriers in the subthreshold regime. No conventional MOSFET can have $S < 60$ mV/decade at room temperature. To go below this, one needs **new device physics** (e.g., TFET, NCFET, negative capacitance devices).

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`**
**Explain DIBL. How does it constrain threshold voltage scaling?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Definition (1 mark):** Drain-Induced Barrier Lowering (DIBL) is a short-channel effect in which a high $V_{DS}$ **lowers the source-side potential barrier**, effectively **reducing $V_t$**:

$$V_t(V_{DS}) \;=\; V_t(V_{DS0}) - \eta_{DIBL}(V_{DS} - V_{DS0})$$

- **Physical origin (1 mark):** In short channels, the drain's depletion region extends under the gate, **competing with the gate's electrostatic control**. This 2-D field effect is negligible in long-channel devices.

- **Constraint on $V_t$ scaling (1 mark):** DIBL causes $V_t$ to **decrease** as $V_{DS}$ rises. At low $V_t$ (e.g., 0.3 V), a $\eta_{DIBL} = 100$ mV/V with $V_{DD} = 1$ V can reduce $V_t$ by 100 mV, increasing $I_{off}$ by a factor of $\exp(100/nV_T) \approx 46\times$. DIBL therefore **multiplicatively amplifies leakage** and sets a hard floor on $V_t$ for any given $V_{DD}$.

---

### 📕 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]`**
**(a)** With the necessary derivation, obtain the expression for the **subthreshold current** $I_{sub}$ of a MOSFET. From this, derive the **off-state leakage** $I_{off}$ and the **subthreshold swing** $S$. Discuss the **Boltzmann tyranny** and its implications on $V_t$ scaling. **\[7 marks\]**

**(b)** Explain **Drain-Induced Barrier Lowering (DIBL)** and **gate oxide tunneling** as the two major constraints on $V_t$ reduction. Derive the DIBL-induced leakage amplification factor and the Fowler-Nordheim tunneling current density expression. **\[7 marks\]**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Subthreshold Current, $I_{off}$, and Subthreshold Swing**

- **Step 1 — Diffusion model (2 marks):** Use the Boltzmann distribution for electron concentration at source and drain ends, then Fick's law. This yields:

$$I_{sub} \;=\; \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L}(n-1) V_T^2 \exp\!\left(\frac{V_{GS} - V_t}{nV_T}\right)\left[1 - \exp\!\left(-\frac{V_{DS}}{V_T}\right)\right]$$

Show all steps from $n(0)$ and $n(L)$ to the final form (as in Section 3.1).

- **Step 2 — Off-state (1 mark):** $V_{GS} = 0$, $V_{DS} = V_{DD} \gg V_T$:

$$I_{off} \;=\; I_0 \exp(-V_t / nV_T)$$

- **Step 3 — Subthreshold swing (2 marks):** Differentiate $\log_{10}(I_{sub})$ w.r.t. $V_{GS}$, invert:

$$S \;=\; nV_T \ln(10) \;\geq\; 60\;\text{mV/decade}$$

- **Step 4 — Boltzmann tyranny (2 marks):** Discuss the 60 mV/decade floor, the role of $n = 1 + C_{dm}/C_{ox}$, and advanced devices (FinFET, NCFET) that can approach or beat this limit.

**Part (b) — DIBL and Gate Tunneling**

- **DIBL derivation (3.5 marks):** From 2-D Poisson, get $\Delta \Phi_B = \eta_{DIBL} V_{DS}$ and the $V_t(V_{DS})$ equation. Plug into $I_{off}$ to get:

$$I_{off}^{DIBL} \;=\; I_{off,noDIBL} \cdot \exp\!\left(\frac{\eta_{DIBL} V_{DD}}{nV_T}\right)$$

- **Gate tunneling derivation (3.5 marks):** From the WKB approximation, get the Fowler-Nordheim expression:

$$J_{FN} \;=\; A E_{ox}^2 \exp(-B/E_{ox})$$

with $E_{ox} = V_{ox}/t_{ox}$. Discuss the direct-tunneling regime for $t_{ox} < 1.5$ nm and the role of high-$\kappa$ dielectrics.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** *(Internal Choice Alternative)*
**`[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Supplementary]`**
**(a)** Discuss the **short-channel effect of $V_t$ roll-off** and **punchthrough** as constraints on $V_t$ reduction. Derive an expression for the **roll-off** $\Delta V_t$ as a function of channel length $L$ and junction depth $X_j$. **\[7 marks\]**

**(b)** Explain **mobility degradation** and **velocity saturation** in short-channel MOSFETs. Derive the expression for **effective mobility** $\mu_{eff}$ and discuss the **diminishing return** of $V_t$ reduction on $I_{on}$. **\[7 marks\]**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — $V_t$ Roll-off and Punchthrough**

- **Physical origin (1.5 marks):** Charge-sharing between gate, source, and drain depletion regions reduces gate's control over the channel. Define the charge-sharing fraction and write the depletion charge underneath the gate.

- **Roll-off derivation (4 marks):** Use the trapezoidal charge-sharing model to get:

$$\Delta V_{t,rolloff} \;\approx\; \frac{(2\phi_F + V_{SB})}{2}\,\left[\,\frac{X_j}{L}\left(\sqrt{1 + \frac{2X_j}{L}} - 1\right)\,\right]$$

This shows $V_t$ decreases roughly as $X_j/L$ for $L \gg X_j$, but much more steeply as $L \to X_j$.

- **Punchthrough (1.5 marks):** When source and drain depletion regions **merge** ($L < X_{d,s} + X_{d,d}$), the barrier vanishes and the transistor is **always on** — a destructive failure mode. This sets a hard lower limit on $L$ for any given $V_t$.

**Part (b) — Mobility Degradation and Velocity Saturation**

- **Mobility degradation derivation (3.5 marks):** Surface scattering under high transverse field gives:

$$\mu_{eff} \;=\; \frac{\mu_0}{1 + \theta(V_{GS} - V_t)}$$

- **Velocity saturation (1.5 marks):** In short channels, $v_{drift} \to v_{sat}$, so:

$$I_{on} \;\approx\; W C_{ox} (V_{GS} - V_t) v_{sat}$$

- **Diminishing returns (2 marks):** Combining the two effects:

$$I_{on} \;\approx\; \frac{W C_{ox}(V_{DD} - V_t) v_{sat}}{1 + \theta(V_{DD} - V_t)}$$

As $V_t \to 0$, the denominator grows linearly in $V_{DD} - V_t$, so the current saturates. The quadratic gain of $V_t$ reduction **vanishes** in modern nodes — designers must rely on **$V_{DD}$ scaling** and **multi-gate structures** instead.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the bracket term** $[1 - \exp(-V_{DS}/V_T)]$ in the $I_{sub}$ expression — costs **1 mark**.
> 2. **Writing $S = 60$ mV/decade without the $n$ factor** — you must state $S = n V_T \ln(10)$, then evaluate. Skipping the $n$ costs **0.5 mark**.
> 3. **Confusing subthreshold swing with DIBL** — DIBL affects the **position** of $\log I$–$V_{GS}$ curve with $V_{DS}$; subthreshold swing is its **slope**.
> 4. **Forgetting the body-effect factor** $n$ in the $I_{off}$ equation. Examiners are strict about this.
> 5. **In the trade-off question**, students often quote "lower $V_t$ → higher leakage" but **fail to quantify** the exponential relationship — always include $\exp(\Delta V_t / nV_T)$.
> 6. **Derivation of $J_{FN}$** — students often write $J = A \exp(-B/E_{ox})$ without the $E_{ox}^2$ prefactor; the full form is **$J = A E_{ox}^2 \exp(-B/E_{ox})$**.

---

### 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **$V_t$ reduction is bounded by six physical constraints**: subthreshold leakage, DIBL, gate tunneling, $V_t$ roll-off, mobility degradation, and process variability.
- **Off-state leakage scales exponentially**: $I_{off} \propto \exp(-V_t / nV_T)$ — a 100 mV $V_t$ drop gives **~10× leakage** at room temperature.
- **Subthreshold swing $S = n V_T \ln(10)$** has a **60 mV/decade floor** — the **Boltzmann tyranny**. Bulk CMOS gives 80–90 mV/decade; FinFET/Nanowire gives ~65 mV/decade.
- **DIBL** amplifies leakage as $I_{off}^{DIBL} = I_{off,noDIBL} \cdot \exp(\eta_{DIBL} V_{DD} / nV_T)$ — a major leakage booster in sub-100 nm nodes.
- **Gate oxide tunneling** ($J_{FN} = A E_{ox}^2 \exp(-B/E_{ox})$) becomes critical for $t_{ox} < 1.5$ nm; mitigated by **high-$\kappa$ dielectrics** (HfO$_2$, HfSiO$_x$).
- **$V_t$ roll-off** scales roughly as $X_j / L$; mitigated by **retrograde wells, halo implants, FinFET**.
- **Mobility degradation** $\mu_{eff} = \mu_0 / [1 + \theta(V_{GS} - V_t)]$ combined with **velocity saturation** causes **diminishing returns** on $I_{on}$ from $V_t$ scaling in modern nodes.
- **Process variability** in $\sigma_{V_t}$ translates to **exponential** leakage spread — multi-$V_t$ design and adaptive body bias are the standard countermeasures.
- **Designer's three-way trade-off**: Performance ↔ Leakage Power ↔ Variability — all linked through $V_t$. **No free lunch.**
- **Modern solutions** (FinFET, GAA, NCFET, TFET) attempt to push the **Boltzmann tyranny** floor or work around it entirely.
- **KTU CO mapping**: This topic falls under **CO2** ("Analyze sources of power dissipation and limits of voltage scaling").
- **Bloom's level tested**: Primarily **L4 (Analyze)** and **L5 (Evaluate)**, occasionally **L3 (Apply)** for numerical problems.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Effect of Supply Voltage on Delay and Power. Effect of Supply Voltage on Static and Dynamic Power. Multi VDD
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT866 - LOW POWER VLSI
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Effect of Supply Voltage on Delay and Power. Effect of Supply Voltage on Static and Dynamic Power. Multi VDD

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What Does "Effect of Supply Voltage on Delay and Power" Mean?

In CMOS digital VLSI design, the **Supply Voltage (V_DD)** is the single most influential design knob that simultaneously governs **switching speed (delay)**, **dynamic energy consumption**, and **static (leakage) dissipation** of every transistor on the chip. Reducing V_DD lowers power quadratically (for dynamic) and linearly (for static), but it also degrades the drive current of the MOSFETs, thereby increasing the propagation delay.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition:**
> The *effect of supply voltage on delay* refers to the relationship between the drain-to-source current I_DS (which depends on V_DD) and the gate propagation delay t_p, characterized by the alpha-power law model. The *effect of supply voltage on power* refers to how the total power dissipation P_total = P_dynamic + P_static + P_short-circuit scales as a function of V_DD.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Tap" Model

Imagine a water tap connected to a pump:
- **V_DD is like the water pressure** pushing current through the transistor channel.
- **Higher pressure (V_DD)** → water flows faster (faster switching) → but the pump works harder (more power dissipated).
- **Lower pressure (V_DD)** → water trickles slowly (slower switching) → but the pump idles (less power consumed).

The catch? If pressure is **too low**, the water simply cannot reach the outlet — analogous to V_DD approaching the threshold voltage V_TH, where the transistor refuses to switch ON reliably.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Trade-off Insight:** Lowering V_DD saves power **but** degrades performance. The art of low-power VLSI design is choosing the *minimum* V_DD that still meets the target clock frequency (timing closure).

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

The following parameters are central to the analysis presented in this module:

- **V_DD** — Supply voltage (in Volts, **V**).
- **V_TH** (or V_TN for NMOS, V_TP for PMOS) — Threshold voltage.
- **C_L** — Load capacitance (in Farads, **F**).
- **α** — Switching activity factor (0 ≤ α ≤ 1).
- **f_clk** — Clock frequency (in Hz).
- **I_leak** — Leakage current per gate (in Amperes, **A**).
- **K_n, K_p** — Process transconductance parameters.
- **V_DS, V_GS** — Drain–Source and Gate–Source voltages.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Effect of V_DD on Propagation Delay

### 2.1.1 Long-Channel CMOS Inverter Delay Derivation

The propagation delay of a CMOS inverter driving a load capacitance C_L is determined by how fast the output node can be charged or discharged through the ON transistor.

For an NMOS transistor in saturation transitioning to linear region during discharge:

**Step 1 — Saturation region current (initial discharge):**

$$I_{DS,sat} = \frac{K_n}{2}(V_{GS} - V_{TH})^2 = \frac{K_n}{2}(V_{DD} - V_{TH})^2$$

**Step 2 — Linear region current (later discharge):**

$$I_{DS,lin} = K_n \left[(V_{GS} - V_{TH})V_{DS} - \frac{V_{DS}^2}{2}\right]$$

**Step 3 — Approximate average current (shockley simplification):**

For the long-channel model, the average discharge current is approximately:

$$I_{avg} \approx \frac{K_n}{2}(V_{DD} - V_{TH})^2$$

**Step 4 — Propagation delay expression:**

$$t_p = \frac{C_L \cdot V_{DD}}{I_{avg}} = \frac{C_L \cdot V_{DD}}{\frac{K_n}{2}(V_{DD} - V_{TH})^2}$$

$$\boxed{t_p = \frac{2 \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}}{K_n (V_{DD} - V_{TH})^2}}$$

### 2.1.2 Short-Channel (Alpha-Power Law) Delay Model

For sub-100nm technologies, velocity saturation dominates, and the classical square-law no longer holds. The **alpha-power law model** is used:

$$\boxed{t_p \propto \frac{V_{DD}}{(V_{DD} - V_{TH})^{\alpha}}}$$

where **α** is the velocity saturation index (typically 1.2 ≤ α ≤ 1.5 for short-channel devices).

### 2.1.3 Sensitivity Analysis — The "Decibel-per-Volt" of Delay

For V_DD >> V_TH (high-voltage regime), the delay simplifies to:

$$t_p \approx \frac{C_L}{K_n \cdot V_{DD}}$$

This shows that in the **high-V_DD regime**, delay is **inversely proportional to V_DD** — a halving of V_DD doubles the delay.

For V_DD close to V_TH (near-threshold/subthreshold), delay grows **exponentially**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Rule of Thumb:** For each 100 mV reduction in V_DD, the propagation delay increases by ~15–20% in typical 65nm CMOS.

---

## 2.2 Effect of V_DD on Power Dissipation

Total power dissipation in a CMOS gate has **three** components:

$$P_{total} = P_{dynamic} + P_{static} + P_{short\text{-}circuit}$$

### 2.2.1 Dynamic Switching Power (P_dynamic)

Every time a gate output toggles 0 → 1, energy E = C_L · V_DD² is drawn from the supply. Averaged over time:

$$\boxed{P_{dynamic} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f_{clk}}$$

- **Linear** scaling with frequency f.
- **Quadratic** scaling with V_DD (the *most powerful* knob!).
- **Linear** scaling with activity α and load C_L.

### 2.2.2 Static (Leakage) Power (P_static)

Even when the transistor is OFF, subthreshold and gate-tunneling leakage currents flow:

$$\boxed{P_{static} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{leak}}$$

- **Linear** scaling with V_DD.
- I_leak itself depends *exponentially* on V_TH via the subthreshold slope:

$$I_{leak} \propto e^{\frac{-V_{TH}{nV_T}}}$$

where n ≈ 1.3–1.5 and V_T = kT/q ≈ **25.85 mV at 300 K**.

### 2.2.3 Short-Circuit Power (P_short)

During the brief interval when both NMOS and PMOS are simultaneously ON (during input transition), a direct current flows from V_DD to GND:

$$\boxed{P_{short} \propto \frac{\beta}{12}(V_{DD} - 2V_{TH})^3 \cdot \tau_{in} \cdot f_{clk}}$$

where τ_in is the input rise/fall time. This term typically accounts for **5–15%** of total power.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Parameter / Quantity | Mathematical Expression | V_DD Dependence | Engineering Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Long-channel inverter delay | $t_p = \dfrac{2 C_L V_{DD}}{K_n (V_{DD} - V_{TH})^2}$ | Non-linear (polynomial) | Used for > 250 nm processes |
| 2 | Short-channel delay (alpha law) | $t_p \propto \dfrac{V_{DD}}{(V_{DD} - V_{TH})^{\alpha}}$ | Non-linear (sub-linear) | Used for ≤ 130 nm processes |
| 3 | High-V_DD delay approx. | $t_p \approx \dfrac{C_L}{K_n V_{DD}}$ | $t_p \propto 1/V_{DD}$ | Halving V_DD doubles delay |
| 4 | Dynamic switching power | $P_{dyn} = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f$ | $V_{DD}^2$ (quadratic) | The dominant term historically |
| 5 | Static leakage power | $P_{static} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{leak}$ | Linear in V_DD | Dominant in sub-90nm |
| 6 | Short-circuit power | $P_{short} \propto \beta \tau_{in} f (V_{DD} - 2V_{TH})^3$ | Cubic | Negligible if input ramp is fast |
| 7 | Total power | $P_{tot} = P_{dyn} + P_{static} + P_{short}$ | Mixed | Used in power budgeting |
| 8 | Energy per transition | $E = C_L V_{DD}^2$ | Quadratic | Determines battery life |
| 9 | Energy-Delay Product (EDP) | $EDP = t_p \cdot E$ | Compound metric | Lower = better V_DD choice |
| 10 | Leakage current (BSIM) | $I_{leak} = I_0 \cdot e^{(V_{GS}-V_{TH})/(nV_T)}$ | Exponential in V_TH | Lowered via high-V_TH devices |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Insight for KTU Examinations:** Always state **both** delay and power effects together when explaining V_DD scaling. A common KTU answer marking pitfall is to mention only one (e.g., power) and forget the trade-off on delay.

---

## 2.4 Practical Engineering Utility

- **Mobile SoCs** (smartphones, wearables): Aggressively scale V_DD to extend battery life, accepting slower clock speeds.
- **High-performance CPUs**: Operate at near-nominal V_DD to maximize throughput, paying the thermal cost.
- **DVFS (Dynamic Voltage and Frequency Scaling)**: Real-time adjustment of V_DD based on workload — directly applies the trade-offs above.
- **Multi-VDD chips**: Apply different V_DDs to different functional blocks based on their timing criticality.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Why Dynamic Power Scales as V_DD²

We start from first principles of energy stored on a capacitor.

### Step 1: Energy Required to Charge a Capacitor

When a CMOS gate output switches from 0 to V_DD, charge Q = C_L · V_DD flows from the supply rail onto the load capacitance. The energy supplied by V_DD is:

$$E_{supplied} = \int_{0}^{Q} V_{DD} \, dq = V_{DD} \cdot Q = V_{DD} \cdot C_L V_{DD} = C_L V_{DD}^2$$

**Explanation of logic:** The voltage remains constant at V_DD during the entire charging process (ideal supply), and we integrate power P = V·dq over the charge transferred Q.

### Step 2: Energy Actually Stored on C_L

However, the energy *stored* on the capacitor is half of the supplied energy (the other half is dissipated as heat in the PMOS during charging):

$$E_{stored} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$$

**Explanation of logic:** Using the standard capacitor energy formula $E = \frac{1}{2}CV^2$, which is independent of resistance.

### Step 3: Energy Dissipated During Discharge

During the 1 → 0 transition, ALL energy stored in C_L is dissipated as heat in the NMOS transistor:

$$E_{discharge} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$$

**Explanation of logic:** The capacitor discharges through the NMOS to ground; the stored energy is converted entirely into heat in the channel resistance.

### Step 4: Total Energy per Switching Cycle

For a complete 0 → 1 → 0 cycle (one full toggle):

$$E_{cycle} = E_{charging} = C_L V_{DD}^2$$

(Note: We count only the energy *drawn from the supply*, which equals the total heat dissipated per cycle.)

### Step 5: Average Power Computation

If the gate switches with activity factor α at clock frequency f_clk, the average switching rate is α · f_clk. Therefore:

$$P_{dynamic} = E_{cycle} \cdot (\alpha \cdot f_{clk}) = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f_{clk}$$

**Explanation of logic:** Power = Energy per event × number of events per second. The α factor accounts for the fact that not every clock cycle causes a transition (e.g., a static register holding a value consumes zero dynamic power).

### Step 6: Quadratic V_DD Dependence Verified

Taking the partial derivative of P_dynamic with respect to V_DD (holding all else constant):

$$\frac{\partial P_{dynamic}}{\partial V_{DD}} = 2 \alpha C_L V_{DD} f_{clk}$$

This confirms the **linear sensitivity** of power to V_DD at any operating point, which integrates to the **quadratic** dependence observed in Step 5.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Static Power and the Subthreshold Leakage Equation

### Step 1: Subthreshold Conduction

For V_GS < V_TH, the MOSFET channel is "off," but a small diffusion current still flows between source and drain due to the Boltzmann distribution of carriers.

### Step 2: BSIM Subthreshold Current Equation

$$I_{leak} = \mu C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} (n-1) V_T^2 \cdot e^{\frac{V_{GS} - V_{TH}}{n V_T}} \left[1 - e^{\frac{-V_{DS}}{V_T}}\right]$$

### Step 3: Simplification for Typical Digital Operation

When V_DS >> V_T (≈ 26 mV at room temperature), the bracket term [1 - e^(-V_DS/V_T)] → 1. With V_GS = 0 for an OFF NMOS:

$$I_{leak} \approx I_0 \cdot e^{\frac{-V_{TH}}{n V_T}}$$

where $I_0 = \mu C_{ox} \dfrac{W}{L} (n-1) V_T^2 \cdot e^{0}$ is a process-dependent constant.

### Step 4: Static Power Form

Multiplying by V_DD gives the leakage power per OFF transistor:

$$P_{static} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{leak} = V_{DD} \cdot I_0 \cdot e^{\frac{-V_{TH}}{n V_T}}$$

### Step 5: Subthreshold Slope Interpretation

The **subthreshold slope S** is defined as:

$$S = n V_T \ln(10) \approx 90 \text{ mV/decade (theoretical minimum at 300 K)}$$

This means every 90 mV *increase* in V_TH reduces leakage by 10×.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: V_DD Scaling Effects

| V_DD Regime | Delay Behavior | Dynamic Power | Static Power | Design Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Nominal (1.0 V–1.2 V)** | Fast (minimum t_p) | High (∝ V_DD²) | Moderate | High-performance CPUs |
| **Scaled (0.7 V–0.9 V)** | 2–3× slower | 50–65% lower | Reduced ~30% | General-purpose logic |
| **Near-threshold (0.4 V–0.5 V)** | 10–20× slower | 75–85% lower | Sharply reduced | IoT sensor nodes |
| **Subthreshold (V_DD < V_TH)** | 100–1000× slower | ~99% lower | Becomes dominant | Energy-harvesting, medical implants |

---

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Problem-Style)

> **Problem:** A CMOS inverter drives a load capacitance of **C_L = 50 fF**, operates at **V_DD = 1.0 V**, has **α = 0.2**, **f_clk = 500 MHz**, and **I_leak = 10 nA**. Compute (a) dynamic power, (b) static power, (c) percentage reduction in dynamic power if V_DD is lowered to 0.7 V (assuming same f).

**Part (a) — Dynamic Power:**

$$P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f$$

$$P_{dyn} = 0.2 \times 50 \times 10^{-15} \times (1.0)^2 \times 500 \times 10^6$$

$$P_{dyn} = 0.2 \times 50 \times 10^{-15} \times 5 \times 10^{8}$$

$$P_{dyn} = 5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ W} = \mathbf{5 \,\mu W}$$

**Part (b) — Static Power:**

$$P_{static} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{leak} = 1.0 \times 10 \times 10^{-9} = \mathbf{10 \text{ nW}}$$

**Part (c) — Dynamic Power at 0.7 V:**

$$P_{dyn,0.7} = 0.2 \times 50 \times 10^{-15} \times (0.7)^2 \times 5 \times 10^8$$

$$P_{dyn,0.7} = 0.2 \times 50 \times 10^{-15} \times 0.49 \times 5 \times 10^8$$

$$P_{dyn,0.7} = 2.45 \times 10^{-6} \text{ W} = \mathbf{2.45 \,\mu W}$$

**Percentage Reduction:**

$$\Delta P\% = \frac{5 - 2.45}{5} \times 100\% = \mathbf{51\%}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verifying the Quadratic Law:** Theoretically, the ratio should be (0.7/1.0)² = 0.49, giving a 51% reduction. The numerical answer matches, validating the V_DD² model.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Total Power Decomposition

The following Mermaid block depicts the hierarchy of power dissipation in a CMOS gate and how each component responds to V_DD scaling.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[CMOS Gate Total Power] --> B[Dynamic Power]
    A --> C[Static Power]
    A --> D[Short Circuit Power]
    B --> B1[alpha x C_L x VDDsq x fclk]
    B --> B2[Quadratic in VDD]
    B --> B3[Dominant at high VDD]
    C --> C1[VDD x I_leak]
    C --> C2[Linear in VDD]
    C --> C3[Dominant at low VDD]
    D --> D1[beta x tau x f x VDDcubed]
    D --> D2[Cubic in VDD]
    D --> D3[Typically 5 to 15 percent]
    B1 --> E[Lowering VDD reduces P_dyn by Vsq]
    C1 --> F[Lowering VDD reduces P_static linearly]
    D1 --> G[Short circuit power drops cubically]
```

## 4.2 Architecture Diagram: Multi-VDD Design with Voltage Islands

The following diagram illustrates a **Multi-VDD architecture**, where different functional blocks operate at different supply voltages based on their performance requirements.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SRC[DC-DC Converter] --> VHI[High VDD 1.0 V]
    SRC --> VLO[Low VDD 0.6 V]
    VHI --> BLKA[CPU Core Block]
    VLO --> BLKB[Memory Controller Block]
    BLKA --> LS1[Level Shifter Down]
    LS1 --> BUS[Shared Bus]
    BLKB --> BUS
    BLKA --> LS2[Level Shifter Up]
    LS2 --> BUS
    BUS --> OUT[System Output]
    VHI -. Level Shifter Up .-> BLKB
    VLO -. Level Shifter Down .-> BLKA
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block Legend:**
> - **High V_DD block** (e.g., ALU, critical path) → uses Level Shifter **Down** when driving a low-V_DD block.
> - **Low V_DD block** (e.g., sleep logic, non-critical) → uses Level Shifter **Up** when driving a high-V_DD block.
> - Level shifters ensure signal integrity across voltage domain boundaries.

## 4.3 Concept Diagram: V_DD vs Delay and Power Trade-off Curve

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[VDD Axis] --> B[Increase VDD]
    B --> C1[Delay Decreases]
    B --> C2[Dynamic Power Increases quadratically]
    B --> C3[Static Power Increases linearly]
    A --> D[Decrease VDD]
    D --> E1[Delay Increases]
    D --> E2[Dynamic Power Decreases quadratically]
    D --> E3[Static Power Decreases linearly]
    C1 --> OPT[Optimal VDD Point]
    E2 --> OPT
    OPT --> SEL[Select minimum VDD meeting timing]
```

## 4.4 Functional Flow: Multi-VDD Synthesis Strategy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1 Identify Timing Critical Paths] --> S2[Step 2 Assign High VDD to Critical Blocks]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3 Assign Low VDD to Non Critical Blocks]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4 Insert Level Shifters at Domain Boundaries]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5 Verify Timing Closure]
    S5 --> S6{Meets Constraints?}
    S6 -->|Yes| S7[Multi VDD Netlist Complete]
    S6 -->|No| S8[Adjust VDD Levels or Reassign Blocks]
    S8 --> S5
```

---

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Explain how the dynamic power dissipation in a CMOS inverter depends on the supply voltage V_DD.**

**Model Answer:**
Dynamic power is the power consumed due to charging and discharging of the load capacitance during logic transitions. The governing equation is:

$$P_{dynamic} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f_{clk}$$

The dependence on V_DD is **quadratic** — this arises from the fact that (i) the energy required to charge a capacitor to voltage V is $\frac{1}{2}CV^2$, and (ii) the charge transferred per cycle is $Q = C_L V_{DD}$. Combining both yields an energy of $C_L V_{DD}^2$ per transition. Hence, halving V_DD reduces dynamic power to **25% of its original value**, making V_DD scaling the most effective low-power technique historically.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**What is the effect of lowering V_DD on static (leakage) power dissipation?**

**Model Answer:**
Static power arises from leakage currents (predominantly subthreshold conduction) flowing even when the transistor is OFF. It is given by:

$$P_{static} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{leak}$$

The dependence on V_DD is **linear**. However, leakage current $I_{leak}$ itself depends *exponentially* on the threshold voltage $V_{TH}$. So while lowering V_DD directly reduces P_static linearly, modern designs use **high-V_TH** devices or **power gating** to suppress leakage more aggressively. In sub-90nm technologies, static power can become comparable to or even exceed dynamic power.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — June 2024]*

**(a)** Derive the expression for the propagation delay of a CMOS inverter as a function of V_DD. Discuss how this delay varies in the high-V_DD regime (V_DD >> V_TH) and the near-threshold regime (V_DD ≈ V_TH). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the **Multi-VDD** design technique. Discuss the role of **level shifters** and the **Clustered Voltage Scaling (CVS)** methodology. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Delay Derivation:**

The propagation delay of a CMOS inverter is the time taken to charge or discharge the load capacitance C_L through the ON transistor to 50% of V_DD.

The average discharge current through the NMOS is:

$$I_{avg} = \frac{K_n}{2}(V_{DD} - V_{TH})^2$$

The delay is therefore:

$$t_p = \frac{C_L \cdot V_{DD}}{I_{avg}} = \frac{2 C_L V_{DD}}{K_n (V_{DD} - V_{TH})^2}$$

**High-V_DD Regime (V_DD >> V_TH):**

$$t_p \approx \frac{C_L}{K_n V_{DD}}$$

The delay varies **inversely with V_DD** — a small reduction in V_DD causes a proportional increase in delay.

**Near-Threshold Regime (V_DD ≈ V_TH):**

The denominator $(V_{DD} - V_{TH})^2$ becomes very small, making the delay blow up. For example, with V_DD = 1.0 V and V_TH = 0.4 V:

$$t_p = \frac{2 C_L \cdot 1.0}{K_n (0.6)^2} = \frac{2 C_L}{K_n \cdot 0.36}$$

If V_DD is reduced to 0.5 V:

$$t_p = \frac{2 C_L \cdot 0.5}{K_n (0.1)^2} = \frac{C_L}{K_n \cdot 0.01}$$

This represents a **36× increase in delay** for a 2× reduction in V_DD.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** Near V_TH, the gate barely turns ON, and the device operates in the subthreshold regime where current drops exponentially.

---

**Part (b) — Multi-VDD Design:**

In a **Multi-VDD** design, the chip is partitioned into multiple **voltage islands**, each operating at its own supply voltage chosen to meet local timing requirements while minimizing power.

**Block Diagram:**

```
    +-------+      +-------+      +-------+
    |       |      |       |      |       |
    | VDD_H |      | VDD_M |      | VDD_L |
    |       |      |       |      |       |
    +---+---+      +---+---+      +---+---+
        |              |              |
        +-------+------+------+-------+
                |             |
            Level Shifter   Level Shifter
                |             |
            +---+---+     +---+---+
            |       |     |       |
            |  Bus  |     |  Bus  |
            +-------+     +-------+
```

**Role of Level Shifters:**

A level shifter is required whenever a signal crosses from one voltage domain to another:
- **Level Shifter Up (LSU):** Boosts a low-V_DD signal to high-V_DD level.
- **Level Shifter Down (LSD):** Steps down a high-V_DD signal to low-V_DD level.

The level shifter must guarantee proper logic level restoration, noise margins, and acceptable delay overhead.

**Clustered Voltage Scaling (CVS):**

In CVS, gates are grouped into clusters such that:
1. All gates within a cluster operate at the **same V_DD**.
2. Critical paths receive **higher V_DD**.
3. Non-critical paths use **lower V_DD**.
4. Level shifters are inserted only at cluster boundaries.

This minimizes the number of level shifters (area and power overhead) while achieving significant energy savings compared to single-V_DD designs.

**Extended CVS (ECVS):** Allows a chain of gates within a critical path to have V_DD assigned *individually* based on slack — providing finer granularity at the cost of more level shifters.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]*

**(a)** Explain the different components of power dissipation in a CMOS inverter. Show with suitable equations how each component scales with V_DD. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss **Multi-VDD** and **Multi-Threshold (Multi-V_TH)** design techniques. How do they complement each other in modern low-power SoCs? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Components of Power Dissipation:**

**1. Dynamic Switching Power:**
Energy is drawn from V_DD to charge C_L during 0 → 1 transitions. Energy per cycle = C_L · V_DD². Averaged:

$$P_{dynamic} = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f_{clk} \quad (\text{Quadratic in } V_{DD})$$

**2. Static (Leakage) Power:**
Even when OFF, subthreshold current flows:

$$P_{static} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{leak} \quad (\text{Linear in } V_{DD})$$

**3. Short-Circuit Power:**
During input transitions, both PMOS and NMOS are momentarily ON:

$$P_{short} = \frac{\beta}{12}(V_{DD} - 2V_{TH})^3 \tau_{in} f_{clk} \quad (\text{Cubic in } V_{DD})$$

**4. Total Power:**

$$P_{total} = P_{dynamic} + P_{static} + P_{short}$$

| Component | V_DD Scaling | Typical Magnitude |
|---|---|---|
| Dynamic | V_DD² | Dominant > 90 nm |
| Static | V_DD | Dominant < 65 nm |
| Short-circuit | V_DD³ | 5–15% |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Take-away:** As technology scales below 65 nm, static power becomes comparable to dynamic power — fundamentally changing the V_DD optimization strategy.

---

**Part (b) — Multi-VDD and Multi-V_TH Techniques:**

**Multi-VDD Design:**
Different functional blocks use different V_DDs based on timing criticality. Critical paths use high V_DD for speed; non-critical paths use low V_DD to save power. Level shifters are mandatory at voltage domain crossings.

**Multi-V_TH Design:**
Multiple threshold voltages are available in the process library:
- **Low-V_TH transistors:** Fast but leaky → used on critical paths.
- **High-V_TH transistors:** Slow but low-leakage → used on non-critical paths.
- **Standard-V_TH transistors:** Balanced tradeoff.

**Equation for leakage reduction with high-V_TH:**

$$I_{leak,high} = I_0 \cdot e^{\frac{-V_{TH,high}}{nV_T}}$$

For every **100 mV increase in V_TH**, leakage reduces by ~10×.

**How They Complement Each Other:**

In modern SoCs:
1. **Multi-VDD** primarily attacks **dynamic power** (quadratic reduction).
2. **Multi-V_TH** primarily attacks **static/leakage power** (exponential reduction).
3. **Combined Strategy:**
   - Critical paths: **High V_DD + Low V_TH** → maximum speed.
   - Non-critical paths: **Low V_DD + High V_TH** → minimum power.
   - Sleep blocks: **Power-gated + High V_TH** → near-zero leakage.

This dual-knob approach is essential for sub-65nm designs where both dynamic and leakage components dominate the power budget.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in V_DD Scaling Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the quadratic nature:** Many students write "dynamic power is proportional to V_DD" — this is WRONG. It is V_DD². Lose 1 mark.
> 2. **Ignoring delay trade-off:** When asked about power reduction via V_DD scaling, ALWAYS mention that delay increases. The examiner expects the trade-off to be acknowledged.
> 3. **Confusing V_DD scaling with V_TH scaling:** V_DD affects both delay and power; V_TH primarily affects leakage and delay. Do not interchange.
> 4. **Omitting level shifters:** In Multi-VDD answers, level shifters MUST be mentioned and their role (LSU vs LSD) clarified. Skipping them costs 2–3 marks.
> 5. **Not drawing diagrams:** Multi-VDD questions REQUIRE a labelled block diagram. Text-only answers are penalized.
> 6. **Wrong constant:** Use C_L V_DD² for energy (not ½C_L V_DD² when computing *power drawn from supply*). The ½ factor applies to *stored* energy, not power-supplied.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Dynamic Power:** $P_{dyn} = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f$ — Quadratic in V_DD, dominant historically.
- [x] **Static Power:** $P_{static} = V_{DD} I_{leak}$ — Linear in V_DD, dominant in modern nodes.
- [x] **Short-Circuit Power:** $\propto (V_{DD} - 2V_{TH})^3 \tau_{in} f$ — Cubic, usually minor.
- [x] **Long-Channel Delay:** $t_p = \dfrac{2 C_L V_{DD}}{K_n (V_{DD} - V_{TH})^2}$
- [x] **Short-Channel Delay (alpha law):** $t_p \propto \dfrac{V_{DD}}{(V_{DD} - V_{TH})^{\alpha}}$
- [x] **High-V_DD Approximation:** $t_p \approx C_L / (K_n V_{DD})$ — inverse proportionality.
- [x] **Near-Threshold Penalty:** Delay explodes as V_DD → V_TH (subthreshold regime).
- [x] **Subthreshold Slope:** ~90 mV/decade at 300 K (theoretical minimum).
- [x] **Multi-VDD Definition:** Partitioning chip into voltage islands with different V_DDs.
- [x] **Level Shifters:** Required at all voltage-domain crossings (LSU for low→high, LSD for high→low).
- [x] **CVS (Clustered Voltage Scaling):** Gates grouped into clusters; same V_DD within cluster; level shifters only at cluster boundaries.
- [x] **ECVS (Extended CVS):** Individual gate-level V_DD assignment for finer optimization.
- [x] **Multi-V_TH Complement:** Multi-VDD reduces dynamic power; Multi-V_TH reduces leakage.
- [x] **Optimal V_DD:** Choose minimum V_DD that satisfies timing constraints at the target clock frequency.
- [x] **Energy-Delay Product (EDP):** A combined metric for V_DD optimization trade-offs.
- [x] **Physical Constants to Memorize:** $V_T = kT/q \approx 25.85$ mV at 300 K; subthreshold slope ≈ 90 mV/dec.
- [x] **Process Parameters:** K_n (transconductance), C_L (load), α (activity factor), f_clk (frequency).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dynamic VDD
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEECT866 - LOW POWER VLSI > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Dynamic VDD ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard) **Dynamic VDD Scaling (DVS)** is a run-time, workload-adaptive low-power design technique in which the supply voltage ($V_{DD}$) of a digital CMOS circuit or system is dynamically adjusted **on-the-fly** in response to changing computational performance demands. When full throughput is not required, $V_{DD}$ is lowered to exploit the **quadratic** reduction in dynamic switching power and the **exponential** reduction in subthreshold leakage current; when performance peaks are detected, $V_{DD}$ is raised back to its nominal level to meet the timing constraint of the **critical path**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Terminology Lock:** In KTU board exam answers, the term *Dynamic VDD* is also known as **Dynamic Voltage Scaling (DVS)** or, when combined with frequency adjustment, **Dynamic Voltage and Frequency Scaling (DVFS)**. The principle is the same — the rail voltage is *not* a constant; it is a *time-varying control variable*. The complete family of techniques includes: - **DVS** – Voltage only changes; clock frequency held by a PLL/DLL - **DVFS** – Both voltage and frequency co-vary - **AVS (Adaptive Voltage Scaling)** – Closed-loop feedback using on-chip performance monitors - **DPM (Dynamic Power Management)** – Often used in conjunction with DVS --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Analogy: The Car Cruise Control on a Hilly Road** > > Imagine driving a car up and down hills. On a flat highway at steady 60 km/h, you need very little throttle (energy). On a steep uphill climb, you floor the accelerator to maintain speed. On a downhill, you cut fuel completely. > > A processor running a video is the "highway" — steady load. A processor waking from idle to handle a button-press is the "uphill climb." A traditional fixed-$V_{DD}$ design is like a car that *always* floors the accelerator — it works, but burns enormous fuel even when cruising. > > **Dynamic VDD is the cruise control.** A small on-chip "controller" watches the workload. If the queue of pending tasks is short, it lowers the rail voltage so the transistors switch slower but consume quadratically less power. If the queue grows long (meaning we are falling behind), it raises the rail voltage and the clock so the processor catches up. The *average* power is slashed, sometimes by **5×–10×**, while throughput remains acceptable. --- ### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants Used | Symbol | Quantity | Typical CMOS Value | Unit | |---|---|---|---| | $V_{DD}$ | Supply rail voltage | **0.3 – 1.2** | V | | $V_{th}$ | Threshold voltage | **0.2 – 0.4** | V | | $\alpha$ | Switching activity factor | **0 – 1** | dimensionless | | $C_L$ | Load capacitance | **fF – pF** | F | | $f_{clk}$ | Clock frequency | **10 MHz – 3 GHz** | Hz | | $I_{leak}$ | Subthreshold leakage current | **nA – µA** | A | | $K$ | Boltzmann constant | $1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ | J/K | --- ### 1.4 Why Dynamic $V_{DD}$ is a Cornerstone Topic for KTU Low-Power VLSI > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU 2024 Scheme Module 3 explicitly tests whether the student can *quantify* the power savings of DVS. A direct 7-mark question of the form *"Derive the relationship between dynamic power and $V_{DD}$ scaling, and show how DVS affects the energy-delay product"* is a recurring board pattern. Memorizing $P = \alpha C V^2 f$ is **insufficient** — you must show the *cubic* law of DVFS. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Three Power Components Affected by $V_{DD}$ A CMOS gate draws three distinct power currents. Dynamic VDD scaling interacts with all three in *different* ways: 1. **Dynamic switching power** — proportional to **$V_{DD}^2$** 2. **Short-circuit power** — proportional to $V_{DD}$ and the rise/fall times 3. **Static (leakage) power** — exponentially dependent on $V_{DD}$ via the subthreshold slope The dominant component in modern nanometer CMOS is the **dynamic switching power**: $$P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f_{clk}$$ Because the relationship is **quadratic**, even a modest 30% drop in $V_{DD}$ (e.g., 1.0 V → 0.7 V) cuts dynamic power by **$(0.7/1.0)^2 = 0.49$**, i.e., a **~51% reduction** for no loss of work done. --- ### 2.2 How Transistor Delay Governs the Lower Bound of $V_{DD}$ The propagation delay of a CMOS inverter is given by the alpha-power law model: $$t_d = \frac{K \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}}{(V_{DD} - V_{th})^{\alpha}}$$ where $K$ is a process-dependent constant and $\alpha \approx 1.3$ for short-channel devices. **Key physical insight:** As $V_{DD} \to V_{th}$, the denominator $(V_{DD} - V_{th}) \to 0$, and the delay **diverges to infinity**. This sets the **lower physical limit** on how low dynamic $V_{DD}$ may be driven while still meeting the clock-period budget $T_{clk}$: $$t_d(V_{DD}) \leq T_{clk} = \frac{1}{f_{clk}}$$ --- ### 2.3 The DVFS Cubic Power Law (THE Most Tested Equation) > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Cubic Law** is the single highest-weight derivation for KTU board exams in this topic. When **both** $V_{DD}$ and $f_{clk}$ are scaled by the same factor $k$ (i.e., $V_{DD}' = k V_{DD}$ and $f_{clk}' = k f_{clk}$), the dynamic power scales as $k^3$: $$P' = \alpha C (k V_{DD})^2 (k f) = k^3 \alpha C V_{DD}^2 f = k^3 P$$ **Why?** The frequency can be reduced by $k$ because the gate delay has *increased* by roughly $1/k$ at the lower voltage, and the circuit can therefore still complete one operation per clock period at the lower clock rate. The throughput is preserved (same operations per unit real time), but the power drops by a **cubic** factor. --- ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!TIP] > **Memorize this table verbatim. Every symbol, every boundary condition.** | # | Formula | Description | Unit | Boundary / Limit | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | $P_{dyn} = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f_{clk}$ | Switching power | W | Valid for $\alpha > 0$ | | 2 | $P_{static} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{leak}$ | Static leakage | W | Dominates at low $f$ | | 3 | $I_{leak} = I_0 \exp\!\left(\dfrac{V_{th} - V_{DD}}{n V_T}\right)$ | Subthreshold current | A | $V_T = kT/q \approx 26$ mV | | 4 | $t_d = \dfrac{K C_L V_{DD}}{(V_{DD} - V_{th})^{\alpha}}$ | Alpha-power delay | s | Diverges at $V_{DD} = V_{th}$ | | 5 | $P_{DVFS} = k^3 P$ | Cubic scaling law | W | When $V_{DD}' = kV_{DD}$ and $f' = kf$ | | 6 | $E_{op} = C_L V_{DD}^2$ | Energy per switching event | J | Independent of $f$ | | 7 | $PDP = P \cdot t_d$ | Power-delay product | J | Figure of merit | | 8 | $EDP = E \cdot t_d$ | Energy-delay product | J·s | Better FOM for low-power | | 9 | $V_{DD,min} = V_{th} + \sqrt{\dfrac{I_{sat}}{\beta}}$ | Min operating voltage (subthreshold design) | V | Sub-$V_{th}$ logic | | 10 | $T_{clk} \geq t_{d,critical}(V_{DD})$ | Timing constraint | s | Hard real-time bound | > [!WARNING] > **Notation Trap (KTU examiners deduct 1 mark):** Write $V_{DD}$ with both **D's as subscripts**. Do NOT write $V_d$, $Vdd$, or $V_{dd}$. Also, $V_{th}$ stands for *threshold*, not *thermal* — do not confuse with $V_T = kT/q$. --- ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility Dynamic $V_{DD}$ is **not academic** — it is in every modern device you own: - **Intel SpeedStep®, AMD PowerNow!, ARM big.LITTLE DVFS** — all laptops and phones - **Qualcomm Snapdragon, Apple A-series SoCs** — multi-rail DVS for CPU, GPU, and memory controller - **FPGA fabric (Xilinx, Intel/Altera)** — selectable performance-grade voltage regulators per logic block > [!NOTE] > The KTU 2024 syllabus specifically asks students to *appreciate* these real systems. In a 14-mark answer, dropping the names of two real DVFS implementations adds 1–2 valuation marks for "contextual awareness." --- ### 2.6 Practical Hardware Requirements for Dynamic $V_{DD}$ For DVS to be physically realized, **three** on-chip hardware blocks are mandatory: 1. **Variable-output voltage regulator** — must respond in µs, not ms. Usually an on-chip switching DC-DC converter or a low-dropout (LDO) regulator with a digital control word. 2. **Variable-frequency clock generator** — a Digitally Controlled Oscillator (DCO) or a PLL with reprogrammable divider. 3. **Workload / performance monitor** — a hardware task queue, or an on-chip critical-path replica (the "**canary circuit**") that predicts whether the real critical path will fail at the chosen voltage. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Reactive vs. Predictive DVS:** > - *Reactive* — measures actual workload (e.g., CPU utilization) and reacts > - *Predictive* — uses a *replica* of the critical path running at scaled voltage; if the replica fails, voltage is raised > The predictive (canary) method is the KTU-preferred example in board answers. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Dynamic Power as a Function of $V_{DD}$ This is the foundational derivation. KTU expects to see every step. **Starting point — the energy stored in a capacitor.** When a CMOS gate charges its output node capacitance $C_L$ from 0 to $V_{DD}$, the energy drawn from the supply rail is: $$E_{draw} = \int_{0}^{\infty} V_{DD} \cdot i(t)\,dt = V_{DD} \cdot Q = V_{DD} \cdot C_L V_{DD} = C_L V_{DD}^2$$ **Step 1 — The energy stored in the capacitor itself is exactly half of this:** $$E_{stored} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$$ **Step 2 — The other half is dissipated as heat in the pull-up PMOS during charging:** $$E_{dissipated,charge} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$$ **Step 3 — When the node discharges, the other half of the energy is dissipated in the pull-down NMOS.** Thus one complete 0→1→0 cycle consumes: $$E_{cycle} = C_L V_{DD}^2$$ **Step 4 — If the gate switches at a rate $\alpha f_{clk}$ (where $\alpha$ is the activity factor, $0 \leq \alpha \leq 1$), the average dynamic power is:** $$\begin{aligned} P_{dyn} &= E_{cycle} \cdot \alpha \cdot f_{clk} \\ &= \alpha \, C_L \, V_{DD}^2 \, f_{clk} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 5 — Replacing $V_{DD}$ with the dynamic variable $V_{DD}(t)$:** $$\boxed{P_{dyn}(t) = \alpha \, C_L \, V_{DD}(t)^2 \, f_{clk}(t)}$$ This is the **central equation** of dynamic $V_{DD}$ scaling. The instantaneous power is a *time-varying* quadratic function of the rail voltage. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — The DVFS Cubic Power Law (Full Algebraic Walk-Through) **Given:** A workload executes $N$ operations in a real time interval $T$. Under nominal conditions, $V_{DD} = V_0$ and $f = f_0$. Under DVFS, $V_{DD} = k V_0$ and $f = k f_0$ where $0 < k \leq 1$. **Step 1 — Nominal dynamic power:** $$P_0 = \alpha C V_0^2 f_0$$ **Step 2 — Scaled dynamic power:** $$P' = \alpha C (kV_0)^2 (k f_0) = \alpha C \cdot k^2 V_0^2 \cdot k f_0 = k^3 \alpha C V_0^2 f_0$$ **Step 3 — Form the ratio:** $$\frac{P'}{P_0} = k^3 \quad\Rightarrow\quad \boxed{P' = k^3 P_0}$$ **Step 4 — Energy spent in both cases (same $N$ operations):** $$E_0 = P_0 \cdot T = N \cdot C V_0^2$$ $$E' = P' \cdot \frac{T}{k} = k^3 P_0 \cdot \frac{T}{k} = k^2 N C V_0^2 = k^2 E_0$$ **Step 5 — Total energy reduction ratio:** $$\boxed{\frac{E'}{E_0} = k^2}$$ **Conclusion:** When DVFS maintains *constant throughput*, the total energy per task drops as $k^2$, while the average power drops as $k^3$. This is the **figure of merit** KTU examiners want you to derive from first principles. --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Lower Bound on $V_{DD}$ From the Critical Path Constraint **Problem statement.** A digital block has a critical path delay of $t_{cp}(V_{DD})$. The system clock has period $T_{clk}$. Find the minimum supply voltage that still meets timing. **Step 1 — Write the alpha-power delay expression for the critical path:** $$t_{cp}(V_{DD}) = \frac{K_{cp}\,C_{cp}\,V_{DD}}{(V_{DD} - V_{th})^{\alpha}}$$ **Step 2 — Apply the timing constraint:** $$t_{cp}(V_{DD}) \leq T_{clk} = \frac{1}{f_{clk}}$$ **Step 3 — Solve for $V_{DD}$.** The equation is non-linear in $V_{DD}$ and is solved numerically in practice, but a useful *closed-form approximation* for $\alpha = 1$ is: $$\frac{K_{cp} C_{cp} V_{DD}}{V_{DD} - V_{th}} = T_{clk}$$ **Step 4 — Multiply out:** $$K_{cp} C_{cp} V_{DD} = T_{clk}(V_{DD} - V_{th})$$ $$K_{cp} C_{cp} V_{DD} = T_{clk} V_{DD} - T_{clk} V_{th}$$ $$T_{clk} V_{DD} - K_{cp} C_{cp} V_{DD} = T_{clk} V_{th}$$ $$V_{DD}(T_{clk} - K_{cp} C_{cp}) = T_{clk} V_{th}$$ $$\boxed{V_{DD,min} = \frac{T_{clk}\,V_{th}}{T_{clk} - K_{cp} C_{cp}}}$$ **Step 5 — Sanity check.** As $T_{clk} \to \infty$ (infinitely slow clock), $V_{DD,min} \to V_{th}$, confirming the earlier statement that $V_{th}$ is the theoretical floor. --- ### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Energy-Delay Product Under Dynamic VDD **The EDP** is the gold-standard figure of merit in low-power VLSI design. **Step 1 — Energy per operation:** $$E_{op} = C_L V_{DD}^2$$ **Step 2 — Delay per operation (using $\alpha = 1$ for simplicity):** $$t_d = \frac{K C_L V_{DD}}{V_{DD} - V_{th}}$$ **Step 3 — Product:** $$EDP = E_{op} \cdot t_d = \frac{K C_L^2 V_{DD}^3}{V_{DD} - V_{th}}$$ **Step 4 — Minimize with respect to $V_{DD}$.** Set $\dfrac{d(EDP)}{dV_{DD}} = 0$: $$\begin{aligned} \frac{d(EDP)}{dV_{DD}} &= \frac{3K C_L^2 V_{DD}^2 (V_{DD} - V_{th}) - K C_L^2 V_{DD}^3}{(V_{DD} - V_{th})^2} = 0 \\ &\Rightarrow 3 V_{DD}^2 (V_{DD} - V_{th}) - V_{DD}^3 = 0 \\ &\Rightarrow V_{DD}^2 \bigl[3(V_{DD} - V_{th}) - V_{DD}\bigr] = 0 \\ &\Rightarrow 3V_{DD} - 3V_{th} - V_{DD} = 0 \\ &\Rightarrow 2V_{DD} = 3V_{th} \\ &\boxed{V_{DD,opt}^{EDP} = 1.5\,V_{th}} \end{aligned}$$ **Conclusion — *this is a favourite KTU exam punchline:*** the EDP-minimizing supply voltage is **1.5 times the threshold voltage**, irrespective of the load capacitance or process constant $K$. --- ### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Equation | If the question asks for… | Use… | Marks usually awarded | |---|---|---| | Power at one $V_{DD}$ value | $P = \alpha C V^2 f$ | 2 | | Power ratio after scaling | $P' = k^3 P$ | 4 | | Minimum operating voltage | $V_{DD,min} = T_{clk} V_{th}/(T_{clk} - K_{cp} C_{cp})$ | 5 | | Energy ratio after scaling | $E' = k^2 E$ | 3 | | Optimal $V_{DD}$ for EDP | $1.5 V_{th}$ | 4 | | Static + dynamic total | $P_{total} = \alpha C V^2 f + V I_{leak}$ | 3 | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 High-Level DVFS System Block Diagram The following Mermaid diagram shows how the workload sensor, voltage regulator, and clock generator interact. Every node has an alphanumeric ID and uses plain uppercase text inside quotes (no markdown formatting inside labels). ```mermaid flowchart LR A[WORKLOAD SENSOR] -->|task queue depth| B[DVFS CONTROLLER POLICY] B -->|digital control word| C[VARIABLE VOLTAGE REGULATOR] B -->|divider ratio| D[PHASE LOCKED LOOP] C -->|scaled VDD rail| E[CMOS PROCESSING CORE] D -->|scaled clock| E E -->|measured performance| F[CANARY CRITICAL PATH REPLICA] F -->|timing margin signal| B E -->|temperature and aging drift| G[RELIABILITY MONITOR] G -->|Vth shift compensation| B H[STATIC TIMING ANALYSIS LIBRARY] -->|V to delay curve| B ``` **Block-by-block explanation:** - **WORKLOAD SENSOR** — reads CPU utilization, IPC (instructions per cycle), or queue depth - **DVFS CONTROLLER POLICY** — executes the chosen algorithm (linear, PID, lookup-table based) - **VARIABLE VOLTAGE REGULATOR** — on-chip DC-DC converter with µs-scale response - **PHASE LOCKED LOOP** — frequency synthesizer, programmed by integer-N divider - **CANARY CRITICAL PATH REPLICA** — duplicate of the longest logic path, run at the same $V_{DD}$ - **RELIABILITY MONITOR** — compensates for $V_{th}$ drift due to NBTI (Negative Bias Temperature Instability) and aging - **STATIC TIMING ANALYSIS LIBRARY** — provides the static $t_d(V_{DD})$ curve for the controller --- ### 4.2 Decision Flowchart — When to Raise or Lower $V_{DD}$ ```mermaid flowchart TD S0[START NEW CYCLE] --> S1{IS QUEUE EMPTY} S1 -- YES --> S2[ENTER IDLE STATE
VDD equals VRETENTION] S1 -- NO --> S3{IS QUEUE BELOW LOW WATERMARK} S3 -- YES --> S4[SCALE DOWN VDD AND F BY ONE STEP] S3 -- NO --> S5{IS QUEUE ABOVE HIGH WATERMARK} S5 -- YES --> S6[SCALE UP VDD AND F BY ONE STEP] S5 -- NO --> S7[KEEP CURRENT VDD AND F] S4 --> S8[WAIT FOR VOLTAGE RAMP TIME DELTA T V] S6 --> S9[WAIT FOR VOLTAGE RAMP TIME DELTA T V] S7 --> S10[PROCEED TO NEXT CYCLE] S8 --> S10 S9 --> S10 S2 --> S10 ``` **Why this diagram is exam-relevant:** The **ramp time $\Delta T_V$** (the time the regulator needs to reach the new rail voltage) is *itself* a hidden source of energy overhead. A regulator that takes 50 µs to step from 1.0 V to 0.7 V will *waste energy* if the workload demand changes faster than that. KTU questions often test this overhead: *for what clock period does DVS stop being worthwhile?* --- ### 4.3 Power vs $V_{DD}$ Trade-off Curve (Conceptual) The following diagram is the classic *P vs V* Pareto curve that appears in every KTU lecture slide. It is rendered as a flowchart-style block diagram to keep Mermaid compatibility while conveying the physics. ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[HIGH PERFORMANCE REGION
VDD greater than 0.9 VOLT
P dominant term is DYNAMIC] --> T1[TRANSITION REGION
VDD around 0.6 to 0.8 VOLT
DELAY RISES SHARPLY] T1 --> P2[LOW POWER REGION
VDD between VTH and 0.6 VOLT
LEAKAGE EQUALS DYNAMIC POWER] P2 --> T2[NEAR THRESHOLD REGION
VDD between 1.1 VTH and 1.5 VTH
EDP MINIMUM] T2 --> P3[SUBTHRESHOLD REGION
VDD below VTH
LEAKAGE DOMINATES
SLOWEST OPERATION] ``` **Critical VDD landmarks on this curve (KTU 2024 must-know):** | Region | $V_{DD}$ range | Trade-off | |---|---|---| | High performance | $> 0.9$ V | Dynamic power dominates | | Transition | 0.6 – 0.8 V | Delay begins to climb steeply | | Low power sweet spot | 0.4 – 0.6 V | Dynamic and leakage comparable | | Near-threshold (EDP-optimum) | $\approx 1.5 V_{th}$ | Best figure of merit | | Subthreshold | $< V_{th}$ | Ultra-low power, very slow | --- ### 4.4 Closed-Loop DVFS Control with Critical-Path Replica ```mermaid flowchart TD U[USER APPLICATION LAYER] --> Q[TASK QUEUE OCCUPANCY] Q --> CTRL[DVFS HARDWARE CONTROLLER] CTRL -->|binary code VSET| REG[ON CHIP DC DC CONVERTER] REG -->|actual VDD| CORE[CMOS CORE LOGIC] REG -->|actual VDD| REPL[CRITICAL PATH REPLICA CIRCUIT] CORE -->|propagation delay measured| REPL REPL -->|setup margin voltage signal MARGIN| CTRL CTRL -->|frequency code FSET| PLL[VOLTAGE CONTROLLED OSCILLATOR] PLL -->|clock CLK| CORE S[SOFT SENSOR FOR PROCESS AND TEMPERATURE] --> CTRL ``` **Why the replica circuit matters:** In a *predictive* DVFS scheme, the controller does not wait for the real core to fail. It runs an *intentionally slower* duplicate of the critical path at the same $V_{DD}$. If the replica violates setup time, the controller immediately raises the rail voltage — typically within a few microseconds — *before* the real circuit produces an error. This is the architectural pattern used in **Intel's "Razor"** flip-flops and in many ARM Cortex cores. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper – Set B]** **Q1.** Define **Dynamic Voltage Scaling (DVS)** and state **two** practical reasons why the supply voltage cannot be scaled all the way down to 0 V. **Model Answer (3 marks):** Dynamic Voltage Scaling (DVS) is a low-power design technique in which the supply voltage $V_{DD}$ of a CMOS circuit is **dynamically adjusted at run time** to meet instantaneous performance demands, exploiting the quadratic dependence of dynamic power on $V_{DD}$. **Two reasons $V_{DD}$ cannot be reduced to 0 V:** 1. **Critical-path delay constraint:** As $V_{DD}$ approaches the threshold voltage $V_{th}$, the propagation delay $t_d = \dfrac{K C V_{DD}}{(V_{DD} - V_{th})^{\alpha}}$ **diverges to infinity**. The circuit cannot meet its clock-period budget below a finite lower bound, typically around $1.5 V_{th}$ for the EDP-optimal point. 2. **Signal integrity and noise margin:** CMOS logic levels are defined relative to $V_{DD}$. As $V_{DD}$ shrinks, the noise margin shrinks proportionally, making the circuit vulnerable to coupling noise, soft errors, and process/voltage/temperature (PVT) variation. Below a few hundred millivolts the logic is functionally unreliable. *(Examiner's note: 1 mark for the definition, 1 mark per correct reason with justification.)* --- > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Sample Paper]** **Q2.** What is the **DVFS cubic power law**? Mention **one** on-chip hardware block required to implement it. **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **DVFS cubic power law** states that when both the supply voltage and the clock frequency of a digital system are scaled by the same factor $k$ (with $0 < k \leq 1$), the dynamic power consumption reduces as the cube of the scaling factor: $$P' = k^3 P$$ **Hardware block required:** A **digitally controlled on-chip DC-DC voltage regulator** (e.g., a buck converter with digital PWM control), together with a **variable-frequency clock generator** such as a PLL with programmable divider. *(Examiner's note: 2 marks for the equation and the statement, 1 mark for the hardware block.)* --- ### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks) > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Question Paper – Module 3]** --- #### **Question A (14 marks)** **(a)** Derive, from first principles, the expression for **dynamic switching power** of a CMOS inverter as a function of $V_{DD}$ and clock frequency $f_{clk}$. Clearly state any assumptions made. **\[7 marks\]** **(b)** A processor is operating at $V_{DD} = 1.2$ V, $f_{clk} = 1$ GHz, with effective switched capacitance $C = 5$ pF and activity factor $\alpha = 0.2$. It now enters a DVFS low-power mode where both $V_{DD}$ and $f_{clk}$ are scaled down by a factor $k = 0.6$. Compute: - (i) The dynamic power before scaling. **\[2 marks\]** - (ii) The dynamic power after scaling, using the cubic law. **\[2 marks\]** - (iii) The percentage reduction in **energy per task** (throughput preserved). **\[3 marks\]** --- ### Model Solution — Question A #### Part (a) — Derivation of dynamic switching power **Assumption 1:** The output node is modelled as a lumped capacitance $C_L$ to ground. **Assumption 2:** The PMOS pull-up network and NMOS pull-down network are ideal switches (zero on-resistance) and the power-rail source is ideal (no series resistance). **Step 1 — Charging the output node from 0 to $V_{DD}$:** The instantaneous current is $i(t) = C_L \dfrac{dv}{dt}$. The energy drawn from the supply rail during charging is: $$E_{charge} = \int_{0}^{T_{charge}} V_{DD} \cdot i(t)\,dt = V_{DD} \int_{0}^{V_{DD}} C_L\,dv = C_L V_{DD}^2$$ **Step 2 — Energy stored in the capacitor itself:** $$E_{cap} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$$ **Step 3 — Energy dissipated as heat in the pull-up PMOS:** $$E_{PMOS} = E_{charge} - E_{cap} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$$ **Step 4 — Discharging the node from $V_{DD}$ to 0:** The energy stored in the capacitor is dissipated as heat in the pull-down NMOS, again $\frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$. **Step 5 — Energy for one full 0→1→0 switching cycle:** $$E_{cycle} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2 + \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2 = C_L V_{DD}^2$$ **Step 6 — Multiply by the number of switching events per second $\alpha f_{clk}$:** $$\boxed{P_{dyn} = \alpha \, C_L \, V_{DD}^2 \, f_{clk}}$$ --- #### Part (b) — Numerical computation **Given:** $V_{DD} = 1.2$ V, $f_{clk} = 1 \times 10^9$ Hz, $C = 5 \times 10^{-12}$ F, $\alpha = 0.2$, $k = 0.6$. **(i) Dynamic power before scaling:** $$\begin{aligned} P_0 &= \alpha \, C \, V_{DD}^2 \, f_{clk} \\ &= 0.2 \times 5 \times 10^{-12} \times (1.2)^2 \times 10^9 \\ &= 0.2 \times 5 \times 10^{-12} \times 1.44 \times 10^9 \\ &= 0.2 \times 7.2 \times 10^{-3} \\ &= 1.44 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{W} \\ &= \boxed{1.44\;\text{mW}} \end{aligned}$$ **(ii) Dynamic power after DVFS scaling:** $$\begin{aligned} P' &= k^3 P_0 = (0.6)^3 \times 1.44\;\text{mW} \\ &= 0.216 \times 1.44\;\text{mW} \\ &= \boxed{0.311\;\text{mW}} \end{aligned}$$ **(iii) Energy per task reduction:** Since throughput is preserved, the *time* per task stretches by a factor $1/k$. Energy per task in the scaled mode: $$\begin{aligned} E' &= P' \times \frac{T}{k} = \frac{0.311\;\text{mW}}{0.6} \times T = 0.518\,T\;\text{mW·s} \\ E_0 &= P_0 \times T = 1.44\,T\;\text{mW·s} \\ \text{Reduction} &= \left(1 - \frac{0.518}{1.44}\right) \times 100\% \\ &= (1 - 0.36) \times 100\% \\ &= \boxed{64\%\;\text{reduction}} \end{aligned}$$ **Cross-check via the $k^2$ law:** $$\frac{E'}{E_0} = k^2 = 0.36 \quad\Rightarrow\quad \text{reduction} = 1 - 0.36 = 64\% \;\;\checkmark$$ --- #### **Question B (14 marks) — Alternative Choice** **(a)** Explain the **energy-delay product (EDP)** as a figure of merit for low-power VLSI design. Show that the EDP of a CMOS gate is minimized when $V_{DD} = 1.5\,V_{th}$. **\[7 marks\]** **(b)** A wireless sensor node operates in two modes: - **Active mode:** $V_{DD} = 1.0$ V, $f_{clk} = 50$ MHz, duty cycle = 10% - **Sleep mode:** $V_{DD} = 0.3$ V (data retention), leakage current $I_{leak} = 1\;\mu$A The load capacitance is $C = 2$ pF and activity factor $\alpha = 0.15$. Compute the **average power consumption** and comment on whether dynamic $V_{DD}$ is beneficial. **\[7 marks\]** --- ### Model Solution — Question B #### Part (a) — EDP Derivation **Step 1 — Define EDP.** The Energy-Delay Product combines energy per operation and propagation delay into a single figure of merit that simultaneously penalizes high energy and slow operation: $$EDP = E_{op} \times t_d$$ **Step 2 — Substitute the energy and delay expressions:** $$EDP = C_L V_{DD}^2 \times \frac{K C_L V_{DD}}{(V_{DD} - V_{th})^{\alpha}} = \frac{K C_L^2 V_{DD}^3}{(V_{DD} - V_{th})^{\alpha}}$$ **Step 3 — For analytical tractability, take $\alpha = 1$:** $$EDP(V_{DD}) = \frac{K C_L^2 V_{DD}^3}{V_{DD} - V_{th}}$$ **Step 4 — Minimize by taking the derivative and setting it to zero:** $$\frac{d(EDP)}{dV_{DD}} = \frac{3K C_L^2 V_{DD}^2 (V_{DD} - V_{th}) - K C_L^2 V_{DD}^3}{(V_{DD} - V_{th})^2} = 0$$ **Step 5 — Numerator simplification:** $$3V_{DD}^2 (V_{DD} - V_{th}) - V_{DD}^3 = 0$$ $$3V_{DD}^3 - 3V_{DD}^2 V_{th} - V_{DD}^3 = 0$$ $$2V_{DD}^3 = 3V_{DD}^2 V_{th}$$ $$2V_{DD} = 3V_{th}$$ $$\boxed{V_{DD,opt} = 1.5\,V_{th}}$$ **Interpretation:** The result is *independent* of $C_L$ and $K$, depending only on the threshold voltage. For a process with $V_{th} = 0.3$ V, the EDP-optimal supply is **0.45 V** — far below the conventional 1.0 V rail. --- #### Part (b) — Sensor-node power budget **Step 1 — Active-mode dynamic power:** $$P_{active} = \alpha C V_{DD}^2 f_{clk} = 0.15 \times 2\times10^{-12} \times (1.0)^2 \times 50\times10^6$$ $$= 0.15 \times 2 \times 10^{-12} \times 5 \times 10^7 = 0.15 \times 10^{-4} = 1.5 \times 10^{-5}\;\text{W} = 15\;\mu\text{W}$$ **Step 2 — Sleep-mode static power:** $$P_{sleep} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{leak} = 0.3 \times 1\times10^{-6} = 3 \times 10^{-7}\;\text{W} = 0.3\;\mu\text{W}$$ **Step 3 — Average power with duty cycle 10% active, 90% sleep:** $$P_{avg} = 0.1 \times 15\;\mu\text{W} + 0.9 \times 0.3\;\mu\text{W} = 1.5 + 0.27 = \boxed{1.77\;\mu\text{W}}$$ **Step 4 — Comment on DVS benefit.** Without DVS, the node would run continuously at 15 µW, giving an average of 15 µW — about **8.5× higher** than the DVS-enabled value. Dynamic $V_{DD}$ (combined with power gating to enter sleep) is therefore *highly beneficial* for energy-harvesting wireless sensor nodes. The dominant energy savings come from **dropping the rail voltage during active low-compute intervals** AND from **power gating to retention voltage during sleep**. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Top 5 Ways Students Lose Marks on Dynamic VDD Questions (KTU 2024)** > > 1. **Forgetting the $k^2$ energy law** — they correctly state $P' = k^3 P$ but then forget to multiply by the time-stretching factor $1/k$ when computing per-task energy. *Always convert power to energy.* > 2. **Confusing $V_{DD}$ and $V_{th}$ in the EDP derivation** — slipping in a $V_{th}$ that should be $V_{DD}$ is the single most common algebraic error.
