# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Q: Define Differential Cascode Voltage Switch Logic (DCVS). List any two advantages of DCVS over standard static CMOS logic.**

#### Model Answer (3 marks):

> **Definition (2 marks):**
> DCVS is a differential CMOS logic family that uses an NMOS pull-down logic tree with two complementary branches and a PMOS cross-coupled latch to provide both true (Q) and complement (Q̅) outputs simultaneously. The output is sensed differentially, and the cross-coupled PMOS latch provides regenerative positive feedback for fast switching.

> **Advantages (any two, ½ mark each):**
> 1. **Complementary outputs come for free** — no extra inverter needed, saving area and power.
> 2. **No static current path** between VDD and GND ⇒ very low static power dissipation.
> 3. **High noise immunity** due to differential sensing and regenerative latch gain.
> 4. **Smaller input capacitance** since only NMOS devices form the logic tree.

---

### Question 2 — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Q: What is a clocked CMOS design style? Explain the concept of precharge and evaluate phases in 3 lines.**

#### Model Answer (3 marks):

> **Definition (1 mark):**
> A clocked CMOS (dynamic) design style uses a global clock signal to control precharge and evaluation phases, allowing internal nodes to be precharged to a known voltage during one phase and conditionally discharged during the other.

> **Precharge phase (1 mark):**
> When CLK = LOW, the clocked PMOS precharge transistor is ON, charging the internal dynamic node X to VDD. The clocked NMOS evaluate transistor is OFF, isolating the PDN.

> **Evaluate phase (1 mark):**
> When CLK = HIGH, the clocked PMOS is OFF and the clocked NMOS is ON. The PDN either conducts (discharging X to GND) or remains OFF (X stays at VDD). The output is valid only during this phase.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A — [KTU University Exam — Module 4, Dec 2024 — Internal Choice 1]**

#### **(a) With a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a Differential Cascode Voltage Switch Logic (DCVS) gate. Show how an XOR function is implemented using DCVS. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Step 1 — DCVS Topology Description (2 marks):**

A DCVS gate consists of:
- **PMOS cross-coupled latch** (transistors MP1 and MP2) connected between VDD and the two output nodes Q and Q̅.
- **NMOS logic tree** with two complementary branches — one evaluating $F$, the other evaluating $\bar{F}$.
- Both branches share a common source connection to GND.
- Outputs Q and Q̅ are *always* driven; no high-impedance floating state.

**Step 2 — Operational Principle (2 marks):**

When inputs cause the $F$-branch to conduct, Q is pulled toward GND. The cross-coupled latch senses this voltage drop on Q and rapidly turns ON MP2 (whose gate is tied to Q), pulling Q̅ firmly to VDD. This regenerative action makes Q̅ ≈ VDD very quickly. Conversely, when $\bar{F}$-branch conducts, Q̅ is pulled low and Q is pulled high.

**Step 3 — XOR Implementation using DCVS (3 marks):**

For XOR function $F = A \oplus B = A\bar{B} + \bar{A}B$:
- **F-branch (NMOS tree for F = A ⊕ B):** Two series NMOS pairs in parallel: ($M_{A1}$ in series with $M_{\bar{B}1}$) ∥ ($M_{\bar{A}1}$ in series with $M_{B1}$). This branch conducts when $(A \cdot \bar{B}) + (\bar{A} \cdot B) = 1$.
- **F̅-branch (NMOS tree for F̅ = A ⊙ B = AB + ĀB̅):** Two series NMOS pairs in parallel: ($M_{A2}$ in series with $M_{B2}$) ∥ ($M_{\bar{A}2}$ in series with $M_{\bar{B}2}$). This branch conducts when $AB + \bar{A}\bar{B} = 1$.

The two complementary branches together use **only NMOS transistors** for the entire XOR logic — saving 4 PMOS transistors compared to static CMOS XOR.

| A | B | F = A⊕B | F̅ branch conducts? |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | 0 | Yes (Ā·B̅) |
| 0 | 1 | 1 | No (F-branch A·B̅ conducts) |
| 1 | 0 | 1 | No (F-branch Ā·B conducts) |
| 1 | 1 | 0 | Yes (A·B) |

The PMOS cross-coupled latch provides fast rail-to-rail outputs Q and Q̅.

---

#### **(b) Discuss the advantages and limitations of DCVS. Compare DCVS with static CMOS in terms of transistor count, power, and speed for an N-input XOR gate. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Step 1 — Advantages of DCVS (2 marks):**

1. **Both polarities of output** (Q, Q̅) are available simultaneously — eliminates need for an output inverter in many designs.
2. **Lower input capacitance** since logic tree is NMOS-only ⇒ faster input transition.
3. **No DC power consumption** — full-rail outputs with no crowbar current path.
4. **High noise immunity** due to differential sensing.
5. **Suitable for low-power, high-speed arithmetic** like adders, multiplexers.

**Step 2 — Limitations of DCVS (2 marks):**

1. **Threshold drop issue** — PMOS latch may leave rising output node at $V_{DD} - |V_{Tp}|$, requiring weak keeper or feedback.
2. **Complementary logic trees required** — both F and F̅ must be implemented ⇒ roughly 2× NMOS count vs. static CMOS PDN.
3. **Cross-coupled latch can enter metastable state** if inputs switch simultaneously (rare but possible).
4. **Routing complexity** — every signal must be routed in true/complement pairs.
5. **Charge sharing** in dynamic versions of DCVS still requires keeper circuits.

**Step 3 — Comparison Table for N-input XOR (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Static CMOS XOR | DCVS XOR |
|---|---|---|
| **Transistor count** | ~$(4N + 2)$ (N PMOS + N NMOS for AOI gate + inverter) | ~$(4N + 2)$ NMOS in tree + 2 PMOS latch ≈ $4N + 4$ |
| **Static power** | Very low | Very low (similar) |
| **Dynamic power** | Higher (glitches on both Q and Q̅) | ~40 % lower (no glitch on static node) |
| **Speed** | Slower (single-ended output needs buffer) | **~1.5–2× faster** (regenerative latch gain) |
| **Area** | Smaller | Larger (complementary trees) |
| **Noise immunity** | Moderate | High (differential) |

**Conclusion:** DCVS is preferred for **critical-path arithmetic blocks** where speed and power matter more than area. Static CMOS is preferred for **general glue logic** where area and simplicity dominate.

---

### **Question B — [KTU University Exam — Module 4, Dec 2024 — Internal Choice 2]**

#### **(a) Explain the basic concept of clocked CMOS logic. With a circuit diagram, describe a clocked CMOS NAND gate and its operation in precharge and evaluate phases. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Step 1 — Concept of Clocked CMOS Logic (2 marks):**

Clocked CMOS (also called dynamic CMOS) logic uses a clock signal to control when the circuit precharges internal nodes and when it evaluates logic conditions. The two phases are:
- **Precharge phase (CLK = 0):** Internal node X is connected to VDD via a clocked PMOS.
- **Evaluate phase (CLK = 1):** The precharge PMOS is OFF; a clocked NMOS connects the bottom of the PDN to GND. The PDN conditionally discharges X based on the input combination.

**Step 2 — Clocked CMOS NAND Gate Circuit (2 marks):**

For $F = \overline{A \cdot B}$, the clocked NAND has:
- **MPclk**: PMOS between VDD and node X, gated by CLK.
- **MNclk**: NMOS between the bottom of PDN and GND, gated by CLK.
- **PDN**: Two NMOS in series (MN_A in series with MN_B), with MN_A's drain at X.
- **Inverter**: Static CMOS inverter between X and output Y.

**Step 3 — Precharge Phase Operation (1.5 marks):**

When CLK = 0:
- MPclk is ON (gate LOW) ⇒ X is charged to VDD.
- MNclk is OFF ⇒ PDN is isolated from GND.
- Output Y = $\overline{X}$ = 0 V (LOW).
- The output is **pre-set to 0** irrespective of inputs A, B.

**Step 4 — Evaluate Phase Operation (1.5 marks):**

When CLK = 1:
- MPclk is OFF ⇒ X is disconnected from VDD (floating).
- MNclk is ON.
- **If A = 1 AND B = 1:** Both MN_A and MN_B conduct; X discharges to GND ⇒ Y = VDD (HIGH).
- **Otherwise:** PDN does not conduct; X remains HIGH (held by capacitance) ⇒ Y = 0 V (LOW).

This implements the NAND function correctly during the evaluate phase.

---

#### **(b) A clocked CMOS logic gate has an internal node capacitance $C_X$ = 40 fF and an internal parasitic capacitance $C_Y$ = 25 fF. The supply voltage is $V_{DD}$ = 1.2 V. The inverter switches at $V_M$ = 0.55 V. Determine whether charge sharing causes a logic error. Also compute the energy dissipated per clock cycle for switching activity $\alpha$ = 0.3 at clock frequency $f_{CLK}$ = 500 MHz. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Step 1 — Charge Sharing Analysis (3 marks):**

Given: $C_X = 40 \text{ fF}$, $C_Y = 25 \text{ fF}$, $V_{DD} = 1.2 \text{ V}$, $V_M = 0.55 \text{ V}$.

Applying the charge-sharing formula (derived in Section 3.2):

$$V_f = V_{DD} \cdot \frac{C_X}{C_X + C_Y}$$

$$V_f = 1.2 \cdot \frac{40}{40 + 25} = 1.2 \cdot \frac{40}{65}$$

$$V_f = 1.2 \cdot 0.6154 = 0.7385 \text{ V}$$

Since $V_f = 0.7385 \text{ V} > V_M = 0.55 \text{ V}$, **the inverter does NOT switch erroneously**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Margin check:** $\Delta V = V_f - V_M = 0.7385 - 0.55 = 0.1885 \text{ V}$ — a healthy margin. The gate operates correctly.

**Step 2 — Energy Per Transition (2 marks):**

The fundamental switching energy per transition is:

$$E_{\text{switch}} = C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2$$

Assuming the load capacitance on the output is $C_L = C_X + C_Y = 65 \text{ fF}$:

$$E_{\text{switch}} = 65 \times 10^{-15} \cdot (1.2)^2 = 65 \times 10^{-15} \cdot 1.44$$

$$E_{\text{switch}} = 93.6 \times 10^{-15} \text{ J} = 93.6 \text{ fJ}$$

**Step 3 — Average Dynamic Power (2 marks):**

$$P_{\text{dyn}} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f_{CLK}$$

$$P_{\text{dyn}} = 0.3 \cdot 65 \times 10^{-15} \cdot 1.44 \cdot 500 \times 10^6$$

$$P_{\text{dyn}} = 0.3 \cdot 65 \times 1.44 \times 500 \times 10^{-9}$$

$$P_{\text{dyn}} = 14.04 \times 10^{-6} \text{ W} = 14.04 \text{ μW}$$

**Conclusion:** The clocked gate dissipates only ~14 μW at this activity level — confirming the low-power advantage of clocked logic styles.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in DCVS / Clocked Logic Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the cross-coupling**: When drawing a DCVS gate, students often show two independent PMOS pull-ups instead of the **cross-coupled** configuration. This costs 2 marks instantly.
> 2. **Confusing precharge and evaluate phases**: Many students write "precharge when CLK HIGH" — this is **wrong**. CLK HIGH = evaluate. Memorize: **CLK LOW = Precharge (PMOS ON)**.
> 3. **Missing the inverter in Domino**: A Domino gate is **dynamic + static inverter**. A bare dynamic gate is **NOT** Domino. Examiners test this distinction.
> 4. **Ignoring charge sharing in numerical problems**: If the problem gives capacitances and a switching threshold, you **must** compute $V_f$ and compare to $V_M$. Skipping this loses 3 marks.
> 5. **Using the wrong output node in DCVS**: The output is *both* Q and Q̅. Stating only Q indicates incomplete understanding.
> 6. **Forgetting units in numerical answers**: fJ ≠ μJ. Always write units explicitly.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — DCVS & Clocked Design Style (Module 4)**

### DCVS — Must Know

- ⭐ **DCVS = Differential + Cascode (cross-coupled PMOS latch) + Voltage Switch (NMOS tree)**
- ⭐ **Two complementary NMOS trees** evaluate F and F̅ simultaneously
- ⭐ **Cross-coupled PMOS latch** provides regenerative full-rail outputs
- ⭐ **Both Q and Q̅ available** without extra inverter
- ⭐ **Static power ≈ 0** (no DC path VDD→GND)
- ⭐ **Threshold-drop issue** solved by weak keeper or feedback transistor
- ⭐ **40 % lower switching energy** than static CMOS at iso-load
- ⭐ Applications: high-speed adders, multiplexers, low-power datapaths

### Clocked Design Style — Must Know

- ⭐ **Two phases: Precharge (CLK=0) and Evaluate (CLK=1)**
- ⭐ **Clocked PMOS** precharges internal node X to VDD
- ⭐ **Clocked NMOS** isolates/connects PDN to GND
- ⭐ **Domino logic** = Dynamic gate + static inverter (for safe cascading)
- ⭐ **Charge sharing formula**: $V_f = V_{DD} \cdot C_X / (C_X + C_Y)$
- ⭐ **Keeper transistor** mitigates leakage and charge-sharing failures
- ⭐ **Switching activity α** is forced ≤ 1 per node ⇒ low dynamic power
- ⭐ **Cascading without inverter** is unsafe (causes incorrect evaluation)

### Cross-Cutting Concepts

- ⭐ **Dynamic Power Equation**: $P_{\text{dyn}} = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f_{CLK}$
- ⭐ **Switching Energy Quantum**: $E = C_L V_{DD}^2$
- ⭐ **PDP and EDP** are the standard figures of merit
- ⭐ **Differential sensing** inherently cancels common-mode noise
- ⭐ **Clocked logic + DCVS** can be combined: **Differential Dynamic Logic** (used in modern ALUs)

### Examiner's Golden Rule

> *"If your circuit has both Q and Q̅ as outputs and a PMOS cross-couple, it is DCVS. If it has a clocked PMOS precharge and a clocked NMOS evaluate, it is Clocked CMOS. If both, it is Differential Dynamic — used in the fastest arithmetic units."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dynamic Logic
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT866 - LOW POWER VLSI
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Dynamic Logic

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Dynamic Logic** is a CMOS digital circuit design style in which the logical operation is performed during a brief, clock-controlled *evaluation* phase, and the output node is conditionally driven based on the state of the input variables, while the node is precharged to a known voltage level during the complementary *precharge* phase. Unlike static CMOS logic, dynamic logic uses a **clocked PMOS pull-up network** (precharge transistor) and a **clocked NMOS pull-down network** (evaluate transistor) along with a logic network (pull-down NMOS tree) that conditionally discharges the output.

The defining traits of dynamic logic are:

1. Use of a **single clock (CLK)** signal to alternate between two distinct phases.
2. Presence of a **dynamic storage node** (a high-impedance capacitance holding the logic state).
3. Absence of a traditional static pull-up path, which reduces transistor count and input capacitance.
4. **Conditional evaluation** — outputs are only updated when the clock permits.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *"Dynamic logic circuits use a clocked precharge/evaluate mechanism to compute logic functions. They offer higher speed and lower device count compared to static CMOS at the cost of higher design complexity, charge-sharing sensitivity, and noise vulnerability."*

## 1.2 Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: A "Balloon Bucket" System**
> 
> Imagine a water bucket with a balloon valve at the bottom.
> - During **Precharge (CLK = 0)**: A water pipe (PMOS) fills the bucket completely — the bucket is **guaranteed to be full**, regardless of how the tap (logic) is set.
> - During **Evaluation (CLK = 1)**: The water pipe is shut off, and a smart drain network (NMOS pull-down tree) checks the input conditions. If the conditions say *"empty the bucket"*, the water drains out instantly. If the conditions say *"keep the bucket"*, the drain remains sealed and the bucket stays full.
> - The water level itself represents the **logic level (HIGH or LOW)** at the output node.
> - The "balloon" represents the **parasitic capacitance** that stores the charge between clock cycles.

This analogy clarifies why dynamic logic is *fast* (single transistor switching for the pull-up) and *compact* (no need for a complementary PMOS logic network) but *fragile* (any unintended discharge or leak path destroys the stored charge).

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following parameters are **standardized across all KTU Low Power VLSI problems**:

- **V_DD** = **0.7 V – 1.2 V** (modern low-voltage operating range)
- **V_TN** (NMOS threshold) = **0.3 V – 0.5 V** (typical 45 nm/65 nm node)
- **V_TP** (PMOS threshold) = **−0.3 V to −0.5 V**
- **C_L** (Load capacitance) = **10 fF – 1 pF** (typical output node capacitance)
- **T_clk** (Clock period) = **1 ns – 10 ns** (1 GHz – 100 MHz operation)
- **k** (Boltzmann constant) = **1.38 × 10⁻²³ J/K**
- **T** (Temperature) = **300 K** (room temperature, default assumption)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All numerical KTU problems assume **room temperature (T = 300 K)** and a **standard CMOS process node (65 nm or 90 nm)** unless explicitly stated otherwise. Subthreshold swing **S ≈ 70–100 mV/decade** is the standard value for body-effect-influenced calculations.

## 1.4 Why Dynamic Logic is Relevant to Low-Power VLSI

Dynamic logic occupies a special niche in low-power design because it:

- **Reduces switching capacitance** by approximately **30–50%** compared to static CMOS (no complementary PMOS network).
- **Operates at lower V_DD**, allowing aggressive voltage scaling.
- **Enables higher speed** for the same power budget, useful for *critical-path* timing.
- However, it introduces **leakage current challenges** and **charge-sharing issues** that must be carefully managed — these are *core KTU exam topics*.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Phases of Dynamic Logic

Dynamic logic alternates between two phases controlled by a single clock:

### 2.1.1 Precharge Phase (CLK = 0)
- The **PMOS precharge transistor (Mp)** turns ON.
- The **NMOS evaluate transistor (Me)** turns OFF.
- The output node capacitance **C_L** is charged to **V_DD** through Mp.
- Logic output is forced HIGH (or a known precharge value).
- **No logical computation occurs** in this phase.

### 2.1.2 Evaluation Phase (CLK = 1)
- The **PMOS precharge transistor** turns OFF.
- The **NMOS evaluate transistor** turns ON.
- The NMOS pull-down network (PDN) evaluates the inputs.
  - **If PDN conducts** → C_L discharges to GND → output = 0.
  - **If PDN does NOT conduct** → C_L retains its precharged value → output = 1.
- **The output is now a valid logic result**.

> [!NOTE]
> The output is only valid during the evaluation phase. During precharge, the output is always 1. This is why downstream stages often use **inverting buffers** (e.g., Domino logic) so the precharged value is interpreted as a logic 0 at the next stage input.

## 2.2 Circuit Topology and Transistor Roles

| **Transistor Symbol** | **Type** | **Function During CLK = 0** | **Function During CLK = 1** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mp (Precharge) | PMOS | ON — charges C_L to V_DD | OFF — isolates C_L from V_DD |
| Me (Evaluate) | NMOS | OFF — disconnects PDN from GND | ON — enables PDN conduction |
| Pull-Down Network (PDN) | NMOS tree | Inactive (Me is OFF) | Active — conditionally discharges C_L |
| Output Capacitance C_L | Capacitive load | Charges toward V_DD | Holds V_DD or discharges to 0 V |

## 2.3 Why Dynamic Logic is "Dynamic"

The word *dynamic* refers to the fact that the output node relies on the **temporary storage of charge on a capacitor**, not on a steady-state conducting path. This temporary storage has a **finite retention time** limited by:

1. **Subthreshold leakage** of OFF transistors.
2. **Junction leakage** (reverse-biased diodes).
3. **Tunneling current** (gate oxide, band-to-band).

For 65 nm CMOS, the **typical hold time** is in the range of **1–100 μs**, which is far longer than a clock period of 1 ns, so the charge retention is *safe* during normal operation but *risky* at low frequencies.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains **all essential formulas** required for KTU University Exam numerical and conceptual questions on Dynamic Logic. Memorize these before attempting problems.

| **Formula** | **Expression** | **Description** | **Units / Variables** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Dynamic Power (General) | $P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f_{clk}$ | Power dissipated by capacitive switching | $P_{dyn}$ in W, $C_L$ in F, $f$ in Hz |
| Switching Energy per Transition | $E_{sw} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2$ | Energy consumed per 0→1 or 1→0 transition | $E_{sw}$ in Joules |
| Dynamic Logic Avg. Power | $P_{avg} = C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f_{clk} \cdot p_{switch}$ | Power where $p_{switch}$ is discharge probability | $p_{switch} \in [0, 1]$ |
| Static Power (Leakage) | $P_{leak} = I_{leak} \cdot V_{DD}$ | Power dissipated in OFF state | $I_{leak}$ in A |
| Subthreshold Current | $I_{sub} = I_0 \cdot 10^{-V_{GS}/S}$ | Leakage in subthreshold region | $S$ = subthreshold swing (mV/decade) |
| High-to-Low Delay | $t_{pHL} = \frac{C_L \cdot (V_{DD} - V_{TN})}{I_{D,sat}}$ | Discharge time during evaluation | $t_{pHL}$ in seconds |
| Low-to-High Delay | $t_{pLH} = \frac{C_L \cdot V_{DD}}{I_{D,sat,PMOS}}$ | Precharge time | $t_{pLH}$ in seconds |
| Charge Sharing Error | $\Delta V = V_{DD} \cdot \frac{C_L}{C_L + C_{int}}$ | Voltage drop on output due to internal node capacitance | $\Delta V$ in Volts |
| Voltage Scaling Power Saving | $P_{new} = P_{old} \cdot \left(\frac{V_{DD,new}}{V_{DD,old}}\right)^2$ | Quadratic power reduction by V_DD scaling | Dimensionless ratio |
| Threshold Voltage Drop Limit | $V_{out,min} = V_{DD} - V_{TN}$ | Minimum output in pass-transistor logic | $V_{out,min}$ in Volts |
| Noise Margin (Dynamic) | $NM_H = V_{DD} - V_{OH}$ | High noise margin in dynamic logic | $V_{OH} = V_{DD}$ during precharge |
| Activity Factor | $\alpha = p_0 \cdot p_1$ | Probability of a transition in a clock cycle | $\alpha \in [0, 0.25]$ typical |
| Energy-Delay Product | $EDP = E \cdot t_{delay}$ | Figure of merit for logic families | EDP in J·s |
| Short-Circuit Power | $P_{sc} = \frac{\beta}{12} \cdot (V_{DD} - 2V_T)^3 \cdot \tau \cdot f$ | Power during input transitions | $P_{sc}$ in W |

> [!CAUTION]
> **Pipe Symbol Warning:** The vertical bar $\vert$ has been used above (e.g., $V_{GS}\vert$) to ensure no conflict with KTU markdown table parsing. Do not confuse $\vert$ with logical OR or absolute value without context.

## 2.5 Power Consumption Components in Dynamic Logic

Dynamic logic has **three principal power components**, all of which appear in KTU 14-mark design problems:

1. **Switching Power** ($P_{sw}$): Power to charge and discharge the output capacitance. This is the **dominant component** in well-designed dynamic logic.
2. **Short-Circuit Power** ($P_{sc}$): Power dissipated when both Mp and Me are momentarily ON during clock transitions. Usually small (5–10% of total).
3. **Leakage Power** ($P_{leak}$): Power consumed by subthreshold and gate leakage even when the circuit is "idle". Critical for low-power operation.

The total power is therefore:

$$P_{total} = P_{sw} + P_{sc} + P_{leak}$$

where $P_{sw} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f_{clk}$, $P_{sc}$ is the short-circuit component, and $P_{leak}$ is the static leakage.

## 2.6 Real-World Utility of Dynamic Logic

Dynamic logic is **commercially deployed** in:

- **High-performance microprocessors** (e.g., Intel Pentium 4 used dynamic logic in critical paths of execution units).
- **ALUs and adders** in DSP chips.
- **Critical path optimization** in standard-cell libraries.
- **Domino logic** is the most common variant, used in cache tag comparators and high-speed decoders.

In modern 22 nm and below, **domino logic has been largely replaced by static CMOS** due to leakage and noise challenges, but the **design principles are still taught** for fundamental understanding and for niche high-speed applications.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Energy per Switching Event in Dynamic Logic

The energy stored in a capacitor charged to voltage $V_{DD}$ is given by the standard electrostatic formula. We will derive this from first principles.

### Step 1: Define the Energy Stored in a Capacitor

The fundamental energy stored in a capacitor is the integral of the instantaneous power delivered to it over the charging interval. Power at any instant is the product of voltage across the element and current flowing into it.

$$E = \int_0^{\infty} v(t) \cdot i(t) \, dt$$

### Step 2: Substitute the Capacitor I-V Relation

The current-voltage relationship of a capacitor is $i(t) = C \cdot \frac{dv}{dt}$. Substituting this into the integral:

$$E = \int_0^{V_{DD}} v(t) \cdot C \cdot \frac{dv}{dt} \, dt$$

### Step 3: Simplify the Integral

The $dt$ in numerator and denominator cancel, leaving a simple integral over voltage:

$$E = \int_0^{V_{DD}} C \cdot v \, dv$$

### Step 4: Evaluate the Integral

Integrating $v$ with respect to $v$ gives $v^2 / 2$. Applying the limits:

$$E = C \cdot \left[ \frac{v^2}{2} \right]_0^{V_{DD}} = C \cdot \frac{V_{DD}^2}{2}$$

### Step 5: Final Energy Expression

The energy delivered to the capacitor is therefore:

$$E_{sw} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2$$

**Key Insight:** Note that this is the energy *delivered by the supply*, but **half of this energy is dissipated as heat** in the PMOS precharge transistor's on-resistance. The other half remains stored on $C_L$ as $\frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic Check:** If $C_L = 1$ pF and $V_{DD} = 1$ V, then $E_{sw} = 0.5 \times 10^{-12} \times 1 = 0.5$ pJ per transition. This matches industry-standard values for 65 nm CMOS.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Power Dissipation in a Dynamic Logic Gate

The total power is obtained by multiplying the energy per transition by the number of transitions per second.

### Step 1: Define the Number of Transitions

A clock running at frequency $f_{clk}$ produces $f_{clk}$ evaluation edges per second. The probability that a particular evaluation results in a discharge (a 0-to-1 transition at the output) is $p_{switch}$. The expected number of full-swing transitions per second is therefore:

$$N_{trans} = f_{clk} \cdot p_{switch}$$

### Step 2: Multiply Energy by Transition Count

The total switching power is the product of energy per transition and transitions per second:

$$P_{sw} = E_{sw} \cdot N_{trans} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f_{clk} \cdot p_{switch}$$

### Step 3: Combine with Activity Factor

Defining $\alpha = p_{switch}$ as the activity factor:

$$P_{sw} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f_{clk}$$

This is the **canonical CMOS power equation**, applicable to both static and dynamic CMOS with appropriate interpretation of $\alpha$.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Charge-Sharing Voltage Drop

Charge sharing is a critical failure mechanism in dynamic logic. We derive the output voltage drop when an internal node capacitance $C_{int}$ shares charge with the output capacitance $C_L$.

### Step 1: Initial Conditions

At the start of evaluation, both the output node (C_L) and the internal node (C_int) hold different voltages:

$$V_{out}(0^-) = V_{DD}, \quad V_{int}(0^-) = 0$$

### Step 2: Conservation of Charge

When the PDN partially conducts, charge redistributes between $C_L$ and $C_{int}$. By charge conservation (no charge is added from V_DD since the precharge transistor is OFF):

$$Q_{initial} = C_L \cdot V_{DD} + C_{int} \cdot 0 = C_L \cdot V_{DD}$$

$$Q_{final} = (C_L + C_{int}) \cdot V_{final}$$

### Step 3: Equate Initial and Final Charge

Setting $Q_{initial} = Q_{final}$:

$$C_L \cdot V_{DD} = (C_L + C_{int}) \cdot V_{final}$$

### Step 4: Solve for V_final

Dividing both sides by $(C_L + C_{int})$:

$$V_{final} = V_{DD} \cdot \frac{C_L}{C_L + C_{int}}$$

### Step 5: Define Voltage Drop

The voltage drop is the difference between the precharge value and the post-sharing value:

$$\Delta V = V_{DD} - V_{final} = V_{DD} - V_{DD} \cdot \frac{C_L}{C_L + C_{int}}$$

$$\Delta V = V_{DD} \cdot \left( 1 - \frac{C_L}{C_L + C_{int}} \right) = V_{DD} \cdot \frac{C_{int}}{C_L + C_{int}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examination Result:** If $C_{int} = C_L$, then $\Delta V = 0.5 \cdot V_{DD}$. This is a 50% voltage drop, which is catastrophic — the output may be misinterpreted as a logic 0 by the next stage. This is the **most-tested charge-sharing formula in KTU exams**.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Dynamic Logic Propagation Delay (High-to-Low)

The propagation delay $t_{pHL}$ is the time for the output to fall from $V_{DD}$ to $\frac{1}{2} V_{DD}$.

### Step 1: Capacitor Discharge Equation

When the PDN conducts, the output capacitor discharges through an effective resistance $R_{eq}$. The voltage as a function of time follows the standard RC discharge:

$$V_{out}(t) = V_{DD} \cdot e^{-t / (R_{eq} \cdot C_L)}$$

### Step 2: Set the 50% Threshold

We seek the time at which $V_{out}(t) = \frac{V_{DD}}{2}$:

$$\frac{V_{DD}}{2} = V_{DD} \cdot e^{-t_{pHL} / (R_{eq} C_L)}$$

### Step 3: Simplify

Dividing both sides by $V_{DD}$ and taking the natural logarithm:

$$\frac{1}{2} = e^{-t_{pHL} / (R_{eq} C_L)}$$

$$\ln(2) = \frac{t_{pHL}}{R_{eq} C_L}$$

### Step 4: Solve for Delay

$$t_{pHL} = R_{eq} \cdot C_L \cdot \ln(2) \approx 0.69 \cdot R_{eq} \cdot C_L$$

> [!NOTE]
> The factor $\ln(2) \approx 0.69$ is the standard CMOS delay constant. For dynamic logic, $R_{eq}$ is the effective ON-resistance of the NMOS PDN, which depends on the number of series transistors and the input pattern.

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Leakage Current in Dynamic Logic Hold State

In modern sub-100 nm CMOS, leakage current determines the maximum allowable clock period for dynamic logic.

### Step 1: Subthreshold Current Expression

The subthreshold current of an NMOS in weak inversion is given by the empirical formula:

$$I_{sub} = I_0 \cdot \frac{W}{L} \cdot 10^{-(V_{GS} - V_{TH} + \eta V_{DS}) / S}$$

where $S$ is the subthreshold swing and $\eta$ is the DIBL (Drain-Induced Barrier Lowering) coefficient.

### Step 2: Apply to OFF Transistor in Dynamic Logic

For an OFF NMOS in the PDN with $V_{GS} = 0$ and $V_{DS} = V_{DD}$:

$$I_{sub,OFF} = I_0 \cdot \frac{W}{L} \cdot 10^{(V_{TH} - \eta V_{DD}) / S}$$

### Step 3: Compute the Hold Time

The voltage on $C_L$ will decay according to:

$$\Delta V = \frac{I_{sub,OFF} \cdot \Delta t}{C_L}$$

For the voltage to stay within the noise margin (say, decay by no more than 10% of $V_{DD}$):

$$\Delta t_{hold,max} = \frac{0.1 \cdot V_{DD} \cdot C_L}{I_{sub,OFF}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 14-Mark Insight:** With $V_{DD} = 1$ V, $C_L = 50$ fF, and $I_{sub} = 10$ nA, the hold time is $5 \mu s$. This means dynamic logic can operate at clock frequencies **above 200 kHz** safely. Below this, charge retention becomes unreliable.

## 3.6 Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)

> [!NOTE]
> **Example 1: Power Calculation**
> 
> A dynamic logic gate has $C_L = 100$ fF, $V_{DD} = 1$ V, $f_{clk} = 500$ MHz, and discharge probability $p_{switch} = 0.2$. Find the average power dissipation.

**Solution:**

$$P_{sw} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f_{clk}$$

$$P_{sw} = 0.2 \times 100 \times 10^{-15} \times (1)^2 \times 500 \times 10^6$$

$$P_{sw} = 0.2 \times 100 \times 10^{-15} \times 5 \times 10^8$$

$$P_{sw} = 0.2 \times 5 \times 10^{-5} = 1 \times 10^{-5} \text{ W} = 10 \text{ μW}$$

**Verification:** This value is consistent with typical low-power dynamic logic gates.

> [!NOTE]
> **Example 2: Charge-Sharing Effect**
> 
> A dynamic NAND gate has $C_L = 50$ fF and internal node capacitance $C_{int} = 25$ fF. Find the output voltage drop during evaluation.

**Solution:**

$$\Delta V = V_{DD} \cdot \frac{C_{int}}{C_L + C_{int}}$$

$$\Delta V = 1 \cdot \frac{25}{50 + 25} = \frac{25}{75} = 0.333 \text{ V}$$

This is a **33% drop**, severely compromising noise margin. A **precharge of internal nodes** or a **bleeder PMOS** would be required.

## 3.7 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Dynamic vs. Static CMOS

| **Parameter** | **Static CMOS** | **Dynamic CMOS** | **Implication for Low Power** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Transistor Count | 2N (N PMOS + N NMOS) | N + 2 (N NMOS + 1 Mp + 1 Me) | Dynamic uses **fewer transistors** → less cap |
| Switching Capacitance | High (both networks switch) | Low (only NMOS network switches) | Dynamic saves **30–50% dynamic power** |
| Static Power | Zero (no path from V_DD to GND) | Non-zero (leakage only) | Static CMOS better for **standby** |
| Noise Margin | High (rail-to-rail output) | Reduced (charge sharing, leakage) | Dynamic needs **keeper circuits** |
| Logic Style | Inverting and non-inverting | Inverting only (Domino) | Cascading requires inverters |
| Operating Voltage | Down to 0.7 V | Down to ~0.5 V | Dynamic allows **more aggressive V_DD scaling** |
| Clock Load | No clock | CLK drives 2 transistors | Clock tree power overhead |
| Design Complexity | Simple | High (timing, charge sharing) | Trade-off: **performance vs. complexity** |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Dynamic Logic Gate Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **block-level functional architecture** of a generic dynamic logic gate with its two phases and the keeper circuit for noise immunity.

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[Input Vector: In1, In2, ..., InN]
    nodeB[Clock Signal: CLK]
    nodeC[PMOS Precharge Transistor: Mp]
    nodeD[NMOS Evaluate Transistor: Me]
    nodeE[NMOS Pull-Down Network: PDN Logic Tree]
    nodeF[Dynamic Output Node: C_L]
    nodeG[Keeper PMOS: Mp_keeper]
    nodeH[Inverter Buffer for Domino]
    nodeI[Output: Q]
    nodeJ[Ground Reference: GND]
    nodeK[Supply Voltage: V_DD]

    nodeK --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeF
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeB --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeA --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeJ
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeF --> nodeH
    nodeH --> nodeI
    nodeG --> nodeF

    subgraph PhaseA["Precharge Phase CLK equals 0"]
        nodeC
        nodeF
    end

    subgraph PhaseB["Evaluation Phase CLK equals 1"]
        nodeD
        nodeE
        nodeG
        nodeH
    end

    classDef clock fill:#ffd966,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef pmos fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef nmos fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef output fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px

    class nodeB clock
    class nodeC,nodeG pmos
    class nodeD,nodeE nmos
    class nodeF,nodeH,nodeI output
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The diagram clearly shows the dual-phase nature of dynamic logic. The **PMOS precharge transistor (Mp)** is active during CLK = 0, while the **NMOS evaluate transistor (Me)** and the **PDN logic tree** are active during CLK = 1. The **keeper PMOS (Mp_keeper)** continuously compensates for leakage and charge-sharing losses, ensuring the output remains stable.

## 4.2 Timing Diagram: Precharge and Evaluation Sequence

```mermaid
graph LR
    stepA[Time t0: CLK rises to 1]
    stepB[Time t0 to t1: Evaluation begins]
    stepC[Inputs valid and stable]
    stepD[PDN evaluates conditions]
    stepE[Output settles to 0 if PDN conducts]
    stepF[Output stays at 1 if PDN blocks]
    stepG[Time t1: CLK falls to 0]
    stepH[Precharge phase begins]
    stepI[Mp turns ON, charges C_L to V_DD]
    stepJ[Output forced to V_DD]
    stepK[Time t2: Next clock cycle]

    stepA --> stepB
    stepB --> stepC
    stepC --> stepD
    stepD --> stepE
    stepD --> stepF
    stepE --> stepG
    stepF --> stepG
    stepG --> stepH
    stepH --> stepI
    stepI --> stepJ
    stepJ --> stepK

    classDef clk fill:#ffd966,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef evl fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef pch fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px

    class stepA,stepB,stepG,stepH,stepK clk
    class stepC,stepD,stepE,stepF evl
    class stepI,stepJ pch
```

## 4.3 Functional Block: Charge Sharing Mitigation Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeIn[Logic Inputs]
    nodePrechargeMp[Precharge PMOS controlled by CLK]
    nodeEvalMe[Evaluate NMOS controlled by CLK]
    nodePDN[PDN Logic Tree]
    nodeOutput[Dynamic Output Node C_L]
    nodeCint[Internal Node Capacitance C_int]
    nodeKeeper[Weak Keeper PMOS]
    nodePrechargeInternal[Internal Precharge PMOS]
    nodeBuffer[Output Inverter Buffer]

    nodePrechargeMp --> nodeOutput
    nodeEvalMe --> nodePDN
    nodePDN --> nodeOutput
    nodeIn --> nodePDN
    nodeCint --- nodePDN
    nodePrechargeInternal --- nodeCint
    nodeOutput --> nodeKeeper
    nodeOutput --> nodeBuffer

    subgraph MitigationTech["Charge Sharing Mitigation Techniques"]
        nodePrechargeInternal
        nodeKeeper
    end
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Architectural Insight:** The **precharge of internal nodes** (bottom-middle) eliminates the charge-sharing problem by ensuring all internal nodes are at V_DD before evaluation. The **weak keeper PMOS** compensates for subthreshold leakage, ensuring the output is robust to noise and process variation.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Q: Define dynamic CMOS logic. List any two advantages of dynamic logic over static CMOS logic.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:**
Dynamic CMOS logic is a clocked circuit design style in which the output is **precharged** to V_DD during one clock phase (CLK = 0) and **conditionally evaluated** to either V_DD or GND during the other clock phase (CLK = 1), using a single NMOS pull-down network and a clocked PMOS precharge transistor.

**Two Advantages:**
1. **Reduced transistor count:** A dynamic logic gate with N inputs requires only N + 2 transistors (N in the PDN + 1 precharge + 1 evaluate), compared to 2N transistors in static CMOS. This reduces the **input capacitance** by approximately 30–50%.
2. **Faster operation and lower dynamic power:** Since only the NMOS network switches (the PMOS network is replaced by a single precharge transistor), the switching capacitance and hence the dynamic power consumption are significantly reduced for the same logic function.
3. *(Optional third advantage for partial credit):* **No static power consumption** during the evaluation phase as long as the PDN does not conduct.

**Mark Distribution:**
- Correct definition: 1 mark
- Two clear advantages with brief justification: 2 marks (1 each)

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Q: What is charge sharing in dynamic logic? Explain its effect on circuit operation.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:**
Charge sharing is a **failure mechanism** in dynamic logic where charge stored on the output node capacitance $C_L$ is redistributed to an internal node capacitance $C_{int}$ when the pull-down network partially conducts during the evaluation phase, causing an unwanted **voltage drop** at the output.

**Effect on Circuit Operation:**

When the PDN turns on, the output capacitance $C_L$ (initially at $V_{DD}$) and the internal capacitance $C_{int}$ (initially at 0 V) share their charge. The final output voltage becomes:

$$V_{out,final} = V_{DD} \cdot \frac{C_L}{C_L + C_{int}}$$

This voltage drop may be sufficient to **falsely trigger** the next logic stage if it falls below the inverter threshold voltage $V_{M}$. For example, if $C_{int} = C_L$, the output voltage drops to $0.5 \cdot V_{DD}$, which is **catastrophic**.

**Mitigation Techniques (brief mention for extra credit):**
- Precharging internal nodes to $V_{DD}$.
- Using a **bleeder PMOS** (weak keeper) at the output.
- Minimizing internal node capacitances through careful layout.

**Mark Distribution:**
- Definition: 1 mark
- Effect explanation with formula: 2 marks

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question Paper]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a 4-input dynamic NAND gate. Discuss the precharge and evaluation phases with the help of a timing diagram.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A dynamic CMOS logic gate operates at $V_{DD} = 1.2$ V with a load capacitance of $C_L = 80$ fF. The clock frequency is $f_{clk} = 1$ GHz and the discharge probability is $\alpha = 0.25$. Calculate the dynamic power dissipation. If $V_{DD}$ is scaled down to 0.8 V, what is the percentage power saving?

**Model Solution for Part (a):**

A 4-input dynamic NAND gate consists of:
- **One PMOS precharge transistor (Mp)** with source at $V_{DD}$, gate at CLK, and drain at the output node.
- **One NMOS evaluate transistor (Me)** with source at GND, gate at CLK, and drain connected to the bottom of the PDN.
- **Four NMOS transistors (N1, N2, N3, N4)** connected in **series** in the pull-down network.
- The **output node** is at the drain of Mp and the drain of the topmost NMOS (N1).

**Precharge Phase (CLK = 0):**
- Mp is ON, Me is OFF.
- Output node capacitance $C_L$ is charged to $V_{DD}$ through Mp.
- Output = V_DD (logic HIGH).
- All four inputs (A, B, C, D) must be in a known state (typically 0), but the state is irrelevant because Me is OFF and no discharge path exists.

**Evaluation Phase (CLK = 1):**
- Mp is OFF, Me is ON.
- If **all four inputs are HIGH** (A = B = C = D = 1), the four series NMOS transistors conduct, creating a path from $C_L$ to GND through Me. $C_L$ discharges, and output = 0.
- If **any input is LOW** (at least one of A, B, C, D = 0), the series path is broken. $C_L$ holds its precharged value, and output = 1.
- This realizes the **NAND function**: Output = 0 only when all inputs are 1.

**Timing Diagram Description:**
- CLK: Square wave alternating between 0 and 1.
- Output: Remains at $V_{DD}$ during precharge; transitions to 0 during evaluation only if all inputs are HIGH; remains at $V_{DD}$ otherwise.

**Mark Distribution for (a):**
- Circuit description: 2 marks
- Precharge phase explanation: 2 marks
- Evaluation phase explanation: 2 marks
- Timing diagram: 1 mark

---

**Model Solution for Part (b):**

**Given:**
- $V_{DD,old} = 1.2$ V
- $C_L = 80$ fF $= 80 \times 10^{-15}$ F
- $f_{clk} = 1$ GHz $= 10^9$ Hz
- $\alpha = 0.25$

**Step 1: Calculate Original Dynamic Power**

$$P_{sw,old} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD,old}^2 \cdot f_{clk}$$

$$P_{sw,old} = 0.25 \times 80 \times 10^{-15} \times (1.2)^2 \times 10^9$$

$$P_{sw,old} = 0.25 \times 80 \times 10^{-15} \times 1.44 \times 10^9$$

$$P_{sw,old} = 0.25 \times 80 \times 1.44 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$P_{sw,old} = 0.25 \times 115.2 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$P_{sw,old} = 28.8 \text{ μW}$$

**Step 2: Calculate New Dynamic Power with V_DD = 0.8 V**

$$P_{sw,new} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD,new}^2 \cdot f_{clk}$$

$$P_{sw,new} = 0.25 \times 80 \times 10^{-15} \times (0.8)^2 \times 10^9$$

$$P_{sw,new} = 0.25 \times 80 \times 10^{-15} \times 0.64 \times 10^9$$

$$P_{sw,new} = 0.25 \times 51.2 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$P_{sw,new} = 12.8 \text{ μW}$$

**Step 3: Calculate Power Saving**

$$\text{Power Saving} = P_{sw,old} - P_{sw,new} = 28.8 - 12.8 = 16.0 \text{ μW}$$

$$\text{Percentage Power Saving} = \frac{16.0}{28.8} \times 100\% = 55.56\%$$

**Step 4: Verification Using Quadratic Scaling Formula**

$$P_{sw,new} = P_{sw,old} \cdot \left( \frac{V_{DD,new}}{V_{DD,old}} \right)^2 = 28.8 \cdot \left( \frac{0.8}{1.2} \right)^2 = 28.8 \times 0.444 = 12.8 \text{ μW} \checkmark$$

**Answer:** Original power = 28.8 μW; New power = 12.8 μW; Power saving = **55.56%**.

**Mark Distribution for (b):**
- Correct formula application: 2 marks
- Calculation of $P_{sw,old}$: 2 marks
- Calculation of $P_{sw,new}$: 1 mark
- Percentage saving calculation with verification: 2 marks

---

### Question B [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the different sources of power consumption in dynamic CMOS logic. Explain how dynamic logic achieves power reduction compared to static CMOS.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A dynamic logic gate has an output capacitance of 100 fF and an internal node capacitance of 40 fF. If $V_{DD} = 1$ V, find:
(i) The voltage drop on the output node due to charge sharing.
(ii) Whether the output is still interpreted as a logic HIGH by the next stage, assuming the next stage has an inverter threshold of $V_M = 0.45$ V.

**Model Solution for Part (a):**

**Sources of Power Consumption in Dynamic CMOS Logic:**

1. **Switching Power ($P_{sw}$):** Power consumed to charge and discharge the output node capacitance $C_L$ during clock transitions. This is the **dominant component** and is given by $P_{sw} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f_{clk}$.

2. **Short-Circuit Power ($P_{sc}$):** Power dissipated when both the PMOS precharge transistor and the NMOS evaluate transistor are simultaneously ON during clock transitions. This is typically small (5–10% of total) and can be minimized by ensuring sharp clock edges.

3. **Leakage Power ($P_{leak}$):** Power consumed by subthreshold and gate-oxide leakage currents even when the circuit is in a steady state. This becomes significant in sub-100 nm processes where the threshold voltage $V_T$ is low and the supply voltage $V_{DD}$ is reduced.

**Total Power:**
$$P_{total} = P_{sw} + P_{sc} + P_{leak}$$

**Power Reduction Compared to Static CMOS:**

Dynamic logic achieves power reduction through the following mechanisms:

1. **Reduced switching capacitance:** Static CMOS requires both an NMOS pull-down network (N transistors) and a PMOS pull-up network (N transistors), each contributing input capacitance. Dynamic logic uses only the NMOS pull-down network, plus a single small precharge transistor. The total input capacitance is reduced by **30–50%**, leading to a proportional reduction in dynamic power.

2. **Elimination of complementary switching:** In static CMOS, every logic transition switches both an NMOS and a PMOS transistor. In dynamic logic, only the NMOS network switches, reducing the per-transition energy.

3. **No crowbar (short-circuit) current during logic evaluation:** During the evaluation phase, only the NMOS PDN conducts; the PMOS precharge is OFF, so there is no DC path from $V_{DD}$ to GND.

4. **Lower V_DD operation:** Dynamic logic can operate at lower supply voltages while maintaining acceptable noise margins, providing **quadratic power savings** through voltage scaling.

**Trade-off:** Dynamic logic has higher leakage power and noise vulnerability, requiring keeper circuits which add to the power budget.

**Mark Distribution for (a):**
- Identification of three power sources: 3 marks (1 each)
- Explanation of dynamic logic power reduction mechanisms: 4 marks (2 each for two key mechanisms)

---

**Model Solution for Part (b):**

**Given:**
- $C_L = 100$ fF
- $C_{int} = 40$ fF
- $V_{DD} = 1$ V
- $V_M = 0.45$ V (inverter threshold of next stage)

**Step 1: Apply the Charge-Sharing Formula**

The output voltage after charge sharing is:

$$V_{out,final} = V_{DD} \cdot \frac{C_L}{C_L + C_{int}}$$

**Step 2: Substitute the Values**

$$V_{out,final} = 1 \cdot \frac{100}{100 + 40} = \frac{100}{140} = 0.714 \text{ V}$$

**Step 3: Calculate the Voltage Drop**

$$\Delta V = V_{DD} - V_{out,final} = 1 - 0.714 = 0.286 \text{ V}$$

Alternatively, using the direct formula:

$$\Delta V = V_{DD} \cdot \frac{C_{int}}{C_L + C_{int}} = 1 \cdot \frac{40}{140} = 0.286 \text{ V} \checkmark$$

**Step 4: Determine Logic Interpretation by Next Stage**

The next stage is an inverter with threshold $V_M = 0.45$ V. The inverter interprets its input as:
- **Logic HIGH** if input > $V_M$
- **Logic LOW** if input < $V_M$

Since $V_{out,final} = 0.714$ V $> V_M = 0.45$ V, the next stage correctly interprets the output as a **logic HIGH**.

**Step 5: Noise Margin Assessment**

The remaining noise margin is:
$$NM_{remaining} = V_{out,final} - V_M = 0.714 - 0.45 = 0.264 \text{ V}$$

This is a **marginal** noise margin. The circuit may fail if there is additional noise or process variation.

**Answer:** (i) Voltage drop = **0.286 V**; (ii) Output is still interpreted as logic HIGH, but with reduced noise margin.

**Mark Distribution for (b):**
- Correct formula application: 2 marks
- Calculation of $V_{out,final}$ and $\Delta V$: 3 marks
- Logic interpretation with comparison to $V_M$: 2 marks

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mistakes Resulting in Marks Deduction:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing static and dynamic power:** Many students incorrectly add static power to dynamic logic without realizing that **dynamic logic has near-zero static power** during evaluation. The static component is purely leakage-based.
> 
> 2. **Wrong charge-sharing direction:** Students often write $\Delta V = V_{DD} \cdot C_L / (C_L + C_{int})$ instead of $C_{int} / (C_L + C_{int})$. **The voltage drop depends on the INTERNAL capacitance, not the load capacitance.**
> 
> 3. **Ignoring the activity factor:** In power calculations, students often set $\alpha = 1$ instead of using the given value. The activity factor is **critical** and must be explicitly multiplied.
> 
> 4. **Forgetting units:** $C_L$ in fF must be converted to Farads (multiply by $10^{-15}$). The clock frequency in MHz or GHz must be in Hz.
> 
> 5. **Confusing $V_{OH}$ and $V_{OL}$:** In dynamic logic, $V_{OH} = V_{DD}$ (during precharge) and $V_{OL} = 0$ V (after discharge). Students sometimes incorrectly assume $V_{OL} > 0$ due to subthreshold leakage.
> 
> 6. **Not addressing charge sharing in Part B design questions:** Examiners award full marks only if the student **explicitly identifies** charge sharing as a failure mode and **proposes a mitigation technique** (keeper PMOS, internal node precharge, or charge-sharing analysis).

## 5.3 Topic Recap and Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU Module 4: Dynamic Logic**
> 
> ### Core Concepts
> - Dynamic logic uses **two clock-controlled phases**: precharge (CLK = 0) and evaluate (CLK = 1).
> - The **output node is a dynamic storage capacitor** that holds charge temporarily.
> - **PMOS precharge transistor** charges C_L to V_DD; **NMOS evaluate transistor** enables conditional discharge.
> - Dynamic logic is **faster and uses fewer transistors** than static CMOS.
> 
> ### Key Formulas to Memorize
> - $P_{sw} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f_{clk}$
> - $E_{sw} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$
> - $\Delta V = V_{DD} \cdot \frac{C_{int}}{C_L + C_{int}}$
> - $t_{pHL} = 0.69 \cdot R_{eq} \cdot C_L$
> - Voltage scaling: Power ratio = $(V_{DD,new} / V_{DD,old})^2$
> 
> ### Failure Mechanisms (High-Yield for KTU)
> - **Charge sharing:** Internal node capacitance causes output voltage drop.
> - **Capacitive coupling noise:** Adjacent wires can inject charge into the dynamic node.
> - **Clock feedthrough:** Rapid CLK transitions couple to the output via parasitic capacitance.
> - **Subthreshold leakage:** Limits the maximum clock period.
> - **Supply noise:** V_DD variations can corrupt the precharged value.
> 
> ### Variants of Dynamic Logic
> - **Domino logic:** Dynamic gate + inverting buffer; allows cascading of inverting stages.
> - **NP-Domino (Zipper):** Alternating n-type and p-type dynamic stages.
> - **Footless dynamic logic:** No separate evaluate transistor (saves area, but causes contention).
> 
> ### Design Techniques
> - **Keeper PMOS (bleeder):** Weak feedback to maintain output during leakage.
> - **Precharge of internal nodes:** Prevents charge sharing.
> - **Output buffering:** Domino logic uses inverters for fan-out and noise immunity.
> - **Multiple V_T devices:** Use high-V_T to reduce leakage, low-V_T for speed on critical paths.
> 
> ### Quick Numerical Conversions
> - 1 fF = $10^{-15}$ F
> - 1 pF = $10^{-12}$ F
> - 1 MHz = $10^6$ Hz
> - 1 GHz = $10^9$ Hz
> - 1 μW = $10^{-6}$ W
> - Boltzmann constant $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K
> - Thermal voltage $V_T = 26$ mV at 300 K
> 
> ### KTU Exam Strategy
> - Always state the **assumed phase** (precharge or evaluate) explicitly.
> - For numerical problems, **show all unit conversions** to avoid partial mark deduction.
> - For 14-mark questions, **draw the circuit diagram** even if not explicitly asked.
> - Mention **at least one design technique** (keeper, internal precharge) in any descriptive answer.
> - Use the **canonical power equation** with the activity factor for any power calculation.

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Domino CMOS Logic** is a class of **dynamic CMOS logic family** that uses a single-phase clock to control a two-stage operation: a **precharge phase** and an **evaluation phase**. It is constructed using a dynamic stage (a PMOS pull-up precharge transistor and an NMOS pull-down network driven by an evaluation transistor) cascaded with a **static CMOS inverter** (the buffer stage), which restores full logic levels and isolates the dynamic node.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Domino logic is a *dynamic* logic style, not a static one. Its output is valid only during the evaluation half-cycle, and the dynamic internal node is *not* static. This is the single most important conceptual point examiners test.

The defining features of Domino CMOS are:
- A **clocked PMOS precharge transistor** at the top of the pull-down network (PDN).
- A **clocked NMOS evaluation transistor** in series with the PDN.
- An **always-on, ratioed static CMOS inverter** that converts the dynamic (precharged) node into a static rail-to-rail output.
- Optional **keeper transistor** to combat sub-threshold leakage on the dynamic node.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

Imagine a **water tank on a rooftop** with a single pipe (clock-controlled valve) at the bottom.

- When the **valve is closed (Precharge phase, CLK = 0):** water fills the tank up to the brim. The tank represents the dynamic node charged to **V_DD**.
- When the **valve opens (Evaluation phase, CLK = 1):** if the *logic* of the house says "open the tap" (i.e., the PDN conducts), the water rushes out, the tank becomes empty (dynamic node → **GND**). If the logic says "keep the tap shut" (PDN does not conduct), the tank remains full (dynamic node stays at **V_DD**).
- The **little gauge on the wall** (the static inverter) simply displays "Empty" or "Full" as a clear, full-swing signal to the rest of the house.

The water tank is **dynamic** — it forgets its state when emptied and must be refilled every clock cycle. The gauge is **static** — it holds its reading steadily.

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory aid:** The name *Domino* comes from the *cascading* behavior — when one gate's output falls, it can "knock down" the next gate in a chain, just like a row of falling dominoes, because each gate's output is monotonic during evaluation.

## 1.3 Why Domino Logic Exists — The Design Motivation

Static CMOS suffers from:
- **Large input capacitance** because every input drives both a PMOS and an NMOS gate.
- **High short-circuit current** during signal transitions.
- **Area overhead** of complementary PDN and PUN networks.

Domino logic addresses these by using **only NMOS transistors in the logic network** (smaller, faster, lower capacitance) and a **single clocked precharge device**, drastically reducing the load on the preceding stage and saving silicon area.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Punchline (memorize):** "Domino logic trades static robustness for **higher speed, smaller area, and lower input capacitance**, at the cost of needing a clock and suffering charge-sharing/leakage issues."
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Circuit Topology — Anatomy of a Standard Domino Gate

A standard Domino gate implementing a generic Boolean function **F** (realized by the PDN) has the following components, from **V_DD to GND**:

1. **Precharge PMOS (M_p):** Source = V_DD, Drain = Dynamic node (X), Gate = **CLK**.
2. **Pull-Down Network (PDN):** A network of NMOS transistors implementing the logic function F. Its top terminal connects to node X, bottom terminal connects to the evaluation transistor.
3. **Evaluate NMOS (M_e):** Drain = bottom of PDN, Source = GND, Gate = **CLK**.
4. **Static CMOS Inverter:** Input = X, Output = **F** (the buffered, full-swing output).
5. **Keeper PMOS (M_k) — optional but practically universal:** Source = V_DD, Drain = X, Gate = **F** (the output). It weakly holds node X high against leakage.

## 2.2 Two-Phase Operation — Precharge and Evaluation

### Phase 1: Precharge (CLK = 0)
- M_p is **ON** (PMOS conducts when gate is low).
- M_e is **OFF** (NMOS blocks when gate is low).
- Node X is **pulled up to V_DD** through M_p.
- The static inverter sees X = 1, so output **F = 0**.
- The PDN is *isolated* from GND by M_e, so inputs have no effect.
- Any prior evaluation result is **erased** — dynamic nodes are volatile.

### Phase 2: Evaluation (CLK = 1)
- M_p is **OFF**, M_e is **ON**.
- The PDN is now connected to GND.
- If the inputs cause the PDN to **conduct** (i.e., F is logically "1" in the chosen convention), X is **discharged to GND** through the PDN and M_e.
- The inverter then drives output **F = 1** (full V_DD).
- If the PDN does **not** conduct (logic "0"), X stays at V_DD and **F = 0**.
- The output can make **at most one transition per cycle** (0 → 1) — this is the famous **monotonic, glitch-free property** of domino logic.

> [!NOTE]
> **Monotonicity Requirement:** During the evaluation phase, inputs to a domino gate **must not change from 0→1** while evaluation is in progress, otherwise charge injection or unexpected discharge can corrupt the result. This is the key constraint that makes Domino logic unsuitable for asynchronous or highly irregular datapaths.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Variables / Units | Engineering Utility |
|---|----------|---------|-------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | Energy per output transition | $E_{sw} = C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2$ | $C_L$ = load capacitance (F), $V_{DD}$ (V) | Sets the dynamic power budget |
| 2 | Average dynamic power | $P_{avg} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f_{clk}$ | $\alpha$ = switching activity (0 to 1), $f_{clk}$ (Hz) | Used for power budgeting of datapaths |
| 3 | Evaluation delay (first-order) | $t_{pd,eval} \approx 0.69 \cdot R_{eq,PDN} \cdot C_X$ | $R_{eq,PDN}$ = equivalent ON-resistance of PDN stack, $C_X$ = capacitance at dynamic node | Determines critical path delay |
| 4 | Precharge delay | $t_{pd,pre} \approx 0.69 \cdot R_{on,Mp} \cdot C_X$ | $R_{on,Mp}$ = ON-resistance of precharge PMOS | Sets the clock-low half-cycle requirement |
| 5 | Effective load reduction vs static CMOS | $C_{in,domino} \approx 0.5 \cdot C_{in,static}$ | — | Explains speed advantage |
| 6 | Charge-sharing voltage drop | $\Delta V = \dfrac{C_{PDN}}{C_X + C_{PDN}} \cdot V_{DD}$ | $C_{PDN}$ = parasitic capacitance of internal PDN node | Quantifies worst-case error voltage |
| 7 | Leakage-limited hold time | $t_{hold} \approx \dfrac{C_X \cdot \Delta V_{noise}}{I_{leak}}$ | $I_{leak}$ (A) | Bounds minimum clock period |
| 8 | Sub-threshold leakage current (short-channel) | $I_{leak} \approx I_0 \cdot e^{\frac{V_{GS}-V_{th}}{nV_T}}$ | $V_T$ = thermal voltage ≈ **26 mV** at 300 K | Justifies need for keeper transistor |
| 9 | Energy ratio Domino vs Static | $\dfrac{E_{domino}}{E_{static}} \approx \dfrac{C_X}{C_{in,static} + C_{out,static}} \cdot \dfrac{1}{f_{clk} \cdot t_{pd}}$ | — | Shows when domino is more energy-efficient |
| 10 | Effective activity factor (glitch-free) | $\alpha_{domino} \approx \alpha_{logic} \le 0.5$ | — | Lower than static CMOS glitches ($\alpha$ can exceed 0.5) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on absolute values in formulas:** In the table above, ratios and divisions are shown using the fraction form (e.g., $\dfrac{C_X}{C_X + C_{PDN}}$) **deliberately** to keep the markdown table rendering clean. Never write ratios using the vertical pipe `\vert` in exam answers either — KTU examiners prefer the explicit fraction form.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Domino logic is the **workhorse of high-performance arithmetic blocks** in modern microprocessors, DSPs, and crypto accelerators:
- **Carry-skip, carry-lookahead, and Kogge-Stone adders** in CPU ALUs.
- **High-speed register files** and tag comparators in cache controllers.
- **Wide fan-in OR/NOR planes** in CAMs and priority encoders.
- **Pipeline-critical paths** in superscalar issue logic, where every picosecond of delay matters.

Its lower input capacitance is also why the same driving logic can be made **physically smaller** in domino compared to static CMOS for the same drive strength.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations

## 3.1 Derivation: Charge-Sharing Voltage Error

Charge sharing is the **classic domino logic failure mechanism** in KTU exams. The dynamic node X is precharged to **V_DD**, but during evaluation, charge can redistribute between X and the internal parasitic capacitance of the PDN, *even when the PDN does not conduct*. The following derivation shows the worst-case error voltage.

### Setup
- Let $C_X$ be the total capacitance at the dynamic node X (gate + drain + wire).
- Let $C_{PDN}$ be the total parasitic capacitance at the internal node(s) of the PDN (e.g., the source of the topmost NMOS in the stack).
- The PDN is *not* conducting during the scenario (no path to GND), so total charge is conserved.

### Step 1 — Initial charge on $C_X$ before evaluation
$$Q_{initial} = C_X \cdot V_{DD} + C_{PDN} \cdot 0 = C_X \cdot V_{DD}$$

*Reason:* Before evaluation, node X sits at $V_{DD}$ (precharged). The internal PDN node was discharged to 0 V in the previous cycle (or initially).

### Step 2 — Inputs to the PDN switch ON, momentarily connecting $C_X$ and $C_{PDN}$

When the evaluation transistor $M_e$ turns ON and at least one NMOS in the PDN switches ON, $C_X$ and $C_{PDN}$ momentarily share charge. No path to GND exists because not all series NMOS devices in the stack are ON.

### Step 3 — Apply charge conservation
$$Q_{final} = (C_X + C_{PDN}) \cdot V_{final}$$

### Step 4 — Equate initial and final charge
$$C_X \cdot V_{DD} = (C_X + C_{PDN}) \cdot V_{final}$$

### Step 5 — Solve for $V_{final}$
$$V_{final} = \frac{C_X}{C_X + C_{PDN}} \cdot V_{DD}$$

### Step 6 — Voltage drop (error)
$$\Delta V = V_{DD} - V_{final} = V_{DD} - \frac{C_X}{C_X + C_{PDN}} \cdot V_{DD}$$

$$\Delta V = \frac{C_{PDN}}{C_X + C_{PDN}} \cdot V_{DD}$$

### Step 7 — Physical interpretation

If $C_{PDN} \gg C_X$, the dynamic node collapses almost to GND even though the PDN is *not* supposed to conduct — the output flips incorrectly. This is why Domino logic is **forbidden in wide-fan-in dynamic gates** without charge-sharing mitigation (precharge of internal nodes, charge-equalizing transistors, etc.).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common student error in KTU exams:** Writing $V_{final} = \frac{C_{PDN}}{C_X + C_{PDN}} \cdot V_{DD}$. This is **inverted**. The *larger* the dynamic capacitance $C_X$, the *less* the voltage drops. Memorize the formula and the sign.

## 3.2 Derivation: Why Domino Logic Has Lower Input Capacitance

A static CMOS gate with $N$ inputs has $N$ PMOS + $N$ NMOS gate capacitances at the input. An equivalent domino gate has **only $N$ NMOS gates** at the input (the PMOS precharge has its gate on CLK, not on the data input).

### Step 1 — Capacitance per input
Static CMOS input cap per input:
$$C_{in,static} = C_{ox} \cdot (W_P \cdot L_P + W_N \cdot L_N)$$

Domino CMOS input cap per input:
$$C_{in,domino} = C_{ox} \cdot (W_N \cdot L_N)$$

### Step 2 — Typical sizing ratio
For matched drive strength, $W_P \approx 2 \cdot W_N$ (to compensate for lower hole mobility). Substituting:
$$C_{in,static} \approx C_{ox} \cdot W_N \cdot (2L_P + L_N)$$

$$\frac{C_{in,domino}}{C_{in,static}} = \frac{L_N}{2L_P + L_N} \approx 0.3 \text{ to } 0.5$$

### Step 3 — Consequence for speed

Since delay $\propto R_{driver} \cdot C_{load}$, halving the input capacitance roughly **halves the delay of the preceding stage**, which is why domino is preferred in critical paths despite its dynamic nature.

## 3.3 Derivation: Activity Factor and Power Advantage

Static CMOS gates can produce **glitches** (spurious transitions) due to input skew, raising the switching activity $\alpha$ above the logical transition rate. Domino logic produces **at most one 0→1 transition per cycle** because the dynamic node only falls.

### Step 1 — Power equation
$$P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f_{clk}$$

### Step 2 — Glitch factor for static CMOS
A wide static gate with skewed inputs may have $\alpha_{glitch} = 0.2$ to $0.4$ in addition to the logical $\alpha_{logic}$.

### Step 3 — Domino advantage
Domino's $\alpha_{eff}$ is bounded by $\alpha_{logic}$ only, so:
$$\alpha_{domino} \le \alpha_{static}$$

For wide adders, this can yield **20–40% dynamic power savings**, justifying Domino in low-power high-performance designs.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Circuit-Level Architecture (Block / Functional Flow)

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeVDD["V_DD SUPPLY"] --> nodeMp["PMOS PRECHARSE M_p"]
    nodeCLK["CLOCK CLK"] --> nodeMp
    nodeCLK --> nodeMn["NMOS EVALUATE M_e"]
    nodeMp --> nodeX["DYNAMIC NODE X"]
    nodeX --> nodePDN["NMOS PULL DOWN NETWORK LOGIC F"]
    nodePDN --> nodeMn
    nodeMn --> nodeGND["GND"]
    nodeX --> nodeINV["STATIC CMOS INVERTER"]
    nodeINV --> nodeOut["RAIL TO RAIL OUTPUT F"]
    nodeOut --> nodeKeep["WEAK KEEPER PMOS M_k FEEDBACK"]
    nodeKeep --> nodeX
    nodeInA["INPUT A"] --> nodePDN
    nodeInB["INPUT B"] --> nodePDN
    nodeInC["INPUT C"] --> nodePDN
```

## 4.2 Two-Phase Operation Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeStart["CLOCK EDGE"] --> nodeDecide{"CLK EQUAL 0"}
    nodeDecide -- YES --> nodePre["PRECHARGE PHASE"]
    nodePre --> nodePreA1["M_p TURNS ON"]
    nodePre --> nodePreA2["NODE X CHARGED TO V_DD"]
    nodePre --> nodePreA3["M_e TURNS OFF ISOLATES PDN"]
    nodePre --> nodePreA4["OUTPUT F IS HELD AT LOGIC 0 BY INVERTER"]
    nodeDecide -- NO --> nodeEval["EVALUATION PHASE"]
    nodeEval --> nodeEv1["M_p TURNS OFF DISCONNECTS V_DD"]
    nodeEval --> nodeEv2["M_e TURNS ON CONNECTS PDN TO GND"]
    nodeEval --> nodeEv3["PDN CONDUCTS NODE X FALLS OR STAYS HIGH"]
    nodeEval --> nodeEv4["INVERTER DRIVES F TO LOGIC 0 OR 1"]
    nodeEval --> nodeEv5["OUTPUT F CANNOT GLITCH MONOTONIC"]
```

## 4.3 Domino Logic Family — Classification Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeRoot["DYNAMIC DOMINO CMOS FAMILY"]
    nodeRoot --> nodeStd["STANDARD DOMINO SINGLE RAIL"]
    nodeRoot --> nodeDR["DUAL RAIL DOMINO DRDL DIFFERENTIAL"]
    nodeRoot --> nodeFoot["FOOTLESS DOMINO NO EVALUATE TRANSISTOR"]
    nodeRoot --> nodeHS["HIGH SPEED DOMINO HS DOMINO"]
    nodeRoot --> nodeMODL["MULTIPLE OUTPUT DOMINO MODL"]
    nodeRoot --> nodeDDL["SAMPLE SET DIFFERENTIAL LOGIC SSDL"]
    nodeStd --> nodeStd1["USED FOR MONOTONIC SIGNALS"]
    nodeDR --> nodeDR1["RAIL TO RAIL DIFFERENTIAL OUTPUTS"]
    nodeDR --> nodeDR2["IMPLEMENTS BOTH F AND F BAR"]
    nodeFoot --> nodeFoot1["FASTER BUT CHARGE SHARING RISK"]
    nodeHS --> nodeHS1["CROSS COUPLED PMOS FOR FAST EVALUATION"]

```

## 4.4 KTU Visual Mnemonic — Mapping Waveforms to Circuit States

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant CLK as Clock Signal
    participant X as Dynamic Node X
    participant F as Output F
    participant PDN as Pull Down Network

    Note over CLK, PDN: PRECHARGE PHASE
    CLK->>X: CLK LOW NODE X CHARGED TO V_DD
    X->>F: X HIGH DRIVES F LOW
    Note over CLK, PDN: EVALUATION PHASE
    CLK->>PDN: CLK HIGH PDN ENABLED
    alt PDN CONDUCTING F SHOULD BE 1
        PDN->>X: DISCHARGE X TO 0
        X->>F: X LOW DRIVES F HIGH
    else PDN NOT CONDUCTING F SHOULD BE 0
        PDN-->>X: X HELD AT V_DD
        X->>F: F STAYS LOW
    end
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define Domino CMOS logic. List any two advantages over static CMOS logic.**

**Model Answer (Memorize):**

Domino CMOS logic is a dynamic CMOS logic family that uses a clocked PMOS precharge transistor and a clocked NMOS evaluation transistor in series with an NMOS pull-down network (PDN), followed by a static CMOS inverter to produce a full-swing static output. The circuit alternates between a precharge phase (CLK = 0, dynamic node charged to V_DD) and an evaluation phase (CLK = 1, PDN evaluated).

**Two advantages over static CMOS:**
1. **Lower input capacitance** because only NMOS gates are driven by the inputs (PMOS precharge gate is on CLK), so the preceding stage sees roughly half the capacitive load — yielding higher speed.
2. **Glitch-free, monotonic output transition** because the dynamic node can only fall during evaluation, suppressing spurious switching activity and reducing dynamic power in wide datapaths.

*(For full 3 marks: also mention smaller silicon area and absence of complementary PUN.)*

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**What is charge sharing in a Domino logic gate? How is it mitigated?**

**Model Answer:**

Charge sharing is the phenomenon where, during the evaluation phase, the precharged charge on the dynamic node $C_X$ redistributes with the internal parasitic capacitance $C_{PDN}$ of the pull-down network, causing an unintended voltage drop $\Delta V = \frac{C_{PDN}}{C_X + C_{PDN}} \cdot V_{DD}$ on the dynamic node, even when the PDN is *not* conducting. This can corrupt the output.

**Mitigation techniques:**
1. **Precharge the internal PDN nodes** using extra clocked PMOS transistors, so $C_{PDN}$ is also precharged to $V_{DD}$ and cannot pull $C_X$ down.
2. **Add a weak keeper transistor** (PMOS with gate tied to output F) that holds node X at $V_{DD}$ against any leakage or charge-sharing loss.
3. **Limit the fan-in** of the dynamic gate to keep the parasitic ratio $C_{PDN}/C_X$ small.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

### Question Choice A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a standard Domino CMOS logic gate. Discuss the **precharge** and **evaluation** phases and show the waveforms of clock, dynamic node and output. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Circuit:** A standard Domino gate consists of a PMOS precharge transistor $M_p$ (gate = CLK, source = $V_{DD}$), an NMOS evaluate transistor $M_e$ (gate = CLK, source = GND), an NMOS pull-down network between them that implements the Boolean function (e.g., $F = \overline{A \cdot B + C}$), and a static CMOS inverter whose input is the dynamic node X and whose output is the buffered full-swing signal F. A weak PMOS keeper (gate = F, source = $V_{DD}$, drain = X) is added to hold X high against leakage.

**Precharge phase (CLK = 0):**
- $M_p$ is ON, $M_e$ is OFF.
- The dynamic node X is pulled to $V_{DD}$ through $M_p$ regardless of inputs.
- The inverter drives F = 0.
- Charge stored on $C_X$ is $Q = C_X \cdot V_{DD}$.

**Evaluation phase (CLK = 1):**
- $M_p$ OFF, $M_e$ ON.
- If the inputs make the PDN conduct (logic evaluates to "1"), X discharges to GND through the PDN and $M_e$, and the inverter drives F to $V_{DD}$.
- If the PDN does not conduct, X remains at $V_{DD}$ and F stays at 0.
- The output is **monotonic** — it can rise at most once per cycle, never glitches.

**Waveforms (described):**
- CLK: square wave, period T.
- X: rises to $V_{DD}$ during precharge, holds or falls during evaluation.
- F: opposite of X (buffered), so F = 0 during precharge, may rise to $V_{DD}$ during evaluation.

**Why the name "Domino":** The monotonic 0→1 output of one gate can drive the next gate's PDN directly, causing a cascade of evaluations like falling dominoes — but only in the 0→1 direction.

---

**(b)** Derive the expression for the charge-sharing voltage drop in a Domino gate. Suggest two circuit techniques to overcome this problem. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

*See Section 3.1 of these notes for the full derivation. Summary here:*

Let $C_X$ be the dynamic node capacitance, $C_{PDN}$ be the internal parasitic capacitance of the PDN, and assume the PDN is *not* conducting so charge is conserved.

**Initial charge (after precharge):**
$$Q_i = C_X \cdot V_{DD}$$

**Final shared charge (after inputs cause a brief connection):**
$$Q_f = (C_X + C_{PDN}) \cdot V_f$$

**Equating $Q_i = Q_f$ and solving:**
$$V_f = \frac{C_X}{C_X + C_{PDN}} \cdot V_{DD}$$

**Voltage drop (error):**
$$\Delta V = V_{DD} - V_f = \frac{C_{PDN}}{C_X + C_{PDN}} \cdot V_{DD}$$

**Two mitigation techniques:**

1. **Internal-node precharge:** Add a clocked PMOS precharge transistor at every internal node of the PDN, so that $C_{PDN}$ is also precharged to $V_{DD}$ during CLK = 0. Then both $C_X$ and $C_{PDN}$ start at the same potential, and charge sharing causes no net drop.

2. **Keeper transistor:** Insert a weak PMOS between $V_{DD}$ and X with its gate tied to the output F. Whenever F = 0 (i.e., the gate is supposed to evaluate low), the keeper weakly reinforces X = $V_{DD}$ against any sub-threshold leakage, partial charge sharing, or noise that would otherwise collapse X.

*(Additional acceptable techniques: charge-equalizing transistor between X and the critical internal node; limiting fan-in to 4 or less; using a footer NMOS to fully isolate during precharge.)*

---

### Question Choice B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Compare **Standard Domino logic** and **Dual-Rail Domino logic (DRDL)** in terms of structure, output signaling, robustness, and typical application. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | Standard Domino | Dual-Rail Domino (DRDL) |
|-----------|-----------------|-------------------------|
| Number of output rails | One (F) | Two (F and $\overline{F}$) |
| Structure | Single dynamic node + static inverter | Two complementary dynamic nodes + cross-coupled sense amplifier or two inverters |
| Output signaling | Single-ended, monotonic 0→1 | Differential, monotonic but can drive both polarities |
| Robustness to noise | Lower; depends on keeper | Higher; common-mode noise rejected by differential sensing |
| Charge sharing sensitivity | Higher | Lower (complementary node cancels errors) |
| Power consumption | Lower (single dynamic node) | Higher (two dynamic nodes toggle) |
| Number of clocked transistors | 2 (Mp, Me) | 4 (2 Mp, 2 Me) |
| Application | Wide OR/NOR planes, high-speed adders | High-performance CAMs, sense amplifiers, register files, low-swing signaling |
| Cascading | Direct cascade allowed (monotonic) | Direct cascade also allowed, but both rails must be considered |

**Key insight:** Standard domino is area- and power-efficient for *monotonic* functions; DRDL is the *differential* extension that handles inverting functions and provides noise immunity at the cost of doubled dynamic capacitance.

---

**(b)** Explain the concept of **keeper transistor** in a Domino logic gate. Why is it essential in sub-100 nm technologies? With a suitable diagram, show how it is connected. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Concept:** A keeper transistor is a **weak PMOS** ($M_k$) connected between $V_{DD}$ and the dynamic node X, with its gate driven by the *output* F of the static inverter. It is deliberately sized with a large channel length and narrow width so that its drive current is much smaller than the PDN's pull-down current.

**Operation:**
- When the gate evaluates to **F = 0** (no discharge, output is low), the keeper turns ON and supplies a small current to *hold* X at $V_{DD}$, counteracting sub-threshold leakage from the PDN.
- When the gate evaluates to **F = 1** (PDN discharged X, output high), the keeper turns OFF, and the PDN can freely pull X to GND.

**Why essential in sub-100 nm technologies:**
1. **Exponentially increasing sub-threshold leakage:** As $V_{th}$ scales down, off-state NMOS leakage can be of order $\mu$A per transistor. A wide dynamic gate (large fan-in → many parallel NMOS) can leak enough to collapse X during a long evaluation phase, causing a *false evaluation* (1 → 0 error).
2. **Reduced noise margin:** Lower $V_{DD}$ in scaled nodes shrinks the noise budget; even a small voltage droop on X can flip the inverter.
3. **Wider datapaths:** Modern ALUs have 32-, 64-, 128-bit wide domino gates with massive fan-in; the cumulative leakage is unmanageable without a keeper.

**Sizing trade-off:** The keeper must be **strong enough to defeat worst-case leakage** (across PVT corners) but **weak enough to not oppose intentional PDN discharge** (which would slow the gate). Typical ratio: keeper ON-current is **5–10× weaker** than PDN ON-current.

**Diagram (described):**
- $M_k$: PMOS, source = $V_{DD}$, drain = X, gate = F (output of the static inverter).
- This forms a *positive feedback loop*: F low → $M_k$ on → X held high → F stays low.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark Deductions:**
> 1. Drawing the keeper with the wrong source/drain orientation (it must conduct from $V_{DD}$ to X, never in reverse).
> 2. Confusing the **keeper** with the **precharge PMOS** — they are *different* transistors with different jobs. Precharge is on CLK; keeper is on F.
> 3. Forgetting to mention **ratioed sizing** — the keeper must be *weak*; otherwise it fights the PDN and slows evaluation. Examiners specifically look for the word "weak" or "ratioed."
> 4. Inverting the gate and drain of the keeper in circuit diagrams — a single such error can cost a full mark.
> 5. Claiming the keeper "amplifies" or "buffers" the output — it does neither. It is a *leakage-compensation* device.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Domino CMOS** = PMOS precharge + clocked NMOS evaluate + NMOS PDN + static CMOS inverter.
- **Two phases:** Precharge (CLK = 0) charges dynamic node to **V_DD**; Evaluation (CLK = 1) conditionally discharges it.
- **Output is monotonic 0 → 1** during evaluation — never glitches. This is the source of the name "Domino" (cascading like falling dominoes).
- **Advantages:** ~50% lower input capacitance, smaller area, glitch-free, high speed.
- **Disadvantages:** Needs a clock, charge sharing, sub-threshold leakage, hard to cascade with inverting signals, monotonicity requirement on inputs.
- **Charge sharing error:** $\Delta V = \dfrac{C_{PDN}}{C_X + C_{PDN}} \cdot V_{DD}$ — mitigated by internal-node precharge, charge-equalizing transistors, or fan-in limitation.
- **Keeper transistor:** A weak PMOS (gate = output F) that holds X high against leakage during evaluation. Essential below 100 nm.
- **Dual-Rail Domino (DRDL):** Differential version providing both F and $\overline{F}$ — used for robust sensing and inverting functions.
- **Footless Domino:** Omits the evaluate NMOS for speed; susceptible to charge sharing — use only with precharge of internal nodes.
- **MODL (Multiple-Output Domino Logic):** Single shared PDN generates multiple Boolean outputs — saves area in arithmetic circuits.
- **High-Speed (HS) Domino:** Cross-coupled PMOS at the output for faster transition — used in ultra-high-speed pipelines.
- **Power equation:** $P = \alpha \cdot C \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f_{clk}$. Domino reduces both $\alpha$ (glitch-free) and $C$ (smaller input cap).
- **Exam mantra:** *"Single clock, dynamic node, monotonic output, weak keeper — that is Domino."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
