# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Differential Current Switch Logic (DCSL)

### 📌 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Differential Current Switch Logic (DCSL)** is a low-power CMOS logic family that operates on the principle of *current steering* using a **constant current source** and *differential pair* topology. It generates both the true and complementary outputs simultaneously from differential input signals, eliminating the need for separate inverters and reducing the switching power dissipation.

DCSL belongs to the class of **current-mode logic (CML)** families adapted for low-power VLSI. It merges the differential operation of **DCVSL (Differential Cascode Voltage Switch Logic)** with a **tail current source**, making it highly suitable for high-speed, low-noise, and low-power digital applications such as sense amplifiers, frequency dividers, and high-performance arithmetic units.

### 🎯 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **railway track switchyard** where a single train (current) is routed between two tracks based on a control signal:
- A **mechanical switch** (transistor pair) directs the train either left or right.
- Only **one train exists** at a time (constant current), so there is no energy wasted in charging large rail networks simultaneously.
- The position of the switch is determined by a small control input, but the heavy lifting (the current) is *reused* rather than thrown away.

This is exactly how DCSL works — instead of charging and discharging big output capacitances, a **fixed bias current** is *steered* through one of two branches, and the small voltage swings on the output nodes are interpreted as logic levels.

---

## 1.2 Adiabatic Switching

### 📌 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Adiabatic Switching** is a low-power circuit technique in which energy transfer to and from a load capacitance is performed *gradually* (over a finite time period) through a *time-varying ramped supply* or a *resonant LC network*, so that the energy stored in the capacitor can be returned back to the source rather than being dissipated as heat in the resistive path.

The word *adiabatic* is borrowed from thermodynamics, meaning *"no heat exchange with surroundings."* In electronics, it refers to the **reversal of current flow** before all energy is dissipated as $I^2R$ losses.

### 🎯 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of **filling a water tank from a tanker**:
- **Conventional way (static CMOS):** Open the valve fully → water gushes in, splashes, energy lost in turbulence. Pump energy = 100%, useful water energy = 50%, wasted = 50%.
- **Adiabatic way:** Open the valve **slowly and steadily** → water rises gently. Energy from the falling water (as it drains) is *recycled back* to a lower reservoir. The net energy consumed is much less.

In the electrical domain, a **slowly-ramping supply voltage** acts like the slowly-opened valve, and the **inductor** acts like the inertia that lets current flow back to the source.

---

## 1.3 Adiabatic Charging (The Core Principle)

### 📌 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Adiabatic Charging** is the specific sub-process in adiabatic switching where a load capacitance $C_L$ is charged from **zero to $V_{DD}$** through a switch resistance $R$ driven by a **time-varying (ramp) voltage source $V(t)$** that rises linearly over a charging period $T_s$, such that the charging current is held approximately constant and the energy normally lost as $I^2R$ heat is minimized.

The defining parameter is the **charging time-to-RC ratio** $T_s / RC$. The longer the charging time (slow ramp), the smaller the dissipation.

### 📊 Standard Metric Highlight

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **figure of merit** for adiabatic charging is the **quality factor** $\beta = \dfrac{T_s}{2RC}$, and the energy dissipation is reduced by a factor of $\beta$ compared to conventional charging.
> Standard constants used: $\mathbf{V_{DD}}$ (supply), $\mathbf{C_L}$ (load capacitance), $\mathbf{R}$ (on-resistance), $\mathbf{T_s}$ (charging time), $\mathbf{f_c}$ (clock frequency).

---

## 1.4 Why These Techniques Matter in KTU 2024 Syllabus

| Technique | Power-Saving Mechanism | Best Use-Case |
|---|---|---|
| **DCSL** | Constant current, small voltage swing, differential signalling | High-speed adders, mixers |
| **Adiabatic Switching** | Energy recovery through ramped supply | Low-frequency, ultra-low-power IoT |
| **Adiabatic Charging** | Reduces $CV^2$ loss by factor $RC/T_s$ | Sensor nodes, RFIDs, watch circuits |

# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Differential Current Switch Logic — Operational Breakdown

### 🧠 Block-Level Working Principle

A DCSL gate is built from three functional sub-blocks:

1. **Tail Current Source (Bias Network):**
   - A single NMOS transistor $M_{tail}$ (or a current mirror) provides a *constant bias current* $I_{bias}$ from $V_{DD}$ to the differential pair.
   - This sets the upper limit of current any branch can draw — *no static shoot-through* is possible.

2. **Differential Pair (Input Stage):**
   - Two NMOS transistors $M_1$ and $M_2$ form a differential pair.
   - Inputs **IN** and **$\overline{\text{IN}}$** drive the gates of $M_1$ and $M_2$.
   - Depending on the differential input polarity, the entire $I_{bias}$ is *steered* through one branch while the other branch sees near-zero current.

3. **Differential Load (Output Stage):**
   - Two PMOS transistors $M_3$ and $M_4$ act as **diode-connected or cross-coupled loads**.
   - They convert the steered current into a *small-swing differential voltage* at outputs **OUT** and **$\overline{\text{OUT}}$**.

### 🔁 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

- **Step 1:** Differential input (e.g., IN = HIGH, $\overline{\text{IN}}$ = LOW) turns $M_1$ ON and $M_2$ OFF.
- **Step 2:** The full $I_{bias}$ flows through the left branch ($M_1$ → $M_3$).
- **Step 3:** The left output node voltage $V_{OL}$ is pulled down (small swing), and the right output $V_{OH}$ rises to $V_{DD} - V_{DS,sat}$ of $M_3$.
- **Step 4:** A subsequent **DCVSL-like cross-coupled latch** (in some DCSL variants) restores full-rail logic levels and provides complementary outputs without an extra inverter.

### 🔑 Why is Power Lower than Static CMOS?

- The total current drawn from $V_{DD}$ is **fixed at $I_{bias}$**, independent of switching activity.
- Output voltage swing $\Delta V$ is small, so **switching energy = $C_L \cdot \Delta V^2 \ll C_L V_{DD}^2$**.
- No direct path from $V_{DD}$ to GND exists — hence *no short-circuit current*.
- Short-circuit power and dynamic charging of large output nodes are both **eliminated or drastically reduced**.

---

## 2.2 Adiabatic Switching — Theoretical Framework

### 🧠 The "Why" Behind Adiabatic Operation

In **conventional CMOS**, a capacitor $C_L$ is charged from $0$ to $V_{DD}$ abruptly through a switch of resistance $R$. Half of the energy supplied ($CV_{DD}^2 / 2$) is **dissipated as heat** in the resistor, regardless of how fast the switching happens.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The fundamental limit of conventional switching:
> $$\boxed{E_{diss,conv} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2 \quad \text{(50\% of total energy)}}$$

In **adiabatic switching**, the charging is *slow* (quasi-static), and the supply voltage is a **time-varying ramp** $V_{DD}(t) = (V_{DD} / T_s) \cdot t$ for $0 \le t \le T_s$. The current drawn is also ramp-shaped, so the $I^2R$ dissipation becomes proportional to $(RC/T_s)^2$ — a *quadratic* improvement.

### 🔁 Four-Phase Adiabatic Charging Protocol

1. **Phase 1 — Hold:** All nodes isolated; no current flow; $V_{out} = 0$.
2. **Phase 2 — Charge (Evaluate):** Supply ramp rises from 0 to $V_{DD}$ over $T_s$; constant current $I = C_L \cdot V_{DD} / T_s$ charges $C_L$ slowly.
3. **Phase 3 — Hold:** Supply at $V_{DD}$; output is valid.
4. **Phase 4 — Recover (Discharge):** Supply ramp falls from $V_{DD}$ to 0; energy returns to the supply through the inductor (the "recycled" part).

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Description / Boundary |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Conventional Switching Energy (dissipated) | $E_{diss,conv} = \dfrac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$ | 50% of $CV_{DD}^2$ always lost as heat |
| 2 | Conventional Total Energy Supplied | $E_{total,conv} = C_L V_{DD}^2$ | Energy from supply = $CV^2$ |
| 3 | Adiabatic Charging Energy Dissipated | $E_{diss,adiab} = \dfrac{R C_L}{T_s} \cdot C_L V_{DD}^2$ | Falls as $1/T_s$ when $T_s \gg RC$ |
| 4 | Adiabatic Charging (exact) | $E_{diss,adiab} = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \dfrac{R C_L}{T_s} \cdot C_L V_{DD}^2$ | From integration of $I^2(t) R \, dt$ |
| 5 | Adiabatic Quality Factor | $\beta = \dfrac{T_s}{2 R C_L}$ | Larger $\beta$ ⇒ more energy saved |
| 6 | Reduction Ratio | $\dfrac{E_{diss,adiab}}{E_{diss,conv}} = \dfrac{1}{\beta}$ | E.g., $\beta = 10$ ⇒ 10× less power |
| 7 | Charging Current (adiabatic) | $I(t) = \dfrac{C_L V_{DD}}{T_s}$ | Constant for linear ramp |
| 8 | Energy Recovered | $E_{recovered} = C_L V_{DD}^2 \left(1 - \dfrac{R C_L}{T_s}\right)$ | Net energy consumed = $E_{supplied} - E_{recovered}$ |
| 9 | DCSL Static Power | $P_{static} = I_{bias} \cdot V_{DD}$ | Constant, independent of input |
| 10 | DCSL Dynamic Power | $P_{dyn} = f \cdot C_L \cdot \Delta V^2$ | With $\Delta V \ll V_{DD}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Condition Check:** The adiabatic approximation is valid **only when $T_s \gg R C_L$**. If $T_s \le R C_L$, the circuit degenerates into conventional (abrupt) switching.

---

# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Conventional Charging Energy Dissipation

**Given:** A load capacitor $C_L$ is charged from $0$ to $V_{DD}$ through a switch of resistance $R$ using a step supply $V_{DD}$.

**Step 1 — Initial conditions:**
At $t = 0^-$: $V_C(0) = 0$, $i_C(0) = 0$.

**Step 2 — KVL around the loop:**
Applying Kirchhoff's Voltage Law:

$$
V_{DD} = i(t) \cdot R + V_C(t)
$$

where $V_C(t) = \dfrac{1}{C_L} \displaystyle\int_0^t i(\tau) \, d\tau$.

**Step 3 — Solve the first-order RC ODE:**
Differentiating and rearranging:

$$
R \frac{di}{dt} + \frac{i}{C_L} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad i(t) = \frac{V_{DD}}{R} e^{-t/RC_L}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute energy dissipated in $R$:**
The instantaneous power in $R$ is $p(t) = i^2(t) R$, so:

$$
E_{diss} = \int_0^{\infty} i^2(t) R \, dt = \int_0^{\infty} \frac{V_{DD}^2}{R} e^{-2t/RC_L} \, dt
$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the integral:**

$$
E_{diss} = \frac{V_{DD}^2}{R} \cdot \frac{RC_L}{2} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2
$$

> ✅ **Result:** Regardless of $R$ or switching speed, **exactly half** of $CV_{DD}^2$ is *always* dissipated.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Adiabatic Charging Energy Dissipation

**Given:** A load capacitor $C_L$ is charged through resistance $R$ using a **linear ramp** supply:

$$
V_{DD}(t) = \frac{V_{DD}}{T_s} \cdot t, \quad 0 \le t \le T_s
$$

**Step 1 — KVL equation for the ramped supply:**

$$
\frac{V_{DD}}{T_s} \cdot t = i(t) R + \frac{1}{C_L} \int_0^t i(\tau) \, d\tau
$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate to obtain a linear ODE:**

$$
\frac{V_{DD}}{T_s} = R \frac{di}{dt} + \frac{i}{C_L}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve using integrating factor $\mu(t) = e^{t/(RC_L)}$:**

Multiply both sides by $\mu$:

$$
\frac{d}{dt}\left[i(t) e^{t/(RC_L)}\right] = \frac{V_{DD}}{R T_s} e^{t/(RC_L)}
$$

**Step 4 — Integrate from 0 to $t$:**

$$
i(t) e^{t/(RC_L)} - i(0) = \frac{V_{DD}}{R T_s} \cdot RC_L \left[e^{t/(RC_L)} - 1\right]
$$

With $i(0) = 0$:

$$
i(t) = \frac{C_L V_{DD}}{T_s} \left[1 - e^{-t/(RC_L)}\right]
$$

**Step 5 — In the quasi-static limit $T_s \gg R C_L$:**

For $t \gg R C_L$, the exponential term $e^{-t/(RC_L)} \to 0$, leaving:

$$
i(t) \approx \frac{C_L V_{DD}}{T_s} = \text{constant}
$$

This is the **constant current** regime — the heart of adiabatic operation.

**Step 6 — Energy dissipated in resistor $R$ during charging:**

$$
E_{diss} = \int_0^{T_s} i^2(t) R \, dt = \int_0^{T_s} \left(\frac{C_L V_{DD}}{T_s}\right)^2 R \, dt
$$

Pulling constants out:

$$
E_{diss} = \frac{R C_L^2 V_{DD}^2}{T_s^2} \int_0^{T_s} dt = \frac{R C_L^2 V_{DD}^2}{T_s}
$$

**Step 7 — Final compact result:**

$$
\boxed{E_{diss,adiab} = \frac{R C_L}{T_s} \cdot C_L V_{DD}^2 = \frac{1}{\beta} \cdot \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2}
$$

> ✅ **Interpretation:** As $T_s \to \infty$, dissipation $\to 0$ (truly adiabatic). The energy drawn from the supply is approximately $C_L V_{DD}^2$, of which most is *returned* to the source during the discharge phase.

**Step 8 — Energy recovery fraction:**

Since the supply provided $E_{supplied} \approx C_L V_{DD}^2$ over the ramp, and only $E_{diss}$ was lost:

$$
E_{recovered} = C_L V_{DD}^2 - E_{diss} = C_L V_{DD}^2 \left(1 - \frac{R C_L}{T_s}\right)
$$

---

## 3.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix

| Parameter | Conventional Switching | Adiabatic Switching |
|---|---|---|
| Supply waveform | Step $V_{DD}$ | Linear ramp $V_{DD}(t) = V_{DD}\, t/T_s$ |
| Current waveform | Impulse $\propto e^{-t/RC}$ | Constant $C_L V_{DD} / T_s$ |
| Energy supplied | $C_L V_{DD}^2$ | $\approx C_L V_{DD}^2$ |
| Energy dissipated | $\frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$ | $\dfrac{R C_L}{T_s} \cdot C_L V_{DD}^2$ |
| Energy recovered | $0$ (all lost) | $C_L V_{DD}^2 \left(1 - \dfrac{R C_L}{T_s}\right)$ |
| Scaling with frequency | $\propto f$ (linear) | $\propto f^2$ (quadratic) |
| Speed penalty | None | Yes — must operate slowly |
| Hardware cost | Standard CMOS | Requires ramped/AC supply + inductors |

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

> **Problem:** A CMOS gate drives a load $C_L = 10\,\text{pF}$ from a $1.2\,\text{V}$ supply through an on-resistance $R = 1\,\text{k}\Omega$.
> **Case (a):** Conventional abrupt charging.
> **Case (b):** Adiabatic charging with $T_s = 10\,\text{ns}$.
> **Find:** Energy dissipated in each case and the percentage saving.

**Solution:**

**Case (a) — Conventional:**

$$
E_{diss,conv} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2 = \frac{1}{2} (10 \times 10^{-12})(1.2)^2
$$

$$
E_{diss,conv} = 7.2 \times 10^{-12}\,\text{J} = 7.2\,\text{pJ}
$$

**Case (b) — Adiabatic:**

$$
\beta = \frac{T_s}{2 R C_L} = \frac{10 \times 10^{-9}}{2 \cdot (10^3) \cdot (10 \times 10^{-12})} = \frac{10 \times 10^{-9}}{2 \times 10^{-8}} = 0.5
$$

$$
E_{diss,adiab} = \frac{1}{2\beta} C_L V_{DD}^2 = \frac{1}{2 \cdot 0.5} \cdot 7.2\,\text{pJ} = 7.2\,\text{pJ}
$$

> ⚠️ Here, $T_s = 10\,\text{ns}$ is **not slow enough** to give savings ($RC_L = 10\,\text{ns}$ as well).

**Re-try with $T_s = 100\,\text{ns}$:**

$$
\beta = \frac{100 \times 10^{-9}}{2 \times 10^{-8}} = 5
$$

$$
E_{diss,adiab} = \frac{7.2\,\text{pJ}}{2 \cdot 5} = 0.72\,\text{pJ}
$$

$$
\text{Saving} = \left(1 - \frac{0.72}{7.2}\right) \times 100\% = 90\%
$$

> ✅ **Conclusion:** A charging time **10× larger** than $RC_L$ yields **90% energy saving**, confirming the $\beta$-scaling law.

# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture of a DCSL Gate

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    VDD["VDD Supply"]
    BIAS["Tail Current Source Mtail"]
    P1["PMOS Load M3"]
    P2["PMOS Load M4"]
    N1["NMOS Diff Pair M1"]
    N2["NMOS Diff Pair M2"]
    IN["Input IN"]
    INB["Complementary Input INB"]
    OUT["Output OUT"]
    OUTB["Complementary Output OUTB"]
    GND["Ground"]

    VDD --> BIAS
    BIAS --> N1
    BIAS --> N2
    N1 --- P1
    N2 --- P2
    P1 --> OUT
    P2 --> OUTB
    IN --> N1
    INB --> N2
    N1 --> GND
    N2 --> GND
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The constant current $I_{bias}$ from $M_{tail}$ is steered through either the left or right branch, depending on whether IN or $\overline{\text{IN}}$ is HIGH. The two PMOS loads act as diode-connected (or cross-coupled) pull-ups, converting the steered current into complementary output voltages.

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Architecture of an Adiabatic Charging Stage

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Ramped AC Supply VDD_t"]
    B["Switch Transistor Msw"]
    C["On Resistance R"]
    D["Load Capacitance CL"]
    E["Energy Recovery Inductor L"]
    F["Ground Rail"]

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    D --> E
    E --> A
    D --> F
```

> **Reading the diagram:** A ramped (or sinusoidal) supply drives current through the switching transistor into the load capacitor. During the discharge phase, the inductor returns the stored energy back to the supply — the hallmark of *adiabatic energy recovery*.

---

## 4.3 Functional Flow: Adiabatic Charging Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepA["Phase 1 HOLD — All nodes isolated"]
    stepB["Phase 2 CHARGE — VDD ramps 0 to VDD over Ts"]
    stepC["Phase 3 EVALUATE — Output stable logic level"]
    stepD["Phase 4 RECOVER — VDD ramps VDD to 0, energy returned"]
    stepE["Phase 5 NEXT CYCLE — Repeat for new input"]

    stepA --> stepB
    stepB --> stepC
    stepC --> stepD
    stepD --> stepE
    stepE --> stepA
```

> **Key insight:** Only the *charge* phase dissipates energy $E = (RC/T_s) \cdot CV_{DD}^2$. The *recovery* phase is lossless (ideally), making the net energy per cycle vanishingly small when $T_s \gg RC$.

---

## 4.4 Conceptual Energy-Flow Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph conv["Conventional CMOS Charging"]
        V1["Supply VDD"]
        V2["Switch R"]
        V3["Capacitor CL"]
        V4["Heat Loss 50 percent CV squared"]
        V1 --> V2 --> V3
        V2 -. dissipates .-> V4
    end

    subgraph adiab["Adiabatic Charging"]
        W1["Ramped Supply VDD of t"]
        W2["Slow Switch R"]
        W3["Capacitor CL"]
        W4["Inductor L"]
        W5["Recovered Energy to Supply"]
        W1 --> W2 --> W3
        W3 --> W4 --> W5
        W4 -. feeds back .-> W1
    end
```

> **Comparison takeaway:** In conventional CMOS, half the energy is *wasted as heat*. In adiabatic charging, the *majority* of the energy is returned to the supply via the LC tank, leaving only a small $RC/T_s$-scaled dissipation in the switch.

# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 📝 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**What is Differential Current Switch Logic (DCSL)? Mention its key advantages over static CMOS logic.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

DCSL is a low-power CMOS logic family that uses a **constant current source** and a **differential pair** of NMOS transistors to steer current between two output branches, producing **complementary outputs** from differential inputs.

**Key advantages (1 mark each):**
1. **No short-circuit power:** A constant bias current $I_{bias}$ flows at all times; $V_{DD}$-to-GND shoot-through is impossible.
2. **Lower dynamic power:** Output voltage swing $\Delta V$ is small, so $C_L \Delta V^2 \ll C_L V_{DD}^2$.
3. **Built-in complementary outputs:** Eliminates the need for separate inverters, saving area and power.

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define adiabatic switching. Why is it called "adiabatic"?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Adiabatic switching is a low-power circuit technique in which a load capacitance is charged/discharged **gradually** through a **time-varying supply** so that the energy stored in the capacitance is **returned** to the source rather than dissipated as heat (1 mark).

**Etymology (1 mark):** The term *adiabatic* is borrowed from **thermodynamics**, meaning *"no heat exchange with the surroundings."* In electronics, it refers to the **reversal of current flow** through the supply inductor such that the net $I^2R$ dissipation in the switch becomes negligible (1 mark).

**Key relation:** $E_{diss,adiab} = \dfrac{R C_L}{T_s} C_L V_{DD}^2$, which approaches zero as $T_s \to \infty$.

---

## 5.2 📝 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries two sub-parts of **7 marks each**.

---

### **🅰️ Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a **Differential Current Switch Logic (DCSL)** gate. Compare its power consumption with that of static CMOS logic. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for **energy dissipation in adiabatic charging** of a load capacitance $C_L$ through a switch resistance $R$ from a ramped supply $V_{DD}(t) = (V_{DD}/T_s) \cdot t$, and show that it can be made arbitrarily small by increasing $T_s$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q.A(a) — 7 Marks

**Circuit Operation (3.5 marks):**

A DCSL gate consists of:
- A **tail current source** $M_{tail}$ (NMOS biased in saturation) that supplies a constant $I_{bias}$ from $V_{DD}$.
- A **differential pair** $M_1$ and $M_2$ with inputs IN and $\overline{\text{IN}}$.
- **PMOS loads** $M_3$ and $M_4$ providing pull-up to the output nodes OUT and $\overline{\text{OUT}}$.

**Working principle:** When IN = HIGH, $M_1$ turns ON, $M_2$ turns OFF. The current $I_{bias}$ flows through $M_1$ and $M_3$, pulling OUT low. Meanwhile, no current flows through $M_2$, so $\overline{\text{OUT}}$ remains HIGH (near $V_{DD}$). The roles reverse when IN = LOW.

**Power Comparison (3.5 marks):**

| Parameter | Static CMOS | DCSL |
|---|---|---|
| Static power | Leakage only ($\sim$nW) | $I_{bias} \cdot V_{DD}$ (always on) |
| Dynamic power | $f C_L V_{DD}^2$ | $f C_L \Delta V^2$ with $\Delta V \ll V_{DD}$ |
| Short-circuit power | Present during transition | **Zero** (constant current) |
| Total power at low $f$ | Low | **Higher** (due to bias) |
| Total power at high $f$ | High | **Lower** (small swing) |

**Conclusion:** DCSL is preferred for **high-frequency**, **high-speed** applications, but loses at **low frequencies** because of the static bias current.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q.A(b) — 7 Marks

**Setup (1.5 marks):**
Given: $V_{DD}(t) = (V_{DD}/T_s) \cdot t$, switch resistance $R$, load $C_L$.

**KVL equation (1 mark):**

$$
\frac{V_{DD}}{T_s} t = i(t) R + \frac{1}{C_L} \int_0^t i(\tau) \, d\tau
$$

**Differentiate to obtain linear ODE (1 mark):**

$$
R \frac{di}{dt} + \frac{i}{C_L} = \frac{V_{DD}}{T_s}
$$

**Solve using integrating factor (1.5 marks):**

$$
i(t) = \frac{C_L V_{DD}}{T_s} \left[1 - e^{-t/(RC_L)}\right]
$$

**Quasi-static limit $T_s \gg RC_L$ (1 mark):**

$$
i(t) \approx \frac{C_L V_{DD}}{T_s} \quad \text{(constant current)}
$$

**Energy dissipated in $R$ (1 mark):**

$$
E_{diss} = \int_0^{T_s} i^2 R \, dt = \frac{R C_L^2 V_{DD}^2}{T_s^2} \cdot T_s = \frac{R C_L}{T_s} C_L V_{DD}^2
$$

**Conclusion (1 mark):**

$$
\boxed{E_{diss,adiab} = \frac{R C_L}{T_s} C_L V_{DD}^2 \xrightarrow{T_s \to \infty} 0}
$$

Thus, by increasing the charging time $T_s$, the energy dissipation can be made **arbitrarily small** — the essence of adiabatic operation.

---

### **🅱️ Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain the concept of **adiabatic charging** with a suitable circuit schematic. Compare its energy dissipation with conventional CMOS charging. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A CMOS gate drives a load of $C_L = 5\,\text{pF}$ with $V_{DD} = 1.8\,\text{V}$ through a transistor of on-resistance $R = 500\,\Omega$. Compute the energy dissipated per cycle for:
    (i) Conventional charging
    (ii) Adiabatic charging with $T_s = 25\,\text{ns}$ and $T_s = 100\,\text{ns}$.
  Find the percentage energy saved in each adiabatic case. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q.B(a) — 7 Marks

**Concept (2.5 marks):** Adiabatic charging is a slow, controlled charging technique in which a load capacitor is charged through a switch driven by a **time-varying (ramped) supply** $V_{DD}(t)$ over a finite interval $T_s$. Because the current is small and nearly constant, the $I^2R$ dissipation in the switch resistance is reduced by the factor $RC_L/T_s$.

**Circuit schematic (2 marks):** A ramped voltage source connected through a MOSFET switch $M_{sw}$ to a load capacitance $C_L$, with the source's return path through an inductor $L$ to the supply (forming an LC resonant tank for energy recovery).

**Comparison (2.5 marks):**

$$
E_{diss,conv} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2 \quad ; \quad E_{diss,adiab} = \frac{R C_L}{T_s} C_L V_{DD}^2
$$

For $T_s \gg R C_L$, $E_{diss,adiab} \ll E_{diss,conv}$, with the savings growing linearly with $T_s$.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q.B(b) — 7 Marks

**Given:** $C_L = 5 \times 10^{-12}\,\text{F}$, $V_{DD} = 1.8\,\text{V}$, $R = 500\,\Omega$.

**Time constant:**

$$
RC_L = 500 \times 5 \times 10^{-12} = 2.5\,\text{ns}
$$

**(i) Conventional charging (2 marks):**

$$
E_{diss,conv} = \frac{1}{2} (5 \times 10^{-12})(1.8)^2 = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 5 \times 10^{-12} \cdot 3.24
$$

$$
E_{diss,conv} = 8.1 \times 10^{-12}\,\text{J} = 8.1\,\text{pJ}
$$

**(ii-a) Adiabatic with $T_s = 25\,\text{ns}$ (2 marks):**

$$
\beta = \frac{T_s}{2 R C_L} = \frac{25}{2 \times 2.5} = 5
$$

$$
E_{diss,adiab} = \frac{C_L V_{DD}^2}{2\beta} = \frac{8.1\,\text{pJ}}{5} = 1.62\,\text{pJ}
$$

**Saving:** $\left(1 - \frac{1.62}{8.1}\right) \times 100\% = 80\%$

**(ii-b) Adiabatic with $T_s = 100\,\text{ns}$ (3 marks):**

$$
\beta = \frac{100}{2 \times 2.5} = 20
$$

$$
E_{diss,adiab} = \frac{8.1\,\text{pJ}}{20} = 0.405\,\text{pJ}
$$

**Saving:** $\left(1 - \frac{0.405}{8.1}\right) \times 100\% = 95\%$

**Conclusion:** Increasing $T_s$ from $25\,\text{ns}$ to $100\,\text{ns}$ (a $4\times$ increase) improves the energy saving from $80\%$ to $95\%$, confirming the inverse-linear scaling of dissipation with $T_s$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor ½ in adiabatic dissipation** — many students write $E = (RC/T_s) CV^2$ instead of $E = (RC/T_s) \cdot \frac{1}{2} CV^2$. This is a 1-mark deduction.
> 2. **Confusing DCSL with DCVSL** — DCSL uses a *current source* and *constant current*; DCVSL uses *cross-coupled PMOS loads* without a tail current source. Examiners will deduct 1 mark if you interchange them.
> 3. **Not stating the boundary condition** $T_s \gg RC_L$ — this is *required* for the adiabatic approximation. Omitting it costs 0.5–1 mark.
> 4. **Mismeasuring $\beta$** — remember $\beta = T_s / (2RC_L)$, not $T_s / (RC_L)$. Off-by-factor-of-2 errors are the most common.
> 5. **Drawing DCSL with a single-ended output** — DCSL by definition gives *differential* outputs OUT and $\overline{\text{OUT}}$. A single-ended output is a 1-mark error.

---

## 5.3 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

| # | Key Take-Away | Quick Memory Hook |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | DCSL uses a **tail current source + differential pair** to steer constant $I_{bias}$ | "*One current, two paths, opposite ends*" |
| 2 | DCSL output swing $\Delta V \ll V_{DD}$ ⇒ **dynamic power reduced** | "Small swing = small $CV^2$" |
| 3 | DCSL has **zero short-circuit power** | "No path from $V_{DD}$ to GND ever opens fully" |
| 4 | Adiabatic switching = **slow charging + energy recovery** | "*Ramped supply + LC tank = recycling*" |
| 5 | Conventional charging wastes **50%** of $CV^2$ as heat | "*Half the energy always dies in $R$*" |
| 6 | Adiabatic dissipation $E = (RC/T_s) \cdot \frac{1}{2} CV^2$ | "*Slow down by $\beta$ → save by $\beta$*" |
| 7 | Quality factor $\beta = T_s / (2RC_L)$ governs the savings | "*Larger $\beta$ → more saving, less speed*" |
| 8 | Adiabatic logic is best at **low frequencies** (kHz–MHz) | "*Watch chips, RFIDs, sensor nodes*" |
| 9 | Energy recovery requires an **LC tank or ramped supply** | "*Inductor is the recycling bin*" |
| 10 | KTU 2024 expects **derivations + numerical substitution** | "*Always show the integration limits and the boundary $T_s \gg RC$*" |

> 🎯 **Final Exam Tip:** When asked for *comparison*, always quote **both** the conventional **and** the adiabatic formula side-by-side, and explicitly state the **boundary condition** $T_s \gg RC_L$. This single sentence can fetch the difference between a 6 and a 7 in the sub-part.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Adiabatic Amplification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT866 - LOW POWER VLSI
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Adiabatic Amplification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Adiabatic Amplification** is a low-power circuit design technique used in CMOS VLSI where the energy stored on the load capacitance during a switching event is recovered (recycled) back into the power supply instead of being dissipated as heat. The term **adiabatic** (Greek: *adiabatos* = "impassable") refers to a thermodynamic process in which no heat is exchanged with the surroundings; in the electrical domain, it is interpreted as a switching process with **minimal energy dissipation**.

The core principle is that energy transfer to and from a load capacitor $C_L$ should be performed **slowly and reversibly** through a controlled current source (typically implemented using a time-varying **power-clock** supply), so that the $I^2R$ loss across the switching transistor is made arbitrarily small.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4 – Adiabatic Logic Family):**
> Adiabatic amplification is the foundational circuit primitive that enables **reversible computing** and **energy-recovery logic**, forming the theoretical basis of families such as **2N-2N2P, 2N-2N2N2P, PFAL, SCAL, and CAL**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Water Wheel"

Imagine a water tank (the **load capacitor $C_L$**) being filled from a higher reservoir (the **power supply $V_{DD}$**):

- **Conventional (non-adiabatic) filling:** You open a wide pipe suddenly. Water rushes in violently, splashes (energy lost as heat/sound), and the tank fills to the brim. Energy is **dissipated** in the turbulence.
- **Adiabatic filling:** You open the valve **very slowly** so water trickles in gently. No splashing occurs, and when the tank is full, you can **slowly drain it back into the reservoir**, recovering the potential energy.

In CMOS, the "wide pipe opened suddenly" corresponds to connecting $C_L$ directly to $V_{DD}$ through a low-resistance transistor — a **sudden voltage step** that dissipates $\frac{1}{2}CV_{DD}^2$ as heat. The "slow trickle" is the **adiabatic ramp**, which makes the dissipation approach zero (in the limit of infinitely slow switching).

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value / Expression |
|---|---|---|
| Load Capacitance | $C_L$ | $\mathbf{fF}$ to $\mathbf{pF}$ range |
| Energy dissipated in conventional CMOS | $E_{conv}$ | $\frac{1}{2}C_LV_{DD}^2$ |
| Energy dissipated in adiabatic switching | $E_{adiab}$ | $\left(\frac{RC_L}{T}\right)C_LV_{DD}^2$ |
| Figure of merit | $PDP$ | Power $\times$ Delay |
| Adiabaticity factor | $\tau / RC_L$ | $\gg 1$ for "true" adiabatic operation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** In adiabatic circuits, the dissipation is **proportional to the switching speed** — the slower you switch, the less energy is wasted. This is the exact **opposite** of conventional CMOS, where dissipation is independent of switching speed.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Conventional Charging — The Baseline

When a capacitor $C_L$ is charged from $0$ to $V_{DD}$ through a transistor modeled as a resistor $R_{on}$ in the conventional (step-voltage) manner:

- A total charge $Q = C_L V_{DD}$ is delivered.
- The energy supplied by the source = $C_L V_{DD}^2$.
- The energy stored in the capacitor = $\frac{1}{2}C_L V_{DD}^2$.
- The energy dissipated in $R_{on}$ = $\frac{1}{2}C_L V_{DD}^2$ (independent of $R_{on}$).
- The energy left on $C_L$ is **wasted** during discharge to ground.

## 2.2 Adiabatic Charging — Step-by-Step Logic

To avoid dissipation, two conditions must be satisfied:

1. **Never open a switch while there is a non-zero voltage across it** (avoids $V \cdot I$ instantaneous dissipation).
2. **Never close a switch while there is a non-zero current through it** (avoids inductive kick and reflection losses).

This is achieved by:

- Using a **ramping supply** (trapezoidal or sinusoidal **power clock**) instead of a DC supply.
- The transistor behaves as a **variable resistor** in series with the ramp generator.
- At every instant, the current $I(t) = C_L \frac{dV(t)}{dt}$ is small and bounded.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description / Boundary Condition | KTU Use-Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_{conv} = \alpha \cdot \frac{1}{2}C_L V_{DD}^2$ | Conventional CMOS switching energy; $\alpha$ = switching activity factor | Baseline for comparison |
| 2 | $P_{conv} = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f_{clk}$ | Dynamic power in conventional CMOS | Module 1 recall |
| 3 | $E_{adiab} = \frac{R_{on} C_L}{T} \cdot C_L V_{DD}^2$ | Energy dissipated in adiabatic ramp of period $T$ | Core topic formula |
| 4 | $P_{adiab} = \frac{R_{on} C_L}{T} \cdot C_L V_{DD}^2 f_{clk}$ | Average adiabatic power | Direct KTU question |
| 5 | $E_{adiab} = 2 \left(\frac{R_{on} C_L}{T}\right) \cdot \frac{1}{2}C_L V_{DD}^2$ | Full charge-recovery cycle (charge + discharge) | Energy-recovery analysis |
| 6 | $I(t) = C_L \frac{dV_{clk}(t)}{dt}$ | Instantaneous current drawn by load capacitance | Ramp supply design |
| 7 | $E_{stored}(t) = \frac{1}{2}C_L V_{clk}^2(t)$ | Instantaneous energy stored in $C_L$ | Recovery calculation |
| 8 | $Q_{gate}(t) = \int_{0}^{t} I(\tau) \, d\tau$ | Charge delivered to gate capacitance | Charge-recovery proof |
| 9 | $f_{res} = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC_L}}$ | Resonant frequency of LC tank | Power-clock generation |
| 10 | $\eta_{recovery} = 1 - \frac{2R_{on}C_L}{T}$ | Energy-recovery efficiency | Performance metric |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Condition for True Adiabaticity:** $T \gg R_{on} C_L$ (the ramp period must be much larger than the RC time constant). When $T \rightarrow \infty$, $E_{adiab} \rightarrow 0$ — *theoretically* zero dissipation.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Adiabatic amplification is foundational in:
- **Ultra-low-power IoT sensor nodes** (energy harvesting)
- **Wearable biomedical implants** (pacemakers, cochlear implants)
- **RF ID tags and smart cards** (passive power, no battery replacement)
- **Reversible / quantum computing front-ends** (Landauer-bound architectures)
- **Asynchronous / globally-asynchronous locally-synchronous (GALS)** VLSI systems

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Conventional (Step-Voltage) Charging Energy

**Setup:** A capacitor $C_L$ initially at $0\,V$ is connected at $t = 0$ to a DC source $V_{DD}$ through a switch of on-resistance $R_{on}$.

**Step 1.** Write the KVL equation around the loop:

$$
V_{DD} = I(t) R_{on} + V_C(t)
$$

**Step 2.** Substitute the capacitor relation $I(t) = C_L \frac{dV_C}{dt}$:

$$
V_{DD} = R_{on} C_L \frac{dV_C}{dt} + V_C(t)
$$

**Step 3.** Solve this first-order ODE with initial condition $V_C(0) = 0$:

$$
V_C(t) = V_{DD}\left(1 - e^{-t/R_{on}C_L}\right)
$$

**Step 4.** Compute the current:

$$
I(t) = \frac{V_{DD}}{R_{on}} e^{-t/R_{on}C_L}
$$

**Step 5.** Compute the energy delivered by the source from $0$ to $\infty$:

$$
E_{src} = \int_{0}^{\infty} V_{DD} \cdot I(t) \, dt = \frac{V_{DD}^2}{R_{on}} \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-t/R_{on}C_L} \, dt
$$

**Step 6.** Evaluate the integral:

$$
E_{src} = \frac{V_{DD}^2}{R_{on}} \cdot R_{on} C_L = C_L V_{DD}^2
$$

**Step 7.** Compute the energy stored in $C_L$ at $t = \infty$:

$$
E_{stored} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2
$$

**Step 8.** By conservation of energy, the energy dissipated in $R_{on}$:

$$
E_{diss} = E_{src} - E_{stored} = C_L V_{DD}^2 - \frac{1}{2}C_L V_{DD}^2 = \frac{1}{2}C_L V_{DD}^2
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion 1:** $\frac{1}{2}C_L V_{DD}^2$ is dissipated **every switching event**, regardless of how small $R_{on}$ is, and this energy is **not recoverable** when $C_L$ is later discharged to ground.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Adiabatic (Constant-Current Ramp) Charging Energy

**Setup:** Now drive the RC network with a **linearly ramping** supply $V_{clk}(t) = \frac{V_{DD}}{T} t$ for $0 \le t \le T$, where $T$ is the ramp duration.

**Step 1.** KVL with ramping supply:

$$
\frac{V_{DD}}{T} t = R_{on} C_L \frac{dV_C}{dt} + V_C(t)
$$

**Step 2.** Assume a quasi-static regime where the ramp is slow compared to $R_{on} C_L$ ($T \gg R_{on} C_L$). In this limit, the capacitor voltage **tracks** the supply:

$$
V_C(t) \approx \frac{V_{DD}}{T} t
$$

**Step 3.** The current through the resistor is therefore small and constant:

$$
I(t) = C_L \frac{dV_C}{dt} = C_L \frac{V_{DD}}{T} = \text{constant}
$$

**Step 4.** Compute the instantaneous power dissipated in $R_{on}$:

$$
P_{diss}(t) = I^2(t) R_{on} = \left(\frac{C_L V_{DD}}{T}\right)^2 R_{on} = \frac{R_{on} C_L^2 V_{DD}^2}{T^2}
$$

**Step 5.** Integrate over the ramp duration to obtain total dissipation:

$$
E_{diss} = \int_{0}^{T} P_{diss}(t) \, dt = \frac{R_{on} C_L^2 V_{DD}^2}{T^2} \cdot T = \frac{R_{on} C_L^2 V_{DD}^2}{T}
$$

**Step 6.** Rewrite in canonical KTU form:

$$
E_{diss} = \left(\frac{R_{on} C_L}{T}\right) \cdot C_L V_{DD}^2
$$

**Step 7.** Compare with conventional result. Define the **adiabaticity factor** $\kappa = \frac{R_{on} C_L}{T}$:

- If $T \gg R_{on} C_L$ (slow ramp), then $\kappa \to 0$ and $E_{diss} \to 0$.
- If $T \to R_{on} C_L$ (fast ramp), $\kappa \to 1$ and we recover $E_{diss} \to C_L V_{DD}^2$ (the source-delivered energy of the conventional case, since the conventional $\frac{1}{2}C_L V_{DD}^2$ in the resistor is only half — the rest is stored; in the adiabatic case the "source" still delivers $C_L V_{DD}^2$ but the resistor loss is reduced).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion 2:** Adiabatic dissipation is **quadratically reduced** by lengthening the ramp period $T$. This is the central trade-off: **power for speed** (the energy-delay product is roughly constant).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Full Charge–Discharge Recovery Cycle

**Setup:** A complete adiabatic cycle consists of (a) ramp-up charge from $0$ to $V_{DD}$, and (b) ramp-down discharge from $V_{DD}$ back to $0$, both with period $T$.

**Step 1.** Energy lost during charge ramp (from §3.2):

$$
E_{charge} = \frac{R_{on} C_L^2 V_{DD}^2}{T}
$$

**Step 2.** Energy recovered during the discharge ramp (the current flows **back** through the same resistor, dissipating equally):

$$
E_{discharge} = \frac{R_{on} C_L^2 V_{DD}^2}{T}
$$

**Step 3.** Total dissipation per cycle:

$$
E_{cycle} = E_{charge} + E_{discharge} = 2 \frac{R_{on} C_L^2 V_{DD}^2}{T}
$$

**Step 4.** Rewrite using the conventional reference energy $\frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$:

$$
E_{cycle} = 2 \left(\frac{R_{on} C_L}{T}\right) \cdot \frac{1}{2}C_L V_{DD}^2
$$

**Step 5.** The fraction of energy **recovered back to the supply** is therefore:

$$
\eta_{recovery} = 1 - \frac{2R_{on} C_L}{T}
$$

**Step 6.** Average power when the cycle repeats at clock frequency $f_{clk} = 1/T_{clk}$:

$$
P_{adiab} = E_{cycle} \cdot f_{clk} = 2 \frac{R_{on} C_L^2 V_{DD}^2}{T} \cdot f_{clk}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** The recovered energy is what makes adiabatic logic qualitatively different from merely slowing down a conventional circuit. The energy is not "wasted slowly" — it is **explicitly pumped back** into the supply rail or the next gate.

## 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Parameter | Conventional CMOS | Adiabatic CMOS | Reduction Factor |
|---|---|---|---|
| Energy per switch | $\frac{1}{2}C_L V_{DD}^2$ | $\frac{R_{on}C_L}{T} \cdot C_L V_{DD}^2$ | $\frac{T}{2R_{on}C_L}$ |
| Energy dependence on $V_{DD}$ | Quadratic | Quadratic | — |
| Energy dependence on speed | **None** | Linear in $1/T$ | $\propto$ speed |
| Recoverable? | **No** | **Yes** | — |
| Power-clock required? | No | **Yes** | — |
| Practical $V_{DD}$ range | $0.7$ to $1.2\,V$ | $0.5$ to $3.3\,V$ | — |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Architecture of an Adiabatic Amplifier

The following Mermaid diagram models the **functional architecture** of a generic adiabatic amplifier cell. Because adiabatic amplification is a circuit-level concept, the block diagram emphasizes signal flow and energy flow rather than transistor-level physical layout.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PCLK[Power Clock Generator] -->|V_clk t| DRV[Driver Stage]
    INP[Input Logic Signal] --> CTRL[Control Logic]
    CTRL -->|gate drive| DRV
    DRV -->|controlled current| NOD[Amplification Node]
    NOD -->|energy stored| CL[Load Capacitance C_L]
    CL -->|recovered charge| REC[Energy Recovery Latch]
    REC -->|recycled current| PCLK
    OUT[Buffered Output] --> NOD
    NOD --> OUT
    style PCLK fill:#e8f0ff,stroke:#1f4e89
    style NOD fill:#fff4d6,stroke:#a67c00
    style CL fill:#d6f5d6,stroke:#2e7d32
    style REC fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#b00020
```

**Interpretation of the block diagram:**

- **Power Clock Generator** — produces the trapezoidal or sinusoidal AC supply $V_{clk}(t)$.
- **Control Logic** — synchronizes the amplifier with the four phases of the power clock (evaluate, hold, recover, wait).
- **Driver Stage** — acts as the variable resistance that converts the ramp voltage into a bounded current $I(t) = C_L \frac{dV_{clk}}{dt}$.
- **Amplification Node** — the internal high-impedance node where the amplified signal develops.
- **Load Capacitance $C_L$** — stores the recovered energy.
- **Energy Recovery Latch** — funnels the discharged charge back into the power clock rail, achieving a near-closed energy loop.

## 4.2 Phased Operation of the Power Clock

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant PC as Power Clock
    participant DR as Driver
    participant LD as Load C_L
    participant ER as Energy Recovery
    Note over PC: Phase 1: Ramp-Up (Evaluate)
    PC->>DR: V_clk rises 0 to V_DD
    DR->>LD: Constant current charges C_L
    Note over PC: Phase 2: Hold (Plateau)
    PC->>DR: V_clk constant at V_DD
    DR-->>LD: Output stable, no current
    Note over PC: Phase 3: Ramp-Down (Recover)
    PC->>DR: V_clk falls V_DD to 0
    LD->>ER: Discharge current back to source
    Note over PC: Phase 4: Wait (Idle)
    PC-->>DR: V_clk at 0
    DR-->>LD: No activity
```

## 4.3 Energy-Flow Comparison — Conventional vs Adiabatic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CONV[Conventional CMOS Energy Flow]
        V1[V_DD Source] -->|E = half C V squared| R1[R_on Switch]
        R1 -->|E = half C V squared LOST| H1[Heat Dissipation]
        R1 -->|E = half C V squared STORED| C1[Load C_L]
        C1 -->|next cycle LOST| H1
    end
    subgraph ADB[Adiabatic CMOS Energy Flow]
        V2[Power Clock] -->|E delivered| R2[R_on Switch]
        R2 -->|small E = RC over T times CV squared LOST| H2[Minimal Heat]
        R2 -->|E stored reversibly| C2[Load C_L]
        C2 -->|E RECOVERED back to source| V2
    end
    style H1 fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#b00020
    style H2 fill:#d6f5d6,stroke:#2e7d32
```

> [!NOTE]
> The conventional path always terminates in **dissipation** (red). The adiabatic path forms a **closed loop** with only a small residual loss (green), proportional to the adiabaticity factor $\frac{R_{on}C_L}{T}$.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper PEECT866]`
**Explain the term "adiabatic" in the context of low-power VLSI design. Why is energy recovery the central idea behind adiabatic switching?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- *Adiabatic* is a thermodynamic term meaning "no heat exchange with the surroundings." (1 mark)
- In VLSI, an adiabatic switching event is one in which the energy stored on the load capacitance is **recovered back to the supply** instead of being dissipated in the transistor's on-resistance. (1 mark)
- It is central because it transforms the fundamental $\frac{1}{2}CV^2$ switching loss of conventional CMOS from an **irreversible dissipation** into a **reusable, recirculating** energy transfer, achieving asymptotic zero-energy switching in the limit of slow, constant-current charging. (1 mark)

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**Compare the switching energy expressions of a conventional CMOS gate and an adiabatic CMOS gate. Identify the role of the ramp period $T$.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- Conventional: $E_{conv} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$ — **independent of speed**. (1 mark)
- Adiabatic: $E_{adiab} = \left(\frac{R_{on} C_L}{T}\right) C_L V_{DD}^2$ — **inversely proportional to ramp period $T$**. (1 mark)
- $T$ is the duration of the linear voltage ramp supplied by the power clock. Increasing $T$ reduces the current $I = C_L V_{DD}/T$ and hence the $I^2 R$ dissipation, giving a **power-for-speed trade-off** characterized by a roughly constant energy-delay product. (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**(a)** Derive the energy dissipated in the on-resistance of a CMOS transistor when a load capacitance $C_L$ is charged from $0$ to $V_{DD}$ using:
  (i) a **step-voltage (conventional)** supply,
  (ii) a **linear-ramp (adiabatic)** supply of period $T$.  **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A $0.18\,\mu m$ adiabatic gate has $C_L = 100\,fF$, $R_{on} = 1\,k\Omega$, $V_{DD} = 1.8\,V$, and ramp period $T = 10\,ns$. The clock frequency is $f_{clk} = 100\,MHz$. Compute: **(i)** the energy dissipated per cycle, **(ii)** the average power, and **(iii)** the percentage reduction in dissipation compared to a conventional CMOS gate with the same parameters. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for A(a) — Derivation (7 marks)

**(i) Step-voltage (conventional) charging:** (3.5 marks)

- KVL: $V_{DD} = R_{on} C_L \dfrac{dV_C}{dt} + V_C(t)$, with $V_C(0) = 0$. (1 mark)
- Solution: $V_C(t) = V_{DD}\!\left(1 - e^{-t/R_{on}C_L}\right)$, and $I(t) = \dfrac{V_{DD}}{R_{on}} e^{-t/R_{on}C_L}$. (1 mark)
- Energy delivered by source: $E_{src} = \int_0^{\infty} V_{DD} I(t)\,dt = C_L V_{DD}^2$. (0.5 mark)
- Energy stored in $C_L$: $E_{stored} = \dfrac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$. (0.5 mark)
- Energy dissipated in $R_{on}$: $E_{diss} = E_{src} - E_{stored} = \dfrac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$. (0.5 mark)

**(ii) Linear-ramp (adiabatic) charging:** (3.5 marks)

- Supply: $V_{clk}(t) = \dfrac{V_{DD}}{T} t$. (0.5 mark)
- For $T \gg R_{on} C_L$, the capacitor follows the ramp: $V_C(t) \approx V_{clk}(t)$. (0.5 mark)
- Constant current: $I(t) = C_L \dfrac{dV_C}{dt} = \dfrac{C_L V_{DD}}{T}$. (1 mark)
- Power dissipated: $P_{diss}(t) = I^2 R_{on} = \dfrac{R_{on} C_L^2 V_{DD}^2}{T^2}$. (0.5 mark)
- Energy dissipated over one ramp: $E_{diss} = \int_0^T P_{diss}(t)\,dt = \dfrac{R_{on} C_L^2 V_{DD}^2}{T}$. (1 mark)

---

#### Model Solution for A(b) — Numerical (7 marks)

**Given:** $C_L = 100\,fF = 100 \times 10^{-15}\,F$, $R_{on} = 1\,k\Omega = 10^3\,\Omega$, $V_{DD} = 1.8\,V$, $T = 10\,ns = 10^{-8}\,s$, $f_{clk} = 100\,MHz$.

**(i) Energy dissipated per cycle (charge + discharge):** (2 marks)

- Compute adiabaticity factor: $\kappa = \dfrac{R_{on} C_L}{T} = \dfrac{10^3 \times 100 \times 10^{-15}}{10^{-8}} = \dfrac{10^{-10}}{10^{-8}} = 10^{-2} = 0.01$. (0.5 mark)
- Energy per ramp (charge only): $E_{diss,charge} = \kappa \cdot C_L V_{DD}^2$. (0.5 mark)
- $C_L V_{DD}^2 = 100 \times 10^{-15} \times (1.8)^2 = 100 \times 10^{-15} \times 3.24 = 3.24 \times 10^{-13}\,J$. (0.5 mark)
- $E_{diss,charge} = 0.01 \times 3.24 \times 10^{-13} = 3.24 \times 10^{-15}\,J$. (0.5 mark)

**(ii) Average power (charge + discharge cycle):** (2 marks)

- $E_{cycle} = 2 \times 3.24 \times 10^{-15} = 6.48 \times 10^{-15}\,J$. (0.5 mark)
- $P_{avg} = E_{cycle} \times f_{clk} = 6.48 \times 10^{-15} \times 100 \times 10^{6} = 6.48 \times 10^{-7}\,W = 0.648\,\mu W$. (1.5 marks)

**(iii) Percentage reduction vs conventional:** (3 marks)

- Conventional energy per cycle (charge only — discharging to ground also dissipates $\frac{1}{2}CV^2$ in the nMOS):
- $E_{conv,cycle} = C_L V_{DD}^2 = 3.24 \times 10^{-13}\,J$ (full energy delivered = full dissipation for a one-time use). (1 mark)
- Alternatively, single-switch dissipation = $\frac{1}{2}CV^2 = 1.62 \times 10^{-13}\,J$. (0.5 mark)
- Using the charge-only figure: $\text{Reduction} = \dfrac{3.24 \times 10^{-13} - 3.24 \times 10^{-15}}{3.24 \times 10^{-13}} \times 100\% = 99\%$. (1.5 marks)

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]`
**(a)** With the aid of a neat diagram, describe the **operating principle of an adiabatic amplifier** using a power-clock supply. Explain the four phases of operation and the role of the energy-recovery latch. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define the **adiabaticity factor** $\kappa = \frac{R_{on} C_L}{T}$. Show that the **energy-delay product (EDP)** of an adiabatic gate is approximately constant, and discuss the practical implications of this trade-off for high-performance VLSI design. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for B(a) — Operating Principle (7 marks)

1. **Block diagram** — draw the standard adiabatic cell with: input logic, control logic, power-clock generator, driver transistor, amplification node, load $C_L$, and energy-recovery latch. (2 marks)
2. **Power-clock waveform** — sketch a trapezoidal waveform with four phases: ramp-up, plateau (hold), ramp-down, and wait (idle). (1 mark)
3. **Phase description:**
   - *Ramp-up (Evaluate):* The power clock rises from $0$ to $V_{DD}$. A small constant current $I = C_L V_{DD}/T$ charges $C_L$. (1 mark)
   - *Plateau (Hold):* $V_{clk}$ is constant at $V_{DD}$; no current flows, output is stable. (0.5 mark)
   - *Ramp-down (Recover):* $V_{clk}$ falls from $V_{DD}$ to $0$. The capacitor discharges back into the power-clock rail, recycling the energy. (1 mark)
   - *Wait (Idle):* Both clock and output are at $0$, preparing for the next evaluation. (0.5 mark)
4. **Role of the energy-recovery latch** — it ensures that the discharge current returns to the supply source (or to a subsequent gate) rather than being dumped to ground, closing the energy loop. (1 mark)

---

#### Model Solution for B(b) — Adiabaticity Factor and EDP (7 marks)

**Definition (1 mark):** $\kappa = \dfrac{R_{on} C_L}{T}$ — the ratio of the RC time constant of the charging path to the ramp period. **True adiabatic operation requires $\kappa \ll 1$.**

**Energy expression (2 marks):**

$$
E_{diss} = \kappa \cdot C_L V_{DD}^2
$$

**Delay is proportional to the ramp period (1 mark):** $t_d \propto T$.

**Energy-delay product (2 marks):**

$$
EDP = E_{diss} \times t_d = \kappa \cdot C_L V_{DD}^2 \times T = (R_{on} C_L) \cdot C_L V_{DD}^2
$$

This is **independent of $T$** — the EDP is approximately constant.

**Practical implications (1 mark):** For high-performance, high-frequency designs, the constant EDP implies that one cannot simultaneously achieve **high speed and ultra-low energy**; adiabatic logic therefore targets **low-to-medium frequency** applications (kHz to a few hundred MHz) such as biomedical implants, IoT sensors, and RFID, where energy per operation (not throughput) is the primary concern.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2:** Many students compute only the *charge* dissipation and forget to double it for the full charge–discharge recovery cycle. Always state $E_{cycle} = 2 E_{charge}$.
> 2. **Confusing ramp period with clock period:** The ramp period $T$ is the time taken for $V_{clk}$ to rise from $0$ to $V_{DD}$ (typically $T_{clk}/4$ for a 4-phase trapezoidal clock), not the full clock period. Read the question carefully.
> 3. **Ignoring the activity factor $\alpha$:** In conventional CMOS dynamic power, $\alpha$ is essential; in adiabatic energy expressions, it is sometimes already embedded in $f_{clk}$.
> 4. **Stating "zero power":** Adiabatic circuits do not dissipate *zero* power — they dissipate *small* power, proportional to $\kappa$. Infinite ramp time is non-physical.
> 5. **Confusing $R_{on}$ with output resistance:** $R_{on}$ in the adiabatic formula is the **effective on-resistance of the path** during the ramp, which may be time-varying for short-channel devices.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Adiabatic = energy recovery**, not "zero power." Dissipation scales as $\kappa = R_{on}C_L/T$.
- Conventional switching energy $\frac{1}{2}CV_{DD}^2$ is **independent of speed**; adiabatic energy is **inversely proportional to ramp period**.
- A **trapezoidal or sinusoidal power clock** replaces the DC $V_{DD}$ to enable slow, reversible, constant-current charging.
- The **two golden rules** of adiabatic switching: (i) never break a path with current, (ii) never make a path with voltage.
- The **energy-delay product is constant** — fundamental power–speed trade-off.
- A complete adiabatic cycle includes both **charging and recovery** — multiply the one-ramp dissipation by **2**.
- Practical adiabatic families: **2N-2N2P, PFAL, SCAL, CAL, ECRL, DCPAL** — all derived from the basic adiabatic amplification principle.
- Adiabatic logic is best suited to **low-frequency, energy-constrained** applications (biomedical, IoT, RFID), not to high-performance processors.
- Power-clock generation requires **on-chip LC resonators or multi-phase clock trees** — a significant area and design overhead.
- For KTU numerical problems, always compute the **adiabaticity factor first**, then the **energy**, then the **power** — this is the evaluator's expected step sequence.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Adiabatic Logic Gates
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEECT866 - LOW POWER VLSI > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Adiabatic Logic Gates ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview > [!NOTE] > **Adiabatic (Definition):** The word *adiabatic* comes from the Greek word meaning "impassable." In thermodynamics, an adiabatic process is one in which no energy (heat) is exchanged with the surroundings. In Low Power VLSI, *adiabatic switching* refers to a technique where charge transfer happens so gradually that minimal energy is dissipated as heat into the substrate. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):** *Adiabatic Logic Gates* are a class of CMOS logic circuits in which the energy stored in the load capacitance is **recovered back into the power supply** (rather than being dissipated as heat) by driving the circuit with a time-varying (ramping/AC) power-clock supply, instead of a constant DC rail. ### Conceptual Analogy — "The Water Wheel" Imagine pouring water into a bucket from a height (conventional CMOS charging). - **Conventional CMOS:** The water falls rapidly from the tap into the bucket. The kinetic energy of the water sloshing around converts into heat (energy lost forever). This is exactly what happens when a constant V_dd abruptly charges a capacitance — the sudden current rush dissipates P = CV²/2 as heat. - **Adiabatic Logic:** Instead, imagine pumping the water very slowly through a thin pipe into the bucket. The water enters gently, and when you empty the bucket, you pump it back slowly into the reservoir. The pump (the power clock) recaptures the energy. *No energy is wasted as heat.* The two golden rules of adiabatic switching are: 1. **Never switch the transistor abruptly** (turn-on should be slow and gradual). 2. **Never discharge the capacitance to ground through the transistor** (recover the charge back to the source). > [!NOTE] > **Key Physical Insight:** In adiabatic logic, the energy dissipation per switching event scales linearly with frequency (P ∝ f·C·V·V_dd·(RC/T)) instead of being frequency-independent as in standard CMOS (E = ½CV²). At low frequencies, this can yield orders-of-magnitude lower power. ### Salient Metrics at a Glance | Metric | Conventional CMOS | Adiabatic (Quasi) | Adiabatic (Fully) | |---|---|---|---| | Energy per Cycle | **½ C V_dd²** (fixed) | **C V_dd² (RC/T)** | C V_dd² (RC/T)² | | Charge Recovery | None | Partial | Full | | Reversibility | Irreversible | Partially reversible | Fully reversible | | Complexity | Low | Moderate | High | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Why Conventional CMOS Wastes Power In a standard CMOS inverter, when the input transitions, the PMOS or NMOS turns on abruptly, connecting the output node (load capacitance $C_L$) directly to $V_{dd}$ or GND. The instantaneous current is: $$i(t) = \frac{V_{dd}}{R_{on}} e^{-t/R_{on}C_L}$$ The energy dissipated in the channel resistance, regardless of how fast or slow the switching occurs, is **always** $\frac{1}{2} C_L V_{dd}^2$. Half the energy drawn from the supply is lost as heat in the PMOS; the other half is stored on the capacitor. During discharge, the stored energy is burned in the NMOS. ### 2.2 The Adiabatic Principle — "Slow Charging Through a Resistor" The fundamental trick of adiabatic logic is to replace the abrupt DC supply with a **ramping power-clock (PC)** waveform. A constant current source is used to charge $C_L$ through a switch (transistor). The charging time $T$ is made much larger than the RC time constant of the gate. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Core 'Why':** A current source delivering charge slowly does so at near-zero voltage drop across the transistor, hence near-zero $I^2R$ dissipation. Energy that is not dissipated is recoverable. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Concept | Physical Meaning | Typical Units / Boundary | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $E_{conv} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{dd}^2$ | Energy per charge/discharge in standard CMOS | Joules; **frequency-independent** | | 2 | $i(t) = C_L \dfrac{dV(t)}{dt}$ | Capacitor i-v relation used to derive adiabatic loss | Amps | | 3 | $P_{adiabatic} = \dfrac{R_{on} \, C_L}{T} \, C_L \, V_{dd}^2 \, f$ | Adiabatic dissipation (quasi-adiabatic) | Watts; scales with $1/T$ | | 4 | $E_{adiabatic} = \dfrac{R_{on} C_L}{T} \, C_L V_{dd}^2$ | Energy per adiabatic switching event | Joules | | 5 | $f_{max} \approx \dfrac{1}{10 R_{on} C_L}$ | Practical upper frequency limit of adiabatic circuits | Hz; **slow-clock constraint** | | 6 | $\eta = \dfrac{E_{recovered}}{E_{supplied}}$ | Energy-recovery efficiency (0 → 1) | Dimensionless | | 7 | $V_{out}(t) = \dfrac{1}{C_L} \int_0^t i(\tau) \, d\tau$ | Linear ramp output if $i$ = constant | Volts | | 8 | $V_{th} \leq V_{clock}(t) \leq V_{dd}$ | Transistor ON condition for adiabatic ramp | Constraint | > [!WARNING] > In KTU examinations, **never write the adiabatic dissipation as $\frac{1}{2}CV^2$** unless the question specifies a fully reversible lossless system. For the standard *quasi-adiabatic* case, use $\frac{R_{on}C_L}{T} \cdot C_L V_{dd}^2$. This is a very common mark-deduction pitfall. ### 2.4 Classification of Adiabatic Logic Families A clean taxonomic tree is essential for KTU long-answer questions: 1. **Fully Adiabatic (Reversible) Logic** — Theoretically zero dissipation; complex clocking and routing. - **SCRL** (Split-level Charge Recovery Logic) - **PAL** (Pass-transistor Adiabatic Logic) - **2LAL** (2-Level Adiabatic Logic) - **CAL** (Clocked Adiabatic Logic) 2. **Quasi-Adiabatic (Partially Recoverable) Logic** — Practical, with some non-adiabatic loss; widely used in KTU syllabus. - **ECRL** (Efficient Charge Recovery Logic) — 2 transistors + diodes - **PFAL** (Pass-transistor / Positive Feedback Adiabatic Logic) — 4 transistors - **2N-2N2P** — 2 NMOS + 2 PMOS per gate - **TSEL** (True Single-Phase Energy-Recovering Logic) ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Battery-powered IoT sensors:** energy recovery extends battery life by 5×–10× in ultra-low duty-cycle designs. - **RF-ID tags and implantable medical devices** (pacemakers, cochlear implants) where heat dissipation is unacceptable. - **Wearable and biomedical electronics** with strict thermal budgets. - **Cryogenic computing** (quantum control hardware) where heat removal is impossible. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation — Conventional CMOS Energy Loss per Cycle > **Goal:** Prove that a standard CMOS gate dissipates exactly $\frac{1}{2} C_L V_{dd}^2$ per switching event. **Step 1 — Charging Phase.** When the PMOS turns on, the supply $V_{dd}$ charges the load capacitance $C_L$ through the on-resistance $R_{on}$. The current is: $$ i(t) = \frac{V_{dd}}{R_{on}} \, e^{-t/R_{on}C_L} $$ **Step 2 — Instantaneous Power in the Channel.** The voltage across the PMOS is $V_{dd} - V_{out}(t) = V_{dd} e^{-t/R_{on}C_L}$. Hence the power dissipated: $$ P_{pmos}(t) = i^2(t) \, R_{on} = \frac{V_{dd}^2}{R_{on}} \, e^{-2t/R_{on}C_L} $$ **Step 3 — Integrate Over the Full Charging Period** ($t = 0$ to $t \to \infty$): $$ \begin{aligned} E_{pmos} &= \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{V_{dd}^2}{R_{on}} \, e^{-2t/R_{on}C_L} \, dt \\[4pt] &= \frac{V_{dd}^2}{R_{on}} \cdot \left[ \frac{-R_{on}C_L}{2} \, e^{-2t/R_{on}C_L} \right]_{0}^{\infty} \\[4pt] &= \frac{V_{dd}^2}{R_{on}} \cdot \frac{R_{on}C_L}{2} \\[4pt] &= \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{dd}^2 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Symmetry of Discharge.** The NMOS during the discharge path dissipates the **same** $\frac{1}{2} C_L V_{dd}^2$ from the stored charge on $C_L$. **Step 5 — Total Energy per Cycle:** $$ E_{total} = E_{pmos} + E_{nmos} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{dd}^2 + \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{dd}^2 = C_L V_{dd}^2 $$ Of this total, only $\frac{1}{2} C_L V_{dd}^2$ is stored on the capacitor; the other half is lost as **irreversible heat**. Hence the per-cycle dissipation is $\boxed{\frac{1}{2} C_L V_{dd}^2}$ — independent of $R_{on}$, frequency, or transistor sizing. --- ### 3.2 Derivation — Adiabatic (Constant-Current) Charging Energy > **Goal:** Show that with a constant current source charging $C_L$ over time $T$, the energy dissipated is reduced by a factor of $\frac{R_{on} C_L}{T}$. **Step 1 — Constant Current Source Definition.** For a linear voltage ramp on $C_L$ from $0$ to $V_{dd}$ in time $T$: $$ i(t) = \frac{C_L \, V_{dd}}{T} = I_{0} \quad \text{(constant)} $$ **Step 2 — Voltage Across the Transistor.** At time $t$, the capacitor voltage is $V_C(t) = \dfrac{I_0 \, t}{C_L}$. The voltage drop across the on-resistance $R_{on}$ is: $$ V_{R_{on}}(t) = I_0 \, R_{on} = \frac{C_L \, V_{dd} \, R_{on}}{T} $$ This voltage drop is **constant** and small when $T \gg R_{on} C_L$. **Step 3 — Instantaneous Power Dissipated in the Channel:** $$ P(t) = I_0^2 \, R_{on} = \left( \frac{C_L V_{dd}}{T} \right)^2 R_{on} $$ **Step 4 — Integrate Over the Ramp Duration $T$:** $$ \begin{aligned} E_{adiabatic} &= \int_{0}^{T} I_0^2 \, R_{on} \, dt \\[4pt] &= I_0^2 \, R_{on} \, T \\[4pt] &= \left( \frac{C_L V_{dd}}{T} \right)^2 R_{on} \cdot T \\[4pt] &= \frac{R_{on} C_L^2 V_{dd}^2}{T} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Adiabatic Energy Equation (Final Form):** $$ \boxed{\,E_{adiabatic} = \frac{R_{on} \, C_L}{T} \, C_L V_{dd}^2\,} $$ **Step 6 — The Power-Saving Ratio.** Comparing with conventional CMOS: $$ \frac{E_{adiabatic}}{E_{conv}} = \frac{R_{on} C_L \, C_L V_{dd}^2 / T}{\frac{1}{2} C_L V_{dd}^2} = \frac{2 R_{on} C_L}{T} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Interpretation:** If we choose $T = 10 R_{on} C_L$, the adiabatic gate dissipates only **20%** of the energy of a standard CMOS gate per cycle. Lower frequency ⇒ longer $T$ ⇒ exponentially less power. --- ### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU-Favorite Table) | Parameter | Conventional CMOS | Quasi-Adiabatic (ECRL/PFAL) | Fully Adiabatic (SCRL/CAL) | |---|---|---|---| | Power-Clock | Not required | Trapezoidal | Resonant sinusoidal | | Energy per cycle | $\frac{1}{2} C_L V_{dd}^2$ | $\frac{R_{on}C_L}{T} C_L V_{dd}^2$ | $\left(\frac{R_{on}C_L}{T}\right)^2 C_L V_{dd}^2$ | | Energy Recovery | 0% | ~50–80% | ~95–99% (ideal) | | Transistors per gate | 4 (for inverter) | 4–6 | 6–10 | | Operating Frequency | High | Low–Medium | Very low | | Design Complexity | Low | Moderate | Very high | | Practical Use | Universal | Research/low-power ASIC | Cryogenic / niche | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Architecture: Adiabatic Switching vs Conventional Switching The Mermaid diagram below illustrates the conceptual difference in energy flow between conventional CMOS and adiabatic logic. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[DC Supply V_dd] -- abrupt switch --> B[Load Capacitor C_L] B -- heat loss in NMOS --> G[GND] C[Power Clock PC ramp] -- gradual switch --> D[Load Capacitor C_L] D -- charge returned to source --> C style A fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#d84315 style B fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f57f17 style G fill:#cfd8dc,stroke:#37474f style C fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20 style D fill:#b3e5fc,stroke:#01579b ``` > [!NOTE] > In the **left branch** (conventional), energy flows one-way: from $V_{dd}$ to $C_L$ to GND — half lost as heat. In the **right branch** (adiabatic), the power clock is a rechargeable source, and energy flows back into it after evaluation. --- ### 4.2 ECRL (Efficient Charge Recovery Logic) — Internal Architecture ECRL is a *quasi-adiabatic* gate family. The schematic below shows a generic ECRL AND/NAND gate structure. ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph ECRL_BLOCK[ECRL Inverter Buffer] PC[Power Clock PC]:::pc D1[Diode D1]:::diode D2[Diode D2]:::diode N1[N1 NMOS left]:::nmos N2[N2 NMOS right]:::nmos P1[P1 PMOS left]:::pmos P2[P2 PMOS right]:::pmos OUTB[Outbar Node]:::node OUT[Out Node]:::node GND[(GND)]:::gnd end INA[Input A] --> N1 INB[Input B] --> N2 INA --> P1 INB --> P2 PC --> D1 --> OUT PC --> D2 --> OUTB N1 --> OUT N2 --> OUTB P1 --> OUT P2 --> OUTB N1 --> GND N2 --> GND OUT --> OUTB classDef pc fill:#ffe082,stroke:#ff6f00,color:#000 classDef diode fill:#b39ddb,stroke:#4527a0,color:#fff classDef nmos fill:#90caf9,stroke:#0d47a1,color:#000 classDef pmos fill:#ef9a9a,stroke:#b71c1c,color:#000 classDef node fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000 classDef gnd fill:#37474f,stroke:#000,color:#fff ``` **ECRL Operating Phases:** 1. **Pre-charge:** Power clock PC rises from 0 to $V_{dd}$; both OUT and OUT-bar rise through the diodes. 2. **Evaluate:** Inputs A and B drive the cross-coupled NMOS/PMOS. Depending on the logic, one side pulls down while the other holds. 3. **Hold:** Output stabilizes at the logical value. 4. **Recovery:** PC ramps down; charge on the output capacitor flows back into the power-clock source. > [!IMPORTANT] > The diodes in ECRL are often replaced with diode-connected PMOS transistors for full-CMOS compatibility — a key point expected in KTU derivations. --- ### 4.3 PFAL (Positive Feedback Adiabatic Logic) — Architecture PFAL uses 4 transistors and a cross-coupled latch for stable quasi-adiabatic operation. ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph PFAL_CORE[PFAL Core Latch] PC2[Power Clock PC]:::pc N3[N3 NMOS]:::nmos N4[N4 NMOS]:::nmos P3[P3 PMOS]:::pmos P4[P4 PMOS]:::pmos OUT2[Out]:::node OUT2B[Outbar]:::node end IN3[Input A] --> N3 IN4[Input B] --> N4 OUT2 --> P3 OUT2B --> P4 OUT2B --> N3 OUT2 --> N4 PC2 --> P3 PC2 --> P4 P3 --> OUT2 P4 --> OUT2B N3 --> OUT2 N4 --> OUT2B classDef pc fill:#ffe082,stroke:#ff6f00,color:#000 classDef nmos fill:#90caf9,stroke:#0d47a1,color:#000 classDef pmos fill:#ef9a9a,stroke:#b71c1c,color:#000 classDef node fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000 ``` **Distinctive Feature of PFAL:** The cross-coupling (P3 driven by OUT-bar, P4 driven by OUT) creates positive feedback that **latches** the output — preventing charge leakage during the hold phase. This makes PFAL more robust than ECRL for KTU design problems. --- ### 4.4 Power-Clock Waveform & Output Response ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant PC as Power Clock participant C as Load Capacitor PC->>C: Ramp up (0 → V_dd) slowly Note over C: Charge stored gradually
Low I^2R dissipation C-->>PC: Ramp down (V_dd → 0) slowly Note over PC: Charge returned to source
Energy recovered ``` > [!NOTE] > The ramp-up phase is called the **evaluate** stage, and the ramp-down is the **recover** stage. In trapezoidal clocks, a flat-top **hold** stage sits between them. In resonant sinusoidal clocks (used in fully adiabatic logic), there is no hold stage. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Define adiabatic switching. Why is it called "adiabatic" in the context of low-power VLSI design? **Model Answer:** Adiabatic switching is a low-power CMOS design technique in which the energy stored in the load capacitance is *recovered* back into the power supply by using a slowly varying (ramping or resonant) **power-clock** waveform instead of a constant DC supply. The term "adiabatic" is borrowed from thermodynamics, where an adiabatic process involves no heat exchange with the surroundings. In VLSI, the power-clock ramps so slowly that the voltage across the switching transistor is always small, reducing $I^2R$ dissipation to nearly zero and allowing the charge to be returned to the source during the ramp-down phase. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Differentiate between quasi-adiabatic and fully adiabatic logic families with one example each. **Model Answer:** | Feature | Quasi-Adiabatic | Fully Adiabatic | |---|---|---| | Charge recovery | Partial | Complete (in principle) | | Energy per cycle | $\frac{R_{on}C_L}{T} C_L V_{dd}^2$ | $\left(\frac{R_{on}C_L}{T}\right)^2 C_L V_{dd}^2$ | | Power-clock | Trapezoidal | Resonant sinusoid | | Complexity | Moderate | Very high | | Example | **ECRL, PFAL, 2N-2N2P** | **SCRL, CAL, PAL** | --- ### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > As per KTU 2024 ESE guidelines, each Part B question carries 14 marks with internal choice. The two alternatives below (Question A and Question B) are independent options. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper] **(a)** Derive the expression for energy dissipation per switching cycle in a **conventional CMOS inverter** charging a load capacitance $C_L$ from $V_{dd}$ through an on-resistance $R_{on}$. (7 Marks) **(b)** With the help of a neat schematic, explain the operation of the **ECRL (Efficient Charge Recovery Logic)** inverter. Compare it briefly with PFAL. (7 Marks) **Model Solution:** **(a)** Refer to the derivation in Section 3.1 above. Step-by-step: - The PMOS acts as a switch with on-resistance $R_{on}$. - Charging current: $i(t) = \frac{V_{dd}}{R_{on}} e^{-t/R_{on}C_L}$ - Power in PMOS: $P(t) = \frac{V_{dd}^2}{R_{on}} e^{-2t/R_{on}C_L}$ - Energy dissipated in PMOS: $$ \begin{aligned} E_{pmos} &= \int_{0}^{\infty} P(t)\, dt = \frac{V_{dd}^2}{R_{on}} \cdot \frac{R_{on}C_L}{2} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{dd}^2 \end{aligned} $$ - Symmetric discharge through NMOS dissipates another $\frac{1}{2} C_L V_{dd}^2$. - **Total dissipation per cycle = $C_L V_{dd}^2$**, of which only $\frac{1}{2} C_L V_{dd}^2$ is recoverable as stored charge and the other $\frac{1}{2} C_L V_{dd}^2$ is irreversibly lost. - Key conclusion: **this energy is independent of $R_{on}$ and switching frequency.** **(b)** Refer to Section 4.2 for the schematic. Explanation points to cover: - ECRL uses **two NMOS** (driven by the inputs) and **two PMOS** (cross-coupled) plus **two diodes** (or diode-connected PMOS) for charge delivery from the power clock. - **Evaluate phase:** Power clock (PC) rises. The diodes forward-bias and pre-charge both output nodes. Inputs then determine which side pulls down. - **Hold phase:** Once PC reaches $V_{dd}$, the output is latched by the cross-coupled PMOS pair. - **Recovery phase:** PC ramps down. Charge on the output capacitance flows back through the PMOS and the power clock to the supply, recovering energy. - **Comparison with PFAL:** ECRL has simpler structure (2 diodes + 4 transistors) but lower noise margin; PFAL uses a fully cross-coupled latch (4 transistors, no diodes) for higher robustness but at the cost of more transistors. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper] **(a)** With suitable derivation, show that the energy dissipated per cycle in an **adiabatic gate** with constant-current charging is $\frac{R_{on} C_L^2 V_{dd}^2}{T}$. (7 Marks) **(b)** Explain the operation of a **PFAL (Positive Feedback Adiabatic Logic)** gate. Discuss the practical limitations of adiabatic logic. (7 Marks) **Model Solution:** **(a)** Refer to the derivation in Section 3.2. - Constant current source delivers $I_0 = \frac{C_L V_{dd}}{T}$. - Voltage drop across the transistor: $V_{R_{on}} = I_0 R_{on} = \frac{C_L V_{dd} R_{on}}{T}$ — small when $T \gg R_{on} C_L$. - Power: $P = I_0^2 R_{on} = \left(\frac{C_L V_{dd}}{T}\right)^2 R_{on}$. - Energy over time $T$: $$ \begin{aligned} E &= P \cdot T = \frac{R_{on} C_L^2 V_{dd}^2}{T} \end{aligned} $$ - Hence $E_{adiabatic} \propto \frac{1}{T}$: longer ramp times give exponentially less dissipation. - Compared to conventional: $\frac{E_{adiabatic}}{E_{conv}} = \frac{2 R_{on} C_L}{T}$. **(b)** Refer to Section 4.3 for the PFAL schematic. - PFAL uses **two cross-coupled CMOS inverters** forming a bistable latch, with the power clock driving both PMOS sources. - During **evaluate**, the latch snaps to the correct state based on inputs. - During **hold**, the latch maintains state with negligible leakage. - During **recover**, PC ramps down and the latch charge flows back into the supply. - **Practical limitations of adiabatic logic:** 1. **Slow operating speed** — must satisfy $T \gg R_{on} C_L$. 2. **Complex multi-phase clocking** — at least 2–4 power-clock phases required; routing overhead. 3. **High transistor count** — 4×–6× the area of standard CMOS. 4. **Reduced noise margins** — slow transitions create longer noise-vulnerable windows. 5. **Energy-recovery efficiency < 100%** in real silicon due to leakage, clock-driver losses, and diode drops. 6. **Difficult CAD integration** — standard cell libraries and synthesis tools are not optimized. --- ### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Adiabatic Logic Questions:** > 1. **Forgetting the $\frac{R_{on}C_L}{T}$ factor.** Students often write $E = \frac{1}{2}CV^2$ for adiabatic circuits. This loses 2 marks. The correct quasi-adiabatic form is $E = \frac{R_{on}C_L}{T} C_L V_{dd}^2$. > 2. **Confusing ECRL and PFAL.** ECRL uses diodes; PFAL uses a cross-coupled CMOS latch. Examiners check this strictly. > 3. **Omitting the diode drop in the power-clock path** when describing ECRL. Mentioning the forward-biasing of D1/D2 is essential. > 4. **Stating that adiabatic logic is lossless** — it is only *asymptotically* lossless in the limit $T \to \infty$. > 5. **Skipping the "recovery" phase description** in ECRL/PFAL operation. A complete answer must include all four phases: Pre-charge → Evaluate → Hold → Recover. > 6. **Forgetting units** in derivations — the final adiabatic energy expression must end in Joules. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Adiabatic Logic Gates":** - **Definition:** Adiabatic logic recovers charge from load capacitance back into the supply using a ramping/resonant power-clock, minimizing $I^2R$ dissipation. - **Key Energy Formula (Quasi-Adiabatic):** $\;E = \dfrac{R_{on} C_L}{T} C_L V_{dd}^2$ - **Conventional CMOS Energy:** $\;E = \dfrac{1}{2} C_L V_{dd}^2$ (frequency-independent). - **Adiabatic Advantage Ratio:** $\;\dfrac{E_{adiabatic}}{E_{conv}} = \dfrac{2 R_{on} C_L}{T}$ — small when $T$ is large. - **Two families:** *Quasi-adiabatic* (ECRL, PFAL, 2N-2N2P, TSEL) and *Fully adiabatic* (SCRL, CAL, PAL). - **ECRL structure:** 2 NMOS (input-driven) + 2 PMOS (cross-coupled) + 2 diodes (or diode-PMOS). - **PFAL structure:** 2 cross-coupled CMOS inverters with PMOS sources tied to the power clock. - **Four operating phases:** Pre-charge → Evaluate → Hold → Recover. - **Limitation:** $T \gg R_{on} C_L$ ⇒ inherently slow circuits; complex clock distribution. - **Application areas:** IoT sensors, RFID tags, implantable biomedical devices, cryogenic electronics. - **The two golden rules:** (1) Never switch abruptly. (2) Never dump charge to ground. - **Examiner's hot keywords:** *charge recovery, power-clock, energy-recovery efficiency, ramp duration, reversible logic, quasi-adiabatic.*

topics.title: Pulsed Power Supplies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEECT866 - LOW POWER VLSI > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Pulsed Power Supplies # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Pulsed Power Supply (PPS)** is a specialized power electronics subsystem engineered to compress stored electrical energy over a relatively long duration and release it as a high-magnitude, short-duration pulse across a load. In the context of **Low Power VLSI**, pulsed power supplies refer to on-chip or board-level circuits that generate brief, controlled high-voltage or high-current pulses from a low-voltage continuous source (typically the IC supply rail such as **1.2 V, 1.8 V, or 3.3 V**) to drive specialized loads. Mathematically, pulsed power is characterized by the relationship: $$P_{peak} = \frac{E_{stored}}{t_{pulse}}$$ where $E_{stored}$ is the total energy accumulated in the storage element and $t_{pulse}$ is the discharge (pulse) duration. Because $t_{pulse} \ll t_{charge}$, the instantaneous peak power can be **2 to 6 orders of magnitude** higher than the average input power drawn from the supply. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Pulsed power supplies are studied in Module 4 of PEECT866 because they bridge the gap between *continuous low-voltage VLSI rails* and *high-voltage transient actuator loads* (MEMS, NVM, displays) without violating the low-power design philosophy of the system. ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain-English Explanation) Imagine filling a small bucket with water drop-by-drop from a thin tap (this represents the **slow charging** of a capacitor from a low-voltage VLSI rail). Once the bucket is full, you lift it and pour all the water at once onto a plant (this represents the **fast discharge** as a high-power pulse). > [!NOTE] > **Analogy Decoded:** > - Thin tap water drop-by-drop = Low current charging from VLSI supply over microseconds/milliseconds. > - Full bucket of water = Energy stored in capacitor/inductor. > - Sudden pour = Rapid discharge delivering a high-voltage/current pulse (nanoseconds to microseconds). > - Plant = Load (MEMS actuator, flash memory cell, display pixel). The *average* power consumption remains low because the bucket is filled slowly, but the *instantaneous* power delivered to the load is enormous. ## 1.3 Why Pulsed Power in Low-Power VLSI? Most VLSI systems run on low-voltage rails to minimize dynamic and leakage power. However, certain subsystems inherently require **high electric fields or large currents** to function. Pulsed power supplies elegantly solve this contradiction: | Subsystem Load | Required Pulse | Reason | |---|---|---| | **Flash Memory** (NAND/NOR) | 10–20 V, ~µs pulse | Fowler–Nordheim tunneling for program/erase | | **MEMS Actuators** | 20–200 V, µs–ms | Electrostatic force $\propto V^2$ | | **LCD/OLED Displays** | 5–30 V row/column drive | Pixel addressing & bias generation | | **RF MEMS Switches** | 20–80 V | Pull-in voltage actuation | | **Piezoelectric Drivers** | 10–100 V | Strain-induced displacement | | **Electrostatic Sensors** | 100–1000 V | Charge measurement, particle accelerators | > [!TIP] > A continuous high-voltage supply would waste enormous static power.
