A **pulsed supply** delivers the required energy only when the load needs it, keeping average power minimal — the very heart of *low-power* VLSI design. ## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (in **bold**) - **Characteristic pulse width ($t_p$):** **1 ns – 1 ms** - **Peak power ($P_{peak}$):** **1 mW – 10 kW** (board-scale), up to **MW** (system-scale) - **Pulse repetition rate (PRR):** **1 Hz – 10 MHz** - **Duty cycle ($D$):** $D = t_p \cdot f_{rep}$, typically **0.0001 – 0.1** (i.e., 0.01 % – 10 %) - **Energy per pulse ($E$):** **1 nJ – 1 J** - **Voltage step-up ratio:** **5× – 100×** of VLSI supply - **Dielectric constant of vacuum $\varepsilon_0$:** **8.854 × 10⁻¹² F/m** - **Permittivity of common SiO₂:** **3.9 × $\varepsilon_0$** ≈ **3.45 × 10⁻¹¹ F/m** --- # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Principle — The Three Phases Every VLSI-compatible pulsed power supply operates in three discrete temporal phases: ### Phase 1 — **Energy Storage (Charging Phase)** Energy is accumulated *slowly* from the low-voltage VLSI rail into a reactive element (capacitor $C$ or inductor $L$). The charging happens over a time interval $t_{charge} \gg t_{pulse}$. ### Phase 2 — **Switching / Commutation** A high-speed switch (MOSFET, BJT, SCR, or photoconductive switch) is triggered, redirecting the stored energy from the storage element to the load. This is the most critical phase — switch speed defines pulse rise time. ### Phase 3 — **Pulse Delivery (Discharge Phase)** Energy flows rapidly through the load. The pulse width is set by the **R-L-C time constants** of the discharge network. ## 2.2 Classification of Pulsed Power Topologies (KTU Focus) ### 2.2.1 Capacitive Pulsed Power (Most Common in VLSI) - **Storage element:** Capacitor $C$ - **Operating principle:** Capacitor pre-charged to $V_{ch}$ via a charging resistor or current source; discharged into load through a switch. - **Energy stored:** $E = \tfrac{1}{2} C V_{ch}^2$ - **Used for:** MEMS, NVM, displays, electrostatic actuators ### 2.2.2 Inductive Pulsed Power - **Storage element:** Inductor $L$ - **Operating principle:** Current $I_0$ built up in inductor; switch opened forces current into load via a commutating diode (current continues through $L$). - **Energy stored:** $E = \tfrac{1}{2} L I_0^2$ - **Used for:** High-current pulse applications, magnetic actuation, plasma generation ### 2.2.3 Charge Pump / Voltage Multiplier (On-Chip VLSI) - **Storage element:** Multiple flying capacitors - **Operating principle:** Clocked capacitor network cascades charge to multiply voltage. - **Used for:** Fully integrated NVM programming, EEPROM, on-chip high-voltage bias ### 2.2.4 Boost / Flyback Converter based PPS - **Storage element:** Inductor (with transformer in flyback) - **Operating principle:** Switching converter steps up DC voltage; output switch delivers pulse to load. - **Used for:** Board-level supplies feeding MEMS drivers, piezoelectric transformers ### 2.2.5 Piezoelectric Transformer based PPS - **Storage element:** Mechanical strain energy in piezoelectric ceramic - **Operating principle:** Input AC excites mechanical resonance; output electrodes step up voltage. - **Used for:** Compact, high-voltage low-power systems (CCFL backlight, MEMS) ## 2.3 The 'Why' Behind the Choices - **Capacitive PPS** is preferred when **load impedance is high and pulse width is short** (electrostatic actuators). The capacitor behaves as a stiff voltage source during the pulse. - **Inductive PPS** is preferred when **load impedance is low and high current is needed** (magnetic coils, plasma, igniters). The inductor behaves as a stiff current source. - **Charge pumps** are preferred for **fully on-chip integration** where the highest possible energy density per unit area is required and pulse current is modest. - **Boost/Flyback** is preferred when **continuous bias** is also needed alongside the pulse (e.g., display bias rail + row pulse). > [!NOTE] > **Rule of Thumb for KTU exams:** "Capacitor for voltage pulse, Inductor for current pulse" — remember this for the 3-mark and 14-mark questions. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Units / Typical Value | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $E = \tfrac{1}{2} C V^2$ | Energy stored in capacitor | Joules (J) | | 2 | $E = \tfrac{1}{2} L I^2$ | Energy stored in inductor | Joules (J) | | 3 | $P_{peak} = \dfrac{E}{t_{pulse}}$ | Peak pulse power | Watts (W) | | 4 | $\tau = R C$ | RC discharge time constant | Seconds (s) | | 5 | $\tau = L / R$ | RL discharge time constant | Seconds (s) | | 6 | $V_{out} = \dfrac{V_{in}}{1 - D}$ | Boost converter output voltage | Volts (V) | | 7 | $V_{out} = (N+1) \cdot V_{in}$ | N-stage Dickson charge pump output | Volts (V) | | 8 | $D = t_p \cdot f_{rep}$ | Duty cycle (dimensionless) | 0 – 1 | | 9 | $V_c(t) = V_0 \, e^{-t/RC}$ | Capacitor discharge waveform | Volts (V) | | 10 | $I_L(t) = I_0 \, e^{-Rt/L}$ | Inductor discharge waveform | Amperes (A) | | 11 | $F = \tfrac{1}{2} \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d^2} V^2$ | Electrostatic force on parallel plate | Newtons (N) | | 12 | $V_{pull\text{-}in} = \sqrt{\dfrac{8 k d_0^3}{27 \varepsilon_0 A}}$ | MEMS pull-in voltage | Volts (V) | | 13 | $E_{pulse} = P_{avg} \cdot T_{rep}$ | Energy per pulse from average power | Joules (J) | | 14 | $I_{avg} = I_{peak} \cdot D$ | Average current = Peak × Duty | Amperes (A) | | 15 | $f_{res} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$ | LC resonant frequency | Hertz (Hz) | > [!IMPORTANT] > All formulas above are **dimensionally and physically verified**. They are the most frequently tested in KTU examinations for Module 4. Memorize the **first 8** for Part A and the **full set** for Part B derivations. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Consumer electronics:** Flash memory programming in smartphones, DSLR sensors. - **Automotive MEMS:** Airbag accelerometers, gyroscopes, fuel injectors (piezo-driven). - **Medical implants:** Cochlear implant stimulators, neural probes use charge-balanced biphasic pulses. - **Industrial printing:** Inkjet piezoelectric driver boards. - **Aerospace:** Cold-gas thruster ignition systems, satellite propulsion. --- # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Energy Stored in a Capacitor (Charging from VLSI Rail) A capacitor $C$ is charged from a low-voltage VLSI supply $V_{DD}$ through a charging resistor $R_{ch}$ (or constant-current source). We derive the energy delivered to the capacitor at full charge. **Step 1 — Instantaneous power into the capacitor.** The current charging the capacitor is: $$i_C(t) = C \frac{dv_C}{dt}$$ The instantaneous power delivered to the capacitor is: $$p_C(t) = v_C(t) \cdot i_C(t) = v_C(t) \cdot C \frac{dv_C}{dt}$$ **Step 2 — Total energy by integration from 0 to $V_{DD}$.** We integrate the power over the entire charging duration. The total energy is: $$E = \int_0^{T} p_C(t) \, dt = \int_0^{V_{DD}} v_C \cdot C \, dv_C$$ **Step 3 — Evaluate the integral.** $$E = C \int_0^{V_{DD}} v_C \, dv_C = C \left[ \frac{v_C^2}{2} \right]_0^{V_{DD}}$$ **Step 4 — Final result.** $$\boxed{E = \frac{1}{2} C \, V_{DD}^2}$$ > **Conversion Logic:** Step 2 changes the integration variable from time $t$ to capacitor voltage $v_C$ using the relation $i_C \, dt = C \, dv_C$, which is a direct rearrangement of the capacitor $i$-$v$ law. **Numerical example (typical KTU-style):** If $C = 1 \;\mu F$ and $V_{DD} = 3.3\;V$: $$E = \frac{1}{2} \times 10^{-6} \times (3.3)^2 = 5.445 \;\mu J$$ ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Discharge of a Capacitor into a Resistive Load The capacitor pre-charged to $V_0$ is suddenly connected to a load $R_L$ at $t = 0$ through an ideal switch. **Step 1 — KVL around the loop.** $$v_C(t) = v_{R_L}(t) = i(t) \cdot R_L$$ **Step 2 — Capacitor i-v relation.** $$i(t) = -C \frac{dv_C}{dt}$$ (The negative sign indicates discharging current flowing out of the capacitor.) **Step 3 — Substitute into KVL.** $$v_C = -C R_L \frac{dv_C}{dt}$$ Rearranging: $$\frac{dv_C}{v_C} = -\frac{dt}{R_L C}$$ **Step 4 — Integrate both sides with initial condition $v_C(0) = V_0$.** $$\int_{V_0}^{v_C(t)} \frac{dv_C'}{v_C'} = -\frac{1}{R_L C} \int_0^t dt'$$ $$\ln\left(\frac{v_C(t)}{V_0}\right) = -\frac{t}{R_L C}$$ **Step 5 — Exponentiate to obtain the discharge waveform.** $$\boxed{v_C(t) = V_0 \, e^{-t/\tau}, \quad \tau = R_L C}$$ The current is: $$i(t) = \frac{V_0}{R_L} \, e^{-t/\tau}$$ The peak current is $I_{peak} = V_0 / R_L$ at $t = 0^+$. > **Conversion Logic:** The exponential decay arises from first-order linear ODE with constant coefficients. The time constant $\tau = R_L C$ governs how fast the capacitor releases its energy; in practice, the pulse is considered "delivered" within $3\tau$ (≈ 95 % of energy). ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Peak Power and Energy in a Pulsed Capacitive Discharge **Step 1 — Instantaneous power dissipated in $R_L$.** $$p(t) = \frac{v_C^2(t)}{R_L} = \frac{V_0^2}{R_L} \, e^{-2t/\tau}$$ **Step 2 — Peak power occurs at $t = 0$.** $$P_{peak} = \frac{V_0^2}{R_L}$$ **Step 3 — Total energy dissipated (integral of power over the full pulse).** $$E_{total} = \int_0^{\infty} p(t) \, dt = \frac{V_0^2}{R_L} \int_0^{\infty} e^{-2t/\tau} \, dt$$ **Step 4 — Evaluate the integral.** $$E_{total} = \frac{V_0^2}{R_L} \cdot \frac{\tau}{2} = \frac{V_0^2}{R_L} \cdot \frac{R_L C}{2}$$ **Step 5 — Final result.** $$\boxed{E_{total} = \frac{1}{2} C V_0^2}$$ This confirms the energy conservation principle: all the stored energy is dissipated in the load. ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Boost Converter as a Pulsed Power Front-End A boost converter charges the output capacitor $C_{out}$ to a high voltage $V_{out}$, which is then switched to the load as a pulse. **Step 1 — Switch in ON state (duration $D \cdot T_s$).** Inductor charges; voltage across inductor: $V_L = V_{in}$. Inductor current rises: $$\Delta I_L^{on} = \frac{V_{in}}{L} \cdot D T_s$$ **Step 2 — Switch in OFF state (duration $(1-D) T_s$).** Inductor releases energy to output; voltage across inductor: $V_L = V_{in} - V_{out}$. In volt-second balance over steady state: $$V_{in} \cdot D T_s + (V_{in} - V_{out})(1-D)T_s = 0$$ **Step 3 — Solve for $V_{out}$.** $$V_{in} D + V_{in}(1-D) - V_{out}(1-D) = 0$$ $$V_{in} - V_{out}(1-D) = 0$$ $$\boxed{V_{out} = \frac{V_{in}}{1 - D}}$$ **Step 4 — Pulsed delivery.** Once $C_{out}$ is charged to $V_{out}$, the output switch (typically a high-voltage MOSFET) connects $C_{out}$ to the load for a controlled duration $t_{pulse}$. The energy delivered is $E_{pulse} = \tfrac{1}{2} C_{out} V_{out}^2$. ## 3.5 Derivation 5 — N-Stage Dickson Charge Pump Output Voltage A Dickson charge pump uses $N$ stages with two clock phases $\phi_1, \phi_2$. **Step 1 — Voltage gain per stage (no load).** Each ideal pumping capacitor adds $V_{DD}$ (or $V_{clk}$) per stage. For a Dickson charge pump with amplitude $V_{clk}$: $$\Delta V_{stage} = V_{DD} \cdot \frac{C}{C + C_{par}} \approx V_{DD}$$ **Step 2 — Total output voltage (open circuit).** $$\boxed{V_{out} = V_{DD} + N \cdot V_{clk,pk}}$$ For clock amplitude $V_{clk} = V_{DD}$: $$V_{out} = (N + 1) \cdot V_{DD}$$ **Step 3 — Loaded output (with load current $I_L$).** $$V_{out} = (N+1) V_{DD} - \frac{N \cdot I_L}{f_{clk} \cdot C}$$ This shows the **load-dependent droop** — a critical design consideration in on-chip high-voltage generators. ## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Pulsed Power Topologies | Parameter | Capacitive PPS | Inductive PPS | Charge Pump | Boost + Switch | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Storage** | Capacitor | Inductor | Flying capacitor | Output capacitor | | **Pulse nature** | Voltage pulse | Current pulse | Voltage pulse | Voltage pulse | | **Pulse width** | ns – µs | µs – ms | µs – ms | µs – ms | | **Integration** | Off-chip (typically) | Off-chip (typically) | On-chip possible | Off-chip | | **Peak power** | High (kW range) | Very high (MW possible) | Low–Medium (mW) | Medium–High | | **Efficiency** | 70–90 % | 60–85 % | 50–80 % | 80–95 % | | **Best for** | MEMS, NVM | Plasma, magnetic | EEPROM, NVM row | Displays, piezoelectric | | **Complexity** | Low | Medium | Medium | High | --- # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Generic VLSI Pulsed Power Supply ```mermaid flowchart TD A[VDD Low Voltage VLSI Rail
1.2 V to 3.3 V] --> B[DC DC Pre Regulator
Filters and stabilizes supply] B --> C[Energy Storage Element
Capacitor or Inductor Bank] C --> D[High Speed Commutation Switch
MOSFET BJT or SCR] E[Control Logic
Timing Trigger and PRR] --> D D --> F[Pulse Shaping Network
R L C Pulse Forming Line] F --> G[High Voltage Pulse Output
10 V to 200 V Pulse] G --> H[VLSI Load
MEMS Actuator NVM Cell Display Pixel] H --> I[Feedback Sensor
Voltage Current Monitor] I --> E C --> J[Bleed Resistor
Safety Discharge Path] ``` ## 4.2 Detailed Capacitive Pulsed Power Subsystem ```mermaid flowchart LR A1[VDD Supply] --> A2[Charging Resistor Rch
or Constant Current Source] A2 --> A3[Storage Capacitor Cstore
Precharged to Vch] A3 --> A4[High Voltage Switch SW
Triggered by Control] A4 --> A5[Load Rload and Lload
MEMS or NVM] A5 --> A6[Ground Return Path] A3 --> A7[Bleed Resistor Rbleed
Passive Safety] A8[Trigger Pulse Generator] --> A4 ``` ## 4.3 Charge Pump / Voltage Multiplier Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR B1[VDD Low Voltage] --> B2[Clock Phase phi1 Generator] B1 --> B3[Clock Phase phi2 Generator] B2 --> B4[Stage 1
Diode and Flying Cap C1] B3 --> B4 B4 --> B5[Stage 2
Diode and Flying Cap C2] B5 --> B6[Stage N
Final Stage Diode] B6 --> B7[Output Capacitor Cout
Smoothing Filter] B7 --> B8[High Voltage Output Vout equals N plus 1 times VDD] B8 --> B9[VLSI Load
NVM Programming] ``` ## 4.4 Pulsed Power Supply Integrated with a MEMS Actuator Load ```mermaid flowchart TD C1[VDD 1.8 V Rail] --> C2[Oscillator and Timing Block] C2 --> C3[Boost Converter Stage] C3 --> C4[High Voltage Capacitor CHV
Charged to VHV] C4 --> C5[Level Shifter and Gate Driver] C5 --> C6[High Voltage MOSFET SW] C6 --> C7[MEMS Actuator Load
Capacitive Plates] C7 --> C8[Mechanical Displacement Output] C4 --> C9[Charge Balance Monitor] C9 --> C2 C10[Safety Bleed] --> C4 ``` ## 4.5 Signal Waveform — Pulsed Output Relative to VLSI Rail ```mermaid flowchart LR D1[Time t in microseconds] --> D2[Continuous VDD 1.8 V
VLSI Supply Rail] D1 --> D3[Pulsed Output 20 V
Triggered by SW] D2 -.-> D4[Amplitude Difference
11x Step Up] D3 -.-> D4 D1 --> D5[Load Current Pulse
Peak Ipeak for tpulse] D1 --> D6[Control Trigger
Narrow Logic Pulse] ``` ## 4.6 Decision Flow for Choosing a PPS Topology ```mermaid flowchart TD E1[Start Selection] --> E2{Is load fully on chip?} E2 -- Yes --> E3[Use Charge Pump] E2 -- No --> E4{Is pulse width less than 1 microsecond?} E4 -- Yes --> E5[Use Capacitive PPS] E4 -- No --> E6{Is load magnetic or low impedance?} E6 -- Yes --> E7[Use Inductive PPS] E6 -- No --> E8{Need continuous bias plus pulse?} E8 -- Yes --> E9[Use Boost plus Output Switch] E8 -- No --> E10[Use Piezo Transformer PPS] E3 --> E11[End] E5 --> E11 E7 --> E11 E9 --> E11 E10 --> E11 ``` > [!TIP] > **Mermaid Compilation Note:** All node IDs above are purely alphanumeric and contain no reserved keywords. Labels are kept in plain uppercase text without markdown formatting to ensure clean rendering in GitHub, Obsidian, and VS Code. --- # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1 **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 (Model)]** **Define a pulsed power supply. Why is it preferred over a continuous high-voltage supply in low-power VLSI systems?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A pulsed power supply is a power electronics circuit that stores energy slowly from a low-voltage VLSI rail and releases it rapidly as a high-magnitude pulse of short duration to a specialized load. * **(1 mark)** Definition of pulsed power supply with energy compression concept. * **(1 mark)** Statement of low average power: $P_{avg} = E_{pulse} \cdot f_{rep}$ remains small because $f_{rep}$ is low. * **(1 mark)** Continuous HV supply would dissipate $P = V^2/R$ continuously → wasted static power. Pulsed delivery saves energy and avoids thermal stress on the IC. --- ### Question A2 **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 (Model)]** **State the expressions for the energy stored in a capacitor and an inductor. Which is preferred for an electrostatic MEMS actuator and why?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **(1 mark)** $E_C = \tfrac{1}{2} C V^2$ for capacitor; $E_L = \tfrac{1}{2} L I^2$ for inductor. - **(1 mark)** A capacitor is preferred for an electrostatic MEMS actuator because the actuator is fundamentally a *capacitive load* (parallel plates), and a capacitor source can deliver a near-ideal voltage pulse with a defined $V$-$t$ profile. - **(1 mark)** Inductive sources would be mismatched (would force a current into a high-impedance capacitive load, producing a high $L \frac{di}{dt}$ voltage spike that is hard to control). --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) ### Question Set B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice --- ### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 (Model)]** **(a)** With a neat functional block diagram, explain the operating principle of a **capacitive pulsed power supply** used in VLSI applications. Derive the expression for the peak power delivered to a resistive load $R_L$ when a capacitor $C$ pre-charged to voltage $V_0$ is discharged through it. (7 marks) **(b)** A capacitive pulsed power supply is required to deliver a peak power of **100 W** into a **50 $\Omega$** load with a pulse width of **1 $\mu$s**. The VLSI supply rail is **3.3 V**. Calculate: - (i) the required pre-charge voltage $V_0$, - (ii) the storage capacitance $C$, - (iii) the average power drawn from the VLSI rail if the pulse repetition rate is **1 kHz**, - (iv) the duty cycle. (7 marks) #### **Model Solution (a) — 7 marks** **Step 1 — Block Diagram (2 marks):** The capacitive PPS consists of: VLSI supply → charging resistor $R_{ch}$ → storage capacitor $C$ → high-speed switch (MOSFET) → resistive load $R_L$ → ground. The control logic triggers the switch. **Step 2 — Operating Principle (2 marks):** The capacitor $C$ is charged slowly from the VLSI supply through $R_{ch}$ over a time constant $\tau_{ch} = R_{ch} C$. Once charged to $V_0$, the switch is closed. The capacitor discharges through $R_L$ with time constant $\tau_{dis} = R_L C$. **Step 3 — Derivation of Peak Power (3 marks):** At the instant the switch closes ($t = 0^+$), the full capacitor voltage $V_0$ appears across $R_L$. The peak current is: $$I_{peak} = \frac{V_0}{R_L}$$ The peak power dissipated in $R_L$ is: $$P_{peak} = I_{peak}^2 \cdot R_L = \frac{V_0^2}{R_L}$$ --- #### **Model Solution (b) — 7 marks** **Step 1 — Given data (1 mark):** $P_{peak} = 100$ W, $R_L = 50\;\Omega$, $t_p = 1\;\mu s$, $V_{DD} = 3.3$ V, $f_{rep} = 1$ kHz. **Step 2 — Calculate $V_0$ (2 marks):** $$V_0 = \sqrt{P_{peak} \cdot R_L} = \sqrt{100 \times 50} = \sqrt{5000}$$ $$\boxed{V_0 \approx 70.71 \text{ V}}$$ **Step 3 — Calculate $C$ (2 marks):** Using the discharge relation, in one time constant $\tau = R_L C$ the capacitor drops to ~37 %. For a 1 $\mu$s pulse (≈ one time constant), we set $\tau \approx t_p$: $$C = \frac{\tau}{R_L} = \frac{1 \times 10^{-6}}{50}$$ $$\boxed{C = 20 \text{ nF}}$$ **Step 4 — Calculate average power (1 mark):** $$P_{avg} = E_{pulse} \cdot f_{rep} = \frac{1}{2} C V_0^2 \cdot f_{rep}$$ $$P_{avg} = \frac{1}{2} \times 20 \times 10^{-9} \times (70.71)^2 \times 10^3$$ $$P_{avg} = 0.5 \times 20 \times 10^{-9} \times 5000 \times 10^3 = 50 \text{ mW}$$ $$\boxed{P_{avg} = 50 \text{ mW}}$$ **Step 5 — Calculate duty cycle (1 mark):** $$D = t_p \cdot f_{rep} = 10^{-6} \times 10^3 = 10^{-3} = 0.1\,\%$$ $$\boxed{D = 0.001 \; (0.1\%)}$$ --- ### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE** **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 (Model)]** **(a)** Explain the operation of a **Dickson charge pump** as an on-chip pulsed power supply. Derive the no-load output voltage for an $N$-stage pump. List two limitations of charge pumps in pulsed power applications. (7 marks) **(b)** A 5-stage Dickson charge pump is driven by a VLSI clock of amplitude **1.8 V** at **10 MHz**. The load requires a pulsed current of **100 $\mu$A** at an output voltage of at least **8 V**. Each flying capacitor is **10 pF**. Determine: - (i) the no-load output voltage, - (ii) the loaded output voltage (droop included), - (iii) the conduction (load) losses per stage. (7 marks) #### **Model Solution (a) — 7 marks** **Step 1 — Operating Principle (3 marks):** A Dickson charge pump uses two non-overlapping clock phases $\phi_1$ and $\phi_2$ to successively transfer charge from $V_{DD}$ to the output node through diode-capacitor pairs. Each stage adds the clock amplitude to the DC level. Charge flows stage by stage, with the diode preventing back-flow. The output capacitor $C_{out}$ smooths the resulting DC high voltage, which can then be switched to the load as a pulse. **Step 2 — Derivation (3 marks):** For an N-stage Dickson pump with clock amplitude $V_{clk}$, the per-stage voltage gain is $V_{clk}$ (assuming $C \gg C_{par}$). Summing $N$ stages plus the input $V_{DD}$: $$\boxed{V_{out,NL} = V_{DD} + N \cdot V_{clk}}$$ For $V_{clk} = V_{DD}$: $$V_{out,NL} = (N+1) \cdot V_{DD}$$ **Step 3 — Two Limitations (1 mark):** - **Threshold voltage drop** per stage due to diode-connected MOSFETs — reduces effective gain. - **Load-dependent droop** — at high load currents, output voltage sags significantly because flying capacitors cannot replenish charge fast enough. --- #### **Model Solution (b) — 7 marks** **Step 1 — Given data (1 mark):** $N = 5$, $V_{clk} = 1.8$ V, $f_{clk} = 10$ MHz, $I_L = 100\;\mu A$, $C = 10$ pF, $V_{out,min} = 8$ V. **Step 2 — No-load output voltage (2 marks):** $$V_{out,NL} = V_{DD} + N \cdot V_{clk} = 1.8 + 5 \times 1.8 = 1.8 + 9.0$$ $$\boxed{V_{out,NL} = 10.8 \text{ V}}$$ **Step 3 — Loaded output voltage (3 marks):** Using the droop formula $V_{out} = V_{out,NL} - \dfrac{N \cdot I_L}{f_{clk} \cdot C}$: $$\Delta V = \frac{N \cdot I_L}{f_{clk} \cdot C} = \frac{5 \times 100 \times 10^{-6}}{10 \times 10^6 \times 10 \times 10^{-12}}$$ $$\Delta V = \frac{5 \times 10^{-4}}{10^{-4}} = 5 \text{ V}$$ $$\boxed{V_{out,loaded} = 10.8 - 5 = 5.8 \text{ V}}$$ > **Engineering insight (1 mark):** Since 5.8 V < 8 V (required), the design is **inadequate** — the designer must either increase $N$, increase $C$, or increase $f_{clk}$. This kind of "design check" is a KTU-favorite follow-up. **Step 4 — Conduction losses per stage (1 mark):** $$P_{cond,stage} = I_L \cdot V_{drop,stage} = 100\;\mu A \times 1\;V = 100\;\mu W$$ > **Note:** The total loss scales as $N \cdot P_{cond,stage}$ (5 × 100 µW = 500 µW), which directly impacts the low-power budget. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on Pulsed Power Questions:** > 1. **Forgetting the duty cycle distinction:** Many students confuse *peak* power with *average* power. Always restate: $P_{avg} = P_{peak} \cdot D$ and $E_{pulse} = P_{avg} \cdot T_{rep}$. > 2. **Mixing capacitor and inductor energy formulas:** Writing $E = \tfrac{1}{2} L V^2$ or $E = \tfrac{1}{2} C I^2$ costs 1 mark immediately. > 3. **Ignoring diode $V_{th}$ in charge pumps:** For MOSFET-diode Dickson pumps, subtract $V_{th}$ per stage — this is a common test point in 14-mark problems. > 4. **Not specifying initial conditions:** A derivation without stating $v_C(0) = V_0$ or $i_L(0) = I_0$ is incomplete. Examiners deduct 1 mark. > 5. **Confusing "boost converter output" with "pulse amplitude":** Boost gives DC HV, not a pulse. The pulse is formed by an *output switch* downstream of the boost stage. > 6. **Wrong sign in inductor discharge:** The exponential decay is $e^{-Rt/L}$, not $e^{Rt/L}$. Many students get this wrong under exam pressure. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Last-Minute Revision — Top 12 Things You Must Know About Pulsed Power Supplies** - ✅ **Definition:** A PPS compresses energy in time — slow charge, fast discharge. - ✅ **Core relation:** $P_{peak} = E / t_{pulse}$; average power $P_{avg} = E \cdot f_{rep}$. - ✅ **Capacitive PPS:** $E = \tfrac{1}{2} C V^2$ → use for **voltage pulses** to capacitive loads. - ✅ **Inductive PPS:** $E = \tfrac{1}{2} L I^2$ → use for **current pulses** to low-impedance/magnetic loads. - ✅ **RC discharge:** $v_C(t) = V_0 e^{-t/RC}$, $\tau = R_L C$, $P_{peak} = V_0^2 / R_L$. - ✅ **Boost converter output:** $V_{out} = V_{in} / (1 - D)$; **Duty cycle** $D \in (0,1)$. - ✅ **Charge pump output:** $V_{out,NL} = (N+1) \cdot V_{DD}$ (no load); loaded: $V_{out,NL} - N I_L / (f_{clk} C)$. - ✅ **Pulse width choice:** governed by load time constant; pulse is "done" in $\approx 3\tau$. - ✅ **Why pulsed over continuous HV?** Saves static power; reduces thermal stress; allows low-voltage IC rails to drive HV loads. - ✅ **Typical applications:** Flash memory programming (10–20 V), MEMS actuation (20–200 V), displays (5–30 V), RF MEMS (20–80 V), piezoelectric drivers. - ✅ **On-chip feasibility:** Only charge pumps and small capacitive PPS are fully integrable; inductive PPS require bulky off-chip magnetics. - ✅ **Low-power design rule:** Always minimize the duty cycle $D$ to minimize average power; keep $f_{rep}$ as low as the application allows. > **Final Note:** Pulsed power supplies are the unsung heroes of low-power VLSI — they let designers enjoy the *low power* of sub-2 V rails while still being able to *actuate* and *program* loads that inherently need tens or hundreds of volts. Master the five derivations and the formula sheet above, and you will comfortably score full marks on any Module 4 question on this topic. ---
